"I know enough." Jiang Chen unrolled the scroll and pointed at the signature. "This seal is real. This signature is real. Judge Zhao approved my execution, and he did it knowing the evidence was fabricated."

"Evidence." Cui's voice was cold. "You have proof of this?"

"I have a witness."

"A witness." Cui's expression flickered. "Who?"

"The woman I was chasing. Madam Liu. She possessed the prosecutor. She saw the files. She knows everything."

Cui was silent for a long moment. Then he did something Jiang Chen didn't expect.

He smiled.

"Good," he said. "That's very good."

"What?"

"You've done exactly what I hoped you would." Cui stepped closer, his voice dropping to a whisper. "I've been waiting for someone to investigate Judge Zhao for forty years. I've been waiting for someone who could get close to him. Someone who had a personal stake in his downfall."

"You knew?"

"I suspected." Cui's eyes were hard, ancient, tired. "But I couldn't prove it. Zhao is too careful. He covers his tracks too well. I needed someone to rattle his cage. Someone he wouldn't see coming."

"Someone like me."

"Someone with nothing to lose." Cui reached out and took the scroll from Jiang Chen's hands. "You've given me what I need. Your testimony. The witness. The forged evidence. It's enough to open an investigation."

"Then let's go."

"We can't. Not yet." Cui rolled the scroll and tucked it into his robe. "Zhao has allies. Powerful ones. If we move too soon, he'll destroy the evidence and bury this so deep no one will ever find it."

"Then when?"

"When he least expects it." Cui turned and walked toward the archives entrance. "Go home, apprentice. Get some sleep. Tomorrow, we begin the real work."

"Wait."

Cui stopped but didn't turn around.

"What about Madam Liu?"

"She's your witness. Keep her safe. Keep her hidden." A pause. "And Jiang Chen?"

"Yeah?"

"Trust no one. Not even me."

And then the old man was gone, swallowed by the darkness of the archives.

Jiang Chen stood alone in the silent room, the weight of the truth pressing down on him like a mountain.

He'd been framed.

His execution was a murder.

And the man who'd signed his death warrant was one of the most powerful beings in the underworld.

He pulled out his phone to check the time. A message was waiting for him.

Unknown number: *I know who killed you. I know why. Meet me at the North Bridge. Midnight. Come alone.*

Jiang Chen stared at the message.

It was from Madam Liu.

She'd called him early.

And she'd changed the deal.

title: The Furnace Keeper's Confession
content:
The black light from Jiang Chen's staff didn't just push her back—it *burned*. The con woman's soul form twisted, smoke curling from where the staff had struck her chest. She released his mind with a shriek that rattled the windows of the empty apartment.

"You—" She clutched at herself, her form flickering like a candle in wind. "That was *soul-fire*. Where did you get soul-fire? New reapers don't get soul-fire."

Jiang Chen didn't answer. He was too busy trying to stay upright. The blast had drained something from him—a warmth that was seeping out of his chest, leaving him cold. He looked down at his hands. They were slightly translucent. *Fading.*

"The staff," he gasped. "It's connected to... something."

"Connected to *you*, idiot." She circled him, keeping her distance. "Every reaper's staff is forged from their own soul-thread. It's why you can't drop it, can't lose it, can't give it away. It's *you*. And when you channel that much power through it, you burn yourself."

He looked at the staff. The black wood was warm, almost hot. He could feel it pulsing in rhythm with his heart. *His heart.* He was dead. He shouldn't have a heart.

"What are you?" he whispered.

"Your only chance at the truth." She stopped circling. "But you already know I'm not going to give it to you for free. So let's make a real deal. Not the one I was faking."

"You tried to possess me."

"I'm a con woman. It's what I do." She shrugged. "But you stopped me. Twice. That means you're either very lucky or very stubborn. I'm betting on stubborn." She extended her hand again. "Real deal this time. I'll take you to someone who knows what happened to you. Not me—someone who was *there*."

"Where?"

"Diyu. But not the Hall of Judgment. The Furnace."

Jiang Chen's training flashed through his mind. The Furnace. The sixth level of Diyu, where souls were purified through fire before reincarnation. It was off-limits to junior reapers. It was off-limits to *everyone* except the Furnace Keeper.

"The Keeper won't see me."

"He'll see *me*." Her smile was sharp. "I know his daughter."

"His daughter is dead. That's why he's the Furnace Keeper—he's been down there for three hundred years, burning souls, because he couldn't save her."

"Who do you think I am?"

The words hit him like a physical blow. He stared at her—this woman who had stolen bodies, stolen lives, stolen everything she could get her hands on. This con woman who had killed two reapers.

"You're his daughter?"

"I was." Her form flickered. "Three hundred years ago, I was Mei Lin, daughter of the Furnace Keeper. I died of a fever when I was nineteen. My father couldn't accept it. He broke every rule in Diyu trying to bring me back. The judges gave him a choice: take the Furnace and burn souls for eternity, or have me destroyed completely. He chose the Furnace."

"And you?"

"I chose to run." Her voice hardened. "I escaped Diyu before they could process me. I've been possessing bodies ever since. Staying alive. Staying hidden. Staying one step ahead."

"Your father has been burning souls for three centuries, and you've been running for three centuries. And you think he'll help me?"

"I think he'll help *you*." She met his eyes. "Because you're the first reaper who's ever caught me. And he needs to know why."

"Why?"

"Because someone in Diyu has been feeding him false souls." She lowered her voice. "For the past fifty years, the souls he burns—they're not the ones the judges sentenced. They're innocent. Framed. Just like you."

Jiang Chen's grip on the staff tightened. "How do you know this?"

"Because I've been possessing people who work in Diyu." She smiled, but there was no humor in it. "You think I've been hiding for three centuries? I've been *investigating*. Someone is corrupting the entire system. They're feeding innocent souls to the Furnace, and the judges don't know—or they don't care."

"And you want to stop them."

"I want to save my father." Her voice cracked. "He's been burning innocents for fifty years. When he finds out, it will destroy him. But if I can bring him the proof—if I can show him who's behind it—maybe he can stop. Maybe he can finally let me go."

"Let you go?"

"He's been holding my soul-thread." She touched her chest. "That's why I can't move on. That's why I'm stuck here, possessing bodies, running from reapers. He's been keeping me tethered to this world because he can't bear to let me go. But if I can give him this—if I can show him the truth—he'll release me. And I can finally die."

The apartment was silent. Outside, the first light of dawn was creeping over the buildings. Jiang Chen's shift was almost over. He'd failed his mission. He'd let a rogue soul talk to him, bargain with him, *manipulate* him.

But she wasn't lying. He could feel it. Everything she'd said—about his execution, about the corruption, about the Furnace—it all rang true.

"The names," he said. "Give me one name."

"One name?"

"Someone who's alive. Someone who was involved in my framing. Give me one name, and I'll take you to the Furnace."

She studied him for a long moment. Then she nodded.

"Chen Wei."

The name hit him like a punch to the gut. Chen Wei. His former boss at the accounting firm. The man who had recommended him for the promotion. The man who had taken him out for drinks the night before his arrest.

"Chen Wei was the one who set me up?"

"He was the one who collected the payment." She watched his face. "Someone paid him to make you disappear. He found the evidence. He planted the documents. He made sure you were convicted."

"How do you know this?"

"I possessed him." She smiled. "Three weeks after your execution, I possessed Chen Wei. I wanted to see what kind of man would sell his employee for money. And I found everything. The payment records. The emails. The names of everyone involved."

"Give me the names."

"Take me to the Furnace first."

"You already have one name. That's more than I had five minutes ago." He stepped closer. "Give me more."

"No. The Furnace first."

"Then we're at an impasse." He raised his staff. "And I still have a job to do."

"You won't use that again." She didn't flinch. "You can barely stand. One more blast and you'll fade completely. You'll be a soul without a body, and then you'll be the one running from reapers."

She was right. He could feel his strength seeping away, his form growing weaker. He needed to return to Diyu soon, or he'd be stuck in the mortal realm as a ghost.

"Fine." He lowered the staff. "I'll take you to the Furnace. But we do this my way."

"Your way?"

"I call for backup. We go in with another reaper watching your every move. If you try anything, they'll destroy you before I can blink."

"You don't have another reaper."

"No. But I have a signal." He pulled a small jade token from his pocket. "Every reaper gets one. If I break it, a senior reaper appears within thirty seconds."

"And you're willing to use it?"

"I'm willing to risk it." He held the token between two fingers. "This is my first mission. If I fail, I'll be scrubbing soul-stains for a century. But if I bring you to the Furnace and you deliver on your promise, I'll have something worth more than a successful mission."

"Revenge."

"Justice." He corrected her. "I want the truth. I want the people who killed me to pay for it. And I want to make sure no one else gets framed and executed while the real killers walk free."

She looked at him for a long moment. Then she laughed—a real laugh, not the sharp, manipulative one she'd used before.

"You really are stupid," she said. "That's why you caught me. You actually believe in justice."

"Someone has to."

"Fine." She extended her hand. "Break the token. Call your backup. I'll behave."

He broke the token.

The jade shattered into green dust, and the air twisted. A figure emerged from the shadows—tall, gaunt, wearing the red robes of a senior auditor. The man's eyes glowed with cold fire as he surveyed the scene.

"Jiang Chen." The auditor's voice was like grinding bones. "You've captured the rogue soul."

"Yes, sir."

"And yet she's still free. Still talking. Still breathing." The auditor's gaze shifted to the con woman. "Explain."

"She has information about corruption in Diyu," Jiang Chen said quickly. "She's agreed to testify before the Furnace Keeper."

"The Furnace Keeper?" The auditor's eyebrows rose. "That's above your clearance, reaper."

"I know, sir. But she won't speak to anyone else. She claims the Keeper is her father."

The auditor's expression didn't change, but something flickered in his eyes. Recognition. Interest.

"Her father," he repeated. "The Furnace Keeper's daughter. The one who escaped."

"Yes, sir."

"And you believe her?"

Jiang Chen hesitated. "I believe she has information worth pursuing."

The auditor turned to the con woman. "You have one chance. One. If you waste our time, I will personally escort you to the Furnace and watch you burn."

"I won't waste your time." She smiled. "Take me to my father."

The auditor nodded. He raised his hand, and the world twisted again.

Jiang Chen felt himself falling, the apartment dissolving around him, replaced by heat and darkness and the roar of endless flames.

When he opened his eyes, he was standing at the edge of an inferno.

The Furnace stretched before him—a lake of fire that consumed the entire horizon. Souls writhed in the flames, their screams harmonizing into a terrible chorus. And at the center of it all, standing on a platform of black stone, was a man with eyes like molten gold.

The Furnace Keeper.

"Mei Lin." The Keeper's voice cut through the screams. "You came back."

"Father." The con woman stepped forward. "I brought someone. He needs to hear what you have to say."

The Keeper's golden eyes fixed on Jiang Chen. "You're the new reaper. The one who was executed."

"Yes."

"I know who killed you." The Keeper's voice dropped. "I've been burning his souls for fifty years."

Jiang Chen's blood ran cold. "What?"

"I've been burning the souls of everyone involved in your execution." The Keeper smiled, and it was the most terrible thing Jiang Chen had ever seen. "All except one."

"Who?"

The Keeper raised his hand. The flames parted, revealing a figure chained to the black stone.

Jiang Chen's breath caught in his throat.

It was a woman.

The woman from his execution. The judge who had

title: The Witness in the Wall
content:
The black light swallowed her scream.

Jiang Chen felt her claws retract from his mind, felt her soul recoil and snap back into her stolen body like a rubber band breaking. She stumbled backward, gasping, her face pale and slick with sweat.

But she was still standing.

The staff had done something—he'd felt it connect, felt the pure force of Diyu's authority slam into her spirit—but it hadn't banished her. It hadn't even knocked her unconscious.

She looked at him with new eyes. Fear. Real fear.

"What was that?" she whispered.

"Diyu's justice."

"Bullshit." She touched her chest where the staff had struck. Her fingers came away smoking, the flesh blackened and cracked like old leather. "That was something else. That was personal."

Jiang Chen didn't answer. He was too busy trying to understand what had just happened. The staff had responded to him—not to the office of Soul Reaper, not to the authority of the judges—but to *him*. To his anger. To his need.

It had never done that before.

"Your staff," she said, watching his face. "You don't know what it does, do you? They gave you a weapon and didn't tell you how it works."

"Shut up."

"Classic Diyu. Here's your tool, figure it out yourself. They've been running that place on cheap labor for centuries." She laughed, but it was hollow, strained. "You're no different from me, kid. You're just another body they're using up."

"I said shut up."

"I'm trying to help you."

"You're trying to possess me."

"I'm trying to survive." She straightened, brushed the ash from her clothes. The wound on her chest was already healing, the blackened skin flaking away to reveal fresh pink flesh beneath. "You want to take me back? Fine. Take me. But you'll never get the truth. The judges will wipe me clean, and you'll spend the rest of your unlife wondering who killed you and why."

Jiang Chen tightened his grip on the staff. The wood felt warm now, almost alive. It hummed against his palm like a second heartbeat.

"I have another idea," he said.

Her eyes narrowed. "I don't like your tone."

"You want to live. I want the truth. We can both get what we want."

"How?"

"Tell me the names now. Not in Diyu. Here. Now. Tell me who framed me, and I'll find a way to keep the judges from erasing you."

She stared at him for a long moment. The street was empty. The moon had disappeared behind clouds. Somewhere in the distance, a dog barked once and fell silent.

"You're serious," she said.

"I am."

"You'd betray Diyu for revenge?"

"I'd betray anyone for the truth."

She smiled. It was a small smile, almost sad. "You really are new at this. You think the truth matters? You think the judges care about what really happened? They don't. They care about order. They care about the system. If your death was part of that system, they'll bury the truth deeper than you can dig."

"Then I'll dig deeper."

"With what? You're a trainee. You've been dead for three weeks. You don't have allies, you don't have power, you don't even know what your staff can do." She shook her head. "You're not ready for this fight."

"Then help me get ready."

"Why would I do that?"

"Because if I win, you live. If I lose, you get erased anyway. What do you have to lose?"

She considered this. Her fingers drummed against her thigh—a nervous habit, he realized. The confident con woman act was cracking. Underneath it, she was terrified.

"One name," she said finally. "I'll give you one name. If you can verify it, if you can find proof, I'll give you the rest."

"Deal."

"The judge who signed your death warrant. Judge Lin Wei of the Third People's Intermediate Court. He's the one who took the bribe."

Jiang Chen felt his stomach drop. He'd seen Judge Lin's name on the warrant. He'd assumed it was just paperwork, just another signature on another execution order. But now—

"He's still alive?"

"Very much so. He retired last year. Lives in a villa outside the city with his third wife and a collection of antique jade that's worth more than most people make in a lifetime." She smiled. "I know because I possessed one of his servants. Saw everything. The bribes, the payoffs, the secret accounts. He's been selling verdicts for twenty years."

"Why didn't you expose him?"

"I was busy not dying. And exposing a judge means exposing the system that protects him. One man's corruption is just a scandal. The system's corruption is a revolution."

Jiang Chen's mind was racing. Judge Lin Wei. He'd stood in front of that man during his trial. He'd looked him in the eye and begged for mercy. And Lin Wei had looked back with empty eyes and pronounced the death sentence like he was ordering tea.

"Where does he live?"

"Dragon's Rest Villa. Number 7, Lotus Hill Road. Eastern district." She tilted her head. "But if you go there now, you'll find nothing. He's too smart to keep evidence at home. You need to find his accountant."

"Name."

"Zhao Ming. Runs a small accounting firm in the Old City. He keeps the real books—the ones that show where the money went."

Jiang Chen committed the names to memory. Judge Lin Wei. Zhao Ming. He had threads to pull.

"Thank you," he said.

"Don't thank me yet. You're still taking me to Diyu."

She was right. He had a job to do. He lifted the staff, and this time, instead of striking her, he tapped it against the ground. The black light rippled outward, forming a circle of darkness at their feet.

"Step in," he said.

"You promised—"

"I promised to keep the judges from erasing you. I didn't promise to let you go." He met her eyes. "You killed two Soul Reapers. You've been evading Diyu for years. I can't just let you walk away."

"And the names?"

"I'll verify the first one. If it's true, I'll come back for the rest."

She studied him for a long moment. Then she stepped into the circle.

"One more thing," she said. "When you find Zhao Ming, be careful. He's not just an accountant. He's a cultivator."

Jiang Chen froze. "A cultivator?"

"Low level. Qi Condensation stage. But he knows enough to protect himself. And he knows enough to recognize a Soul Reaper when he sees one." She smiled. "Welcome to the real underworld, kid. The one that exists above ground."

The circle closed around them, and the world dissolved into black light.

---

They emerged in Diyu's central hall.

The Ten Judges' thrones were empty. The hall was quiet. Only a few clerks moved in the shadows, carrying scrolls and ledgers, their faces blank and empty.

Judge Ma's assistant was waiting for them.

"You found her," he said, his voice flat.

"I did."

"And you captured her without killing her."

"I did."

The assistant looked at the woman. She stood quietly, her hands at her sides, her eyes downcast. The perfect picture of submission.

"Impressive. Most trainees would have destroyed her on sight."

"I had questions."

"Questions are for the judges." The assistant gestured, and two guards emerged from the shadows. They were tall, gaunt, their faces hidden behind iron masks. They took the woman by the arms and led her away.

She didn't resist. But as she passed him, she whispered:

"Remember your promise."

Jiang Chen watched her go. Then he turned to the assistant.

"I need to speak with Judge Ma."

"The judge is occupied."

"It's urgent."

"It always is." The assistant's eyes flickered with something—annoyance? Amusement? "Wait here. I'll see if he's available."

The assistant disappeared into the shadows. Jiang Chen stood alone in the vast hall, the silence pressing down on him like a weight.

He thought about Judge Lin Wei. About Zhao Ming, the cultivator-accountant. About the woman's words: *Your death wasn't legal.*

He thought about his mother. She'd died while he was in prison. He never got to say goodbye. Never got to explain that he wasn't a murderer, that he'd been framed, that he'd taken a bribe but never killed anyone.

She'd died thinking her son was a monster.

He gripped the staff so hard his knuckles turned white.

"Jiang Chen."

He turned. Judge Ma was standing behind him, his face unreadable.

"Your report."

"The target has been delivered to the holding cells."

"And?"

"And nothing. She's secured."

Judge Ma studied him. "You're lying."

"I'm not."

"You're hiding something. I can see it in your eyes." The judge stepped closer. "What did she tell you?"

"She told me a lot of things. Most of them were lies."

"But one of them was true."

Jiang Chen didn't answer.

Judge Ma sighed. "You're not the first Soul Reaper to hear whispers about your death. You won't be the last. Everyone who comes through Diyu carries baggage. The question is whether you let that baggage weigh you down or use it to anchor yourself."

"What do you know about my death?"

"I know what the file says."

"And what does the file say?"

"That you were convicted of murder and executed according to the laws of the People's Republic." Judge Ma's eyes were cold. "Everything else is noise."

"Even if the noise is true?"

"Truth is a luxury, Jiang Chen. We deal in justice. They're not the same thing."

Jiang Chen wanted to argue. Wanted to scream. But he held himself back.

"Thank you for your time, Judge Ma."

"Where are you going?"

"Back to the mortal realm. I have souls to collect."

Judge Ma watched him go. And as Jiang Chen stepped through the gate, he heard the judge's voice echo behind him:

"Be careful what you dig up, Soul Reaper. Some graves are better left closed."

---

The mortal realm was dark when he returned.

He stood on a street corner in the Old City, the neon lights of night markets flickering in the distance. The air smelled of frying oil and exhaust fumes.

He pulled out his phone—a cheap burner he'd bought with stolen money from a pickpocket's wallet—and typed in the address: Zhao Ming, Accounting Services. 47 Old Temple Road.

The GPS showed a small building wedged between a noodle shop and a massage parlor.

He started walking.

The streets were crowded with night owls—students, workers, tourists. None of them looked at him. None of them saw the dead man walking among them.

He reached the building and climbed the stairs to the second floor. The door was old wood, the paint peeling. A brass plaque read: ZHAO MING — ACCOUNTING & TAX SERVICES.

He knocked.

No answer.

He knocked again.

The door creaked open.

Inside, the office was dark. A desk. A computer. Filing cabinets. Everything neat and orderly.

But the window at the back was open. The curtains fluttered in the night breeze.

And on the desk, pinned to a stack of papers, was a note:

*I know who you are, Soul Reaper. I've been expecting you.*

*Come find me at Dragon's Rest Villa. Bring your staff

title: The Memory Thief's Bargain
content:
The black light from my staff ripped through her like lightning through water. She convulsed, her grip on my mind tearing away in jagged fragments. I felt her go—felt the possession break like a snapped rope—and suddenly I was alone in my own head again.

But she didn't fall.

She staggered back, smoke rising from her chest where the staff had struck, but she stayed standing. Her borrowed body trembled. Her eyes—her real eyes, not the host's—glowed yellow through the woman's pupils.

"You're stronger than the other two," she coughed. "But not strong enough to kill me. Not yet."

I raised the staff again. "I don't need to kill you. I just need to get you to Diyu."

"And I need you to listen." She held up her hands. "You want the names. I want to live. We can both get what we want, or we can fight until one of us is too broken to do either."

The alley was silent. The rain had stopped. Somewhere above, a window opened and closed—someone checking the noise, deciding it wasn't their problem.

"How do I know you're telling the truth about my death?"

"You don't." She shrugged. "But you've got nothing else. The judges sent you here to catch a soul. They didn't tell you about the corruption in your own case. Why would they? You're just a reaper to them. Replaceable."

The words hit harder than they should have. Because she was right. Old Man Zhao had given me a staff and a mission. He hadn't asked about my death. He hadn't offered to investigate. I was a tool. A useful dead man.

"What's your name?" I asked.

"Does it matter?"

"I'm not making a deal with a ghost. I need a name."

She studied me for a long moment. Then: "Lin Mei. But I haven't used that name in fifteen years. Too many people looking for me."

"Lin Mei. Seventeen years of possession. Two reapers killed. How many bodies?"

"Enough."

"That's not an answer."

"It's the only one you're getting." She crossed her arms. "Here's how this works. You take me to Diyu. I tell the judges what I know about your case. They verify it. If I'm lying, they can wipe me. If I'm telling the truth..." She let the sentence hang.

"If you're telling the truth, what?"

"Then you get to decide what happens to me. Reapers have that authority, don't they? Judgment and execution?"

She was right again. Soul reapers could pass immediate judgment on escaped souls. Kill them on the spot or drag them back for trial. The power was absolute.

"You'd trust me with your life?"

"I'd trust your need for revenge." She smiled. "That's stronger than any promise."

I hated that she was right. I hated that she saw through me so clearly. But most of all, I hated that I was considering her offer.

"One wrong move," I said. "One flicker of possession, and I'll send you to the ninth hell myself."

"Understood."

I reached into my pocket and pulled out the binding rope Old Man Zhao had given me—a coil of black silk that could restrain any soul, no matter how powerful. "Put your hands out."

She extended them. I wrapped the rope around her wrists, double-knotting it. The moment it touched her skin, the yellow glow in her eyes dimmed. The rope was working, suppressing her soul's power.

"Comfortable?" she asked.

"Shut up."

I led her out of the alley, keeping the staff ready. The streets of Shanghai were wet and empty. It was past midnight, and even the night markets were closing. We walked in silence, me dragging her by the rope, her following like a obedient dog.

But she wasn't obedient. I could feel her watching me, studying me, cataloging my weaknesses.

"You were an accountant," she said.

"I don't want to talk."

"You worked for a logistics company. Kept two sets of books. One for the tax bureau, one for your boss. You knew what you were doing was illegal. You did it anyway."

"How do you know that?"

"I saw it in your memory. The possession gave me a glimpse." She tilted her head. "You weren't a bad person, Jiang Chen. You were just weak. You took the money because you needed it. Your mother was sick. Hospital bills were piling up. Your boss offered you a solution, and you took it."

I stopped walking. "Stop."

"I'm not judging you. I'm explaining why you were an easy target. Someone like you—someone with secrets, with guilt, with a desperate need to protect someone—you're perfect for framing. The police find your fingerprints on the murder weapon, your bank account shows a suspicious deposit, your alibi falls apart because you were too afraid to tell anyone where you really were that night."

My hands were shaking. "I said stop."

"Where were you that night, Jiang Chen? The night of the murder. Where were you?"

I remembered. God, I remembered. I'd been at the hospital. My mother had taken a turn for the worse. I'd sat by her bed for twelve hours, holding her hand, watching her chest rise and fall. But I couldn't prove it. The hospital records showed I'd checked in at noon, checked out at midnight. But the murder happened at 9 PM, and the distance between the hospital and the crime scene was forty minutes.

I had no alibi. No witnesses. Just a dying mother and a empty bank account.

"I was at the hospital."

"I know. I saw it in your memory. But the prosecution argued that you could have left, committed the murder, and come back. And your mother was too sedated to confirm your presence."

"The evidence was fabricated."

"Of course it was. The real killer left no trace. The evidence against you was too perfect. A single hair at the crime scene. A partial fingerprint. A witness who saw someone matching your description. It was a professional job."

We reached a bridge crossing the Huangpu River. The water below was black and still. In the distance, the lights of Pudong glittered like a fake paradise.

"Why are you telling me this?" I asked. "You're a con woman. You don't give information for free."

"I'm investing." She leaned against the railing. "Right now, you want to drag me to Diyu and collect your reward. But if I can make you want something more—something the judges can't give you—then you'll protect me. You'll keep me alive. You'll fight for me."

"You want me to owe you."

"I want you to need me." She smiled. "That's much more valuable."

I turned away from her, looking at the river. The water seemed to be moving faster now, swirling in patterns that didn't make sense. I blinked, and the patterns disappeared.

"I need to think," I said.

"Take your time. We've got until sunrise."

I walked a few steps away, trying to clear my head. Everything she said made sense. Too much sense. But that was her skill—making lies sound like truth, making truth sound like salvation.

My hand went to my chest, where the execution needle had entered. The wound was gone, but the memory remained. The cold table. The bright lights. The masked faces.

They killed you for someone else's crime.

If it was true, then everything I'd accepted—my death, my rebirth, my purpose—was built on a lie. The judges didn't know. Or they didn't care. Either way, I was just a pawn.

I turned back to tell her I'd made my decision, but she was gone.

The rope lay on the ground, still knotted. She'd slipped out of it somehow—souls could phase through physical restraints if they tried hard enough. The rope was meant to suppress her power, not contain her body.

"Lin Mei!"

No answer. The bridge was empty. The river below churned and bubbled.

I ran to the railing and looked down. The water was moving in that strange pattern again, swirling into a spiral. And in the center of the spiral, I saw faces. Dozens of faces. All of them screaming silently.

They weren't alive. They weren't dead. They were trapped—souls caught in some kind of current, pulled toward a darkness at the bottom of the river.

And at the edge of the water, standing on a concrete pillar, was Lin Mei. She'd discarded her host body somewhere—it was probably floating downstream—and now she stood in her true form: a woman in her forties, grey hair, sharp eyes, wearing a prison uniform that had faded to rags.

"I found something," she called up to me. "Something the judges didn't tell you about."

"What are you talking about?"

She pointed at the swirling water. "There's a soul market down there. Black market. Souls are bought and sold like commodities. The powerful ones—the ones with money, with connections—they trade in death." She looked up at me. "And your death was purchased there."

My blood went cold. "Purchased?"

"Someone paid to have you killed. Not just executed—set up, framed, eliminated. They bought a package deal." She smiled, but it was a sad smile. "You were merchandise, Jiang Chen. And if you want to know who bought you, you have to come down here with me."

The river churned. The faces screamed. The darkness at the bottom pulsed like a heartbeat.

"Trust me," she said. "Or spend eternity wondering."

I looked at my staff. I looked at the rope. I looked at the woman who had killed two soul reapers and who now stood in the middle of a soul-trafficking operation, offering me the truth.

I jumped.

title: The Warden's Secret
content:
The black light from Jiang Chen's staff ripped through Wei Lian's spectral form like thunder through silk. She didn't just scream — she *fractured*, her soul splitting into three distinct shards that scattered across the warehouse floor like startled insects.

Jiang Chen stumbled back, gasping. The staff's recoil numbed his arm to the shoulder. He'd never used that much force before. The old reaper's warning echoed in his skull: *The staff is a scalpel, not a hammer. Use too much power, and you'll break what you're trying to save.*

Wei Lian's shards pulsed on the concrete, each one a flickering candle flame of her consciousness. They began crawling toward each other, slowly, painfully, like severed worms trying to reconnect.

"You *broke* me," she whispered, but the voice came from all three pieces at once, creating a haunting stereo effect.

Jiang Chen leaned on his staff, trying to catch his breath. "You tried to possess me again."

"You were going to betray me."

"I was going to do my job." He watched her shards merge back together, her form rippling like heat haze before stabilizing. She looked weaker now. Translucent. The confidence had drained from her face, replaced by something raw and genuine.

Fear.

"I wasn't lying," she said quietly. "About your death. I saw it in your memories when I touched you. The execution. The needle. The witnesses."

"I know."

"Then why won't you help me?"

"Because you're a killer." Jiang Chen straightened. "You murdered two soul reapers. You stole a woman's life and wore it like a costume for seventeen years. You're not a victim, Wei Lian. You're a predator who got caught."

She laughed, but there was no triumph in it. "And what are you, Jiang Chen? An innocent man framed for murder? A righteous soul doing the work of the underworld?" She shook her head. "You're a dead man walking. Literally. You're as much a prisoner of Diyu as I am. The only difference is they gave you a leash and called it a promotion."

The words hit harder than they should have. Because they were true.

"Take me to the Judges," she said. "Let me testify. If they decide to erase my memory, fine. But at least you'll know the truth about your death." She met his eyes. "Don't you want to know who killed you?"

Jiang Chen's grip tightened on his staff. The question burned in his chest like a coal. He'd accepted his execution as justice. He'd made peace with it. But that peace was a lie built on a foundation of corruption, and now that he knew the foundation was rotten, the whole structure was collapsing.

"One wrong move," he said. "One attempt to escape, one try at possession, one anything—"

"I know. You'll break me again." She smiled weakly. "I'm not as strong as I was. It'll take days for me to recover from that blast. I'm at your mercy."

Somehow, that didn't make him feel better.

---

The journey back to Diyu took longer than Jiang Chen expected. Wei Lian's damaged soul was slow, struggling to maintain coherence. He had to stop twice to let her rest, watching her flicker like a dying candle in the shadows of abandoned buildings.

They traveled through the back alleys of the city, avoiding the living. The veil between worlds was thinner in places where life was hard — empty lots, condemned buildings, the spots where homeless men froze to death in winter. Jiang Chen had learned to recognize these places as shortcuts to the underworld.

But tonight, something was wrong.

The veil felt *thicker* than usual. Resistant. Like pushing through mud.

"You feel that?" Wei Lian asked.

"Yeah."

"Something's blocking the path."

Jiang Chen didn't answer. He'd been a soul reaper for three weeks. He didn't know what normal felt like yet. But he knew that the air around them had grown cold, and the shadows seemed deeper than they should be.

They reached the entrance — a rusted door in the wall of an abandoned factory. Through this door was the Gray Path, the bridge between worlds. Jiang Chen pushed it open.

Darkness stared back at him.

Not just regular darkness. This was the kind of darkness that felt *alive*, pressing against his skin like a thousand tiny hands. The Gray Path was supposed to glow with ambient soul-light, a soft luminescence that guided travelers between realms.

This was a void.

"What did you do?" Jiang Chen spun on Wei Lian.

"Nothing! I've been with you the whole time!"

"Someone else, then." He pulled out his staff, holding it like a torch. The weapon glowed faintly, pushing back the darkness just enough to see a few feet ahead. "Stay close. Don't touch anything."

They stepped through the door together.

The Gray Path stretched before them, but it was wrong. The walls were cracked, bleeding black ichor. The ground was sticky with something that squelched under Jiang Chen's boots. The familiar landmarks — the twisted trees, the stone markers, the distant lights of Diyu — were gone, replaced by a featureless corridor of corrupted reality.

"This isn't the Gray Path," Wei Lian whispered. "This is something else."

"It's the Gray Path. Just... broken."

"Nothing breaks the Gray Path. It's the fabric between worlds. It's supposed to be eternal."

Jiang Chen kept walking. He didn't have a choice. The only way to Diyu was forward.

They walked for what felt like hours. Time moved strangely in the Gray Path — minutes could feel like days, days like minutes. But the corruption grew worse the deeper they went. The ichor on the walls began to pulse, and Jiang Chen realized with horror that it was *breathing*.

*Thump. Thump. Thump.*

Slow. Rhythmic. Like a heartbeat.

"Something lives here now," Wei Lian said. "Something that shouldn't."

"Keep moving."

They rounded a corner and found the first body.

It was a soul reaper. Jiang Chen recognized the uniform — the same dark robes he wore, the same staff, now broken in half. The reaper's face was frozen in an expression of absolute terror, eyes wide, mouth open in a silent scream.

But that wasn't the worst part.

The worst part was the hole in his chest. Clean. Precise. As if something had reached inside and pulled out his soul entirely.

"Second tier," Jiang Chen muttered. "He had a full staff. He was stronger than me."

"And something killed him like he was nothing."

They found more bodies as they walked. Fourteen in total. All soul reapers. All with the same wound — a perfect hole where their souls should have been.

Wei Lian's spectral form trembled. "I've been running from Diyu for seventeen years. I've seen their hunters, their enforcers, their Judges. I've never seen anything that could do this."

"Neither have they." Jiang Chen knelt beside the last body, examining the wound. "Look. No burn marks. No residue. Whatever did this didn't fight them. It just... took them."

"Took their souls?"

"Took *something*." He stood, his mind racing. "Soul reapers aren't fully alive. We're bound to Diyu. Our souls are anchored to the underworld. To kill us, you'd have to sever that anchor."

Wei Lian's eyes went wide. "Unless you could take the anchor itself."

"The Judges." Jiang Chen felt cold spread through his chest. "They're the anchors. They hold the threads that bind us to Diyu. If someone could reach the Judges—"

"—they could unmake every soul reaper in existence."

They stared at each other in the darkness.

Then the heartbeat changed.

*THUMP. THUMP. THUMP.*

Louder now. Closer. The walls of the Gray Path began to *move*, contracting and expanding like a throat swallowing.

"We need to run," Wei Lian said.

"Where? The path is closed."

"Anywhere. *Now.*"

The heartbeat became a roar. The darkness ahead of them *cracked*, splitting open like an egg, and something stepped through.

It was human-shaped, but wrong. Its skin was the color of old bruises, stretched too tight over bones that seemed too long. Its eyes were empty sockets filled with swirling black smoke. And in its chest — where a heart should be — was a swirling vortex of blue light.

Soul-light.

Dozens of them. Hundreds. The stolen souls of every reaper it had killed, churning inside its ribcage like a storm.

It looked at Jiang Chen.

It smiled.

"You," it said, and its voice was a thousand voices speaking at once, "are the new one."

Jiang Chen raised his staff. "What are you?"

"I am the answer to a question you haven't asked yet." It took a step forward, and the ground *bled* beneath its feet. "I am the end of Diyu. I am the silence that follows the final judgment."

"Poetic. You practice that in the mirror?"

The thing laughed, and the sound made Wei Lian's soul flicker. "I like you. You'll taste interesting."

"You're not taking me without a fight."

"Oh, I'm not here to take you." It tilted its head, the black smoke in its eyes swirling faster. "I'm here to deliver a message."

"From who?"

"From the one who killed you." It reached into its chest and pulled out a single thread of golden light. "Your death wasn't an accident. Your execution wasn't a mistake. You were sentenced by the mortal court, yes. But the orders came from *much* higher."

It tossed the thread at Jiang Chen's feet.

He didn't pick it up.

"Your death was ordered by a Judge of Diyu," the thing said. "One of the Ten. Your mortal execution was just the tool. The underworld was the hand that wielded it."

Jiang Chen's blood turned to ice. "Which one?"

"The one who sits in the Seventh Court. The Judge who presides over the punishment of the deceitful." It smiled wider, its mouth stretching too far, too wide. "Judge Bao."

Silence.

Judge Bao. The most famous of the Ten Judges. The one who had personally recruited Jiang Chen. The one who had given him the staff. The one who had told him that his death was justice.

The one who had lied.

"I don't believe you."

"I don't need you to believe me." The thing stepped backward, dissolving into the darkness. "But I've delivered my message. The next reaper I meet, I won't talk. I'll just take."

It vanished.

The Gray Path began to repair itself, the corruption receding, the familiar glow returning. The bodies of the reapers dissolved into motes of light, their souls finally released to whatever afterlife waited for them.

Wei Lian grabbed Jiang Chen's arm. "We have to warn the Judges."

"Which Judges?" he asked, his voice hollow. "The ones who want me dead?"

She had no answer.

Jiang Chen picked up the golden thread. It pulsed with warmth, with *life*, with a signature he recognized.

Judge Bao's signature.

The man who had given him a second chance.

The man who had killed him in the first place.

He looked at Wei Lian. "We're not going to Diyu."

"Where are we going?"

"To find the truth."

He crushed the golden thread in his fist, and the Gray Path carried them into the unknown.

title: The Thirteenth Execution
content:
The black light from Jiang Chen's staff threw Madam Zhao across the chamber. She crashed through a stack of funeral paper goods—burning houses, paper servants, spirit money—and landed in a heap of torn offerings.

She was laughing again.

"You can't kill me, boy. I'm already dead."

Jiang Chen advanced, staff raised. His hands were still shaking. The names. She'd promised him names. The people who framed him. The judge who took the bribe. The witnesses who lied.

"I don't have to kill you," he said. "I just have to bring you back."

"Back to what? Memory wipe? Oblivion?" She rose, brushing paper ash from her stolen body. "That's not justice. That's convenience."

"What do you know about justice?"

"I know it's the first thing they sacrifice when the system gets comfortable." She circled him, her movements no longer those of a middle-aged woman but of something older, something that had learned to read people like ledgers. "You were an accountant, weren't you? You understand numbers. You understand that every system has a balance sheet, and sometimes the numbers don't add up."

Jiang Chen's jaw tightened. "Stop reading my mind."

"I don't need to read it. You're an open book." She pointed at his chest. "That need you feel? That hunger? I've seen it in a hundred faces. You want to know who took everything from you. You want to make them pay."

"You don't know anything about me."

"I know you took a bribe. Small one. Three thousand yuan to look the other way on some falsified invoices. You told yourself it was nothing. Everyone does it. Just one time." She tilted her head. "But it wasn't one time, was it? It became two. Then five. Then you stopped counting."

Jiang Chen's grip on the staff tightened until his knuckles went white.

"By the time they caught you, you'd taken forty-seven thousand yuan over three years. Not a fortune. Not enough to buy a house or a car. Just... pocket money. Coffee money. The price of your integrity."

"Shut up."

"And then someone murdered a woman. Strangled her in her apartment. Left your fingerprints everywhere. Used your bank card to withdraw money from her account. Made it look like you'd killed her for the cash." She smiled. "Amateur hour framing. Any competent lawyer would have torn it apart in ten minutes. But you didn't get a competent lawyer. You got a public defender who'd already been paid to lose."

Jiang Chen's vision went red at the edges.

"How do you know all this?"

"I told you. I've been possessing people for seventeen years. Lawyers. Judges. Police officers. I've been in their heads. I've seen their secrets." She stepped closer. "I know who framed you. I know who paid for the evidence. I know the name of the man who ordered your death."

"Tell me."

"Take me to Diyu."

"You'll just try to escape again."

"I won't."

"You're a liar."

"I'm a survivor." She met his eyes. "There's a difference. Liars lie for pleasure. Survivors lie for the next breath. I've been dead for seventeen years, Jiang Chen. Do you know what that's like? To exist in the space between worlds? To watch the living forget you, one day at a time?"

He didn't answer.

"At first, people remembered. They lit incense. They burned paper money. They visited my grave." Her voice dropped. "But the living have short memories. After a year, only my mother came. After five years, no one. I was erased. As if I'd never existed."

"Is that supposed to make me feel sorry for you?"

"No. It's supposed to make you understand." She spread her arms. "I've done terrible things. I've stolen bodies. I've ruined lives. I've killed two of your predecessors. I'm not asking for forgiveness. I'm asking for a deal."

"What kind of deal?"

"You take me to Diyu. I tell the judges everything I know about your case. They re-examine your file. You get your name cleared. The people who killed you face justice." She paused. "And I get to keep my memories."

"That's not your choice. The judges decide punishment."

"The judges decide based on what they know. I have information. Valuable information. Information about corruption in the mortal realm that goes higher than they realize." She smiled. "I've been inside the heads of politicians, police commissioners, even a deputy minister. I know where the bodies are buried. Literally."

Jiang Chen's mind raced. She was dangerous. She was manipulative. Everything she said was designed to appeal to his desires.

But she was also right.

He needed those names. He needed justice. And the judges of Diyu had given him a staff and a job without ever asking if he wanted it. They'd processed his death like another form, another number, another case closed.

"They executed me," he said slowly, "based on fabricated evidence. And no one checked. No one questioned. They just... stamped the form and moved on."

"The system is broken," Madam Zhao agreed. "On both sides of the veil. The judges of Diyu are overworked. They process thousands of deaths every day. They don't have time to investigate every claim of innocence."

"How do you know so much about Diyu?"

"Because I've been running from it for seventeen years." She sat down on an overturned bier, suddenly looking tired despite her stolen body. "I've studied their methods. Their weaknesses. Their blind spots. I know how they operate because I've been staying one step ahead."

"Until now."

"Until you." She looked at him with something like respect. "You're different from the others. They were hunters. You're... something else. You listen. You think. You don't just swing that staff without understanding."

"I'm not different. I'm just new."

"Newness has its advantages." She extended her hand again. "So. Do we have a deal?"

Jiang Chen stared at her hand. The same hand that had tried to possess him twice. The same fingers that had clawed through his memories.

"One condition," he said.

"Name it."

"No possessing anyone between here and Diyu. Not for any reason. Not even if you think you're saving yourself."

"Agreed."

"And when we get there, you tell the judges everything. Not just about my case. Everything you know about the corruption in the mortal world."

"That's a lot of information. It might take days."

"Then we'll take days."

Madam Zhao's smile was small, almost genuine. "You're trusting me."

"No. I'm using you. The same way you're using me."

"Fair enough." She stood. "Lead the way, Soul Reaper."

---

The path back to Diyu was different from the path out.

When Jiang Chen had first crossed the veil, he'd walked through a mist that seemed to stretch forever. Now, the mist parted like curtains, revealing a road paved with black stone that glowed faintly in the darkness.

"It's easier going back," Madam Zhao observed, walking beside him. "The dead are always drawn home eventually. That's why most souls don't run. They feel the pull."

"Why don't you?"

"I've been dead too long. The pull faded years ago." She looked at the sky, which was neither day nor night, but something in between. "I've become something else. Something that doesn't belong anywhere."

"A rogue spirit."

"If you want to use the official term." She laughed. "I prefer 'independent operator.'"

They walked in silence for a while. The road stretched ahead, lined with withered trees that bent toward them as they passed, like mourners at a funeral.

"How did you die?" Jiang Chen asked finally.

"Does it matter?"

"I want to know who I'm dealing with."

She was quiet for a long moment. "I was a journalist. Investigative. I dug into things I shouldn't have. Corruption in the provincial government. Embezzlement. A murder that had been ruled an accident." She shrugged. "They found me in my apartment. Gas leak, they said. But I'd been dead for three days before anyone came looking."

"That's why you know so much about corruption."

"That's why I know so much about everything." She glanced at him. "When you're a ghost, you can go anywhere. See anything. No one notices a dead woman in the corner. I've spent seventeen years collecting secrets."

"And now you're going to trade them for a chance to keep your memories."

"Everyone has a price. Mine is not being forgotten."

The road curved, and ahead of them, Jiang Chen saw the gates of Diyu. They were taller than he remembered, carved from bone and obsidian, guarded by two figures that could only be described as nightmares given form.

"Ah." Madam Zhao stopped. "I was wondering when they'd show up."

"You know them?"

"Ox-Head and Horse-Face." She nodded toward the guards. "The judges' enforcers. They've been hunting me for years."

The two figures turned as one. Ox-Head was enormous, with curved horns and eyes that burned like coals. Horse-Face was taller, thinner, with a skull-like visage and hands that ended in claws.

"So," Ox-Head rumbled, his voice like grinding stone. "The rogue returns."

"And brings a gift," Horse-Face added, his voice a whisper that cut through the air like a blade. "The new Soul Reaper."

"She's my prisoner," Jiang Chen said, stepping forward. "I'm bringing her before the judges."

"Prisoner?" Ox-Head laughed, a sound like an avalanche. "She's a corpse. She's been dead for seventeen years. She's killed two of your predecessors."

"I know what she is."

"And yet you walk beside her." Horse-Face's eyes narrowed. "Why?"

"Because she has information. Valuable information."

"Everything she says is valuable to her." Ox-Head stepped forward, his bulk blocking the gate. "She's a liar and a manipulator. She'll say anything to survive."

"I'm aware."

"Then step aside. We'll take her to the judges."

"No."

The word hung in the air. Ox-Head's eyes widened. Horse-Face's claws twitched.

"No?" Ox-Head repeated. "You refuse the enforcers of Diyu?"

"She's my capture. I brought her in. I'll present her to the judges myself."

"That's not how it works."

"It's how it's going to work today."

Jiang Chen met Ox-Head's gaze. His staff hummed in his hands, the black light pulsing in time with his heartbeat. Behind him, Madam Zhao was silent, watching.

For a long moment, no one moved.

Then Horse-Face laughed. It was a terrible sound, like nails on a coffin lid.

"The new Soul Reaper has teeth," he said. "Let him pass. Let him present his prize to the judges. It will be amusing to see how long she lasts before she betrays him."

Ox-Head grunted, but stepped aside.

Jiang Chen walked through the gates, Madam Zhao at his side. Behind them, the enforcers watched, their eyes burning in the darkness.

"You made enemies," Madam Zhao whispered.

"They were already enemies. I just made it official."

"Bold. Stupid, but bold."

"I learned from the best." He glanced at her. "You."

She smiled. "I'm almost proud of you."

They entered the Hall of Judgment. The Ten Judges sat on their thrones, their faces hidden in shadow

title: Chapter 14
content:
CHAPTER 14: The Truth in His Blood

The black light consumed her scream, swallowing it whole.

Jiang Chen's staff pulsed against his palms, the energy vibrating up through his arms and into his chest. The con woman's soul writhed against the blast, her grip on his mind fracturing like glass under pressure. He felt her claws retract, felt her presence shrink back into her stolen body.

She stumbled away from him, gasping.

The alley was silent again. The rain had stopped. The neon lights flickered overhead, casting their sickly colors across the wet concrete.

"You—" She clutched her chest where the staff had struck her. Her eyes were wide, wild. "That's not supposed to be possible. Soul Reapers can't use judgment energy without a judge's authority. You're just an apprentice."

Jiang Chen lowered the staff. His heart was pounding so hard he could feel it in his throat. He hadn't meant to do that. He'd just reacted. She'd been inside his head, digging through his memories, and he'd grabbed the staff and—

And something had answered.

Not the staff itself. Something *through* it.

"I don't know what that was," he said honestly.

She laughed, but it was brittle now. No triumph left. "You really don't, do you? You're stumbling through this like a blind man in a minefield." She touched her chest again, winced. "That wasn't soul reaper energy. That was something older. Something they haven't taught you yet."

"Stop talking in riddles."

"I'm not. I'm genuinely confused." She tilted her head, studying him like he was a puzzle she couldn't solve. "The staff is a tool. It channels the power of the judges. But what you just did—that was pure. Unfiltered. Like you reached into the foundations of Diyu itself and pulled."

Jiang Chen looked down at the staff. It looked the same as always. Black wood, silver bands, the faint etching of characters he couldn't quite read. But it felt different now. Warmer. Like it was breathing.

"We're getting off track," he said. "You said you'd give me names."

"I did. But you attacked me."

"Because you tried to possess me again."

"I was testing you." She smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "And you passed. Barely." She straightened her stolen clothes, smoothed her hair. "Fine. I'll give you one name. One name, and then you take me to Diyu."

"One name first. Then we negotiate."

She considered this. Then she stepped closer, close enough that he could smell the cheap perfume she'd stolen along with the body.

"Wu Gang," she said.

The name hit him like a physical blow.

Wu Gang. His supervisor at the accounting firm. The man who'd handed him the bribe, who'd told him to look the other way, who'd promised him it was just a one-time thing. The man who'd disappeared the week before Jiang Chen's arrest.

"That's not possible," Jiang Chen said. "Wu Gang was small-time. He didn't have the connections to—"

"Wu Gang was a front." She cut him off. "He worked for someone bigger. Someone who needed a low-level accountant to sign off on some very creative bookkeeping. You were useful. Then you became a liability."

"You're lying."

"I told you. Lying is what I do. But I'm not lying about this." She reached into her pocket and pulled out a folded piece of paper. "I took this off a corpse I possessed three years ago. A man who worked for the same people Wu Gang worked for."

She handed it to him.

Jiang Chen unfolded it. It was a ledger page. Handwritten. Names, dates, amounts. His name was there, next to a series of transactions he didn't recognize. And at the top, in bold red ink, a single line:

*Account closed. Subject eliminated. Payment received.*

He read it three times.

"The execution was a cleanup operation," she said quietly. "You were never supposed to go to trial. But someone made a mistake—left evidence that pointed to you. They had to go through with the legal process to make it look legitimate. But the judge was paid. The prosecutor was paid. Everyone was paid."

Jiang Chen's hands were shaking again. He folded the paper carefully, put it in his own pocket.

"Who paid them?"

"I don't know the top. No one does. But I know the middleman." She smiled. "His name is Chen Wei. City prosecutor. He's the one who made sure the evidence against you was accepted. He's the one who made sure the judge was chosen. He's the one who could give you the whole chain."

"Where do I find him?"

"He's dead."

Jiang Chen stared at her.

"He died six months ago. Heart attack. Very convenient." She shrugged. "But before he died, he kept records. He was paranoid. He hid everything in a safety deposit box under a false name. I know which bank. I know the name. I can get you in."

"Why would you help me?"

"Because I need you alive and cooperative long enough to get me to Diyu. And because I like leverage." She smiled again. "Now you have something you want. And I have something you need. We're partners now, whether you like it or not."

Jiang Chen wanted to argue. Wanted to grab her and drag her back to Diyu and let the judges sort it out. But the paper in his pocket burned like a brand. Someone had killed him. Someone had paid to have him executed for a crime he didn't commit. And that someone was still out there, still alive, still free.

"Fine," he said. "We do this together. But if you try to possess me again, I won't just blast you. I'll send you to Diyu in pieces."

"Threat accepted." She extended her hand again. "Partners?"

He didn't take it.

"Where's the bank?"

---

The bank was in the old district, a crumbling building that had been elegant once, back when the neighborhood had money. Now it was a relic, its marble floors scuffed and yellowed, its brass fixtures tarnished. The teller behind the counter looked at them with the tired eyes of someone who'd seen too many desperate people.

Jiang Chen approached alone. The con woman waited outside, watching through the window.

"I need to access a safety deposit box," he said.

The teller didn't blink. "Name on the box?"

"Zhao Lihua."

The teller typed something into a terminal that looked older than Jiang Chen. "ID?"

Jiang Chen pulled out the fake ID the con woman had given him—stolen from a woman she'd possessed last year. The picture was close enough. He held his breath.

The teller looked at the ID, looked at him, looked back at the ID. Then she shrugged and slid a key across the counter.

"Box 47. Down the hall, second door on the left. You'll need this key and your thumbprint."

Jiang Chen took the key. His hand was steady now. He'd stopped shaking.

The room was small and windowless, lined with metal boxes. Box 47 was near the bottom. He inserted the key, pressed his thumb to the scanner, and heard the click of the lock disengaging.

Inside was a single manila envelope.

He pulled it out, opened it.

Papers. Dozens of them. Bank statements, wire transfer records, handwritten notes. Names he recognized. Names he didn't. And at the bottom, a photograph.

It was him. Jiang Chen. In a restaurant, laughing with someone whose face had been cut out of the photo. The date on the back was three weeks before his arrest.

He was being watched. Followed. Documented.

He was a target long before he knew it.

He tucked the envelope under his arm and walked back out. The con woman was waiting by the door, arms crossed.

"Well?"

"It's real," he said.

"Told you." She grinned. "Now take me to Diyu. I've held up my end."

Jiang Chen looked at her. She was still dangerous. Still lying. Still using him. But she'd given him something no one else had: the truth.

"One more thing," he said. "Who cut the face out of the photo?"

"I don't know. That was in the box before I knew about it."

"You see everything when you possess someone. You said that yourself."

She hesitated. Just a flicker. But he caught it.

"What aren't you telling me?"

She looked away. "I might have seen something. In the memories of the man I took that ledger from. A face. A name. But I need to be sure before I tell you."

"Tell me now."

"Not yet. Take me to Diyu. Let me speak to the judges. Then I'll tell you everything."

She was playing him again. He knew it. But he couldn't afford to call her bluff.

"Fine," he said. "But we do it my way. You stay in your body. You don't try anything. And the moment we reach Diyu, you tell the judges everything you know."

"And you get your justice," she said.

"And I get my justice."

They walked out of the bank together, into the gray morning light. The streets were waking up. Shopkeepers were opening their doors. The living were starting their day, oblivious to the dead walking among them.

Jiang Chen stopped at the curb and looked up at the sky. The veil between worlds was thin here, in the old district. He could feel it. The pull of Diyu. The weight of unfinished business.

He raised his staff and struck the ground once.

The world split open.

Not physically. But the air rippled, the colors bled, and a door appeared where there had been no door. Black wood, iron hinges, a handle shaped like a skull.

"After you," he said.

The con woman stepped through without hesitation.

Jiang Chen followed.

And the door closed behind them, sealing them in the space between worlds, where neither the living nor the dead could follow.

---

The judges' hall was empty.

Jiang Chen stood in the center of the vast chamber, the con woman at his side, and stared at the seven empty thrones. Candles flickered on the altar. The incense had burned down to ash.

"They knew we were coming," the con woman whispered. "They left."

"No," Jiang Chen said slowly. "They didn't leave. They were called away."

The hair on his arms stood up.

Something was wrong.

The air was too still. The shadows were too deep. And the door behind them had disappeared.

"You said the judges would hear me," the con woman said, her voice rising. "You promised."

"I didn't promise anything."

"You brought me here. You said—"

"I said they'd hear you." He turned in a slow circle, scanning the shadows. "But I didn't say they'd be here."

A sound. Footsteps. Coming from the far end of the hall, where a corridor led deeper into Diyu.

Jiang Chen gripped his staff.

The footsteps grew louder. Closer. A figure emerged from the darkness.

It was a woman. Tall. Pale. Dressed in black robes that seemed to drink the light. Her eyes were white, no pupils, no irises. And she was smiling.

"Jiang Chen," she said. "The apprentice. We've been waiting for you."

"Who are you?"

"I'm the one your judges sent to collect you." She stopped a few feet away, tilted her head. "There's been a complication. Your case has been reopened. By someone above the judges."

Jiang Chen's blood went cold

title: The Governor's Shadow
content:
The black light from Jiang Chen's staff ripped through Madame Liang's soul like lightning through water. She didn't just scream—she came apart, her form splintering into a dozen writhing fragments that scattered across the warehouse floor like startled cockroaches.

Jiang Chen fell to his knees, gasping. His head felt split open. She'd been inside him. She'd seen his death. She'd seen *everything*.

The fragments pulsed and throbbed, each one trying to re-form. Madame Liang's voice came from everywhere at once, layered and distorted, like a choir of broken radios.

"You broke me, boy. You actually broke me."

Jiang Chen forced himself upright. His legs shook. His vision blurred. He gripped the staff with both hands, planting it like a crutch.

"You tried to possess me again."

"And you cracked my soul." The fragments began crawling toward each other, leaving slick trails of black smoke. "Do you know how long it takes a soul to heal from that? Decades. Maybe centuries."

"Good."

"Good?" Her laugh was fractured, jagged. "You think you've won something? You've just guaranteed I have nothing left to lose."

The fragments merged. Madame Liang reformed, but wrong. Her edges were frayed. Her face kept shifting between expressions—rage, sorrow, hunger, fear—like she couldn't decide which mask to wear.

"I was going to bargain with you," she said. "Give you names. Let you have your revenge. But now?" She flexed her fingers, and the air around her shimmered. "Now I'm going to take your body and burn your soul to cinders. Then I'm going to find whoever framed you and thank them personally."

Jiang Chen raised his staff. His arms ached. His chest burned. He'd been fighting for hours, and he was running on empty.

"Try it."

She lunged.

But she didn't attack him directly. She dove sideways, crashing through a stack of crates, and emerged with a metal pipe. She wasn't trying to win a spiritual battle anymore. She was going to beat him to death with a piece of plumbing.

Jiang Chen swung the staff. The pipe met it with a deafening CLANG. Sparks flew. The impact sent a shockwave up his arms, rattling his teeth.

Madame Liang was stronger than she looked. Stronger than she should be. Her soul was damaged, but her host body was still alive, still pumping blood and oxygen to muscles that had spent decades hauling stolen goods through back alleys.

She swung again. He blocked. The staff vibrated like a tuning fork.

"You're slowing down," she said.

"You're falling apart."

"Touché."

She feinted left, then kicked him square in the chest. He flew backward, slammed into a concrete pillar, and tasted blood. The staff clattered out of his hands.

Madame Liang was on him before he could breathe. She grabbed his throat with one hand and pressed the pipe against his temple with the other.

"Last chance," she whispered. "Take me to Diyu. Let me talk to the judges. You get your names. I get a second chance. Everybody wins."

"You'll kill the judges."

"I might try. But they're old souls. They've seen worse than me."

Jiang Chen's vision was going dark at the edges. He grabbed her wrist, tried to pry her fingers loose. She didn't budge.

"The staff," he gasped. "You broke me out of it."

"What?"

"You said it was a prison. That the staff was a cage for souls."

She frowned. "So?"

"So it's not just a cage." He forced himself to look at her. Really look at her. "It's a key."

Her grip loosened. "What are you talking about?"

"The judges told me the staff was made from the First Judge's backbone. The one who founded Diyu. The one who wrote the laws." He smiled, blood staining his teeth. "They said it was the most powerful artifact in the underworld. But they didn't tell me what it could *do*."

Madame Liang's eyes narrowed. "What can it do?"

"I don't know yet. But I'm about to find out."

He reached out with his mind. Not to attack her, but to touch the staff. It lay three meters away, humming with black light. He didn't pick it up. He didn't need to.

The staff answered him.

It shot across the floor like a spear and buried itself in Madame Liang's chest.

She looked down. The staff had pierced her host body clean through. Blood soaked her prison uniform. She opened her mouth to speak, but only a wet gurgle came out.

"You killed me," she whispered.

"I killed the body," Jiang Chen corrected. "Your soul is still intact."

He grabbed the staff and pulled. Madame Liang's soul came with it, ripped from the dying host like a fish from a hook. She screamed—a raw, primal sound that shook the walls—and then she was inside the staff, trapped, contained, silenced.

Jiang Chen fell to his knees.

He was alone in the silent warehouse. Two dead bodies. One dead soul reaper in a pool of his own blood. One con woman's soul trapped in a stick.

And somewhere out there, a man who'd framed him for murder was still walking free.

He looked at the staff. It pulsed with a faint light. He could feel Madame Liang inside, pounding against the walls of her new prison.

"Sorry," he said. "But I'm not letting you anywhere near the judges. Not until I know the truth."

He stood up. His body screamed in protest. He had cuts, bruises, a cracked rib, and a concussion that was making the room spin. But he was alive. Sort of.

He limped to the exit, staff in hand.

The door opened before he reached it.

A woman stood in the doorway. She was tall, sharp-featured, dressed in a business suit that cost more than Jiang Chen's entire life. Her hair was pulled back in a tight bun. Her eyes were the color of cold steel.

Behind her, a dozen men in tactical gear fanned out, rifles raised.

"Jiang Chen," the woman said. Her voice was calm. Professional. "I'm Special Investigator Lin from the Ministry of State Security. You're under arrest for the murder of Xu Ming and the attempted murder of Liang Fang."

Jiang Chen stared at her. "Who?"

"The body in the chair," she said, nodding toward the corpse of the soul reaper. "And the woman whose soul you just extracted."

"How do you know about that?"

"We've been watching this warehouse for three weeks. We knew Madame Liang was hiding here. We knew someone would come for her eventually." She smiled. "We just didn't expect it to be a dead man."

Jiang Chen's blood ran cold.

"You know who I am."

"We know everything." She pulled a tablet from her briefcase and tapped it. A photo appeared—his booking photo from the night of his arrest. The murder charge. The trial. The execution date.

"We know you were executed three weeks ago," she continued. "We know your case was corrupt. We know the judge was paid. We know the evidence was fabricated."

"Then why are you arresting me?"

"Because the man who framed you is my boss."

The world stopped.

Jiang Chen's grip on the staff tightened. "What?"

"Deputy Minister Zhao. He's the one who ordered your death. He's the one who paid the judge. He's the one who planted the evidence." She tilted her head. "And he's the one who's been selling classified information to foreign intelligence for the last seven years."

"Then why don't you arrest him?"

"Because I can't prove it. Not yet." She stepped closer. The tactical team didn't move. "I've been building a case against him for two years. Every time I get close, someone dies. Witnesses. Informants. Even other investigators."

"So you need me."

"I need Madame Liang." She nodded at the staff. "She's been possessing government officials for years. She knows where all the bodies are buried. She's seen Zhao's face in the minds of everyone he's corrupted."

"She'll kill you."

"Probably." Lin smiled again. "But not before she gives me what I need."

Jiang Chen looked at the staff. Madame Liang was silent now. Listening. Waiting.

"If I give her to you, she'll escape. She'll possess someone else. She'll disappear."

"Then don't give her to me. Work with me." Lin extended her hand. "Help me take down Zhao. You get your revenge. I get my evidence. Madame Liang gets the execution she deserves."

"And if I refuse?"

Lin's smile faded. "Then I'll have to arrest you. And I'll have to confiscate the staff. And I'll have to spend the next six months extracting Madame Liang's soul through legal channels, by which time Zhao will have fled the country and sold our secrets to everyone who can afford them."

The tactical team shifted. Rifles raised.

Jiang Chen looked at the staff. Then at Lin. Then at the bodies on the floor.

He had a choice to make.

The staff pulsed. Madame Liang's voice echoed in his mind, faint but clear.

*Don't trust her. She's lying. She's one of Zhao's people. She's setting you up.*

Jiang Chen closed his eyes.

"How do I know you're telling the truth?"

Lin reached into her jacket. The tactical team tensed. She pulled out a folder and tossed it at his feet.

Photos spilled out. Photos of Deputy Minister Zhao shaking hands with men Jiang Chen had never seen. Photos of money changing hands. Photos of documents with classified stamps.

And at the bottom, a photo of Jiang Chen's execution.

He picked it up.

The photo showed him strapped to the table, needle in his arm. But the angle was wrong. It wasn't from the official cameras. It was from someone in the room.

Someone who'd watched him die.

"We have a mole in Zhao's organization," Lin said. "He took that photo. He's been feeding me information for a year. But he's getting nervous. He wants out. And the only way out is to take Zhao down."

"Who is he?"

"I can't tell you. Not yet. But he's the one who told me you were coming tonight."

Jiang Chen stared at the photo. His own face, slack and lifeless. The needle. The witnesses.

"You were there," he whispered. "You watched me die."

"No." Lin's voice was soft. "But someone did. And they've been waiting for you to wake up."

The staff pulsed again. Madame Liang's voice, desperate now.

*She's using you. She's going to kill you the moment you're useful. Don't trust her.*

Jiang Chen looked up at Lin.

"What's your plan?"

She smiled.

"We go to Diyu."

title: Chapter 16
content:
CHAPTER 16: The Warden's Invitation

The black light from Jiang Chen's staff sent Lin Mei sprawling across the rooftop. She landed hard, her stolen body skidding across gravel until she crashed into a ventilation unit. The impact should have broken bones. Instead, she laughed.

"You're getting stronger," she said, pushing herself up. "But you still hesitate."

Jiang Chen's hands trembled. The staff hummed in his grip, hungry for more. He could feel it pulling at him, urging him to strike again, to finish what he'd started.

"You tried to possess me again."

"I had to know if you were serious." She wiped dust from her clothes. "You are. That's good. You're going to need that determination in Diyu."

"What's that supposed to mean?"

Lin Mei walked toward him, and he raised the staff again. She stopped.

"Put that thing away. I'm not going to run. I'm not going to fight. If I wanted to escape, I would have done it while you were unconscious."

"What?"

She pointed at the fire escape. "You passed out for about thirty seconds. Right after you used the staff. You don't remember, do you?"

Jiang Chen's memory was fuzzy. There had been the black light, the explosion, and then... nothing. A gap.

"The staff drains you," she continued. "Every time you use it, it takes something from you. Strength. Memory. Life. You're not just a reaper, Jiang Chen. You're a battery."

He looked at the staff. It seemed to pulse in his hand, alive and hungry.

"How do you know that?"

"I told you. I've been possessing people for seventeen years. I've seen things the judges don't want anyone to know." She stepped closer. "That staff isn't a tool. It's a leash. Every time you use it, you become more dependent on Diyu. More bound to their rules. Less human."

"You're lying."

"Am I? Think about it. You've been a reaper for what, three weeks? How many times have you used the staff?"

He didn't answer. He'd lost count.

"How much do you remember about your life before? Your childhood? Your mother's face?"

The words hit him like a cold wave. He tried to picture his mother. He could see her. Sort of. A blurry image. A voice he couldn't quite place.

"It's already started," Lin Mei said softly. "They're erasing you. Making you a weapon. And you're letting them."

"Enough." Jiang Chen's voice was harder than he'd intended. "I'm taking you to Diyu. You're going to tell the judges what you know about my death. That's the deal."

"I never agreed to any deal."

"You shook my hand."

"That was a possession attempt, not a contract." She smiled. "But fine. I'll go with you. Not because I'm helping you, but because there's something I need in Diyu."

"What?"

"My ledger."

Jiang Chen frowned. "The judges said you kept records of all your victims."

"I kept records of everyone I possessed. Their secrets. Their crimes. Their debts." Her eyes gleamed. "Do you know how many corrupt officials I've possessed over the years? How many rich men who got rich by stepping on the poor? I have enough blackmail material to bring down half the government."

"And the judges have it."

"They confiscated it when I was arrested. It's in the Diyu archives, locked away. If I can get it back—"

"You're not getting anything back. You're being processed. Memory wiped. Sentenced."

"Then you'll never know who killed you."

The night air felt colder. Jiang Chen's grip on the staff tightened.

"You said you could give me names."

"I can. But I won't. Not until I have my ledger."

"That wasn't the deal."

"The deal was you wanted to know who framed you." She crossed her arms. "I'll give you the names. But only after you help me get my ledger back."

"You're insane."

"Maybe. But I'm also the only person who can give you the truth." She tilted her head. "Think about it. You could spend the next ten years investigating your own death, following dead leads, trusting people who lie to you. Or you could help me, and I'll hand you everything you need on a silver platter."

Jiang Chen was silent. The wind blew across the rooftop, carrying the sounds of the sleeping city below. Somewhere, a dog barked. A car horn honked. Life continued, indifferent to the dead man standing on a rooftop.

"Why should I trust you?"

"You shouldn't. But you should trust my self-interest. I need my ledger to survive. You need your truth to rest. We help each other, or we both lose."

"And if I just drag you to Diyu and let them wipe you?"

"Then you'll spend the rest of your existence wondering. Wondering if I was telling the truth. Wondering if you could have known. Wondering who hated you enough to kill you." She stepped closer, her voice dropping. "That wondering will eat you alive. I've seen it happen. Reapers who can't let go of their past lives. They go mad. They become shades. Wandering forever, trapped between worlds."
