Jiang Chen's jaw tightened. He could feel the staff pulling at him again, urging him to strike. To end this. To follow the rules.

But the rules had already failed him once. They had condemned him for a crime he didn't commit.

"One condition," he said finally.

"I'm listening."

"If we do this, you don't try to possess me again. Not once. Not ever."

"Agreed."

"And when we get to Diyu, you follow my lead. No tricks. No running."

"Fair."

"And when this is over, you give me everything. Names, evidence, proof. Everything."

Lin Mei smiled. It was a predator's smile, sharp and knowing.

"You have a deal, Soul Reaper."

---

They moved through the city in silence. Lin Mei led, navigating the alleys and backstreets with the confidence of someone who'd spent years running. Jiang Chen followed, staff in hand, watching for any sign of betrayal.

"How do we get to Diyu?" she asked.

"Portal. There's a convergence point in the old temple district."

"The one by the river?"

"You know it."

"I escaped through there once. The judges sealed it after." She glanced back at him. "They won't have sealed all of them. There's always another way."

"There's a graveyard on the edge of town. Old one. Abandoned."

"Perfect. The dead are easier to find where death is concentrated."

They walked for another hour. The city grew quieter, the streets darker. By the time they reached the graveyard, the moon was high overhead, casting silver shadows across the tombstones.

The gate was rusted, the lock broken. They slipped through easily.

"Where's the convergence?" Lin Mei asked.

Jiang Chen pointed to a mausoleum at the center of the graveyard. "There. The energy is strongest there."

They approached carefully. The air grew colder. Jiang Chen could feel the veil thinning, the boundary between worlds growing weak.

"Wait," Lin Mei said suddenly.

"What?"

"Something's wrong."

He felt it too. A presence. Heavy. Watching.

From the shadows of the mausoleum, a figure emerged. Tall. Broad-shouldered. Wearing the black robes of a Diyu official.

"Jiang Chen." The voice was deep, echoing. "We've been looking for you."

Jiang Chen's hand went to his staff. "Who are you?"

"Warden Zhao. Director of Soul Reaper Operations." The man stepped into the moonlight. His face was scarred, his eyes cold. "You've been off the grid for eight hours. The judges were getting concerned."

"I was tracking a soul."

"Clearly." Warden Zhao's gaze shifted to Lin Mei. "And you've captured the escaped soul. Well done."

"She's not captured. She's helping me."

Warden Zhao's eyes narrowed. "Helping you? This soul has killed two reapers. She cannot be helped. She must be processed."

"She has information. About my death."

"Your death was processed. It's closed."

"It was a setup. The evidence was fabricated. The judge was bribed."

Warden Zhao was silent for a long moment. Then he laughed.

"You think you're the first reaper to claim their death was unjust? Every soul who crosses the veil believes they were wronged. It's part of the transition. Denial. Anger. Bargaining." He stepped closer. "You're in the bargaining phase. It's normal. But it's also dangerous."

"I'm not bargaining. I'm investigating."

"Investigate all you want. But first, hand over the soul."

Lin Mei took a step back. "Jiang Chen."

Warden Zhao's hand went to his belt. A staff identical to Jiang Chen's materialized.

"Do not make this difficult, apprentice. Hand over the soul. Return to Diyu. Attend your training. Your doubts will fade."

"Or what?"

"Or I will treat you like any other rogue reaper." His staff began to glow. "The judges gave you a chance. Don't waste it."

Jiang Chen looked at Lin Mei. She was watching him, waiting to see what he would do. The staff in his hand pulsed, pulling at him, urging him to obey.

But he'd already made his choice.

"I'm not handing her over."

Warden Zhao sighed. "So be it."

He raised his staff. The night exploded into light.

title: Chapter 17
content:
CHAPTER 17: The Thirteenth Judge

The black light threw her across the rooftop. She hit the gravel hard, rolled twice, and came up laughing.

"You can't hurt me," she said, wiping blood from her split lip. "Not if you want the truth."

Jiang Chen's hands were still shaking. The staff hummed in his grip, hungry for more. He held it back.

"You tried to possess me again."

"I had to know if you were serious." She sat up slowly, brushing gravel from her stolen body's clothes. "And I found something interesting. You don't actually believe me."

"I don't."

"Good. You're smarter than most soul reapers." She stood, wobbly. "But you're also desperate. That's why you'll take me to Diyu anyway."

She was right. He hated that she was right.

"What guarantee do I have that you'll tell the truth?"

"None. But you've got something I want more than freedom." She tapped her temple. "I've seen your memories now. You know what's in Diyu. The records. The case files. The names of every corrupt official who's ever died and been judged."

"The judges don't share that information."

"They don't have to. I can find it myself." Her eyes gleamed. "I told you. I've been possessing people for seventeen years. I've seen how the system works. The underworld isn't some perfect justice machine. It's a bureaucracy. And bureaucracies have filing cabinets. Filing cabinets have locks. Locks have keys."

"You're planning to steal from the Ten Judges."

"I'm planning to survive." She walked toward him, hands raised. "You take me to Diyu. I find the records about your death. We both get what we want."

"And if I just drag you back and let them wipe your memory?"

"Then you'll never know who killed you. You'll spend eternity wondering. Wondering if maybe you deserved it. Wondering if maybe you really were guilty." She stopped inches from him. "I've seen inside you, Jiang Chen. That doubt is already there. It'll eat you alive."

He wanted to argue. But she was right about that too.

"I have conditions."

"Name them."

"You don't leave my sight. You don't speak to anyone unless I'm present. And the moment we reach Diyu, you go straight to the Records Hall. No detours."

"Agreed."

"I'm serious. No deals with other souls. No possession attempts. No—"

"I said agreed." She rolled her eyes. "You're worse than my parole officer."

"You had a parole officer?"

"I was a con woman, not a monster. I served time." She grinned. "Three years for fraud. Got out early for good behavior."

"You're lying."

"Of course I'm lying. But it's a good story, isn't it?"

Jiang Chen sighed. He was already regretting this.

---

The descent to Diyu took longer than usual.

Jiang Chen had made the journey twice before—once when he died, once when he returned from his first mission. Both times, the path had been clear. A straight road through mist and shadow, leading to the gates of the underworld.

This time, the mist was thicker. The shadows moved wrong.

"You feel that?" Li Hua asked.

She'd stopped calling herself Wei Mei now that they were alone. Jiang Chen didn't know if Li Hua was her real name, but it was the one she'd given him, and he had no reason to doubt it. No more reason to doubt anything else she said.

"Feel what?"

"Someone's watching us."

He looked around. The mist swirled in patterns that didn't match the wind. Shapes moved at the edge of his vision—too fast to see, too slow to ignore.

"It's probably just—"

A figure stepped out of the mist.

Jiang Chen raised his staff. Li Hua moved behind him, her hand on his shoulder, ready to possess him if needed.

The figure was tall. Twice as tall as a man. Its body was wrapped in black robes that seemed to absorb light. Its face was a mask of white jade, featureless except for three characters carved into the forehead:

*The Thirteenth Judge.*

"There is no Thirteenth Judge," Jiang Chen said.

"There is now." The figure's voice came from everywhere at once, vibrating through the ground, the air, Jiang Chen's bones. "I am the Judge of Unfinished Business. The Arbiter of Wrongful Deaths. The—"

"You're not a judge," Li Hua said. "Judges don't leave Diyu. Judges don't patrol the path. Judges don't—"

"I am whatever I need to be." The figure took a step forward. The ground cracked beneath its feet. "I am here for the soul known as Li Hua. She is to be returned to Diyu for immediate processing."

"Processing?" Jiang Chen's grip tightened on his staff. "Processing for what?"

"She has escaped judgment for seventeen years. She has killed two soul reapers. She has stolen bodies and lives and futures. Her crimes require immediate attention."

"Immediate attention." Li Hua laughed, but there was no humor in it. "You mean immediate execution. You're going to destroy my soul."

"Your soul will be recycled. It is the natural order."

"I thought the Ten Judges decided fates," Jiang Chen said. "I thought there was a process. Trials. Evidence. Appeals."

"The process has been accelerated. The Ten Judges have deemed this soul a priority threat."

"Show me the order."

The figure paused. "What?"

"Show me the order. The official decree. Signed by the Ten Judges." Jiang Chen stepped forward, staff held ready. "If you're really a judge, you have authority. If you have authority, you have documentation. Show me."

"I do not answer to you, soul reaper."

"Actually, you do." Jiang Chen reached into his robe and pulled out a bronze token. It was warm against his palm, inscribed with the seal of the Fifth Judge—Judge Yanluo, the King of Hell himself. "I was given this when I was recruited. It grants me the right to request documentation for any soul transfer. Standard procedure."

The figure stared at the token. The jade mask revealed nothing, but the silence stretched too long.

"Where did you get that?"

"Judge Yanluo gave it to me. Said I might need it." Jiang Chen had no idea if that was true. The token had appeared in his pocket after his first mission, with no explanation. He'd assumed it was standard issue. But the figure's reaction suggested otherwise.

"You are not supposed to have that."

"And yet here I am. Holding it." Jiang Chen held it higher. "So show me the order, or get out of our way."

The figure didn't move.

Li Hua's hand tightened on his shoulder. "Jiang Chen..."

"I know."

"She's scared. That's not good."

"I know."

The figure spoke again. "The token you hold is a relic of a previous era. It grants authority that no longer exists. You would be wise to surrender it."

"I'd be wise to do a lot of things. I'm not known for wisdom." Jiang Chen stepped forward until he was inches from the figure's chest. Up close, he could see that the robes weren't fabric—they were made of something darker. Something that moved. "Last chance. Show me the order, or let us pass."

"You cannot pass."

"Then I guess we're at an impasse."

The figure raised its hand. The mist around them solidified into chains, wrapping around Jiang Chen's arms, his legs, his throat.

"I tried to be civil," the figure said. "I tried to follow protocol. But you have forced my hand."

The chains tightened. Jiang Chen couldn't breathe. His staff clattered to the ground. The bronze token fell from his fingers, landing in the mist with a soft thud.

Li Hua screamed. Not in fear—in fury.

Her soul erupted from her stolen body, a torrent of white light that shattered the chains. She slammed into the figure with the force of a freight train, driving it back, tearing through its robes.

"You don't get to take me," she snarled. "I decide when I die. Not you. Not the judges. Not anyone."

The figure staggered. The jade mask cracked.

And beneath it, Jiang Chen saw something he wasn't supposed to see.

A face. Human. Familiar.

Judge Yanluo's face.

"You—" Jiang Chen gasped.

The figure's hand shot out, grabbing Li Hua's soul. It squeezed. She screamed again, but this time it was pain, pure and raw.

"I am sorry," the figure said, and now the voice was different. Softer. Almost kind. "But some secrets are worth killing for."

It opened its hand.

Li Hua's soul began to dissolve.

"No!" Jiang Chen lunged forward, grabbing his staff, swinging it with everything he had. The black light erupted, striking the figure's arm, severing it at the elbow.

The figure howled. Li Hua's soul fell, fragmented but intact.

The figure turned to Jiang Chen, and Judge Yanluo's face was twisted with rage.

"You have no idea what you've done."

"Then explain it to me."

But the figure was already fading, dissolving into mist, leaving behind only a severed arm and a broken jade mask.

Jiang Chen stood in the silence, breathing hard, Li Hua's fragmented soul hovering beside him.

"What was that?" she whispered.

"I don't know."

"You do know. You saw his face."

Jiang Chen picked up the broken mask. The three characters on the forehead were still visible.

*The Thirteenth Judge.*

"There are only Ten Judges," he said. "Everyone knows that. There are only Ten."

"Then who was that?"

He looked at the mask. At the face beneath it. At the man who had recruited him. The man who had given him the token. The man who had— supposedly— entrusted him with the power to judge the dead.

"I don't know," he said again.

But he was lying.

He knew exactly who it was.

And he knew exactly what it meant.

Judge Yanluo was trying to kill Li Hua before she could talk.

Which meant everything she'd said about his death was true.

title: Chapter 18
content:
CHAPTER 18: The Thirteenth Judge

The black light consumed everything.

Jiang Chen felt himself falling, but not through space—through memory. His own memories. They flashed past him like windows in a burning building: his mother's face, his first day of school, the exam results that changed everything, the office where he'd taken that first bribe, the courtroom where they'd read his sentence, the table where they'd killed him.

*She's inside me again.*

But this time was different. This time, he wasn't just being observed. He was being *guided*. Her presence moved through his memories with purpose, not like a thief searching for valuables, but like a detective examining evidence.

*What are you doing?* he tried to say, but his voice didn't work.

*Showing you what I saw.*

She pulled a memory forward. The execution. But from a different angle—from *her* eyes, filtered through his mind. He saw the witnesses in the observation room. Saw their faces clearly for the first time. Saw one man in particular, standing at the back, not wearing a uniform like the others. Civilian clothes. Expensive shoes. A face that seemed familiar but distant, like a photograph seen through fog.

*Who is that?*

*I don't know his name. But I've seen him before. In other memories. Other executions.*

The black light faded. They were standing in Diyu again, but not in the main hall. This was somewhere deeper. Older. The air smelled of wet stone and forgotten contracts. The walls were lined with shelves—thousands of them—each filled with scrolls bound in red silk.

"Where are we?" Jiang Chen demanded, pulling his staff free from her chest.

She stumbled back, gasping. "The Archive of Unfinished Business. Every soul that passes through Diyu leaves a record here. Every crime, every judgment, every loose thread."

"Why did you bring us here?"

"Because I need to show you something." She walked to a shelf, her fingers trailing across the scrolls. "When I possessed you, I didn't just see your memories. I saw your *file*. The one the judges have on you. It's incomplete."

"Everyone's file is incomplete. That's why we have investigations."

"No." She pulled a scroll free and unrolled it. "Your file has been *edited*. Someone removed pages. I saw the gaps. The places where information used to be."

Jiang Chen grabbed the scroll. His name was written at the top in elegant brush strokes. Below it: *Jiang Chen, Case #78421, Judgment: Execution.* But the details were sparse. The crime was listed as "Murder of Chen Wei, Level 3." The evidence was summarized in three sentences. The judge's signature was there, but the name was blurred, like ink that had been smudged while still wet.

"This doesn't prove anything."

"It proves your case was fast-tracked. Look at the date." She pointed to the bottom. "Your crime was committed on the 15th. You were arrested on the 17th. Trial on the 19th. Execution on the 22nd. One week from crime to death. Do you know how long murder cases usually take in the mortal world?"

Years. Sometimes decades. He knew that. He'd worked in the legal system long enough to know that justice moved slowly, especially for capital cases.

"Someone wanted me dead quickly," he said slowly.

"Someone wanted you dead *before* you could talk." She rolled the scroll back up. "The question is: what did you know that was worth killing you over?"

"I was a mid-level accountant. I processed bribe payments for a shell company. I didn't know who was at the top."

"But you knew *something*. Or someone thought you did." She tilted her head. "Think. Before the arrest. Was there anything unusual? Any meeting? Any document that seemed out of place?"

Jiang Chen closed his eyes. The memories were there, but they were jumbled, suppressed by the trauma of execution. He forced himself to go back. Three weeks before his arrest. He'd been working late, processing the monthly accounts. There was a discrepancy. A payment that didn't match any of the usual codes. He'd flagged it, sent it up the chain, and been told to ignore it.

But he hadn't ignored it. He'd made a copy. Kept it in his personal files. A safety measure, he'd told himself. Insurance.

*The copy. Where is it now?*

His apartment. Hidden in the false bottom of his desk drawer. But his apartment had been sealed after his arrest. Everything confiscated.

Unless...

"Someone else has been using my body."

The woman—Madame Bai, he reminded himself—raised an eyebrow. "You're only figuring that out now? Of course someone is using your body. That's how possession works. You die, your soul goes to Diyu, your body becomes available. Usually it gets cremated, but sometimes—"

"No." He grabbed her arm. "You don't understand. I was executed three weeks ago. But my body wasn't cremated. It was *released*. To my family. For burial."

"And?"

"And what if they didn't bury it?"

She stared at him. The con woman's mask slipped, and he saw genuine surprise beneath it. "You think someone is wearing your corpse?"

"I think someone made sure I was executed quickly so they could take my place. Someone who needed a clean identity. Someone who needed to disappear and reappear as someone else."

*Someone who knew I was going to die.*

The thought hit him like a physical blow. His death wasn't just a cover-up. It was a *transaction*. Someone had needed his life—his identity, his body, his place in the world—and they'd paid to have it.

"The man in the observation room," he said. "The one in civilian clothes. I need to know who he is."

Madame Bai smiled. "Now you're thinking like a criminal."

"I'm thinking like someone who wants to stay alive in the afterlife."

"Same thing." She gestured at the archive. "The records here only go so far. They track souls, not living people. If you want to find your mystery man, you need to go back to the mortal realm."

"And if I leave Diyu without permission, I'll be branded a deserter. The judges will send soul reapers after me."

"Then don't get caught."

He looked at his staff. The black crystal at its center pulsed with a dim light. He had power now. Real power. He could cross the veil at will, track souls, even fight if he had to. But he was still an apprentice. Still learning. Against a full soul reaper, he'd last maybe thirty seconds.

"I need to know the names," he said finally. "The names of everyone involved. The judge who signed my death warrant. The prosecutor who rushed the case. The witness who testified against me."

"And if I give you those names?"

"Then I'll consider letting you speak to the judges."

She laughed. "You're learning. But no. Names first, then judgment. That's the deal."

"No. I take you to the judges, but only after I have the names. That's my offer."

She studied him for a long moment. Then she extended her hand again. "Deal."

This time, when he took her hand, there was no trick. Her soul connected with his, and he felt information flowing into him like water through a broken dam. Names. Dates. Places. A web of corruption that stretched from the courtroom to the police station to the office where he'd worked.

And at the center of it all: a name he didn't recognize.

*Lu Fang.*

"Who is Lu Fang?" he asked, pulling his hand back.

"The man who paid for your execution. The man in the observation room. The man who is currently walking around in your body."

The world tilted. The archive walls seemed to close in around him. *Lu Fang.* The name meant nothing to him. He'd never met anyone by that name. Never worked with him. Never crossed him.

And yet this stranger had paid to have him killed.

"How do you know all this?"

"I told you. I've been possessing people for seventeen years. I've seen every secret, every crime, every hidden truth." She smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "I know things that would make the judges weep. I know things that would topple governments. I know things that—"

"Then why are you still here?" he interrupted. "Why haven't you used that knowledge to escape?"

Her smile faded. "Because some secrets are too dangerous to speak aloud. And some people are too powerful to cross." She looked at the scrolls around them. "I've been running for seventeen years. Hiding. Stealing bodies. Staying one step ahead of the soul reapers. But I've never been truly free. Because the people who run this world—*both* worlds—they don't play by the rules. They *make* the rules."

"Then why help me? What do you get out of it?"

"Revenge." The word came out sharp, like a blade. "The same thing you want. The people who killed you are the same people who ruined my life. They took everything from me. My family. My name. My death." She laughed, but it was bitter. "I was a con woman, yes. I cheated people. I stole from them. But I never killed anyone. And they sentenced me to death anyway. Because I knew too much."

"Lu Fang?"

"Lu Fang is a puppet. A face. The real power is someone else. Someone who has been alive for centuries, moving through bodies like changing clothes."

Jiang Chen felt cold. "You're talking about a cultivator."

"Not just a cultivator. A *judge*. One of the Ten Judges of Diyu."

The words hit him like a punch to the chest. "That's impossible. The judges are immortal. They don't—"

"They don't what? Age? Die? They're still human, Jiang Chen. They still have desires. Ambitions. Greed." She stepped closer, her voice dropping to a whisper. "One of the judges has been selling souls. Selling *executions*. If you pay enough, they'll make anyone disappear. No questions asked. No evidence needed."

"Which judge?"

"I don't know yet. But I can find out. If you take me to the main hall, I can access the central records. Cross-reference the names. Find the pattern."

"And if the judge catches us?"

"Then we die. For real this time."

Jiang Chen looked at his staff. The black crystal pulsed again, and for a moment, he thought he saw something moving inside it. A face. A memory. *His* face, reflected in the crystal, but twisted, wrong.

*You're already dead,* he reminded himself. *What more can they take from you?*

"Fine," he said. "Take me to the central records."

Madame Bai smiled. "I thought you'd never ask."

She turned and walked deeper into the archive, her footsteps echoing against the stone. Jiang Chen followed, his staff clutched tight in his hands, the names and dates and secrets burning in his mind like a fever.

*Lu Fang. The judge. The conspiracy.*

*And somewhere, wearing his skin, the man who had him killed.*

The archive seemed to stretch forever, shelf after shelf of red-bound scrolls, each one containing a life, a death, a judgment. And somewhere in this labyrinth, the truth was waiting.

But as they turned a corner, Jiang Chen stopped.

Standing in the middle of the aisle, blocking their path, was a figure in black robes. A soul reaper. Tall. Lean.

title: The Truth That Burns
content:
Jiang Chen's staff pulsed against the con woman's chest, chains of black light coiling around her soul. She thrashed, tried to pull free, but the chains tightened. For a moment, he saw her real face — not the middle-aged woman's body she'd stolen, but her true form underneath. Young. Maybe twenty-five. Pretty in a sharp, dangerous way. Dark eyes that had seen too much.

"You're making a mistake," she snarled.

"I've made a lot of mistakes." He pulled the staff back, and she crumpled to the ground. "Trusting you was almost one of them."

The chains held her arms behind her back. She couldn't move, couldn't shift, couldn't possess. He'd learned that trick from watching Juè capture spirits. A binding of pure soul-force, anchored to the staff. It wouldn't last forever — maybe an hour — but it was enough.

"I wasn't going to possess you," she said.

"You literally just tried."

"I was testing you." She smiled that infuriating smile. "I needed to know if you'd hesitate. You didn't. Good. We're going to need that."

"No more games."

"No more games." She sat up, chains clinking. "I'm going to tell you something, and you're going to want to kill me for it. But you won't, because you need me."

"I need names."

"Then listen." She took a breath. "My name isn't Li Mei. It's not any of the names I've used. It's Xue. Just Xue. I was a prosecutor in Hebei province. Seven years. I put away forty-three criminals — murderers, traffickers, corrupt officials. I was good at my job. Too good."

Jiang Chen's grip on the staff loosened. "You were a prosecutor?"

"Until I prosecuted the wrong person." Her voice dropped. "A man named Chen Wei. He was a mid-level manager at a state-owned construction company. I had evidence he'd taken bribes to approve unsafe buildings. Three schools collapsed. Two hundred and seventeen children died."

"I remember that case." Everyone in China remembered that case. It had been national news for weeks.

"I built a solid case. Witnesses, documents, financial records. I had him dead to rights." She looked down at her hands. "And then my boss called me into his office and told me to drop it. Chen Wei's uncle was a party secretary. Higher up than anyone I'd ever met. They offered me a promotion. A raise. A new apartment."

"You refused."

"I refused." She met his eyes. "Three days later, I was arrested. Embezzlement, they said. I'd stolen evidence from my own cases. The evidence was planted. The witnesses were paid. The judge was a cousin of Chen Wei's uncle."

Jiang Chen felt cold. "What happened to you?"

"I was sentenced to fifteen years. I died in prison two years later. "Suicide," they said. But I didn't kill myself." She touched her throat. "They strangled me with a bedsheet and hung me from the ceiling to make it look like a hanging."

The chains around her wrists flickered. His concentration was slipping.

"I woke up in a morgue," she continued. "I didn't understand what had happened. I wasn't a ghost — I was still alive, sort of. My body was dead, but my soul was... intact. Strong. I could touch things, move things. I walked out of the morgue and no one stopped me."

"That's not possible."

"It is for us." She looked at him meaningfully. "You're the same, aren't you? Not quite alive. Not quite dead. You can cross the veil because you're already halfway there."

"I was executed."

"No." She shook her head. "You were murdered. There's a difference. Execution is justice. What happened to you was assassination."

Jiang Chen's hands were shaking again. He forced them still. "You said you'd give me names."

"Three names." She counted on her fingers. "Judge Wang, who signed your death warrant. He received three million yuan from a shell company owned by... Liu Feng, deputy director of the Ministry of State Security. And Liu Feng took orders from someone higher. Someone who's been dead for six years."

"What?"

"Someone who's been dead for six years," she repeated. "Someone who's been running operations from the underworld."

"That's impossible. You can't contact the living from—"

"I didn't say he contacted them." She leaned forward. "I said he's been dead for six years. But he's still giving orders. How do you think I survived in prison for two years? How do you think I escaped the morgue? Someone helped me. Someone who knew what I was becoming."

"Who?"

"Zhao Ming."

The name hit him like a physical blow. Zhao Ming was a legend — a soul reaper who'd served for three hundred years, one of the most powerful in Diyu's history. He'd been executed six years ago for treason against the Ten Judges.

"Zhao Ming is dead," Jiang Chen said.

"He's dead and he's alive, same as you. Same as me." She smiled. "He's been building something in the shadows. A network. Soul reapers who don't serve the judges. Spirits who refuse to reincarnate. Living people who owe him favors. He wants to tear down Diyu and rebuild it."

"Why?"

"Because the judges aren't fair. You know this. You've seen how they operate. They punish the poor and forgive the rich. They let corrupt officials buy their way out of hell. They gave you a job as a soul reaper because you were useful, not because you deserved redemption."

"She's right."

The voice came from behind them. Jiang Chen spun around, staff raised. A man stood twenty feet away, wearing a black suit that seemed to drink the light around it. He was tall, middle-aged, with silver hair and eyes that glowed faintly gold.

Zhao Ming.

"You've become quite the topic of conversation, Jiang Chen." Zhao Ming walked forward, hands in his pockets. "The judges are furious. They've lost two reapers in three weeks, and now their newest recruit is consorting with an escaped soul."

"Stay back."

"I'm not here to fight." Zhao Ming stopped, raised his hands. "I'm here to offer you the truth. The real truth, not the half-truths Xue has been feeding you."

"You know each other?"

"We created each other," Xue said bitterly. "He's the reason I'm like this. He found me in the prison, taught me how to use my abilities, then abandoned me when I became too dangerous."

"I didn't abandon you. I gave you freedom."

"You turned me into a monster."

"The potential was already there." Zhao Ming's voice was patient, almost kind. "I just showed you how to unlock it."

Jiang Chen looked between them. His head was spinning. Too much information, too fast. He needed time to think, to process, to—

The staff vibrated. A warning.

A portal ripped open ten feet away, and Juè stepped through, her white face blank with fury. Behind her, five more soul reapers emerged, their staffs crackling with black light.

"Jiang Chen." Juè's voice was ice. "Step away from the fugitives."

"Juè, listen—"

"The fugitive known as Xue has killed two of our brothers. The fugitive known as Zhao Ming has been sentenced to death for treason. You will step away, or I will treat you as their accomplice."

"She has information about my death. About the people who framed me."

"I don't care." Juè raised her staff. "The judges have spoken. These two are to be destroyed, soul and memory, with no reincarnation. That's the law."

"Then the law is wrong."

Silence. The other soul reapers shifted uncomfortably.

"What did you say?" Juè's voice dropped to a whisper.

"I said the law is wrong." Jiang Chen planted his staff in the ground. "I've spent the last three weeks chasing souls, delivering justice, doing my job. And you know what I've learned? The system is broken. The rich buy their way out. The powerful get second chances. People like me — people like Xue — we get executed for crimes we didn't commit."

"You're a soul reaper. You took an oath."

"An oath I didn't understand. An oath I was manipulated into taking." He looked at Juè, and for the first time, he saw something in her face that wasn't cold efficiency. Doubt. "You know I'm right. You've been doing this for two hundred years. You've seen it."

Juè's staff lowered an inch. "You don't know what you're saying."

"I know exactly what I'm saying." He turned to Zhao Ming. "Take me with you."

Zhao Ming's golden eyes widened slightly. "You trust me?"

"I trust that the judges want you dead. That's enough for now."

"Jiang Chen, don't—" Xue started.

"Shut up." He looked at Juè. "I'm sorry. I know you were trying to help me. But I can't do this anymore. I can't serve a system that killed me."

Juè's face twisted. Pain, or anger, or both. "If you go with him, you're a traitor. You'll never be able to return to Diyu."

"I know."

"The judges will hunt you. For eternity."

"I know."

"Your soul will be destroyed. No reincarnation. No peace."

"I know." Jiang Chen stepped toward Zhao Ming. "But at least I'll die knowing the truth."

Zhao Ming smiled. He reached into his pocket and pulled out a black jade token, carved with symbols that burned in Jiang Chen's vision. "Hold on."

Jiang Chen grabbed Xue's arm. She grabbed his. Zhao Ming crushed the token.

The world dissolved into black fire.

The last thing Jiang Chen saw was Juè's face — not angry, not betrayed, but something worse.

Disappointed.

Then nothing.

---

He woke on cold stone. Night sky above. Stars he didn't recognize.

"Where are we?"

Zhao Ming stood at the edge of a cliff, looking down at a city that glowed with ghostly blue light. "The other side of the veil. A place the judges don't control."

Xue sat up, rubbing her wrists. The chains were gone. "This is your stronghold?"

"This is the beginning." Zhao Ming turned. "Welcome to the rebellion, Jiang Chen. I hope you're ready to burn."

title: Chapter 20
content:
CHAPTER 20: THE THIRD KILLER

The black light from Jiang Chen's staff sent Madam Zhao flying backward. She crashed through a display case of antique vases, shattering porcelain and glass across the funeral home's showroom floor.

But she didn't scream again.

She laughed.

"You think that hurts?" She picked herself up, brushing ceramic shards from her stolen body's expensive silk blouse. "I've been burned alive. Drowned. Stabbed. Poisoned. I've died seventeen different ways, boy. A little light show doesn't scare me."

Jiang Chen gripped his staff with both hands. His knuckles were white. "You're coming back to Diyu. Willingly or not."

"Or not. Definitely or not." She circled him, stepping over broken glass. "You know what I find interesting? You've been a soul reaper for—what? Three weeks? A month? And you've already learned to use that staff. The previous two took years to get that good."

"I'm a fast learner."

"No." She stopped circling. "You're desperate. There's a difference. The other two were professionals. They had training. Discipline. A sense of duty. You have—" She sniffed the air. "—guilt. You don't want to be here. You want to be back in your old life. You want to wake up and find out this was all a nightmare."

Jiang Chen's jaw tightened.

"Struck a nerve?" She smiled. "Good. Now you know how I feel when you talk about dragging me back to the underworld."

"Your deal was a lie."

"Of course it was a lie. I'm a con woman. I told you that. But the truth about your death—" She paused. "—that wasn't a lie."

"You showed me nothing."

"I showed you enough. You felt it. You know I'm telling the truth."

Jiang Chen wanted to deny it. He wanted to believe she was manipulating him, playing on his emotions, using his own trauma as a weapon. But she was right. He had felt it. The moment her soul touched his, he'd seen flashes of his execution—not through his own eyes, but through hers. She'd witnessed it. She'd been there.

Which was impossible.

"How did you see my execution?"

Madam Zhao's smile flickered. "What?"

"My execution. You saw it. When you tried to possess me, you saw it. But you've been possessing people in the mortal realm for seventeen years. You've never been to Diyu. So how did you see something that happened in the underworld?"

She was silent for a long moment. Then her smile returned, but it was smaller now. Careful. "I saw it in your memories."

"No. You didn't. I wasn't remembering it. You were showing it to me. You knew what happened. You knew the judge was paid. You knew the evidence was fabricated. You knew—" He stopped. A cold realization was creeping up his spine. "—you knew because you were there."

"Don't be ridiculous. I've been stuck in this body for—"

"For three weeks. Since the day I was executed."

Her face went pale.

"You were there," Jiang Chen repeated. "In the courtroom. In the execution chamber. Watching. You're not just a con woman who escaped Diyu. You're connected to my case."

"I'm not connected to anything."

"Then explain it."

"I can't."

"You won't."

"There's a difference."

"No." Jiang Chen raised his staff. "There isn't."

He moved before she could react. The staff arced through the air, trailing black light. Madam Zhao dodged, but she was slower now—the possession was weakening her. She stumbled over broken glass, and the staff caught her shoulder.

She screamed.

Not a fake scream. Not a performance. Real pain.

"What are you doing?" Her voice cracked. "You'll destroy this body!"

"The body's already dead. You're just borrowing it."

"You can't—"

"I'm not killing you. I'm exorcising you." He swung again. The staff connected with her chest, and black light erupted, peeling her soul away from the flesh like skin from a wound. "When I'm done, you'll be nothing but a shade. No body. No power. Just a ghost waiting for judgment."

She clawed at her own skin, trying to hold herself together. "You don't understand what's at stake!"

"Then explain it to me."

"He'll kill me!"

"Who?"

"I can't say his name!"

"Why not?"

"Because he'll know! He watches everything! He has eyes everywhere!"

Jiang Chen paused. "Who are you talking about?"

"The man who killed you." Her voice was desperate now. "The man who framed you. The man who paid the judge. He's not just a criminal. He's not just a politician. He's—" She stopped. Her eyes went wide.

"What?"

"He's here."

The door to the funeral home burst open.

Jiang Chen spun around, staff raised. A man stood in the doorway. Middle-aged. Expensive suit. Calm expression. He looked like a businessman who'd taken a wrong turn.

But his eyes were wrong.

They were dead. Flat. Empty. Like someone had scooped out the soul and left the shell behind.

"Madam Zhao," the man said. His voice was pleasant. Friendly. "I've been looking for you."

"You." Madam Zhao's soul trembled. "You're not supposed to be here."

"I'm everywhere." The man stepped inside. He didn't look at Jiang Chen. Didn't acknowledge the staff or the black light or the shattered glass. He only had eyes for her. "Did you really think you could hide from me? Did you really think I wouldn't find out what you were planning?"

"I wasn't planning anything."

"You were going to tell him." The man nodded at Jiang Chen. "You were going to tell him everything. About the execution. About the conspiracy. About me."

"I wasn't—"

"Don't lie to me." The man's voice didn't change. Still pleasant. Still friendly. But something in the room shifted. The temperature dropped. The shadows grew longer. "I know everything you know. I've always known. Did you really think I would let you run loose for seventeen years without keeping an eye on you?"

Madam Zhao's soul started to shake.

"You're my greatest creation, Zhao Lian. The perfect con. Dead seventeen times, and still alive. Still useful. But you were never loyal. You were never grateful. You always thought you could outsmart me."

"I can outsmart anyone."

"Not me." The man raised his hand. "I made you. And I can unmake you."

"No—"

The man snapped his fingers.

Madam Zhao's soul exploded.

Jiang Chen threw up his arm to shield his face, but there was nothing to shield against. Her soul didn't burn or scream. It simply ceased to exist. One moment she was there, pressed against the stolen body's flesh, desperate and terrified. The next moment she was nothing.

Just gone.

The body collapsed. The expensive silk blouse. The designer shoes. The carefully maintained appearance of a wealthy socialite. It hit the floor like a puppet with cut strings.

Jiang Chen stared at the body. Then at the man. "What did you do?"

"I cleaned up a loose end." The man smiled. "Don't look so shocked. You were going to do the same thing eventually. She was a murderer. A thief. A con artist. The world is better without her."

"She was my only lead."

"Your only lead to what?"

Jiang Chen didn't answer.

"Your execution?" The man laughed. "You think I don't know why you're really here? You think I don't know about the framed evidence? The paid judge? The fabricated case?" He shook his head. "I know everything, Jiang Chen. I've known since the moment you died. I was the one who made it happen."

The words hit Jiang Chen like a physical blow. "You."

"Me." The man spread his arms. "I orchestrated your death. I chose the victim. I planted the evidence. I paid the judge. I made sure you would be executed for a crime you didn't commit."

"Why?"

"Because you saw something you shouldn't have."

"I was an accountant. I looked at numbers."

"Exactly." The man's smile widened. "You looked at the wrong numbers. You saw the discrepancy. You knew about the missing funds. You knew about the offshore accounts. You knew about the bribes. And you were going to report it."

"I never reported anything."

"Because I killed you first."

Jiang Chen's hands were shaking. His staff felt heavy. His vision blurred. "Who are you?"

"My name is Cheng Wei. I'm a businessman. A philanthropist. A pillar of the community." He laughed again. "I'm also the most powerful crime lord in three provinces. I own judges. I own politicians. I own police commissioners. I own everything."

"Then why are you here? Why are you telling me this?"

"Because you can't do anything about it." Cheng Wei stepped closer. "You're a soul reaper. You serve the Ten Judges of Diyu. You're bound by their rules. You can't touch me. You can't hurt me. You can't even reveal my crimes to the living world."

"I can tell the judges."

"Tell them what? That a dead woman told you something before I erased her? That you have no proof, no witnesses, no evidence?" He shook his head. "The judges are bound by law. They can't act on hearsay. They can't investigate the living world without cause. And I've made sure there's no cause."

Jiang Chen gripped his staff. "Then why are you here? Why reveal yourself?"

"Because I want you to know." Cheng Wei's smile vanished. "I want you to know that I won. I want you to know that there's nothing you can do. I want you to live the rest of your existence as a soul reaper, hunting ghosts and collecting souls, knowing that the man who killed you is still out there. Still free. Still winning."

"You're insane."

"I'm practical." Cheng Wei turned toward the door. "Goodbye, Jiang Chen. Enjoy your eternity."

He walked out.

Jiang Chen stood alone in the shattered funeral home, surrounded by broken glass and a dead body and the ashes of his only lead.

His staff trembled in his hands.

And then he heard it.

A voice. Whispering from the shadows.

*"He's lying."*

Jiang Chen spun around. "Who's there?"

The shadows moved. A figure stepped out. Old man. Wrinkled face. Tattered robes. Eyes that glowed faintly green.

*"Cheng Wei didn't kill you. He thinks he did. But he's just a pawn."*

"Who are you?"

*"Someone who's been waiting for you."* The old man smiled. *"Someone who knows the truth. The real truth. About your death. About the conspiracy. About the man who's really pulling the strings."*

"Tell me."

*"Not here. Not now."* The old man gestured. *"Come with me. I'll show you everything."*

Jiang Chen hesitated. He'd been tricked before. By Madam Zhao. By Cheng Wei. By everyone.

But the old man's eyes were different.

They were kind.

*"I know you don't trust me,"* the old man said. *"And you shouldn't. Trust is a luxury the dead can't afford."* He extended his hand. *"But if you want the truth, you'll follow me."*

Jiang Chen looked

title: Chapter 6
content:
CHAPTER 6: The Truth's Sharp Edge

The black light threw her across the room. She crashed through a stack of cardboard boxes, sending old clothes and forgotten treasures scattering across the floor. The body she'd stolen lay limp, but her soul rose from it like smoke from dying embers.

"You're quicker than the others," she said, her voice coming from everywhere and nowhere. "But you still think like a living man. You want names. You want proof. You want answers."

Jiang Chen gripped his staff, the wood warm against his palms. "I want the truth."

"No, you don't." Her soul solidified, taking the form of a woman in her thirties, sharp features, sharper smile. "The truth will destroy you. The truth will make you realize that every second you spend chasing souls for Diyu is a second the people who killed you get to keep breathing."

"Stop playing games."

"I'm not playing." She pointed at his chest. "I'm in your head, remember? I saw your execution. I saw the witnesses. I saw the judge's face when he read the verdict. He didn't look at you with pity or justice. He looked at you like you were a debt he was collecting."

Jiang Chen's jaw tightened. He remembered that face. Old Judge Shen, with his wire-rimmed glasses and permanent scowl. The man had barely looked up from his notes during the entire trial.

"What about Judge Shen?" he asked.

"Oh, he's dead." She laughed. "Heart attack. Three days after your execution. Convenient, isn't it?"

The words hit him like a physical blow. "Dead?"

"Silenced. Just like the witness who placed you at the crime scene. Just like the forensic expert who 'mistakenly' matched your fingerprints to the murder weapon." She counted on her fingers. "That's three people connected to your case, all dead within a month of your execution. And you think it's coincidence?"

Jiang Chen's mind raced. He'd been a soul reaper for less than a month. He'd been too busy learning the ropes, chasing rogue souls, trying to survive. He hadn't even thought about investigating his own death.

"Who?" he demanded. "Who paid for the frame?"

"Take me to Diyu. I'll tell the judges everything."

"You'll tell me first."

She shook her head. "I've been alive for seventeen years past my death date because I know how to make deals. If I tell you now, you have no reason to keep me alive. You'll drag me to Diyu and let them wipe me clean."

"You're a murderer."

"I'm a survivor." Her eyes met his. "And so are you. We're the same, you and me. People who should be dead but aren't. People who've been wronged by the system and have to make their own justice."

"I'm nothing like you."

"You're exactly like me." She stepped closer. "You just haven't admitted it yet."

The body on the floor twitched. Jiang Chen glanced at it, then back at her. "Your host is dying."

"She was already dead. I just borrowed her for a few hours." She waved dismissively. "Her soul was weak. She would have died anyway."

"That's not your call to make."

"It is when I'm trying to survive." Her voice hardened. "You think I enjoy this? You think I wanted to spend seventeen years running, hiding, jumping from body to body like a parasite? I had a life. I had a family. I had a daughter who was seven years old when I died. Do you know what it's like to watch your child grow up from the shadows? To see her wedding from a stranger's eyes?"

Jiang Chen stayed silent.

"I don't want to be this," she continued. "I want to rest. I want to see my daughter one more time. I want to tell her that I didn't abandon her, that I was taken, that I fought to come back." Her voice cracked. "But I can't. Because whatever they killed you for, it's the same thing they killed me for."

"What are you talking about?"

"Your death wasn't random. My death wasn't random. We were both executed for things we didn't do, both silenced for what we knew." She looked at him with something like desperation. "I was a journalist. I was investigating a network of corruption that went all the way to the top. They called me a traitor, said I was selling state secrets. I was sentenced to death in a closed trial. No evidence. No witnesses. Just a signature and a date."

"Who?"

"People who are still alive. People who are still in power. People who have judges, prosecutors, and executioners on their payroll." She held out her hand again. "Take me to Diyu, and I'll give you everything. My notes. My sources. The names of everyone involved. I hid it all before I was arrested."

"Where?"

"Somewhere the judges will have to listen. Somewhere they can't ignore." She smiled. "I know how the underworld works, Soul Reaper. I've been avoiding it for seventeen years. The Ten Judges are bound by rules. They can't ignore evidence of mortal corruption, especially if it involves their own people."

Jiang Chen considered his options. He could drag her to Diyu right now. He could present her to the judges and let them sort it out. But if she was telling the truth, if there was a network of corruption that had already killed three people connected to his case, then the judges might not be safe either.

"Give me one name," he said. "One name I can verify. Then I'll take you to Diyu."

She studied him for a long moment. "You're smarter than you look."

"One name."

"Commissioner Zhao Wei. Internal Affairs. He was the one who signed off on the evidence against me. He's probably the one who signed off on yours too."

Commissioner Zhao Wei. The name was familiar. Jiang Chen had seen it in the news, always attached to anti-corruption campaigns, always smiling in front of podiums.

"The corruption fighter?"

"The biggest corrupt of them all." She laughed bitterly. "He built his career on arresting the people who worked for him. Every time someone got too close to the truth, he'd sacrifice a pawn and take credit for cleaning house."

"And you can prove this?"

"I can prove everything. I just need to get to Diyu alive."

Jiang Chen made a decision. He grabbed the staff and pointed it at her. "Fine. We're going to Diyu. But I'm putting a leash on you."

"A leash?"

He concentrated, and black light threaded from the staff, wrapping around her soul like chains. She struggled, but the binds held.

"This is just temporary," he said. "You behave, and I'll remove them when we get to the judges."

"And if I don't behave?"

"Then I'll drag you there screaming."

She smirked. "I like you, Soul Reaper. You've got teeth."

He ignored her and focused on the body on the floor. The woman was still breathing, barely. He couldn't leave her here. He pulled out his phone and dialed emergency services, gave them the address, then hung up before they could ask questions.

"Sentimental," the soul said.

"I'm not a monster."

"No. You're just a dead man who doesn't know he's still fighting for justice." She floated beside him as he left the apartment. "That's worse."

The walk to the nearest crossing point was silent. The streets were empty, the moon hidden behind clouds. Jiang Chen could feel her soul pressing against the chains, testing them, but she didn't try to escape.

"Why did you kill those soul reapers?" he asked finally.

"Because they would have erased me. The judges don't let souls like me exist. We're anomalies. Problems. They'd rather wipe us clean and start over than deal with the complications."

"Or maybe they don't want people who steal bodies to keep running around."

"They don't want people who know too much." She looked at him. "You think you're the first executed person they've recruited? You think you're special? They've been doing this for centuries. Finding people on death row, offering them a second chance as soul reapers. It's a perfect system. The dead are loyal because they have nothing else. The dead don't ask questions because they're grateful to exist."

"You're saying the judges are corrupt?"

"I'm saying the judges are part of a system. And systems can be manipulated." She paused. "Think about it. You died, and within days, a soul reaper was there to recruit you. Not weeks. Not months. Days. They knew you were coming. They were waiting."

Jiang Chen stopped walking. She was right. He'd never questioned it. He'd been so focused on surviving, on learning, on proving himself, that he hadn't stopped to think about the timing.

"Old Man Chen," he muttered. "He found me the same day I was executed."

"Sounds like they had you marked from the beginning."

"Or maybe it was just coincidence."

"You don't believe that." She smiled. "I can feel it. The doubt. The suspicion. It's growing."

He started walking again, faster this time. "Shut up."

"Just pointing out the obvious."

The crossing point was a subway entrance that didn't exist on any map. During the day, it was just a wall. At night, it was a staircase leading down to a platform that smelled of incense and old bones.

Jiang Chen descended, the soul floating behind him. The air grew colder with each step. The lights flickered.

"You sure about this?" she asked.

"No."

"Good answer."

They reached the platform. A train was waiting, its cars empty, its windows dark. The door slid open.

Jiang Chen stepped inside. The soul followed.

And then the world shattered.

The train car exploded with light. Jiang Chen threw up his arms, but it was too late. He felt hands grabbing him, chains wrapping around his limbs, a hood being pulled over his head.

"Traitor," a voice hissed. "You brought her back."

"No, I—"

"She's gone. We have her. And now we have you."

The hood tightened. Jiang Chen couldn't breathe. He heard footsteps, felt himself being dragged, heard the soul screaming somewhere in the distance.

"You were supposed to be better than the others," the voice said. "But you're all the same. Weak. Gullible. Dead."

Darkness took him.

title: Chapter 7
content:
CHAPTER 7: The Names She Carried

The black light from the staff didn't just push her out of my mind. It ripped her out. She flew backward, her spectral form tumbling across the warehouse floor like a leaf in a storm. The body she'd been wearing — the Dock Master's wife — collapsed in a heap, her chest still, her eyes open and vacant.

The woman's soul rose from the body like smoke from a dying fire. She was visible now. Truly visible. An old woman with sharp features, her hair pulled back in a tight bun, her eyes narrow and calculating. She wore a traditional qipao, the fabric stained with what looked like ink. Or blood.

"You're stronger than the other two," she said, rubbing her chest where the staff had struck her. "They were amateurs. You've had training."

"I've had three weeks of training."

"Three weeks?" She laughed. "I've been possessing people for seventeen years. I've stolen memories from judges, lawyers, police chiefs. I've seen more of your world's secrets than your precious Ten Judges have seen in a thousand years."

"You said you had names."

"I do." She circled me, her feet not quite touching the ground. "But I need something first."

"You need to be in Diyu."

"No." She stopped. "I need you to understand what you're dealing with. The people who framed you don't just exist in your world. They exist in mine too."

"What does that mean?"

She pointed at my staff. "That thing. It's a soul reaper's weapon. It can sever souls from bodies, bind spirits, open gates to Diyu. But it can also read memories. Every soul you capture leaves a trace in the staff. Every secret they carried becomes part of the weapon's knowledge."

I looked at the staff. It hummed in my hand, warm and alive. "I know."

"Do you know how to read them?"

I didn't. Old Li had mentioned it once, but he'd said it was an advanced technique. Something for senior reapers. Not for someone who'd only been dead for three weeks.

"I can teach you," she said. "In exchange for the names I want."

"Who's teaching who?"

"I've been dead for forty-three years, Jiang Chen. I've learned things that no living soul reaper knows. I can show you how to read the staff, how to access every memory it's collected. And then I can show you what I've seen."

"Why would you help me?"

She tilted her head. "Because I'm old. Because I'm tired. Because I've been running for forty-three years, and I want it to end." Her voice cracked, just slightly. "But I don't want the judges to wipe me. I don't want to forget. I've lived too long to die as a blank slate."

"Then why did you try to possess me?"

"Because I didn't trust you." She smiled. "I still don't. But I'm out of options."

I tightened my grip on the staff. "Show me the memories first. Then I'll decide."

She studied me for a long moment. Then she nodded. "Close your eyes."

"Why?"

"Because this will hurt. And I don't want you to see it coming."

I hesitated. Every instinct told me this was a trap. She was a con woman, a manipulator, a killer. She'd already tried to possess me twice. But she'd also seen something in my death that I needed to know.

I closed my eyes.

Her soul touched mine, gentle this time. Not an attack. A connection. I felt her memories flowing into me like water through a broken dam.

I saw a courtroom. Not the one I'd been tried in. This one was older, grander. The judge wore a black robe with gold trim. The prosecutor was a man in a tailored suit, his face smooth and confident. The defendant was a woman — the same woman who was now showing me this memory, but younger. Forty years younger.

Her crime: fraud. Her sentence: death.

I saw the execution. A bullet to the back of the head. Quick. Efficient. Final.

But then I saw what she saw after death. The Diyu judges examining her soul. The judge from her trial standing before them, his soul trembling. He'd been bribed. He'd condemned her to death to cover up his own corruption.

She'd seen it all from the other side. And she'd escaped.

I saw more. Decades of possession. Decades of hiding. Decades of watching the living world from behind borrowed eyes. She'd seen murders, thefts, betrayals. She'd seen politicians trade lives like currency. She'd seen judges sentence innocent people to death while the guilty walked free.

And then I saw my own file.

It appeared in her memory like a photograph. A manila folder stamped with red characters: CLASSIFIED. Inside were documents, photographs, witness statements. My trial. My conviction. My execution.

But there was another folder inside. A darker folder. Black. No stamp. No classification.

Inside were photographs of a man. Young. Handsome. Expensive suit. He was shaking hands with the judge who'd presided over my trial. He was smiling.

I recognized him.

Li Wei. My former boss. The man who'd given me the bribe. The man who'd promised me protection. The man who'd disappeared the day I was arrested.

"You see?" the woman's voice echoed in my mind. "He's still alive. He's still running your company. He's still making deals. And the judge who sentenced you? He's retired. Living on a beach in Hainan. They split the money you were supposed to take the fall for."

"How do you have this?"

"I possessed a secretary in the prosecutor's office. She had access to everything. She made copies of everything. She was going to expose them." The woman's voice grew cold. "I killed her before she could. Not because I cared about the conspiracy. Because I needed leverage."

"Leverage for what?"

"For exactly this moment. For when I needed someone to trust me."

I opened my eyes. The warehouse was quiet. The Dock Master's wife's body lay still on the floor. The woman stood before me, her soul flickering like a candle in the wind.

"I can give you more," she said. "Dates. Times. Bank accounts. Names of everyone involved. But I need you to promise me something."

"What?"

"When you go back to Diyu, don't let them wipe me. Let me testify. Let me face the judges myself. Let me remember what I've done."

"You've killed people."

"I have." She didn't flinch. "Seventeen people over forty-three years. Most of them deserved it. Some of them didn't. I'll face whatever punishment the judges decide. But I want to remember it. I want to carry my crimes with me into whatever hell they send me to."

I looked at my staff. It was pulsing with light, as if it could feel the weight of the decision I was making.

"If I let you live," I said, "you'll try to escape."

"Probably."

"If I let you testify, you'll try to manipulate the judges."

"Almost certainly."

"Why should I trust you?"

She smiled. "You shouldn't. But you're going to anyway." She pointed at my chest. "Because you want revenge more than you want justice. And I'm the only one who can give it to you."

She was right. I hated that she was right.

"Fine," I said. "I'll take you to Diyu. I'll let you testify. But if you try anything—"

"I know. The staff." She held up her hands. "I'm too old for another forty-three years of running. Let's go."

I raised the staff. The black light gathered at its tip, swirling into a portal. The gateway to Diyu. I could feel its pull, its weight, its ancient hunger.

But before I stepped through, I stopped.

"One more thing," I said.

"What?"

"You saw my death. You saw the conspiracy. Is there anyone else involved? Anyone above Li Wei?"

She was quiet for a long moment. Then she said, "There's always someone above. But the trail goes cold after the judge. Either the real mastermind covered their tracks well, or they're already dead."

"Or they're in Diyu."

She looked at me, her eyes sharp. "That's a dangerous thought."

"It's the only thought that makes sense." I gripped the staff. "Someone powerful enough to frame me. Someone connected enough to corrupt a judge. Someone who wanted me dead. And now that I'm dead, I'm in their territory."

"You think one of the judges is involved?"

"I don't know." I stepped toward the portal. "But I'm going to find out."

The woman's soul followed me. The portal opened wide, swallowing us both.

Diyu stretched before us. The gray sky. The endless plains. The distant mountains where the souls of the damned waited for judgment. But something was different. The air was thicker. The ground was cracked. And in the distance, I saw smoke rising from the First Court.

"What is that?" the woman asked.

I didn't answer. I started running toward the smoke.

When I reached the First Court, I found Old Li standing outside. His robes were torn. His face was bloodied. And in his hands, he held the broken pieces of his own staff.

"Jiang Chen," he said, his voice hollow. "You shouldn't have come back."

"What happened?"

He looked at me, and I saw something I'd never seen in his eyes before.

Fear.

"They found out," he said. "About your case. About the conspiracy. About everything."

"Who found out?"

"The Tenth Judge." Old Li's hands trembled. "He's been investigating you since the moment you arrived. He knows you were framed. He knows the judges were involved. And he's coming for you."

"Why would he come for me? I'm the victim."

"Because you're not supposed to exist." Old Li grabbed my shoulder. "You're supposed to be dead, Jiang Chen. Truly dead. Your soul was supposed to be judged and sent to reincarnation. But someone pulled strings. Someone brought you here as a soul reaper. Someone wanted you close."

"Who?"

Old Li opened his mouth to answer.

And then his head exploded.

Blood sprayed across my face. His body crumpled. Behind him, a figure stood in the smoke. Tall. Wearing a black robe. Holding a staff of white bone.

The Tenth Judge.

"You've been a difficult soul to find, Jiang Chen," he said. "But now that I have you, I have questions."

He stepped forward.

The woman behind me screamed.

And everything went black.

title: The Locked Door
content:
The black light from the staff carved through her like a blade through smoke. She didn't just scream—she *unraveled*. Her spectral form split into ribbons of gray mist that scattered across the alley, each ribbon still carrying the echo of her laughter.

"You think that hurts?" Her voice came from everywhere. From the bricks. From the garbage. From the rusted fire escape above. "I've been torn apart before. I always come back."

Jiang Chen gripped the staff with both hands. His knuckles were white. "You tried to possess me again."

"I tried to *help* you." The ribbons began to twist together, reforming. Her face emerged first—that sharp, knowing smile. "You're so desperate for the truth that you'll let me walk right into Diyu without a plan. That's not a deal. That's a suicide mission."

"You said you had names."

"I do." She was whole again, brushing dust off her stolen dress. "But you're not ready for them. You're still thinking like a living man. You want evidence. You want proof. You want to walk into a courtroom and hold up a piece of paper and watch the bad guy get handcuffed." She shook her head. "That's not how this works. The people who killed you? They don't go to court. They *own* the court."

Jiang Chen's jaw tightened. "Then how does it work?"

She pointed at his chest. "You find them. You drag them down to Diyu yourself. You let the judges decide their fate."

"That's not my job. I'm a soul reaper, not an executioner."

"You're whoever you decide to be." She stepped closer. The alley was dark, but her eyes caught the faint light from a distant streetlamp. "You think the other reapers follow the rules? You think they never bend a law or two when justice demands it? Wake up, boy. You're in the underworld now. The only law that matters is the one you enforce."

Jiang Chen wanted to argue. He wanted to tell her she was wrong, that the system worked, that the judges were fair. But he'd been dead for three weeks. He'd seen how the system treated the guilty. He'd seen how it treated the innocent. And he'd seen how easy it was to corrupt a judge with enough money.

"You're still lying to me," he said. "You're a con woman. This is what you do. You find a mark's weakness and you exploit it."

"Yes." She didn't deny it. "But that doesn't mean I'm wrong about your case. You were executed for the murder of a woman named Chen Wei. Stabbed seventeen times in her apartment. The prosecution said you killed her because she threatened to report your bribery scheme. The defense said you were framed. The jury convicted you in two hours. The judge sentenced you to death by lethal injection." She paused. "Sound familiar?"

It did. Every detail was correct. He'd memorized every word of the trial transcript during his months on death row. He'd read it so many times that he could recite it in his sleep.

"How do you know that?"

"I told you. I've been possessing people for seventeen years. I've been inside the minds of criminals, cops, judges, politicians. I've seen the files they keep hidden." She tapped her temple. "It's all in here. Every secret. Every crime. Every dirty deal."

"Then tell me who framed me."

"I will. But not here." She looked around the alley. "We've been standing in one place too long. The living start to notice when spirits linger. And the dead start to notice when soul reapers hesitate."

She was right. He could feel it—a faint pressure building at the edges of his awareness. Other souls, drawn by the commotion. Curious. Hungry.

"We need to move," he said.

"Finally." She started walking toward the mouth of the alley. "I know a place. Safe. Quiet. We can talk there."

"You think I'm going to follow you into some trap?"

"I think you don't have a choice." She didn't look back. "Your master is gone. Your mentors are dead. You're alone in a city full of spirits who want to eat you and a bureaucracy that doesn't trust you. I'm the only friend you've got."

She had a point. A frustrating, infuriating point.

He followed her.

---

The place she led him to was a rooftop garden on top of an abandoned hotel. Weeds had overtaken the flower beds. The fountain was dry. But the view was good—you could see the entire eastern district of the city, a sea of lights and shadows stretching to the horizon.

She sat on the edge of the roof, legs dangling over the drop. "Nice, right? I used to come here when I was alive. Before I became... this."

"You were alive once?"

"Everyone was alive once." She looked at him. "You think I was born a soul-stealing monster?"

"I don't know what you were."

"I was a teacher. Elementary school. I taught math to children who didn't want to learn it." She smiled, but there was no joy in it. "I was good at it, too. I had patience. I had passion. I had a life."

"What happened?"

"Same thing that happened to you. Someone decided I needed to die." She looked away. "I was framed for embezzling from the school's charity fund. They found evidence in my apartment. They showed receipts with my signature. I lost my job. My husband left me. My children stopped speaking to me. I couldn't find work anywhere. No one would hire a thief."

"Were you innocent?"

"Yes." Her voice was flat. "But that didn't matter. The evidence was convincing. The story was compelling. And the person who framed me was the principal's son, who needed a scapegoat because he'd taken the money himself."

Jiang Chen said nothing. He understood. He understood too well.

"I killed myself," she continued. "Six months after the conviction. I couldn't take it anymore. The shame. The isolation. The knowledge that everyone I loved believed I was a monster." She laughed, but it was hollow. "And then I woke up as a spirit. And I realized that death doesn't fix anything. It just makes everything worse."

"So you started possessing people."

"I started surviving. I started hunting. I started *consuming*." She turned to face him. "I've done terrible things, Jiang Chen. I've stolen lives. I've destroyed families. I've killed people who didn't deserve to die. And I'll do it again if I have to. Because that's what this world does to you. It breaks you. And then it asks you to keep going."

"I'm not going to let you keep hurting people."

"Then help me." She stood up. "Help me get to Diyu. Help me talk to the judges. Help me find the people who killed us and drag them down to face justice."

"And then what?"

"And then I'll let them judge me. I'll accept whatever punishment they give. Death. Oblivion. Reincarnation as a cockroach. I don't care." She stepped closer. "But I want to see them suffer first. I want to watch their souls get torn apart. I want to hear them scream the way I screamed when I jumped off that bridge."

Her voice cracked. For a moment, just a moment, she wasn't a con woman or a killer. She was a broken woman holding onto the only thing that kept her going: revenge.

Jiang Chen understood that, too.

"I can't promise you revenge," he said. "I can only promise you a trial."

"That's all I ask." She extended her hand again. "Deal?"

He looked at her hand. He looked at her face. He looked at the city below, full of living people who had no idea that the dead walked among them.

He took her hand.

"Deal."

---

They descended from the rooftop together. She led him through back alleys and abandoned buildings, always moving, never staying in one place too long. She knew the city like no one he'd ever met—every shortcut, every hiding spot, every place where the veil between worlds was thin enough to cross.

"You've been planning this for a while," he said.

"Seventeen years." She ducked through a broken window into an old warehouse. The floor was covered in dust and rat droppings. "I've been looking for a way back to Diyu ever since I escaped. But the judges put wards on the gates. Only soul reapers can open them."

"And you need me to open the gate."

"I need you to open the gate." She stopped in the middle of the warehouse. "There's one nearby. Underneath the old city morgue. The judges built it there because it's a place of death—the veil is thin. If we can get to it, you can open it, and I can walk through."

"And then you'll tell the judges everything."

"And then I'll tell the judges everything." She smiled. "I keep my promises, Jiang Chen. Even if I don't keep anything else."

They walked to the back of the warehouse. There was a trapdoor in the floor, rusted and locked. She pulled a key from her pocket—one of the many she'd collected over the years—and unlocked it.

"The morgue is below," she said. "The gate is in the basement. But there's a problem."

"What problem?"

"The judges know I'm coming. They've sent someone to stop me." She looked at him. "Someone you know."

The trapdoor swung open. Below, standing in the dim light of the basement, was a figure in a black robe. A young woman with sharp eyes and a scar across her cheek.

Lin Mei.

The soul reaper who had trained him. The one who had been assigned to mentor him after his first mission. The one who had disappeared three days ago without a word.

"Jiang Chen," she said. Her voice was cold. "Step away from the spirit."

"What are you doing here?" he asked.

"I'm here to stop her." Lin Mei raised her staff. Black light flickered at its tip. "She's dangerous. She's killed two of our kind. And she's going to kill you if you don't get out of her way."

"She's going to help me," Jiang Chen said. "She knows who framed me. She can prove my innocence."

"She's lying." Lin Mei's grip tightened on the staff. "She's been lying to you since the moment you met. Every word out of her mouth is a manipulation."

"You're one to talk." The con woman stepped forward. "You disappeared for three days. You left him alone. You let him stumble around the city without guidance. You're not here to protect him. You're here to silence him."

"What are you talking about?" Jiang Chen looked between them. "Lin Mei, what is she talking about?"

Lin Mei didn't answer. Her face was stone.

"Ask her," the con woman said. "Ask her why she really left. Ask her who she's working for."

Jiang Chen turned to Lin Mei. "Tell me."

"I can't."

"Tell me!"

"I can't." Lin Mei's voice broke. "Because if I do, they'll kill you."

The warehouse went silent. The con woman smiled.

"They already killed him once," she said. "What makes you think they won't do it again?"

Jiang Chen's blood ran cold. He looked at Lin Mei. She wouldn't meet his eyes.

"Who are you working for?" he asked.

Lin Mei said nothing.

"Who *framed* me?"

She still said nothing.

title: The Price of Names
content:
The black light from Jiang Chen's staff tore through her like a blade through smoke. Madame Yu's scream curdled into something wet and broken as her soul fragmented, pieces of her scattering across the warehouse floor like startled roaches.

Jiang Chen stumbled backward, gasping. His head throbbed where she'd been digging. She'd seen his execution. She'd seen *everything*.

"No," he whispered. "No, no, no."

He hadn't meant to do that. The staff had reacted on its own, triggered by her possession attempt. He looked down at the weapon in his trembling hands. Black smoke still coiled around its tip, hungry and patient.

*You cannot trust her*, the staff seemed to say. *She will use you. She will destroy you.*

But she had the truth. Or part of it. Or enough of it to matter.

The fragments of Madame Yu's soul began to reform, pulling together like mercury finding its center. Her shape was thinner now, more translucent. The con woman's face had aged a decade in seconds.

"You're an idiot," she rasped. "You just destroyed your only lead."

"I didn't destroy anything. You're still here."

"Barely." She tried to stand, failed, settled for sitting on the concrete floor. Her legs passed through a discarded oil drum like it wasn't there. "That thing you're holding? It's not a toy. It's a judge-killer. When soul reapers reach the Eighth Rank, they forge their staff from the bones of a dead judge. It's designed to *erase* souls, not just capture them."

Jiang Chen looked at the staff with new eyes. The wood had a faint grey hue, like bone that had been sanded smooth and polished for centuries. He'd assumed it was just wood. Now he wasn't so sure.

"I didn't know."

"Of course you didn't know. You're a baby reaper. You don't know anything." She coughed, and black sparks flew from her mouth. "The judges handed you a weapon that could kill gods and said 'go catch ghosts.' They didn't tell you what it was because they don't trust you."

"They don't trust anyone. That's the point of Diyu."

"Smart boy. You're learning." She tried to smile, but it came out as a grimace. "But here's the thing about not trusting anyone — it means you don't have allies either. When your enemies come for you, and they *will* come for you, you'll be alone. Just you and that staff that wants to eat you alive."

Jiang Chen sat down across from her. Not too close. Close enough to see the desperation in her hollow eyes.

"Tell me the names."

"I told you. Take me to Diyu—"

"You tried to possess me again. The deal's off."

"The deal was never real. You know that. I know that. Stop pretending we're playing the same game." She leaned forward, and her voice dropped to a whisper. "But I *can* give you something. A starting point. A name that will crack open your case like an egg."

"One name. And I'll take you to Diyu."

"Two names. And you let me make my case to the judges before they wipe me."

"One name. No negotiations."

She stared at him for a long moment. Then she laughed, but it was a hollow sound. "You really are a reaper. Already learning how to squeeze information out of dying souls."

"Who framed me?"

"Wu Xing." The name fell from her lips like a stone into still water. "He's a mid-level official in the Ministry of Justice. Money laundering, mostly. But he's connected to bigger fish. Much bigger."

Jiang Chen's blood went cold. Wu Xing. He knew that name. Everyone in the provincial finance department knew that name. Wu Xing was the man who signed off on audits, the man who approved or rejected investigations, the man whose signature could make or break careers.

The man Jiang Chen had embezzled from.

No. That wasn't right. He'd taken bribes to *help* people avoid investigation. He'd never stolen from the Ministry itself. But Wu Xing had been his handler, the one who'd arranged the bribes, the one who'd promised protection if things went wrong.

And when things went wrong, Wu Xing had vanished. The bribes were traced back to Jiang Chen. The evidence of murder was planted in his apartment. The judge was paid to deliver a fast sentence.

"He set me up from the beginning," Jiang Chen said. It wasn't a question.

"He set you up from the *middle*," Madame Yu corrected. "He didn't plan to frame you from the start. He planned to use you, then discard you when you became a liability. You made a mistake — you found something you weren't supposed to find."

"I found nothing. I was an accountant. I moved numbers around."

"You found a discrepancy. A large one. Millions of yuan that disappeared from the provincial budget and reappeared in a shell company owned by a man named..." She paused, searching her memory. "I don't remember. I possessed a lot of people. But I remember you. I remember the panic in your soul when you realized what you'd found. You didn't report it. You tried to hide it. And that made you complicit."

"I was already complicit. I'd taken bribes."

"Taking bribes is a crime. Finding evidence of a conspiracy to defraud the government of millions is a death sentence." She shook her head. "You were already dead the moment you found that number. Wu Xing just made sure it happened fast."

Jiang Chen closed his eyes. The warehouse was cold. The city outside was silent. Somewhere, a dog barked, and the sound echoed through the empty streets like a warning.

"Who's above Wu Xing?"
