"I don't know. But I can find out. Take me to Diyu. Let me access the records. I can track the money, follow the trail, find the people at the top."

"You're asking me to trust you."

"I'm asking you to use me." She spread her arms wide. "I'm a tool. I've been a tool my whole life. Con woman, thief, parasite. But I'm a *good* tool. I can find things no one else can find. I can see connections no one else can see. And right now, I'm the only person who wants to help you."

"Why would you help me? You tried to kill me."

"Because the alternative is getting my soul wiped and spending eternity as a vegetable in Diyu's detention center. I'd rather help you and maybe, *maybe* earn a second chance." Her eyes met his. "I'm not asking for freedom. I'm asking for a chance to be useful."

Jiang Chen stood up. His legs were shaking. His hands were shaking. Everything was shaking.

"One week."

"One week what?"

"One week. I'll keep you in Diyu. I'll let you access the records. But if you try to escape, if you try to possess anyone, if you do *anything* suspicious, I'll use this staff to erase you completely. No trial. No judgment. Just nothing."

Madame Yu smiled. It was a real smile this time, thin and predatory and full of teeth.

"Deal."

---

The journey back to Diyu took three hours. Jiang Chen had to find a quiet alley, draw the summoning circle on the wall, and chant the words that opened the veil between worlds. The passage felt different this time — heavier, like the underworld was pressing down on him from all sides.

When he emerged, he was standing in the Hall of the First Judge.

The hall was empty. No clerks. No guards. No Judge Zhao waiting to scold him for his incompetence.

That was wrong. The Hall of the First Judge was never empty.

"Hello?" Jiang Chen's voice echoed through the vast chamber. The pillars stretched up into darkness. The braziers had gone cold.

Madame Yu materialized beside him, her soul flickering like a dying candle. "This place is quiet. Too quiet."

"I noticed."

"Something's wrong. The judges never leave their halls unattended. Something happened while you were gone."

Jiang Chen started walking toward the main dais. The First Judge's desk was cluttered with scrolls and documents, as if he'd left in a hurry. A cup of tea sat on the corner, still warm.

"He was here recently. Maybe an hour ago."

"Then where is he now?"

A sound came from the corridor behind the dais. A scraping sound, like something heavy being dragged across stone.

Jiang Chen gripped his staff and moved toward the sound. Madame Yu followed, her form flickering with unease.

The corridor led to the judgment chambers, where souls were processed and sentenced. The doors were open. The torches inside had gone out.

The scraping sound grew louder.

Jiang Chen stepped through the doorway.

The chamber was dark, but he could see well enough. The judgment altar was overturned. The soul mirrors were shattered. And in the center of the room, lying in a pool of black blood, was the body of a man.

No. Not a body. A corpse.

Judge Zhao's corpse.

His throat had been cut from ear to ear. His eyes were open, staring at nothing. His hands were still reaching for something, frozen in the moment of death.

Jiang Chen's staff clattered to the floor.

"Impossible," Madame Yu whispered. "Judges can't be killed in Diyu. The underworld protects them. It's supposed to be impossible."

"Then why is he dead?"

"Because someone found a way." Her voice was trembling. "Someone found a way to kill a judge in his own hall."

Jiang Chen knelt beside the body. He reached out, touched Judge Zhao's cold hand.

He'd hated this man. Hated him for giving him this job, for forcing him to hunt souls, for treating him like a disposable tool.

But Judge Zhao had also been the only person in Diyu who'd treated him like a person. The only one who'd explained things. The only one who'd seemed to care.

And now he was dead.

A scrap of paper was clutched in Judge Zhao's other hand. Jiang Chen pried it loose. It was a note, written in blood.

*The living have found a way in. The dead are not safe. Find the girl. She knows why.*

Jiang Chen read the note three times. Then he read it again.

"Find the girl," he said. "What girl?"

Madame Yu was silent. When he looked at her, her face was pale.

"What girl?"

"I don't know," she said. But she was lying. He could see it in her eyes.

"Tell me."

"I don't *know*. But I have a guess." She looked at the body, then at the note, then back at Jiang Chen. "There's a legend in Diyu. A story the old souls tell each other when the judges aren't listening. About a girl who was executed three hundred years ago. A girl who was innocent."

"Innocent?"

"Completely innocent. No crimes. No sins. Just a girl who was in the wrong place at the wrong time, who got caught up in a conspiracy, who was killed to silence her."

Jiang Chen's blood ran cold. "Like me."

"Exactly like you. But she was special. She didn't become a soul reaper. She became something else. Something the judges couldn't control." Madame Yu's voice dropped to a whisper. "They say she escaped Diyu. They say she's still out there, in the mortal world, hunting the people who killed her."

"And you think she's connected to this?"

"I think she might be

title: The Warden's Whisper
content:
The black light from Jiang Chen's staff consumed the alley. Madame Bai's scream cut through the darkness like broken glass, then dissolved into static. He felt her claws retract from his mind, leaving behind the ache of violated memories.

When the light faded, she was on her knees. Her stolen body trembled, eyes wide and unfocused. The red dress hung loose on her frame, sweat plastering hair to her forehead.

"What did you do to me?" she whispered.

"Pushed you out." Jiang Chen's voice came out rougher than he intended. His hands still shook. "You were inside my head. You don't get to stay."

She laughed, but it was hollow. "You don't understand what you've done."

"Enlighten me."

"You blasted me with Soul Reaper energy. Every reaper in the city just felt that. Every reaper in the province." She looked up at him, and for the first time, he saw genuine fear in her eyes. "They're coming."

Jiang Chen's grip tightened on his staff. "Good. They can help me drag you back to Diyu."

"You're not listening." She stood slowly, brushing dirt from her dress. "The judges didn't send one reaper after me. They sent two. I killed both. Now a third appears, carrying enough power to light up the underworld like a flare. Do you think they'll congratulate you? Or do you think they'll wonder why a probationary Soul Reaper has that much juice?"

The words hit harder than they should have.

He'd wondered about the staff. About the power that flowed through it. Judge Zhao had given it to him with a casual wave, like it was standard equipment. But the way Madame Bai looked at it now — eyes tracking its every movement — told him otherwise.

"That's not a standard issue staff," she said, reading his silence. "I've been possessing people for seventeen years. I've seen reapers come and go. Their weapons hum. Yours *screams*."

"You're stalling."

"I'm warning you." She stepped closer. The alley was dark, but he could see the calculation behind her eyes. "When the other reapers get here, they'll take me back. They'll wipe my memory. You'll never know who framed you. But that's not what scares you, is it?"

"Nothing scares me."

"Liar." She smiled. "What scares you is that I might be right about the staff. That Judge Zhao gave you something special, and you don't know why. That you're a pawn in a game you don't understand."

He wanted to deny it. Wanted to shove the staff down her throat and drag her back to Diyu by her hair. But she'd already seen inside him. Already mapped his doubts.

"I'll make you a new deal," she said. "One you can't refuse."

"I'm listening."

"Don't take me to Diyu. Not yet. Take me to someone who can help us both."

"Who?"

"A ghost. A dead man who knows more about Diyu politics than any living judge." She paused. "He's the one who told me about your death."

Jiang Chen's blood went cold. "You've been planning this."

"I've been *surviving*. There's a difference." She smoothed her dress. "I knew a Soul Reaper would come eventually. I just didn't expect one with a death sentence written by the government. Figured I'd use that to bargain. But now..." She looked at his staff again. "Now I think we can do more than bargain. We can burn the whole system down."

"You're insane."

"Maybe. But I'm also the only person who can give you the names of everyone who signed your death warrant."

The air shifted. A cold wind swept through the alley, carrying the smell of wet earth and incense. Madame Bai's head snapped up.

"They're here."

Jiang Chen felt them before he saw them. Three presences, cold as winter graves, descending from the rooftops. They landed in perfect synchronization, robes billowing, masks covering their faces.

Three Soul Reapers. Official ones. Their staffs hummed with the same energy as his, but quieter. Controlled.

The lead reaper stepped forward. His mask was white, painted with a single black eye. "Probationary Reaper Jiang Chen." His voice was flat. Mechanical. "You are out of your jurisdiction."

"I'm pursuing a target."

"The target is secured." The reaper gestured, and the other two moved to flank Madame Bai. "We'll take it from here."

Jiang Chen stepped between them. "She's mine. Judge Zhao assigned me."

"Judge Zhao assigned you to *learn*. Not to hunt." The reaper's hand tightened on his staff. "You've made enough noise for one night. Step aside."

Madame Bai watched the exchange with hungry eyes. She was reading the room, reading the power dynamics, looking for an opening. Jiang Chen could feel her calculating behind that calm mask.

"She has information," he said. "About a corruption case. High-level."

"All dead people have information." The reaper took another step forward. "That's why we process them. Extract what's useful. Wipe the rest."

"She knows about my death."

Silence.

The lead reaper's head tilted slightly. "Your death was standard procedure."

"It wasn't. She proved it. She saw it when she tried to possess me."

"Ghosts lie."

"She had access to evidence. Dates. Names. Documents."

"I said, ghosts lie." The reaper's voice dropped. "And you're a probationary reaper with three weeks of experience. You don't know what's real and what's planted. You don't know anything."

He snapped his fingers.

The two flanking reapers lunged. One grabbed Madame Bai by the arm. The other raised his staff, black light gathering at the tip.

Jiang Chen moved before he thought.

His staff came up, blocking the strike. Energy exploded between them, sending cracks racing across the alley walls. The reaper stumbled back, eyes wide behind his mask.

"She's not going with you," Jiang Chen said.

The lead reaper laughed. It was a dry, rustling sound. "You'd fight your own kind? For a ghost?"

"I'd fight anyone who gets between me and the truth."

"Brave words for a dead man."

"I'm already dead." Jiang Chen adjusted his grip. "What more can you take from me?"

The reaper studied him for a long moment. Then he reached up and removed his mask.

The face underneath was older. Wrinkled. Scarred. But the eyes were sharp, and they held something Jiang Chen didn't expect.

Recognition.

"You really don't know, do you?" the reaper said softly.

"Know what?"

"About the staff. About why Judge Zhao gave it to you. About why you're still a probationary reaper when you should have been promoted weeks ago."

Jiang Chen's mouth went dry. "Enlighten me."

"Your death wasn't just a setup. It was a *request*." The reaper stepped closer, voice dropping to a whisper. "Someone in the living world paid the underworld to take you out. Not just kill you. *Collect you*. Make sure you ended up in Diyu, where they could keep an eye on you."

"Why?"

"Because you saw something you shouldn't have. At your old job. In the accounting department." The reaper's eyes bored into him. "You processed a payment. A big one. From a company you didn't recognize to a government account that shouldn't exist. You flagged it. Your boss told you to forget it. You didn't."

Jiang Chen's stomach dropped.

He remembered. A wire transfer. Seven figures. The account number had been too clean, too organized. He'd mentioned it to a coworker, joked about it over lunch.

The next week, he was arrested for murder.

"You're telling me this now?" His voice cracked. "After I've been dead for three weeks?"

"I'm telling you because Madame Bai is right." The reaper glanced at her. She stood perfectly still, watching the exchange with predatory patience. "There are people in Diyu who want to bury this. People who were paid to make sure you never found out. But I wasn't one of them."

"Then help me."

"I can't." The reaper put his mask back on. "I have orders. If I don't bring her in, they'll send someone worse. Someone who won't give you a choice."

"Let me talk to her. Give me one hour."

"Can't do that either." He raised his staff. "But I can give you thirty seconds to run."

"What?"

"Thirty seconds. Then I'll hunt you both. I'll pretend I never found you. I'll tell my superiors you escaped." He lowered his voice. "Thirty seconds, Jiang Chen. Use them wisely."

Jiang Chen didn't hesitate.

He grabbed Madame Bai's arm and ran.

They burst out of the alley into a crowded street. Living people walked past, oblivious. The night market was in full swing, vendors hawking skewers and steamed buns, children laughing, couples holding hands.

A world that didn't know the dead walked among them.

"This way." Madame Bai pulled him left, through a narrow gap between two stalls. They emerged into a residential area, gray buildings stacked against each other like dominoes.

"Where are we going?"

"To see my contact. The ghost I told you about."

"Can we trust him?"

"He's dead. What's he going to do, kill us?"

Fair point.

They ran through back alleys, over walls, through courtyards. Jiang Chen's lungs burned. He forgot dead people could still get tired.

Behind them, he heard footsteps. Cold. Measured.

The reaper was giving them exactly thirty seconds. And he was counting.

They rounded a corner and stopped.

Dead end.

A wall, fifteen feet high, covered in rusted pipes and broken glass. Madame Bai cursed.

"Climb," Jiang Chen said.

"I'm wearing heels."

"Should have thought of that before you stole a body."

She kicked off her shoes and grabbed a pipe. Her soul flickered, making her hands translucent, but she pulled herself up with surprising strength.

Jiang Chen followed. The glass cut his palms. Black blood dripped.

They reached the top and dropped into another alley. This one was narrower, darker, the walls covered in faded posters and graffiti.

"Here." Madame Bai pressed her hand against a brick that looked like all the others. It sank inward. A section of the wall swung open, revealing a staircase leading down.

"After you," she said.

He went first.

The stairs descended into darkness. The air grew cold. Damp. The smell of wet stone and something older filled his nose.

At the bottom, a door. Wooden. Ancient. Carved with symbols he didn't recognize.

Madame Bai pushed past him and knocked. Three quick beats. Two slow. One quick.

A voice came from the other side. Rasping. Old.

"Password."

"The warden's whisper."

Silence. Then the door creaked open.

Inside, a single candle burned on a wooden desk. Behind it sat a man. Or what used to be a man. His body was translucent, barely visible, held together by chains of black smoke that wrapped around his limbs like ropes.

He looked up, and Jiang Chen saw that his eyes were gone. Empty sockets stared at nothing.

"You brought company," the ghost said.

"He's the one." Madame Bai stepped forward. "The one I told you about."

"Ah." The ghost smiled. It was not a pleasant smile. "The accountant who died for asking questions."

"You know about me?"

"I know about

title: Chapter 11
content:
CHAPTER 11: The Name That Burns

The black light from my staff didn't just push her out of my head—it *burned* her out.

Madam Zhao's soul shrieked as the light seared through her ethereal form, leaving trails of smoke where my power touched her. She released my mind like a scalded animal, stumbling backward across the rooftop, her stolen body smoking at the edges.

"You—" She clutched her chest, gasping. "That's not supposed to hurt me. I'm a soul. You're supposed to *collect* me."

I didn't answer. I was too busy noticing that my hands were still shaking, that my heart was still pounding, that the black light hadn't faded from my staff yet. It crackled along the bone like living lightning, hungry for more.

*The staff responds to your intent,* Old Zhang's voice echoed in my memory. *It knows what you want before you do.*

What did I want right now?

I wanted the truth. And she was dangling it in front of me like a fishing hook.

"You didn't answer," I said, my voice steadier than I felt. "Who did I offend?"

She laughed through her pain, a wet, broken sound. "You think I'll tell you now? After you just *burned* me?"

"I think you'll tell me because you're out of options." I stepped closer. The staff's light pulsed with each step. "You killed two Soul Reapers. Diyu has a special punishment for that—I've seen the records. They put you in the Boiling Cauldron for a century, then scrape what's left into the Mountain of Knives. And that's just the first three decades."

Her face went pale. Good. She knew what I was talking about.

"But if you cooperate," I continued, "if you give me real names, real evidence—I'll speak for you. I'll tell the judges you helped me. They might reduce your sentence."

"I'd be a fool to trust a dead man's promise."

"You'd be a fool to trust a *living* man's promise. But I'm already dead." I held out my hand. "What do I have to lose by lying to you?"

She stared at my hand for a long moment. The wind whipped her stolen hair across her face. In the distance, a police siren wailed, then faded—someone else's problem, not ours.

"The bribe came through a shell company," she finally said. "Shangri-La Investments. They handle money for people who don't want their hands dirty. But the order—the original order to have you convicted—came from someone in the government."

"Which department?"

"I don't know the department. I know the name."

She paused. Her eyes met mine. There was something almost like pity in them.

"Commissioner Li Wei of the State Security Bureau."

The name hit me like a physical blow.

Commissioner Li Wei.

I'd never met him. Never spoken to him. Never even *seen* his face in a newspaper. But I knew the name—everyone in my old office knew the name. He was the man who'd seized our company's assets during the anti-corruption sweep. The man who'd personally signed the warrant that froze our accounts.

The man who'd put me out of a job six months before I was arrested.

"If he wanted you dead that badly," Madam Zhao said softly, "you must have seen something. Something he couldn't afford to let you walk away with."

"I didn't see anything." But even as I said it, I knew it wasn't true. I'd seen everything. The ledgers. The offshore accounts. The payments to government officials disguised as consulting fees. I'd seen it all, and I'd done nothing because I was too scared, too comfortable, too *alive* to risk it.

And then Li Wei had swept in, frozen everything, and I'd thought I was safe because I'd been a small fish, a nobody, a man who just followed orders.

But Li Wei hadn't seen a small fish. He'd seen a witness.

"You remember now," Madam Zhao said. It wasn't a question.

"I remember." My voice was hollow. "He was cleaning house. Everyone who'd touched those ledgers—"

"Died. Or disappeared. Or ended up on death row for crimes they didn't commit." She shook her head. "You weren't the first. You won't be the last."

The staff's light flickered. My grip on it loosened.

*She's manipulating you,* a voice in my head whispered. *This is what she does. She finds your wounds and pours salt into them.*

But knowing it didn't make it hurt less.

"I need more than a name," I said. "I need proof."

"I told you. I've been possessing people for seventeen years. I've seen everything. But that kind of knowledge—I don't *remember* it like a book. It's more like... echoes. Impressions. I'd need to be near someone who was involved to pull the details out."

"Then we find someone who was involved."

She smiled. It was a dangerous smile. "That's going to be a problem. Because Commissioner Li Wei is dead."

---

The words hung in the air between us.

"Dead?" I repeated. "When?"

"Three weeks ago. Same week you were executed, actually. Interesting coincidence, isn't it?"

Three weeks. The same week I'd died. The same week my case had been rushed through the courts, my appeals denied, my execution date set with suspicious speed.

"Someone cleaned up the loose ends," I said slowly.

"Someone cleaned up *the evidence.*" She crossed her arms. "Li Wei was the one who ordered your conviction. He was the one who knew why you needed to die. And now he's gone—heart attack, official records say. Very convenient."

"Then the trail is cold."

"No." She shook her head. "The trail is *hotter* than ever. Because if someone killed Li Wei to keep him quiet, that means there's someone else above him. Someone who's still alive. Someone who's still pulling strings."

"Who?"

"I don't know. But I know someone who might."

---

The apartment was on the third floor of a crumbling building in the old district, where the elevators hadn't worked since the 90s and the hallway lights flickered like dying fireflies. The nameplate on the door read "Chen — Accounting Services," handwritten on yellowing tape.

Madam Zhao knocked twice, paused, knocked three times.

The door cracked open. A single bloodshot eye peered out.

"Who sent you?"

"The debt collector sends his regards," Madam Zhao said.

The door opened wider. The man inside was in his fifties, thin to the point of emaciation, with the hollow cheeks and yellowed skin of someone who'd spent too many years in windowless rooms. He wore a stained undershirt and slippers. His apartment smelled like stale cigarettes and regret.

"He's dead," the man said flatly. "Li Wei's dead. Whatever debts he owed, they're canceled."

"I'm not here about Li Wei's debts." Madam Zhao stepped inside without waiting for an invitation. I followed. "I'm here about *his* debts." She pointed at me.

The man looked at me for the first time. His eyes narrowed.

"You're the accountant. The one who got the needle."

"Word travels fast," I said.

"Word travels *cheaply* when you know the right people." He shuffled to a desk buried under stacks of paper. "You're dead, boy. You shouldn't be standing in my apartment."

"I'm not alive. But I'm not dead either. It's complicated."

"It always is." He pulled out a ledger, thicker than my arm, and dropped it on the desk with a heavy *thud*. "Li Wei came to me six months before the sweep. Wanted me to cook the books for a transfer. One point five billion yuan, moving through seventeen shell companies, ending up in accounts spread across Macau, Singapore, and the Cayman Islands."

"Whose accounts?"

"Wouldn't tell me. But I'm an accountant. I don't need to be told—I can *read.*" He flipped open the ledger to a page marked with a red tab. "The final destination was a numbered account. Swiss. Untouchable. But the account holder left a signature on the paperwork."

He slid a photocopy across the desk.

My blood went cold.

It wasn't a name. It was a symbol. A black lotus, its petals sharp as knives, its center a single staring eye.

"I've seen this before," I whispered.

"Everyone who works in finance in this city has seen it before." The man's voice was bitter. "It's the mark of the Eternal Lotus Society. Triad. But not just any triad—the one that runs the *government*. The one that decides who lives and who dies."

"The one that killed me."

"The one that killed *a lot* of people." He closed the ledger. "You want to go after them? Fine. But you'll die again. And this time, they won't leave enough of you to be a ghost."

Madam Zhao picked up the photocopy. Her fingers traced the symbol.

"Do you know what happens to people who cross the Eternal Lotus?" she asked quietly.

"I'm about to find out."

"No." She looked up at me, and for the first time, there was something real in her eyes. Not manipulation. Not calculation. *Fear.* "I've possessed members of the Eternal Lotus before. I've seen what's inside their minds. They're not just criminals. They're not just a syndicate."

"Then what are they?"

She opened her mouth to answer—

And the window shattered.

A blade of black energy sliced through the air, missing my throat by inches and burying itself in the wall behind me. The old accountant screamed. Madam Zhao dove for cover.

I spun, staff raised—

And saw a woman standing on the fire escape outside the broken window.

She wore a mask of white porcelain, painted with the face of a smiling fox. Her robes were black silk, embroidered with golden lotuses. In her hand, she held a second blade of black energy, already forming.

"Jiang Chen," she said. Her voice was soft, feminine, almost pleasant. "The Judge of the Dead sends his regards."

"The Judge of the Dead—" I started.

"He's been looking for you." She stepped through the window, her bare feet touching the floor without a sound. "You've been causing quite a stir in Diyu. Escaped souls. Rogue Soul Reapers. And now—" Her eyes, visible through the mask's slits, flicked to the photocopy in Madam Zhao's hand. "—asking questions you shouldn't be asking."

"I was framed for murder. I think I have a right to ask questions."

"Framed?" She tilted her head. "No, Jiang Chen. You weren't framed."

"What?"

"You were *chosen.*"

The black blade flew.

I blocked it with my staff, but the impact sent me crashing through the apartment wall, into a hallway of screaming neighbors and flashing lights. Before I could get up, she was there, standing over me, her mask smiling down.

"The Eternal Lotus doesn't kill random people," she said. "We don't frame innocent men. We *recruit* them. Your execution—your death sentence—it was a test. A trial by fire. If you survived it, if you became a Soul Reaper, you proved yourself worthy."

"Worthy of what?"

She reached up and removed her mask.

Her face was young. Beautiful. Familiar.

She was the woman who'd testified against me at my trial. The "victim" who'd sworn

title: The Warden's Secret
content:
The black light consumed everything.

Jiang Chen felt the recoil through his arms, through his chest, through the hollow space where his heart should have been. The staff pulsed once, twice, three times, each pulse sending a wave of force that pushed the con woman's soul back into her stolen body.

She collapsed.

Her eyes snapped open immediately. "What did you—"

"I stopped you from possessing me." He kept the staff pointed at her throat. "Again."

"You don't understand." She scrambled backward until her spine hit the wall. "That was your only chance. The judges won't let me speak. They'll throw me into the eighteenth level before I can say a word."

"Then I'll speak for you."

"You don't know what to say!"

"Then tell me now."

She opened her mouth. Closed it. Her eyes darted to the side, calculating, searching for angles. He'd seen that look a hundred times in the prison yard. It was the look of a woman building a lie.

"Don't," he said. "I'll know."

"Will you?" She smiled. "You're a dead man pretending to be alive. You can't even feel your own heartbeat. What makes you think you can feel a lie?"

He pressed the staff harder against her throat. "Try me."

She held his gaze for three full seconds. Then she laughed. It was a bitter, broken sound that echoed through the empty hallway.

"Fine. I'll tell you one name. Just one. Li Wei."

The name hit him like a physical blow.

Li Wei. His former supervisor. The man who'd taught him how to cook the books, how to make the numbers disappear, how to smile at auditors while hiding millions in embezzled funds. The man who'd testified against him at trial.

"Your boss," she continued. "He didn't just testify. He paid the judge. He paid the prosecutor. He paid the foreman of the jury."

"How could you possibly know that?"

"I was possessing the judge's wife at the time."

Jiang Chen's hand trembled. The staff wavered. She noticed.

"She was a good woman, the judge's wife. Brought him lunch every day. Sat in on his hearings. She knew everything." The con woman's voice dropped. "Including the envelope Li Wei slipped her husband under the dinner table."

"You're lying."

"I wish I was. If I was lying, maybe I'd get a lighter sentence. But I'm not. And you know I'm not."

She was right. He knew. He'd known from the moment she'd said Li Wei's name. The pieces had always been there—the way Li Wei had avoided eye contact during testimony, the way the judge had barely considered the defense, the way the sentence had come down faster than any capital case in the province's history.

"Why?" Jiang Chen's voice cracked. "Why would he—"

"Because you knew too much. Because you were going to confess. Because the company was going to collapse, and Li Wei needed a scapegoat who couldn't talk back." She stood up slowly, brushing dust off her stolen clothes. "You were the fall guy, Jiang Chen. You were always the fall guy."

He wanted to hit her. He wanted to throw her against the wall and demand more names, more proof, more anything. But his body wouldn't move. His arms hung limp at his sides. The staff clattered to the floor.

"Now you understand." She picked up the staff. "Now you see why I can't go back to Diyu. The judges don't care about justice. They care about order. They care about quotas. They care about making sure every soul gets processed on time." She held out the staff. "I can help you. I can find the proof you need. But I need time. I need freedom."

"You want me to let you go."

"I want you to give me a chance to make things right."

Jiang Chen stared at her. At the staff in her hand. At the woman who'd killed two soul reapers and was now offering him the truth about his own death.

"The other reapers," he said. "Why did you kill them?"

She flinched. "They were going to erase me."

"That's not an answer."

"It's the only one you're going to get."

He reached for the staff. She handed it back without resistance. Their fingers brushed. For a moment, he felt her soul—felt the weight of seventeen years of running, of hiding, of wearing other people's faces like masks. She was tired. She was scared. She was desperate.

Just like him.

"One hour," he said. "I'll give you one hour to find proof. Then I'm taking you back."

"One hour in the mortal world. You know how long that is in Diyu?"

"Long enough for the judges to notice I'm gone."

She nodded. "Deal. But I need to make a call."

She pulled out a phone—the phone of the woman whose body she'd stolen—and dialed a number from memory. It rang three times before someone picked up.

"Is it done?" A man's voice. Familiar.

"Almost. I need your help."

"Who is this?"

"Your worst nightmare, Li Wei."

Jiang Chen's blood went cold.

The con woman smiled. "I've been keeping tabs on your little operation for years. I have files. I have recordings. I have everything. And I've just made a new friend who's very interested in what you did to him."

She held out the phone.

Jiang Chen took it. His hand was steady now.

"Hello, Li Wei."

Silence. Then: "Who is this?"

"You know who I am."

Another silence. Longer this time. When Li Wei spoke again, his voice was different. Harder. The voice of a man who'd just realized he was trapped.

"Jiang Chen. You're dead."

"So are you. You just don't know it yet."

The line went dead.

Jiang Chen looked at the phone. At the con woman. At the staff in his hands. The weight of it had changed. It wasn't just a tool for hunting souls anymore. It was a key. A key to a door he'd thought was sealed forever.

"One hour," he said. "Show me where the files are."

She led him through the apartment building's back stairwell, through an alley littered with trash, through a subway station where the living rushed past without seeing them. They were ghosts in a world of flesh, moving between the cracks of reality.

The safehouse was a storage unit on the edge of the city. She opened it with a key she'd hidden in her shoe. Inside, stacked floor to ceiling, were boxes. Dozens of them. Hundreds.

"Every person I've possessed," she said. "I documented everything. Their secrets. Their crimes. Their connections." She pulled a box from the bottom of a stack. "This one is yours."

Jiang Chen opened it.

Inside were files. Photographs. Bank statements. Recorded conversations. Everything Li Wei had done to frame him, everything the judge had accepted in payment, everything the prosecutors had ignored. It was all there. Every dirty detail. Every crooked deal.

"Why?" Jiang Chen whispered. "Why did you keep this?"

"Because I knew I'd need it someday." She leaned against the wall. "I've been running for seventeen years. I've killed people. I've ruined lives. I've done terrible things to survive. But I always told myself that if I could expose one real crime, one true injustice, it would balance the scales."

"Does it?"

She looked at him. For the first time, there was no calculation in her eyes. No games. Just exhaustion.

"No," she said. "But it's all I have."

Jiang Chen closed the box. He looked at the stacks of evidence around him—the lives this woman had stolen, the secrets she'd hoarded, the truth she'd buried under layers of lies.

"You're still going back to Diyu," he said.

"I know."

"But I'll testify for you. I'll tell the judges what you did for me."

She laughed. "You think that'll matter?"

"It's all I have."

For a moment, she looked almost grateful. Then the alarm on his phone went off. One hour was up.

"Time to go," he said.

She nodded. She didn't fight as he grabbed her arm and opened the passage back to Diyu. She didn't struggle as the red sky swallowed them. She didn't scream as the chains of the underworld wrapped around her wrists.

But just before the judges' hall appeared on the horizon, she whispered: "Li Wei isn't the only one."

Jiang Chen stopped. "What?"

"Your case. The judge. The prosecutor. They're all connected. They're all part of something bigger." Her eyes met his. "I've been running from something too, Jiang Chen. And it's not Diyu."

"Then what is it?"

She opened her mouth to answer.

The gates of the judges' hall swung open.

And a voice thundered across the plains:

"BRING FORTH THE SOUL OF CHEN JIANG. HIS SENTENCE HAS BEEN REVOKED."

Jiang Chen's blood froze.

Revoked.

That was impossible.

He was dead.

He was a soul reaper.

He was—

"By order of the Jade Emperor himself," the voice continued, "the execution of Chen Jiang is declared void. His soul is to be returned to his body immediately."

The con woman stared at him. Her chains rattled as she stepped back.

"They're sending you back," she breathed. "They're making you alive again."

Jiang Chen looked down at his hands.

They were starting to fade.

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER 13: The Warden's Secret

The scream echoed through the warehouse, bouncing off concrete walls and rusted metal beams. Jiang Chen held the staff firm, black light pulsing between them. The Maestra's grip on his mind loosened, then released entirely.

She stumbled backward, gasping. Her stolen body—the middle-aged woman with the accountant's eyes—trembled as she clutched her chest where the staff had struck.

"You—" She coughed. "You could have killed me."

"You tried to possess me again."

"I was testing you." She straightened, adjusting her blouse with practiced dignity. "You passed. You're not as stupid as you look."

Jiang Chen kept the staff pointed at her. The black light had faded, but he could summon it again in an instant. The weight of it in his hands felt more natural now, like an extension of his arm rather than a foreign object.

"You have five seconds to tell me something useful before I drag you back to Diyu and let the judges sort it out."

"Four seconds," she corrected. "You said five. That means you're giving me time. Which means you're curious." She smiled. "Good. Curiosity keeps people alive."

"Three seconds."

"The warden."

Jiang Chen stopped counting. "What?"

"The prison where you were executed. Beishan Prison. Warden Zhao Guangming." She spoke the name like she'd been holding it in her mouth, savoring it. "He's the one who arranged your death."

"You're lying."

"I told you—I'm a con woman. I lie for a living. But I have no reason to lie about this." She stepped closer, careful to stay out of staff range. "I've possessed thirteen people in Beishan Prison over the years. Guards, administrators, even a few inmates. I know everyone who works there. And I know exactly what happened to you."

Jiang Chen's grip tightened on the staff. "How?"

"Because I was possessing one of the guards who processed your intake. He was there when the warden pulled your file. Heard the phone call."

"What phone call?"

"The one where Warden Zhao took orders from someone higher up. Someone who wanted you dead before your trial even ended." She tilted her head, studying his face. "You didn't know. Of course you didn't. You just thought you were unlucky. A bad judge, a corrupt system. But it was personal, Jiang Chen. Someone specifically wanted you dead."

His chest felt hollow. The execution had been three weeks ago. He'd spent his last days in that cold cell, writing letters he never sent, making peace with a life he'd wasted. He'd accepted it. Embraced it, even. Death was the price he paid for his mistakes.

But this woman was telling him it wasn't justice. It wasn't even random cruelty. It was targeted.

"Why?" His voice came out rough. "I was nobody. A small-time accountant who took bribes. I didn't have enemies."

"You had one." The Maestra's eyes flickered with something that might have been sympathy. "The warden didn't know why. He just followed orders. But the person who gave those orders—they knew you. They wanted you gone. Not arrested. Not convicted. Gone."

A name floated up from his memory. A face. A voice he hadn't thought about in years.

No. That was impossible.

"You know who it is." The Maestra saw it in his eyes. "Tell me."

"I don't know anything."

"You're lying. I can see it. When you possess someone long enough, you learn to read micro-expressions. The way your jaw tightened. The slight dilation of your pupils. You know exactly who did this."

Jiang Chen looked away. "Even if I did, what could I do about it? I'm dead. I'm a soul reaper now. I don't have a body, I don't have a life. I can't exactly file a police report."

"No, but you can investigate." The Maestra's voice dropped, becoming conspiratorial. "You have access to Diyu. You have the staff. You can travel between worlds. And you're already dead, which means you can't be killed again."

"That's not true. Soul reapers can be destroyed."

"By other soul reapers. By ancient spirits. Not by mortal men with guns." She stepped even closer, close enough that he could smell her perfume—lavender, cheap, the kind sold at roadside stalls. "Warden Zhao is still alive. Still running Beishan Prison. Still taking orders from whoever killed you. And he has a ledger."

"A ledger?"

"Every bribe, every favor, every backroom deal. He writes them all down. Keeps them in a safe in his office. Insurance, in case his masters ever decide to clean house." She smiled. "I know because I possessed his secretary last year. She saw him open it once. Three names. Phone numbers. Payment records."

"Three names?"

"That's all it takes. One to order the hit. One to arrange it. One to carry it out." She held up three fingers. "Three names, and you'll know everything."

Jiang Chen stared at her. The offer was too perfect. Too convenient. A con woman giving him exactly what he wanted, exactly when he needed it.

"What's the catch?"

"No catch."

"Bullshit. You're a criminal. You don't do favors."

"I want something in return." She lowered her hand. "When you get the ledger, there's a name in it. A woman. She's the one who ruined my life. I want her address."

"You want revenge."

"I want answers." Her voice hardened. "She was my partner. We ran the biggest possession scheme in three provinces. Made millions. Then she sold me out to the soul reapers, took the money, and disappeared. I've been trying to find her for ten years."

"And you think she's in the ledger?"

"She's in everything. That woman touches everyone she meets. If Warden Zhao's been taking bribes from someone powerful, she's connected to it somehow." The Maestra's eyes burned with old hatred. "Help me find her, and I'll help you find your killer."

Jiang Chen was silent for a long moment. The warehouse hummed with the sound of distant traffic, the buzz of a dying fluorescent light. Somewhere outside, the living world went on as if nothing had changed.

But everything had changed.

"One condition," he said.

"Name it."

"You stop possessing people. No more stolen bodies. No more con games. You work with me legitimately, or I drag you to Diyu and let the judges decide your fate."

The Maestra laughed. "You want me to go straight? I've been doing this since I was nineteen. I don't know how to be honest."

"Learn."

She studied him, her smile fading. "You're serious."

"I'm a soul reaper. It's my job to bring in rogue souls. I'm giving you a chance to cooperate. That's more than the judges would give you."

"And if I refuse?"

"Then I'd have to kill you." He said it flatly, without emotion. "I don't want to. But I will."

She held his gaze for ten seconds, twenty. Then she sighed, a long theatrical exhale that seemed to deflate her entire body.

"Fine. No more possessions. But I'm going to need a body eventually. I can't stay in this one forever—she's starting to rot."

"You're possessing a dead woman?"

"The body was dead when I found it. Car accident on the highway. I slipped in before the paramedics arrived." She shrugged. "It's not ideal, but it works. I'll need a fresh one in a month or so."

"No."

"Then find me a way back to Diyu that doesn't involve me being imprisoned for eternity. Give me a job. Make me useful."

Jiang Chen frowned. "Soul reapers can't hire rogue souls."

"Change the rules. You're new. You have leverage." She stepped back, spreading her arms. "I've been possessing people for seventeen years. I know the criminal underworld in three provinces. I know every con, every scheme, every backroom deal. I could be your informant. Your spy. Your eyes and ears in places soul reapers can't go."

"You're asking me to trust you."

"I'm asking you to use me." She smiled, and it was the first genuine expression he'd seen on her face. "Trust is for fools. But usefulness? That's a language everyone understands."

Jiang Chen considered the offer. It was insane. Taking a rogue soul as an informant violated every rule in Diyu's handbook. If the judges found out, he'd be stripped of his position. Maybe worse.

But the Maestra was right about one thing—he was new. He had no reputation to protect, no career to lose. And he had a murder to solve.

"One month," he said. "You help me get the ledger. You behave yourself. No possessions, no cons, no crimes. If you slip up once, I drag you to Diyu personally."

"And after the month?"

"Depends on what we find."

The Maestra extended her hand. "Deal."

Jiang Chen hesitated, then shook it. Her grip was firm, her skin cold. The hand of a dead woman animated by a dead soul.

"Where do we start?" she asked.

"Beishan Prison. Warden Zhao's office." He pulled out his phone, checking the time. "It's midnight. The night shift will be understaffed. Can you get us in?"

"I can get us anywhere." She grinned. "I'm a con woman. Getting through doors is what I do."

---

Beishan Prison sat on the outskirts of the city, a gray concrete fortress surrounded by razor wire and guard towers. Jiang Chen had been processed here three weeks ago, stripped of his belongings, issued an orange jumpsuit, and led to a cell that smelled of urine and despair.

Now he stood outside the main gate, invisible to mortal eyes, the staff humming in his hand.

The Maestra stood beside him, still in her stolen body. She'd found a guard uniform in a locker room and changed into it, complete with a hat that shadowed her face.

"Follow my lead," she said. "And don't use the staff unless I tell you to."

"You're the one with the criminal record."

"Exactly. I know how to break in." She walked toward the gate, her posture shifting into something confident and official. The guard in the booth barely looked up as she flashed a badge—where she'd gotten it, Jiang Chen didn't want to know.

"Late shift," she said. "Warden asked me to check the files."

The guard grunted, waving her through. She pushed open the gate and held it for Jiang Chen, who slipped through invisibly.

The prison yard was quiet. Inmates were locked down for the night, their cells dark and silent. Lights blazed from the administration building, where a few office workers were probably processing paperwork or drinking coffee.

"The warden's office is on the third floor," Jiang Chen said. "Corner room, east wing."

"You've been here before."

"I was executed here."

"Right." The Maestra's voice softened. "That must be strange. Coming back."

"It's not a vacation."

They entered through a side door, climbing stairs that smelled of bleach and cigarette smoke. The third floor was quiet, the hallway empty except for a cleaning cart parked outside the warden's door.

"Security cameras?" Jiang Chen asked.

"Three. One at the elevator, one at the stairwell, one outside the office." The Maestra pulled a small device from her pocket. "Signal jammer

title: Chapter 14
content:
CHAPTER 14: The Names She Carried

The black light from my staff didn't throw her off. It passed through her like she was made of smoke, and her grip on my mind tightened.

I felt her digging through memories I'd buried. My mother's face on the day of my trial. The way she'd aged twenty years in three months. The judge who wouldn't meet my eyes. The prosecutor who'd smiled when the verdict was read.

"Stop," I gasped.

"You want the truth?" Her voice echoed inside my skull. "Then let me find it."

She wasn't trying to possess me anymore. She was searching. Sifting through years of memories like she was looking for a specific file in a cluttered office.

I swung the staff again, but my arms felt heavy. Sluggish. She was draining me somehow, using my own life force to keep herself anchored.

"Found it," she whispered.

A memory I didn't recognize surfaced. A restaurant. Dark wood paneling. Private room. Three men in suits. One of them was writing something on a napkin. The other two were nodding.

I didn't know these people. I'd never been to this restaurant. But somehow, this memory was mine.

"What is this?" I asked.

"This is what happened the night they decided to kill you."

The scene shifted. Now I was in an office. Government seals on the wall. A desk cluttered with files. The man from the restaurant was talking to someone I couldn't see, his voice low and urgent.

"He's a liability. He doesn't know anything, but he's asking questions. Someone's been talking to him."

The unseen person responded, but the words were muffled. I couldn't make them out.

"He's just an accountant," the man said. "We can make it look like he took a bribe. The evidence is easy to plant. The murder weapon's already in his building."

The muffled voice again. Then the man nodded.

"Fine. But it's going to cost you. Framing someone for murder isn't cheap. I need the judge on my payroll, the prosecutor, four witnesses, and a forensics team. That's seven people who need to be paid off."

A pause.

"And the body. We need a body. Preferably someone with no family. Someone no one will miss."

The memory dissolved like smoke.

I was standing in the alley again, my staff in my hands, Chen Yulan watching me with those dead gray eyes.

"You didn't kill anyone," she said simply. "They killed someone else and made it look like you did it. Planted the evidence. Bought the witnesses. Corrupted the entire trial."

"Why?"

"Because you were asking questions. About the money. About where it was really going. Someone was afraid you'd find something they didn't want found."

I thought back to my last few months as a living man. I'd been doing routine audits. Boring work. Checking ledgers, verifying transactions, making sure the numbers added up. I'd found a discrepancy, but it was small. A few thousand yuan that didn't match the paperwork.

I'd mentioned it to my supervisor.

He'd told me not to worry about it.

I'd mentioned it again.

He'd told me to drop it.

I'd mentioned it a third time, to someone higher up.

Three weeks later, I was arrested.

"That small discrepancy," I said slowly. "It was connected to something bigger."

"Much bigger." Chen Yulan's eyes flickered. "I've been possessing powerful people for seventeen years. I've seen a lot of secrets. Your death wasn't just about you. It was about the people you were auditing. They were laundering money. Drug money. Human trafficking money. And you were about to find the trail."

The staff trembled in my hands. The black light flickered, unstable.

"I can give you names," she said. "I can give you bank accounts. Dates. Phone numbers. I can give you everything you need to destroy them."

"Then give it to me."

"Take me to Diyu first."

I laughed. It came out bitter and broken. "You think I trust you? You've been lying since we met."

"I have." She didn't deny it. "But I'm not lying about this. I saw it in your memories. You were framed. Executed. Your mother died two months later. Heart attack. They say it was grief."

The air left my lungs.

"Your sister dropped out of university. Couldn't afford it anymore. She's working at a factory now. Twelve-hour shifts. Sleeping in a dormitory with seven other women."

"How do you—"

"I saw it. In your memories. You think about her every day."

I did. I thought about Xiao Mei every single day. She was supposed to be the successful one. The first in our family to graduate from university. I was supposed to be proud of her.

Instead, I'd destroyed her future just by being her brother.

"The men who framed you," Chen Yulan said. "They're still out there. Still laundering money. Still killing people. And the only person who can stop them is a dead accountant with a magic stick."

"Staff," I corrected automatically.

"Whatever." She shrugged. "The point is, you need me. You can't go after them yourself. You're a soul reaper now. You belong to Diyu. But I can move through the mortal world. I can find evidence. I can talk to people. I can do things you can't."

"And what do you get out of it?"

"Freedom." She said it without hesitation. "The judges want to erase my memories. Turn me into a blank slate. I don't want that. I want to keep being me. I want to keep my skills, my knowledge, my... everything."

"You're a murderer."

"I'm a survivor."

I stared at her. The con woman. The liar. The woman who'd killed two soul reapers and stolen a dozen lives.

She was offering me revenge.

And I wanted it.

"No," I said.

Her face fell. "What?"

"I said no. I'm not making a deal with you."

"Then you'll never know—"

"I know enough." I raised my staff. "I know someone framed me. I know they killed someone else to do it. I know they're still out there. But I also know you're a monster, and if I let you go, you'll kill again."

"You're choosing justice over revenge?"

"I'm choosing not to become you."

Her eyes hardened. "Then you're a fool."

"Maybe." I activated the staff. Black light swirled around the crystal, forming chains that wrapped around her spectral form. "But I'm a fool who still believes in the rules."

She laughed. It was a horrible sound, full of contempt and pity. "The rules? The same rules that let you be executed for a crime you didn't commit? The same rules that let the real killers walk free? Those rules?"

"The rules of Diyu are different."

"Different how?"

I didn't answer. I just pulled the chains tighter, dragging her toward the portal that was opening at the end of the alley.

She struggled. Her soul twisted and writhed, her hands clawing at the chains, but she was weak now. I'd drained too much of her power. The attempt to possess me had cost her.

"No," she hissed. "No, you can't do this. I have information. I have names. I can help you."

"I don't want your help."

"Then you'll never find them. You'll never know who killed you. You'll spend eternity wondering, and they'll keep living their lives, spending their money, killing whoever gets in their way."

I stopped.

She was right.

I hated that she was right.

But she was.

"I'll find them myself," I said.

"With what resources? You're a soul reaper. You hunt ghosts. You don't investigate murders in the mortal world. The judges won't let you."

"The judges might have their own reasons."

Her eyes widened. "You're going to tell them."

"Not yet. First, I'm going to find out everything I can. And then, when I have enough evidence, I'm going to present it to them. They'll have to act."

"Or they'll ignore it. They don't care about mortal justice. They only care about the balance of souls."

"Then I'll find another way."

I pulled her through the portal.

Diyu's sky was the same as always — gray and endless, no sun, no stars, just an infinite void that pressed down on you like a weight. The air was cold and thin, smelling of ash and old stone.

The judges' hall loomed ahead. Massive pillars of black jade. Doors carved with scenes of judgment and punishment. The tortured faces of the damned, frozen in eternal agony.

I dragged Chen Yulan through the doors.

The hall was empty.

No judges. No clerks. No soul reapers.

Just silence.

"What is this?" Chen Yulan looked around, her eyes narrowing. "Where are they?"

I didn't know.

The hall was never empty. There was always someone here. Always a judge on the dais, always clerks shuffling papers, always soul reapers coming and going with their captured souls.

But now, nothing.

Then I saw it.

On the dais, where the Chief Judge's seat should have been, there was a piece of paper.

I walked toward it, my chains dragging Chen Yulan behind me.

The paper was covered in elegant calligraphy. Official seal at the bottom. Red ink that looked like dried blood.

I read it.

*The Ten Judges of Diyu acknowledge receipt of formal complaint #47,831, filed by Soul Reaper Jiang Chen, Third Rank, regarding irregularities in his mortal execution.*

*Due to the severity of the allegations, an investigation has been launched into the conduct of the following individuals:*

*Judge Liu Feng, Fourth Court, for accepting bribes.*
*Prosecutor Wang Tao, in the matter of People v. Jiang Chen, for evidence tampering.*
*Unnamed persons in the mortal government, for conspiracy to commit murder.*

*Until the investigation is concluded, Judge Liu Feng has been suspended from his duties. The remaining nine judges have convened an emergency session to review all related cases.*

*You are ordered to continue your duties as a soul reaper. You are not authorized to participate in the investigation. You are not to contact any mortal authorities. You are to report directly to the Chief Judge should you receive any new information.*

*Failure to comply will result in immediate termination of your soul reaper contract and reincarnation as a cockroach.*

I stared at the letter.

They'd already started an investigation.

They'd already suspended Judge Liu Feng.

They'd already done everything I was planning to do.

"The judges move fast," Chen Yulan said, reading over my shoulder. "I'll give them that."

"Shut up."

"Touchy."

I folded the letter and put it in my pocket. "Where are the holding cells?"

"Downstairs. But you might want to reconsider."

"Why?"

She smiled. It was not a pleasant smile.

"Because while you were dragging me back here, I was sending a message."

"What message?"

"To the men who killed you. I have their names. I have their secrets. And I just told them that the dead accountant is still alive, still working, and still coming for them."

The blood in my veins turned to ice.

"You're lying."

"I'm a con woman." She tilted her head. "Lying is what I do."

She was lying.

She had to be lying.

But the smile on her face told me she wasn't.

And somewhere in the mortal world, the men who'd killed me were already preparing their next move.

title: The Thirteenth Judge
content:
Jiang Chen's staff drove into Liu Mei's chest. Black light erupted, and she flew backward, slamming into the wall. The plaster cracked. She slid down, gasping. But she didn't release his mind.

He felt her there, a splinter in his thoughts. She'd burrowed deep, wrapped herself around memories he'd buried. His mother's face. The prison cell. The needle. She held them like hostages.

"You're going to kill me," she wheezed. "I felt it. You're going to drag me back and let them wipe me clean. I'll forget everything. I'll become a blank slate, a soul recycled into some newborn."

"That's the law."

"The law killed you." She pushed herself up. Blood trickled from her nose, black and thick. Soul blood. She was fading. "The law doesn't care about truth. It cares about order. You follow their rules, you'll never know who destroyed your life."

Jiang Chen gripped the staff. It hummed, hungry for another strike. One more hit and she'd be too weak to resist. He could drag her back, collect his merit, move up in the ranks. Become a real reaper.

But her words stuck. They burrowed deeper than her soul ever could.

*Someone powerful enough to frame you. Someone connected enough to corrupt a judge.*

He lowered the staff.

"I need proof," he said. "Not promises. Proof."

Liu Mei's eyes flickered. She was calculating, running odds, reading him. He saw it happen in real time. The con woman waking up, sniffing for leverage.

"I can't give you proof while I'm dying." She touched her nose, looked at the black blood on her fingers. "Get me somewhere safe. Let me recover. Then I'll show you what I saw."

"You're trying to buy time."

"I'm trying to stay alive." She laughed, but it was hollow. "Funny how that instinct doesn't die when you do."

He should take her back. Every reaper instinct screamed at him to finish the mission. But the other instinct — the human one, the one that had cost him everything in life — whispered louder.

"Where do you want to go?"

She smiled. It was the first genuine expression he'd seen on her face. Still ugly, still sharp, but real.

"There's a place. A temple in the mountains. The living can't find it, but the dead can. It's neutral ground. No reapers, no judges, no hungry ghosts. Just... quiet."

"Never heard of it."

"Because you haven't been dead long enough. There are rules in Diyu, but there are also exceptions. Places where the system doesn't reach." She wiped her nose. "That's where you'll find the truth about your death."

---

The temple sat at the edge of a cliff, half-hidden by mist. It wasn't built for the living. The walls were made of soul-stone, the roof of bone-tiles. No light came from inside, but Jiang Chen could feel it humming with power.

"Welcome to the Court of Echoes," Liu Mei said. "The Thirteenth Judge's domain."

"The thirteenth? There are only ten."

"For now." She pushed open the door. Inside, the temple was empty. No statues, no altars, no offerings. Just a single table in the center of the room, covered in papers.

"Who's the thirteenth?"

"No one. It was never filled. The position was created two thousand years ago, but the candidate died before taking the seat. Since then, the court has remained vacant. But the temple remembers."

She walked to the table, picked up a paper. "These are records. Every soul that passed through here, every judgment, every appeal. The temple records everything that happens in Diyu. It's a backup system."

"Why does it exist?"

"Because the Ten Judges are corrupt." She said it flatly, without malice. "They were created to maintain order, but order requires control. Control requires secrets. And secrets require someone to keep them honest." She held up the paper. "This is your file."

Jiang Chen took it. His hands trembled. The paper was old, yellowed, covered in handwriting that shifted as he read. He saw his name, his crime, his sentence. But the words kept changing, rearranging themselves.

"What is this?"

"Your case file. The real one. Everything that happened, as recorded by the temple. It can't be altered. It can't be erased. It's the truth."

He looked at the paper. The shifting words slowly settled.

*Jiang Chen. Convicted of murder. Sentence: death.*
*Evidence: fabricated by Detective Wang Lei.*
*Witness: paid by Liu Holdings Group.*
*Judge: bribed by Chen Wei, CEO of Liu Holdings.*

He read the names again. Detective Wang Lei. Liu Holdings Group. Chen Wei.

He knew Chen Wei. Everyone in the city knew Chen Wei. Billionaire. Philanthropist. Beloved public figure. The man who built hospitals, funded schools, donated to orphanages.

The man whose books Jiang Chen had cooked.

"I worked for him," Jiang Chen whispered. "I faked his tax records. I buried his offshore accounts. I made his dirty money look clean."

"And when they found out, he needed you gone. But he couldn't just fire you. You knew too much. So he made sure you'd never talk."

Jiang Chen's hands clenched the paper. "He framed me for murder."

"He framed you for murder. Paid a detective to plant evidence. Paid a witness to lie. Paid a judge to sign the death warrant." Liu Mei's voice softened. "You were a loose end. He tied you up and threw you away."

"Why?"

"Because he's still doing it. The money, the accounts, the bribes. He's been running a network for twenty years. You were just one accountant in a system of hundreds. But you were the one who got caught."

"I didn't get caught. I was set up."

"Same result." She sat down at the table. "Now you know. What are you going to do with it?"

Jiang Chen looked at the paper. The names burned into his mind. He could take it to the judges, demand justice. But Liu Mei's words echoed.

*The system doesn't care about truth. It cares about order.*

Chen Wei was powerful. He had money, connections, influence. He'd corrupted one judge. What was to stop him from corrupting the other nine?

And if Jiang Chen went to the judges with this proof, what would happen? Would they investigate? Or would they bury it, protect their own, and destroy the temple that had recorded the truth?

"You knew about this," he said. "You knew about Chen Wei. About the setup."

"I know about a lot of things." She leaned back. "I've been possessing people for seventeen years. I've been inside the minds of CEOs, politicians, generals. I've seen every dirty secret this city has to offer."

"Why didn't you tell anyone?"

"Who would I tell? The judges? The police?" She laughed. "The police are on his payroll. The judges are in his pocket. The only person who ever tried to stop him is sitting at this table, dead for three weeks."

Jiang Chen sat down across from her. "Then what do we do?"

"We do what I was doing before you caught me. We survive. We hide. We wait."

"Wait for what?"

"For him to make a mistake. For his network to crack. For someone else to get caught." She shrugged. "It's not a hero's path. But it's the only one that works."

Jiang Chen stared at the paper. The names. The dates. The amounts. It was all there, a complete record of Chen Wei's crimes. He could publish it. He could leak it. He could destroy the man who'd destroyed him.

But that would take resources. Connections. A network of his own.

And he was just a dead accountant with a magic staff.

"There has to be something we can do," he said.

"There is." Liu Mei's eyes glinted. "We build our own network. We find other loose ends, other people Chen Wei threw away. We gather them, arm them, point them at the right targets."

"You're talking about an army."

"I'm talking about a revolution." She leaned forward. "The dead outnumber the living, Jiang Chen. Every soul that passes through Diyu has a story, a grievance, a reason to hate the system that failed them. We just have to find the ones who are willing to fight."

"And then what?"

"Then we take down Chen Wei. And everyone who helped him." She smiled. "Including the judges."

Jiang Chen looked at the staff in his hands. It was still humming, still hungry. He could feel the power in it, the authority of Diyu. He was a reaper, a servant of the system.

But the system had failed him. It had killed him for a crime he didn't commit.

Why should he serve it?

"One condition," he said.

"Name it."

"When this is over, when Chen Wei is dead and the judges are exposed... you tell the truth. To everyone. About what you did, about the people you hurt, about the bodies you left behind."

Liu Mei's smile faded. "The truth would destroy me."

"I know."

She was silent for a long moment. Then she nodded.

"Fine. When this is over, I'll confess. I'll face whatever judgment comes."

"Good." Jiang Chen stood up. "Then let's start building."

---

They worked through the night. Liu Mei pulled names from her memory, souls she'd encountered in seventeen years of possession. People with grudges, people with skills, people who'd been wronged by the same system that had killed Jiang Chen.

He wrote them down. The list grew. Fifty names. A hundred. Two hundred.

By dawn, they had an army.

Jiang Chen looked at the list. It was the beginning of something dangerous, something that could destroy him as easily as it destroyed his enemies.

But it was also the beginning of something he'd never had in life.

Hope.

"We need a base," he said. "Somewhere the reapers can't find us."

Liu Mei nodded. "I know a place. The abandoned subway tunnels beneath the old city. The dead gather there, the ones who've slipped through the cracks. They'll join us."

"Are you sure?"

"I'm sure." She stood up. "But we need to move fast. The judges will send more reapers. They won't stop until they find us."

Jiang Chen folded the paper, tucked it into his robe. "Then let's not give them time to prepare."

They walked toward the door. The temple hummed around them, the Court of Echoes recording their words, their plans, their revolution.

But as they reached the exit, something blocked their path.

A woman.

She stood in the doorway, her hair white as bone, her eyes black as void. She wore the robes of a judge, but where the Ten Judges wore black, she wore crimson. Blood red.

"Going somewhere?" she asked.

Liu Mei froze. "That's not possible."

The woman smiled. "The Thirteenth Judge was never filled. But that doesn't mean the seat is empty."

Jiang Chen raised his staff. "Who are you?"

"I am the one who has been waiting." The woman stepped forward. "I am the one who recorded your truth. I am the one who kept your secrets." Her eyes fixed on Jiang Chen. "And I am the one who will decide whether you live or die."

The staff hummed. The woman didn't flinch.

"Put that down," she said. "You're going to need both hands for what

title: Chapter 16
content:
CHAPTER 16: The Ledger's True Owner

The black light from Jiang Chen's staff sent Madam Zhao's soul skidding across the warehouse floor like a stone across frozen water. She crashed into a stack of wooden pallets, splintering them into a shower of debris.

"You tried to possess me again," he said, his voice flat. Empty.

Madam Zhao picked herself up, brushing sawdust from her stolen body. The dress was torn now, hanging off one shoulder. She didn't seem to care. "I had to see if you'd fall for it again."

"I didn't fall for it."

"No." She smiled, and it was genuine. "You didn't. You're learning."

Jiang Chen kept the staff pointed at her chest. The black light had faded, but he could feel it humming beneath his skin, waiting. "The names. Now."

"The names won't help you." She walked toward him, slow and deliberate. "You think knowing who framed you will change anything? You're dead, Jiang Chen. Even if the judges re-examine your case, even if they declare you innocent, you're still dead. They can't give you your life back."

"I don't want my life back. I want justice."

"Justice." She laughed. "You've been dead three weeks and you still believe in justice. That's either admirable or pathetic. I haven't decided which."

"You're stalling."

"I'm thinking." She stopped a few feet from him, close enough that he could see the cracks in her stolen skin, the places where the soul beneath was starting to show through. "You want names? Fine. But I need collateral."

"What kind of collateral?"

"Promise me something. When you find out who did this to you—when you stand face to face with them—you'll hesitate."

Jiang Chen frowned. "Why would I hesitate?"

"Because if you don't, you'll become exactly what they wanted you to be." Her voice dropped. "A killer."

"I'm already a killer. I'm a Soul Reaper. I send souls to judgment."

"That's different. That's justice. This would be revenge." She held his gaze. "Promise me you'll hesitate. Just for a second. Just long enough to ask them why."

He didn't understand her. She was a con woman, a murderer, a thief of bodies and lives. She'd killed two Soul Reapers without blinking. Why did she care whether he became a cold-blooded killer?

"Why does it matter to you?"

"Because I've been where you're going." She touched her chest, where the dress was torn. "This body? I killed her. Not quickly, not mercifully. I tortured her because I wanted to hear her scream. I wanted her to suffer the way I'd suffered. And when it was done, when she was dead and I was standing in her skin, I felt nothing. Absolutely nothing." She looked away. "Revenge doesn't fill the hole. It just makes it bigger."

Jiang Chen stood there, staff in hand, and for a moment he saw her differently. Not as a monster. Not as a villain. But as a warning. A mirror of what he might become.

"Promise," she said again.

"I promise."

She nodded, then reached into her dress and pulled out a folded piece of paper. It was yellowed, creased, held together by tape. "I took this from the evidence room when I worked for the Ministry of Justice. Before I was caught. Before I was sentenced."

Jiang Chen unfolded it. It was a ledger page, handwritten, listing names and dates and amounts of money. His eyes scanned down the columns until they landed on a familiar name.

*Jiang Chen — 500,000 RMB — 3/15*

"Someone paid half a million for your death," Madam Zhao said. "That's a lot of money for a nobody accountant."

"Who paid it?"

"The name at the top. Read it."

He looked up. There was a name at the top of the ledger, written in neat, precise characters. A name he recognized.

*Director Lin Wei*

His former boss. The man who'd given him the bribe. The man who'd looked the other way when Jiang Chen had asked him to.

"Lin Wei paid to have me killed?"

"No. Lin Wei is the middleman. The money came from someone else." She pointed to a symbol at the bottom of the page. A small, intricate seal. "This is the mark of the Yun Family. One of the oldest crime syndicates in the province."

Jiang Chen stared at the seal. It meant nothing to him. He'd never dealt with the Yun Family. He'd never heard of them until this moment.

"Why would the Yun Family want me dead?"

"I don't know. But I know someone who does." She took the ledger page back and folded it carefully. "There's a man. He runs a teahouse in the old district. He used to work for the Yun Family. He knows their secrets."

"What's his name?"

"Old Chen. But he won't talk to you. You're a stranger. You're dead. You're a Soul Reaper." She smiled. "You need a guide. Someone he trusts."

"And who would that be?"

"Me." She tapped her chest. "I've known Old Chen for twenty years. I saved his daughter from a possession case. He owes me."

"You want me to take you to the mortal realm."

"I want you to trust me." She held out her hand. "One more time. One more chance. Let me prove I'm telling the truth."

Jiang Chen looked at her hand. He looked at the staff in his other hand. He thought about the judges, about his training, about everything he'd been told about soul reapers and con women and the rules of the underworld.

The rules said to drag her back. The rules said to deliver her to judgment. The rules said to never, ever trust a condemned soul.

But the rules didn't know about Director Lin Wei. The rules didn't know about the Yun Family. The rules didn't know that Jiang Chen had been murdered for a crime he didn't commit.

He took her hand.

"One chance."

She smiled, and this time it wasn't a con woman's smile. It was something softer. Almost human.

"Thank you."

"Don't thank me yet. If you try anything—"

"I know. The staff." She let go of his hand and walked toward the warehouse door. "Come on. We don't have much time."

"Time for what?"

"For the judges to figure out that I escaped." She pushed open the door and stepped into the street. The neon lights of the city cast long shadows across her face. "They'll send someone to collect me. They'll send your mentor, probably. And when they find me with you, they'll assume the worst."

Jiang Chen followed her into the night. The air was cold, heavy with the smell of cooking oil and exhaust fumes. The city hummed with life, unaware that two dead people were walking through its streets.

"Why would they assume the worst?"

"Because I'm a con woman. And you're a rookie Soul Reaper who just got told his death was a setup." She glanced at him. "They'll think you've gone rogue. That I've turned you. And maybe they'll be right."

"I haven't gone rogue."

"Not yet." She turned down a narrow alley, weaving between trash cans and sleeping cats. "But you will. Once you start pulling at the thread of your death, everything else unravels. The Ministry of Justice. The judges. The underworld itself. They're all connected."

"You're saying the judges were involved in my framing?"

"I'm saying nothing. I'm letting you draw your own conclusions." She stopped in front of a weathered door, painted red, with a small sign above it that read *Old Chen's Teahouse*. "This is it."

Jiang Chen looked at the door. It was old, scarred, held together by rusting hinges. A single light flickered through a grimy window.

"How do we do this?"

"You let me talk. You stay quiet. And if Old Chen asks you a question, you answer with a question." She knocked three times, paused, then knocked twice more.

The door creaked open. An old man peered out, his face weathered and lined, his eyes sharp despite his age. He looked at Madam Zhao, then at Jiang Chen, then back at Madam Zhao.

"You," he said. "I heard you were dead."

"Reports of my death have been greatly exaggerated." She smiled. "Can we come in?"

Old Chen studied Jiang Chen for a long moment. "Who's the dead boy?"

"A friend."

"He's a Soul Reaper."

"Yes."

"He's carrying a staff."

"Yes."

Old Chen sighed and opened the door wider. "Come in. But if he tries to arrest me, I'll deny everything."

They stepped inside. The teahouse was small, filled with mismatched furniture and the smell of old tea leaves. A kettle whistled on a stove. A cat slept in a corner.

"Sit." Old Chen gestured to a table. "I'll make tea."

They sat. Jiang Chen kept his staff close, his eyes scanning the room for threats. Madam Zhao seemed relaxed, almost bored.

"What do you want?" Old Chen asked as he poured the tea.

"Information." Madam Zhao took a cup. "The Yun Family paid to have this man killed. I want to know why."

Old Chen's hand paused mid-pour. "Why do you care?"

"Because I'm tired of running. And because he's the first person in twenty years who didn't try to kill me on sight." She sipped the tea. "That makes him worth protecting."

Old Chen looked at Jiang Chen again. "What did you do to the Yun Family?"

"Nothing," Jiang Chen said. "I've never even met them."

"Then why did they pay half a million to have you killed?"

"I don't know."

Old Chen set down the teapot. He sat across from them, his hands folded on the table. "The Yun Family doesn't waste money. If they killed you, they had a reason." He paused. "What did you do before you died?"

"I was an accountant."

"What kind of accountant?"

"I worked for the Ministry of Justice. I handled financial audits for their special investigations division."

Old Chen's eyes widened. "You audited the Yun Family's accounts."

"I audited everyone's accounts. It was my job."

"No." Old Chen shook his head. "You don't understand. Two years ago, the Ministry launched an investigation into the Yun Family's money laundering operations. They brought in an outside auditor. Someone who wasn't connected to the local government." He leaned forward. "That auditor was you."

Jiang Chen remembered. He'd worked sixteen-hour days for three months, sifting through ledgers and bank statements and shell companies. He'd found evidence of money laundering, bribery, and murder. He'd compiled it all into a report and submitted it to his supervisor.

Director Lin Wei.

"Lin Wei buried the report," Jiang Chen said slowly.

"Yes." Old Chen nodded. "And the Yun Family paid him to make sure you never found out. But someone talked. Someone told them you'd made copies."

"I didn't make copies."

"Are you sure?" Old Chen's eyes bore into him. "Think. Did you keep any records? Any notes? Anything at all?"

Jiang Chen thought back. He remembered the long nights, the stacks of paper, the endless columns of numbers. He remembered writing something down. A list. A list of names.

He'd hidden it in his apartment. Behind the loose brick in the wall. The same apartment that had been sealed after his execution.

"The evidence is still there," he said. "In my old apartment."

Old

title: Chapter 17
content:
CHAPTER 17: The Debt Collector's Price

The black light from Jiang Chen's staff sent the woman's soul flying backward into the wall of the bank vault. She hit with a wet smack that left cracks in the concrete, her form flickering like a dying candle.

He'd put too much power into the blast. The staff was still humming, hungry, wanting more.

"I told you," he said, his voice flat. "No more tricks."

She slid down the wall, her stolen body's face twisted in pain. "I wasn't tricking you. I was proving a point. You're too easy to read, Soul Reaper."

"Your point is made. Now tell me the names."

"Or what? You'll kill me again?" She laughed, blood spilling from her lips. "I've been dead for seventeen years. Death doesn't scare me anymore."

Jiang Chen stepped closer, the staff still crackling with black energy. "The judges will make you talk. They have methods."

"The judges won't help you." She wiped blood from her chin, staring at him with eyes that held too much knowledge. "They're the ones who assigned you to hunt me, right? They told you I was just another rogue soul. A con woman. A thief."

"You are."

"I'm a lot of things. But I'm also the person who's been inside the minds of twelve high-ranking officials across four provinces." She smiled, bloody and crooked. "I know where the bodies are buried. Literally. And I know whose hands are dirty enough to frame a man for murder and make it stick."

Jiang Chen's grip tightened on the staff. "The names."
