"Chen Wei. Director of the Third Financial Bureau." She said it casually, like she was reading a menu. "Zhao Ming. Deputy Commissioner of Public Security. And Judge Lin. The one who signed your death warrant."

The names hit him like physical blows. Chen Wei. He'd worked for Chen Wei. Taken orders from Chen Wei. Looked the other way when Chen Wei told him to.

"You're lying."

"I'm not. You know I'm not." She pushed herself up, leaning against the vault wall. "I was inside Zhao Ming's head two years ago. He was drunk. Complaining about a problem they'd solved. A loose end named Jiang Chen who got too curious about the money trail."

"I never—"

"You didn't even know what you'd found." She shook her head. "That's what makes it tragic. You were just doing your job, cross-referencing accounts, and you stumbled onto something that didn't add up. You didn't even report it. You just mentioned it to a coworker. But that coworker told someone, who told someone, and within a week, you were arrested for a murder you didn't commit."

Jiang Chen's hands were shaking. He remembered. A discrepancy in the ledgers. Three million yuan that moved through accounts it shouldn't have. He'd mentioned it to Li Wei, the senior accountant at the desk next to his. Just a casual comment. "Doesn't add up."

Li Wei had smiled, nodded, and said nothing.

Two weeks later, Jiang Chen was arrested.

"Li Wei," he said, the name tasting like poison. "He was the one who reported me."

"No. He was the one who killed the woman they framed you for murdering." She tilted her head. "You didn't know that part, did you? The woman. The one whose body they found in your apartment. Her name was Zhang Mei. She was a prostitute from Fujian. They paid her to stay in your apartment while you were at work. Then Li Wei came by and slit her throat."

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because I need you to understand." She pushed off the wall, standing on unsteady legs. "I'm not your enemy. I'm the only person who can give you the truth. And the truth is, you were murdered by men who are still alive, still powerful, still walking free. And the judges of Diyu? They don't care. They have their rules, their bureaucracy, their quotas. They'll wipe my memory, throw me back into the cycle, and you'll spend eternity hunting souls, never knowing why you're really dead."

The staff hummed. The vault air was thick with tension.

"You said you could prove it."

"I can. I have documents. Recordings. Confessions I pulled from their minds and wrote down while I was possessing them." She reached into her jacket and pulled out a small leather notebook, its pages yellowed and worn. "Seventeen years of secrets. All here."

She held it out to him.

Jiang Chen stared at the notebook. It was a physical object. Real. Tangible. If he took it, he could read the names, the dates, the details. He could know.

He could also be carrying evidence that would get him killed—again—if anyone found it.

"How do I know you're not lying?"

"You don't." She shrugged. "But if I was lying, I'd have made up something more dramatic than a dead accountant who found a mistake in a spreadsheet."

She had a point.

He reached out and took the notebook. The leather was warm from her body heat. He opened it to a random page and saw handwriting—dozens of different handwritings, some neat, some messy, some in languages he didn't recognize.

"Each possession, I wrote down what I learned," she said. "Sometimes in their handwriting, sometimes in mine. It's not organized. But the information is there."

He flipped to a page near the back and found his own name.

*Jiang Chen. Executed 2024. Frame job. Perpetrators: Chen Wei (Director, Third Financial Bureau), Zhao Ming (Deputy Commissioner, Public Security), Lin Wei (Judge, Intermediate People's Court), Li Wei (Accountant, direct murder). Motive: Jiang Chen discovered discrepancy in accounts related to provincial kickback scheme. Value: 3.2 million yuan. Status: All perpetrators still active, still in power.*

The handwriting was different from the rest. Neater. More deliberate. Like she'd written this one with extra care.

"I wrote that one last week," she said. "After I found out the judges assigned you to hunt me. I figured you deserved to know."

"Why didn't you tell me sooner?"

"Because I needed to see if you were worth telling." She met his eyes. "Most soul reapers don't care. They're dead men doing a dead man's job. But you... you hesitated. When you had me trapped in that woman's body, you hesitated. You saw me as a person, not just a soul to be collected. That's rare."

"I'm not special."

"Maybe not. But you're different." She smiled, and for a moment, she looked almost human. "That's why I'm giving you a choice. You can take me back to Diyu, hand me over to the judges, and let them wipe my memory. You'll never know if I was telling the truth. Or you can let me go, keep the notebook, and hunt the men who killed you yourself."

"You're asking me to betray my duty."

"I'm asking you to choose justice over obedience." She stepped closer. "The judges don't care about the living. They don't care about the dead who were murdered. They only care about the rules. But you're still half-alive. You still have a connection to the mortal world. You can do what they can't."

"And what's that?"

"Make them pay."

The words hung in the air between them. Jiang Chen looked at the notebook in his hands. He looked at the woman who had killed two soul reapers, conned dozens of families, and stolen a life that wasn't hers.

She was a monster.

But she was also right.

"What's your name?" he asked.

She blinked, surprised. "What?"

"Your real name. Not the names you've stolen. Your name."

She was silent for a long moment. Then she said, "Su Yan. My name was Su Yan."

"Su Yan." He tested the name on his tongue. "If I let you go, where will you go?"

"I don't know. Somewhere far. Somewhere the judges won't find me."

"And if I need to find you?"

"You won't. But if you do, leave a message at the Temple of the Five Lords in Fuzhou. The priest there owes me a favor."

Jiang Chen looked at the staff in his hand. It was still humming, still hungry, still waiting for him to do his duty. The judges would be expecting him back with the soul. If he returned empty-handed, there would be consequences.

But if he returned with the names of the men who framed him, maybe the consequences would be different.

"I'm going to make a deal," he said. "I'm going to let you go. But you're going to give me something in return."

"What?"

"Your word. If I need you, you'll come. No tricks. No lies. No possession."

She laughed. "You're asking a con woman to give you her word?"

"I'm asking Su Yan to give me her word."

The smile faded. She looked at him, really looked, and something in her eyes shifted. "You have it. On my death, you have it."

"Good." He stepped back, lowering the staff. "Now get out of here before I change my mind."

She didn't hesitate. She slipped past him, through the vault door, into the dark corridor beyond. At the threshold, she paused.

"Jiang Chen."

"What?"

"Be careful. The men who killed you—they're not just corrupt. They're connected. They have friends in high places. And some of those friends..." She glanced back at him. "Some of them are in Diyu."

She vanished into the darkness.

Jiang Chen stood alone in the vault, the notebook heavy in his hands. The staff had stopped humming. The air was still.

He looked down at the open page again. His name. Their names. The truth.

And then he saw something he hadn't noticed before. At the bottom of the page, in tiny handwriting, almost invisible:

*Note: Judge Lin Wei is not a mortal judge. He is a soul reaper. Retired. Still has connections in Diyu.*

Jiang Chen's blood went cold.

A soul reaper. A retired soul reaper had signed his death warrant. Which meant the conspiracy wasn't just mortal. It reached into the underworld. It reached into the very institution that had recruited him.

The judges of Diyu—the ones who had given him the staff, the power, the second chance—had they known?

Or were they part of it?

He closed the notebook and slipped it into his jacket. Then he walked out of the vault, out of the bank, into the cold night air.

The street was empty. The moon was hidden behind clouds. Somewhere in the distance, a dog barked.

Jiang Chen looked up at the sky and wondered if the judges were watching him right now. If they knew what he'd done. If they were already sending someone to collect him.

The staff pulsed once, a warning.

*Finish the mission,* it seemed to say. *Or face the consequences.*

But Jiang Chen had a new mission now.

And he wasn't going to stop until every name in that notebook was crossed out.

He started walking, the notebook burning against his chest, the weight of seventeen years of secrets pressing down on his shoulders.

Behind him, in the shadows of the bank, a figure watched.

It had been watching since the beginning.

And it was already reaching for its phone to make a call.

title: The Thirteenth Floor
content:
The black light consumed everything.

Jiang Chen couldn't see. Couldn't breathe. The staff had become a conduit for something ancient and terrible, and the con woman's scream was the only thing anchoring him to reality. Her soul clawed at his mind even as it unraveled, desperate, furious, refusing to believe she'd lost.

"You idiot," she snarled, her voice fracturing into echoes. "You absolute fool. They'll kill you too. They've already killed you once—"

He wrenched the staff free.

The light collapsed inward. The con woman's spirit form flickered, translucent, diminished. She was still there, but barely. Her eyes burned with hatred, but the smile was gone. The confidence was gone. She looked, for the first time, afraid.

"You didn't let me finish," she whispered.

"I don't need you to finish." Jiang Chen's voice was flat. Hollow. "I need you in Diyu."

"You need me alive to testify."

"You're not alive."

"Close enough." She tried to straighten, but her form kept flickering. "The judges want me processed. Questioned. They won't get anything if I'm a vegetable. You've already damaged me enough that my memories are—"

"Your memories are fine. You're still trying to con me."

Her eyes narrowed. "You're learning."

"I have a good teacher." He grabbed her by the spectral collar and pulled her toward the hospital's exit. The night air hit him like a wall of heat and humidity, the streets of Guangzhou slick with recent rain. He needed to find a quiet place to cross the veil. Somewhere no one would see a man dragging a visible spirit through the mortal realm.

The hospital parking lot was mostly empty. A few cars. A security guard asleep in his booth. Jiang Chen dragged the con woman toward the shadows between two delivery trucks.

"You're making a mistake," she said.

"You've said that already."

"I mean it this time." Her voice dropped. Serious. "Do you know who ordered your death?"

"No. And I won't get answers by letting you run free."

"I'm not asking you to let me run. I'm asking you to let me talk." She stopped resisting, letting him pull her along like dead weight. "Your execution was signed by Judge Zhao. The Third Judge of Diyu. He's the one who approved the death warrant."

Jiang Chen stopped.

"We don't have death warrants in the mortal realm," he said slowly. "We have court orders. Sentencing documents. But the judges of Diyu don't sign execution papers for living people."

"They do when the living person is a problem." She met his eyes. "Your case was special, Jiang Chen. The judges don't just process souls who arrive. They have agents. Informants. They watch the mortal world for souls that will cause trouble in the afterlife. Souls that need to be... expedited."

"Expedited."

"Killed. Framed. Executed." She shrugged. "Whatever works."

"You're lying."

"I'm a con woman. But I'm not stupid." She gestured at her diminished form. "I've been possessing people for seventeen years. Do you think I never got caught? Never slipped up? I've been caught three times. Three times, I was brought before Judge Zhao. Three times, he let me go."

"Why?"

"Because I had information. I knew things. I knew about the soul trade. The body markets. The families who pay to keep their ancestors' spirits from being reincarnated." She leaned closer. "I knew about Judge Zhao's side business. Selling soul reaper positions to the highest bidder."

Jiang Chen's grip tightened on his staff. "You're saying my position was bought?"

"I'm saying your death was ordered to create a vacancy. And someone paid to fill it." She smiled. "You're not a soul reaper because you were chosen. You're a soul reaper because you were convenient."

The words hit him like a physical blow. He'd spent three weeks believing he'd been given a second chance. A purpose. A role in the cosmic order. And now this woman was telling him it was all a transaction.

"Who paid?" he asked.

"I don't know the name. But I know who does." She pointed at the hospital behind them. "The woman whose body I stole. Her name is Lin Mei. She's a junior accountant at the Guangzhou Municipal Court. She processed the paperwork for your execution."

"You're lying."

"I'm a con woman. But I've been possessing Lin Mei for six months. I know everything she knows." She held up her spectral hand. "Swear on my soul. If I'm lying, let the judges erase me completely. No reincarnation. No return. Nothing."

That was a serious oath. A soul oath. In Diyu, such things were binding. If she was lying, she was condemning herself to oblivion.

"Why didn't you tell me this before?"

"Because I needed leverage. And now I need you to trust me." She met his eyes. "Take me to Diyu. Let me testify. But don't let Judge Zhao process me. He'll make me disappear. He'll destroy the evidence. And you'll never know the truth."

Jiang Chen's mind raced. He was a junior soul reaper. Barely trained. He had no authority to request a different judge. The standard procedure was clear: bring the rogue soul to the nearest gate, hand her over to the processing team, let the judges handle the rest.

But if Judge Zhao was corrupt...

"I need proof," he said. "Something I can take to another judge."

"Lin Mei's office. Fourth floor. Filing cabinet seven. Password is 1984." She smiled. "The year. Not the book. She's not that clever."

"You expect me to go back in there?"

"I expect you to want the truth." She settled onto the ground, her form flickering. "I'll wait. I'm not going anywhere. You've damaged me enough that I can barely move."

Jiang Chen looked at the hospital. At the con woman. At the staff in his hand.

He was making a mistake. He knew it. She was manipulating him. Playing on his emotions. Using his death as a tool.

But she'd sworn a soul oath.

He turned and walked back into the hospital.

---

The fourth floor was quiet. The night shift was minimal, and the administrative offices were dark. Jiang Chen moved through the corridors like a ghost, his footsteps swallowed by the carpet. He found Lin Mei's office easily enough—her name was on the door, along with her title: Junior Accountant, Court Financial Division.

The lock was cheap. A credit card popped it open in seconds.

The office was small. A desk. A computer. A filing cabinet. Jiang Chen went straight to cabinet seven, entered the password, and pulled open the drawer.

It was full of manila folders. Hundreds of them. Each labeled with a case number and a name.

He found his own folder near the back.

Case #1847-329. Jiang Chen. Charge: First-degree murder. Status: Executed.

He opened it.

Inside was a single sheet of paper. A financial transfer record. One million yuan, transferred from an offshore account to Judge Zhao's personal registry. The memo line read: "Processing fee for expedited case resolution."

Beneath it was another sheet. A list of names. Other executed prisoners. Each with a similar transfer record.

Jiang Chen counted. Twenty-three names. Twenty-three executions. Twenty-three million yuan.

His hands were shaking.

He took a photo of the documents with his phone. Then he took the originals, folded them, and put them in his pocket.

As he turned to leave, he noticed something on Lin Mei's computer screen. A blinking cursor. A half-typed email.

Subject: Urgent—Transfer Complete

Body: The package has been delivered to Processing. The new recruit has been assigned. Awaiting further instructions.

It was sent ten minutes ago.

Jiang Chen's blood went cold.

He ran.

---

The parking lot was empty.

The con woman was gone.

He searched the shadows, the trucks, the corners. Nothing. She'd vanished. Either she'd recovered enough to escape, or—

A hand grabbed his ankle.

He looked down.

The con woman was lying under a delivery truck, her spectral form barely visible. She was clutching a piece of paper. "They came," she whispered. "Two of them. Other soul reapers. They said Judge Zhao sent them to collect me personally."

"Where did they go?"

"Didn't take me. Couldn't." She smiled weakly. "I hid. I'm good at hiding." She held up the paper. "But they dropped this."

Jiang Chen took it.

It was a list. Names. Dates. Locations.

The names matched the ones in the folder. Twenty-three executed prisoners.

But there was a twenty-fourth name at the bottom.

Jiang Chen.

Next to his name, someone had written: "Still active. Requires immediate termination. Authorization: Zhao."

"They're coming for you," the con woman said. "They know you know. They're going to kill you again. And this time, there won't be a second chance."

Jiang Chen looked at the staff in his hand. At the paper. At the woman who'd tried to possess him, con him, use him.

"You swore a soul oath," he said.

"I know."

"You said if you were lying, you'd be erased."

"I know."

"You weren't lying."

"No." She met his eyes. "I wasn't."

Jiang Chen extended his hand. "Then we need to move. Now."

She took it.

And together, they ran into the night, with two soul reapers hunting them and a corrupt judge pulling the strings.

---

The first one found them three blocks away.

He was tall. Thin. Wearing a black suit that seemed to absorb light. His staff was white, bone-white, carved with symbols that hurt to look at.

"You're Jiang Chen," he said. "Judge Zhao sends his regards."

The con woman stepped in front of Jiang Chen. "Run."

"I can fight."

"You can't. He's been a soul reaper for fifty years. You've been one for three weeks." She pushed him back. "Run. Find Judge Liu. The Fourth Judge. She's the only one who can stand against Zhao."

"What about you?"

"I'm a con woman." She smiled. "I'll think of something."

The tall soul reaper raised his staff.

Jiang Chen ran.

Behind him, he heard the clash of spirits. The scream of the con woman. The crack of bone-white light.

And then, silence.

He didn't look back.

He kept running.

Because if he stopped, everything she'd told him would be for nothing.

If he stopped, he'd never know the truth.

If he stopped, he'd die again.

And this time, there would be no coming back.

title: Chapter 19
content:
CHAPTER 19: The Warden's Secret

The explosion of black light threw Jiang Chen backward. He hit the ground hard, the staff skidding from his grip. His skull rang like a bell. Through blurred vision, he saw her—the con woman, the soul thief, the woman who'd just tried to possess him for the third time—sprawled on her back, smoke rising from her chest.

She wasn't moving.

He crawled to his knees. "Hey."

Nothing.

"Hey!" He shook her shoulder. Her body was warm. Still alive. But her soul—

He couldn't sense it.

The staff lay three meters away, still pulsing with residual energy. He'd hit her with everything. Full power. No restraint. The kind of strike that was supposed to send souls straight to reincarnation, no stops, no questions, no mercy.

He'd killed her.

Not killed killed. She was still breathing. But her soul was gone. Obliterated. Scattered to whatever cosmic wind took the fragments of the dead.

"No, no, no..."

He pressed his fingers to her neck. Pulse. Strong. But her eyes were open, staring at nothing. Vacant. Empty.

He'd made her a vegetable.

"Damn it."

He sat back, breathing hard. The anger had drained out of him, replaced by something cold and hollow. She was going to tell him the truth. She was going to give him names. She was going to show him who'd framed him, who'd paid the judge, who'd wanted him dead.

And he'd panicked.

Because she'd found the crack in his armor. She'd seen his death, his execution, his injustice—and she'd used it. She'd promised him revenge, and he'd wanted it so badly that he'd let her close enough to strike.

And now she was empty.

He picked up the staff. The wood was warm, almost hot. He'd never channeled that much power before. He didn't know he could.

*The staff chooses the reaper.*

Old Man Zhao's voice echoed in his memory. *But it also changes them. Every time you use it, it takes a piece of you. Be careful, boy. Power has a price.*

What price had he just paid?

He stood, looking down at the woman's body. She'd stolen someone's life. She'd killed two reapers. She'd conned families out of their savings. She deserved punishment.

But not this.

He didn't know what she deserved. That was the problem. He was supposed to drag her back to Diyu, let the judges sort it out. He was supposed to follow procedure, follow orders, follow the rules.

Instead, he'd lost control.

A sound. Footsteps. Fast, approaching from the alley's mouth.

Jiang Chen spun, staff raised.

A man stood at the entrance. Mid-fifties. Gray suit, slightly rumpled. Bald head, round glasses. He looked like a mid-level bureaucrat who'd taken the wrong turn on his way home.

But his eyes were wrong.

They glowed. Faintly. Yellow-green, like a cat's eyes catching light.

"You've made a mess," the man said.

"Who are you?"

"The person you're looking for." The man stepped into the alley. His footsteps made no sound. "I'm the one who paid the judge. I'm the one who framed you. I'm the one who wanted you dead."

Jiang Chen's blood went cold. "You're lying."

"I never lie. It's inefficient." The man stopped three meters away, hands in his pockets. "You were supposed to die quietly. Take the fall, disappear, case closed. But then you had to go and become a reaper. That wasn't in the plan."

"Who are you?"

"My name is Wei Zhong. I work for a department you've never heard of, doing work you couldn't understand." He tilted his head. "But you can call me the Warden."

"The Warden of what?"

"Of secrets." Wei Zhong smiled. It wasn't a pleasant smile. "You stumbled into something, Jiang Chen. Something big. Something that involves people much more powerful than you. The bribe you took? The murder you were framed for? That was just cleanup. You were a loose end."

"What did I see?"

"Nothing. That was the problem." Wei Zhong took his hands out of his pockets. He was holding a small black book, bound in leather that looked too old, too dark. "You processed some documents. You filed some reports. You didn't know what they meant, but you saw the signatures. You saw the names. And those names couldn't afford to exist."

Jiang Chen's mind raced. Three years ago. He'd been a junior accountant at a state-owned trading company. He'd processed import records, cross-referenced shipping manifests, signed off on container inspections. Nothing sensitive. Nothing important.

Except one file.

A shipment of medical equipment. Expensive stuff. MRI machines, surgical robots, ventilators. All labeled as donations to a rural hospital. But the manifests didn't match. The weights were wrong. The containers were too heavy for medical equipment.

He'd flagged it. His supervisor told him to let it go. He'd let it go.

And then, six months later, he was arrested for murder.

"The medical equipment," he said.

Wei Zhong's smile widened. "There it is. I knew you were smart. That's why you had to die."

"What was in the containers?"

"Parts. Components. Enough to build a thousand drones. Enough to arm a private army. Enough to start a war." Wei Zhong tapped the black book. "But that's not your concern anymore. Your concern is that woman you just killed."

"She's not dead."

"Her soul is gone. Her body is empty. In the mortal world, she's a vegetable. In Diyu, she's a loose end." Wei Zhong opened the book. "And I have her name."

"What are you talking about?"

"The black book. It's a ledger. Every soul that enters Diyu, every soul that escapes, every soul that gets destroyed—it's all recorded here." He ran his finger down a page. "And right now, I'm writing her name in the destroyed column. Case closed. File stamped. No investigation needed."

"You can't just—"

"I can. I do. I've been doing it for thirty years." Wei Zhong closed the book. "You think you're the first person to discover something they shouldn't have? You think you're the first loose end I've tied? I've killed more people than you've met. I've erased more souls than you've collected. And I'm very, very good at my job."

Jiang Chen gripped his staff. "Then why are you telling me this?"

"Because I'm offering you a choice." Wei Zhong tucked the book back into his pocket. "Join me."

"What?"

"Join me. Work for me. You're a reaper now—you have access to Diyu, to the soul records, to the judges. You can be useful." He spread his hands. "And in return, I'll give you the truth. The whole truth. Who ordered your death, why, and how to get justice."

"Justice? You're the one who killed me."

"I'm a tool. A weapon. Someone pointed me at you, and I fired." Wei Zhong shrugged. "I don't hate you. I don't even know you. I was paid to make you disappear, and I did. But now you're back, and you're valuable. So I'm offering you a promotion."

"You want me to betray Diyu."

"I want you to survive." Wei Zhong's eyes stopped glowing. They were just eyes now. Ordinary. Tired. "The people I work for don't leave witnesses. They don't leave loose ends. The only reason you're still alive is because you became a reaper before they could find you. But now they know. And they will come for you."

"Let them come."

"Bold words. But you couldn't even handle one con woman." Wei Zhong gestured at the empty body on the ground. "What makes you think you can handle a room full of trained killers with government backing and no conscience?"

Jiang Chen had no answer.

"Think about it." Wei Zhong turned and walked toward the alley's mouth. "You have three days. After that, I stop being friendly. And trust me—you don't want to see me unfriendly."

"Wait."

Wei Zhong stopped but didn't turn.

"The woman. She said she could prove I was framed. She said she had names."

"She was lying. That's what con women do."

"She saw my death. She saw the execution. She saw the witnesses."

"She saw what you showed her." Wei Zhong finally turned. "When a soul tries to possess someone, they see memories. But memories are unreliable. They're filtered through emotion, trauma, bias. She saw what she wanted to see. She told you what you wanted to hear."

"How do you know?"

"Because I was there." Wei Zhong's voice was flat. "I watched them execute you. I made sure the needle went in clean. I made sure the witnesses were paid. I made sure the judge signed the right papers." He paused. "And I made sure your death certificate was filed under 'justified execution, no further investigation.' You are dead, Jiang Chen. Legally, officially, permanently dead. There is no justice for you. There is only survival."

He walked away.

Jiang Chen stood in the alley, surrounded by the smell of smoke and ozone, looking at the empty body of the woman who'd promised him answers.

Three days.

He had three days to decide.

Join the man who killed him, or fight the system that framed him.

Join the Warden, or die again.

He looked at his staff. The wood was cool now. Calm. Waiting.

*The staff chooses the reaper.*

But who chose the staff's master?

He picked up the woman's body—light, so light, like carrying a hollow shell—and walked toward the nearest crossing point. He had to get back to Diyu. He had to report. He had to tell Old Man Zhao what happened.

He had to figure out who he could trust.

Because if the Warden was telling the truth, then the conspiracy went higher than he'd imagined. It reached into Diyu. It reached into the government. It reached into places he couldn't even see.

And if the Warden was lying?

Then Jiang Chen was alone. No allies. No answers. No justice.

Just a staff, a badge, and three days to decide his fate.

He stepped through the crossing point, and the mortal world vanished.

Diyu stretched before him—endless, gray, silent. The souls waiting in line. The reapers going about their rounds. The judges sitting in their courts, dispensing justice that was supposed to be absolute.

But nothing was absolute.

Not death.

Not justice.

Not truth.

Jiang Chen walked toward the Hall of Judgment, carrying the empty body of a woman he'd killed, carrying the weight of a secret he'd barely uncovered, carrying the name of a man who'd offered him a deal he couldn't refuse.

Three days.

He had three days to find the truth.

Or die trying.

title: Chapter 20
content:
CHAPTER 20: The Warden's Invitation

The black light from Jiang Chen's staff sent Lin Mei hurtling backward. She crashed through a stack of cardboard boxes, scattering old clothes and yellowed newspapers across the storage unit's concrete floor. Her stolen body lay motionless, but her soul rose from it like smoke, twisted and furious.

"You fool," she snarled. "You absolute fool."

Jiang Chen kept the staff pointed at her. His hands were steady now. The revelation about his death still burned in his chest, but he couldn't let her see it. Couldn't let her use it.

"You tried to possess me," he said. "Again."

"I tried to save you from yourself." She circled him, her translucent form flickering in the dim light. "The judges don't care about justice. They care about order. If you bring me back, they'll wipe me clean and throw me into reincarnation. Your truth dies with me."

"My truth isn't your concern."

"It should be." She stopped circling. "You were executed for a crime you didn't commit. The people responsible are still walking free. They're probably eating dinner right now, sleeping in their beds, living their lives while you rot in a job that isn't even really living."

Jiang Chen's jaw tightened. "I said—"

"I know what you said." She held up her hands. "I'm not going to try again. You're too strong now. The staff protects you. I can't possess you unless you let me."

"And I won't."

"No. You won't." She smiled, but it was bitter. "That's your problem, Jiang Chen. You're too careful. Too controlled. That's why they picked you. They knew you'd go quietly."

The words hit harder than they should have. Because she was right. He had gone quietly. When the police came, he'd put his hands behind his back. When the judge read the verdict, he'd bowed his head. When they strapped him to the table, he'd closed his eyes and accepted it.

He'd never once fought back.

"Even now," Lin Mei continued, "you're following orders. The judges said bring me back, so you're bringing me back. You haven't asked why. You haven't questioned anything."

"I've been a soul reaper for three weeks."

"Long enough to know better."

He wanted to argue, but the words died in his throat. Because she was right about that too. Three weeks of hunting souls, and he'd never once asked where they went. Never questioned the system he was serving. He'd just done his job, collected his souls, reported to his handler.

He'd been a good little cog in the machine.

Just like when he was alive.

"I need to think," he said finally.

"Think fast. The longer I'm out here, the more my soul degrades. Another few hours and I won't have anything left to tell the judges anyway."

Jiang Chen looked at her. Really looked. Her soul was thinning at the edges, translucent in a way that living souls weren't. She was telling the truth about that much. Possession drained the soul. The longer she stayed in the mortal realm, the more she faded.

"So what do you want from me?"

"Time." She stepped closer, slowly, carefully. "Give me time to prove what I know. Let me show you the evidence."

"Where is this evidence?"

"Inside my head. Inside the heads of everyone I've possessed." She tapped her temple. "I remember everything, Jiang Chen. Every face, every name, every whispered secret. Your case isn't the only dirty one I've seen."

"You're asking me to trust a con woman."

"I'm asking you to trust a woman who has nothing left to lose." She held his gaze. "I'm dead. My body is a borrowed shell that's already starting to reject me. The only thing I have left is what I know. And I'm offering to share it with you."

Jiang Chen's grip on the staff loosened. Just slightly. "How do I know you won't try to possess me again?"

"You don't. But I won't. Not because I've suddenly grown a conscience, but because you'd kill me before I got inside. And I'm not ready to die for real."

He believed her. That was the worst part. He actually believed her.

"Fine," he said. "You have one hour. Show me what you know."

Lin Mei's smile was genuine this time. "Follow me."

She drifted toward the storage unit's roll-up door, passing through it like smoke through a screen. Jiang Chen hesitated, then followed. Outside, the night air was cold and wet. Rain had started falling, a thin drizzle that beaded on his jacket.

Lin Mei was already moving down the alley, her soul barely visible in the streetlight.

"Where are we going?"

"To see an old friend." She glanced back at him. "Well. An old enemy. But he has information you'll want."

"Who?"

"A man named Chen Wei. He used to work for the police department. Internal affairs." She paused. "He was investigating your case before someone shut him down."

Jiang Chen's heart stopped. "What?"

"He found discrepancies. The evidence chain was broken. The witness testimony was coached. He wanted to reopen the investigation." She shook her head. "They transferred him to records management. Buried him in a basement with filing cabinets and mold."

"How do you know this?"

"Because I possessed his supervisor." She grinned. "I saw the file. Chen Wei wrote a forty-page report detailing every inconsistency in your case. It's still sitting in a locked drawer in the basement of the police headquarters."

Jiang Chen's mind raced. Forty pages. Someone had written forty pages about his case. Someone had cared enough to dig.

"Can you take me to him?"

"I can take you to the file. Chen Wei himself is harder to reach. He's under surveillance. They watch his phone, his computer, his mail. They even have someone following him."

"Then how do we get to him?"

"We don't." Lin Mei stopped at the edge of the alley. "But I know someone who can."

She pointed across the street. A small tea shop sat between a convenience store and a laundromat. Its lights were off, but a faint glow came from the back room.

"Old Zhang," she said. "He runs the shop. Used to be a cop too. He and Chen Wei go way back."

"How do you know he'll help us?"

"Because I know what he did during the riots. And I know he'd do anything to keep it secret."

Jiang Chen looked at her. "You really are a monster."

"I'm practical." She shrugged. "There's a difference."

They crossed the street. The rain had picked up, soaking through Jiang Chen's jacket. He knocked on the tea shop's door. No answer. He knocked again, harder.

A light flicked on. Footsteps approached. The door cracked open, revealing an old man with a weathered face and suspicious eyes.

"We're closed."

"Old Zhang." Lin Mei stepped forward, letting him see her ghostly form. "I need to talk to you about Chen Wei."

The old man's face went pale. He tried to slam the door, but Jiang Chen caught it.

"Please," Jiang Chen said. "We just need information."

"I don't know anything."

"You know about the file," Lin Mei said. "The forty-page report. The fabricated evidence. The murdered accountant who was supposed to be a murderer."

Old Zhang's eyes darted between them. "Who are you?"

"I'm the accountant," Jiang Chen said quietly. "I'm the one they executed."

The old man stared at him for a long moment. Then he stepped back, opening the door.

"Get inside. Both of you."

The tea shop was small and cramped, filled with wooden shelves and ceramic pots. Old Zhang led them to the back room, where a single lamp cast yellow light over a worn desk.

"Sit."

They sat. Old Zhang poured himself a cup of tea, but didn't offer them any.

"That file," he said finally. "It's real. Chen Wei brought it to me before they buried him in records. He was scared. Said someone high up was involved."

"How high?" Jiang Chen asked.

"High enough to order a execution." Old Zhang took a sip of tea. "I told him to let it go. For his own safety."

"But he didn't," Lin Mei said.

"No. He kept digging." Old Zhang set down his cup. "Three months ago, he found something. A name. He wouldn't tell me who. Just said it was someone connected to the underworld."

"The underworld?" Jiang Chen frowned. "Like... criminals?"

"No." Old Zhang's eyes were dark. "Like Diyu."

The word hit Jiang Chen like a physical blow. "What?"

"Someone in Diyu was involved in your death. A judge, maybe. Or someone with access to the records." Old Zhang leaned forward. "Chen Wei said your execution wasn't just a mistake. It was a message."

"A message for who?"

"For anyone who gets too close to the truth." Old Zhang's voice dropped to a whisper. "He said the Ten Judges aren't just keeping order. They're hiding something. And they'll kill to keep it hidden."

Jiang Chen's mind reeled. A judge. Someone in Diyu had orchestrated his execution. Someone who wanted him dead before he could find... what?

"What did Chen Wei find?" he demanded.

"I don't know. He was supposed to meet me the night he disappeared."

"Disappeared?"

Old Zhang nodded slowly. "He didn't show up. I went to his apartment. It was clean. Too clean. Like someone had wiped it down."

"When was this?"

"Two months ago. He hasn't been seen since."

Lin Mei let out a low whistle. "They got to him."

"Or he's still alive, hiding somewhere." Old Zhang met Jiang Chen's eyes. "He knew too much. If he's smart, he's lying low."

"Where would he go?"

"I don't know. But I know who might."

Old Zhang reached into his pocket and pulled out a worn photograph. It showed two men in police uniforms, arms around each other's shoulders. One was Chen Wei. The other was a younger Old Zhang.

"Chen Wei had a sister. She lives in the countryside, about three hours from here. He used to talk about visiting her when things got bad."

"Does she know where he is?"

"I don't know. But it's the only lead I have."

Jiang Chen took the photograph. The sister's face was partially visible in the background. A woman with kind eyes and a worried expression.

"Thank you," he said.

"Don't thank me yet." Old Zhang stood. "If you find him, tell him Old Zhang says hello. And tell him to stop being a hero. Heroes don't live long in this world."

They left the tea shop. The rain had stopped, leaving the streets slick and shimmering in the streetlight.

Lin Mei floated beside him, her soul brighter now. More solid.

"You believe him?" she asked.

"I don't know." Jiang Chen looked at the photograph. "But I'm going to find out."

"Good." She smiled. "Then we're going to the countryside."

"We?"

"You need me. I know what questions to ask. I know how to read people." She tilted her head. "And I know you're not going to let me out of your sight anyway."

She was right. He wasn't.

"Fine. But if you try anything—"

"You'll kill me. I know." She rolled her eyes. "You're very predictable, Soul Reaper."

They walked in silence for a moment. Then Jiang Chen stopped.

"Lin Mei."

"What

title: Chapter 6
content:
CHAPTER 6: The Price of Names

The black light consumed her scream.

Jiang Chen pulled the staff free and stumbled backward, gasping. His head throbbed where she'd been digging, her claws still phantom-pain inside his skull. The woman's soul lay crumpled on the ground, wisps of black smoke curling off her form like evaporating blood.

She was still alive. Still conscious. He could see her eyes tracking him, calculating even now.

"You're making a mistake," she said. Her voice was weak but steady. "You'll never find them without me."

"I don't need to find them tonight." He raised the staff again. "Right now, I need to get you back to Diyu before you kill someone else."

"I haven't killed anyone in this body."

"No. You just take them over. Steal their lives. Move on when they're used up." He walked closer. "What happens to the bodies after you leave them? Do they wake up? Do they remember?"

She didn't answer. That was answer enough.

"Stand up," he said.

She stood. Slowly. Her eyes never left his staff.

"You're different from the other reapers," she said. "They never asked questions. They just dragged me back. You want to know things."

"I'm three weeks old. I don't know anything."

"You know you were framed."

He felt the words like a knife. "Shut up."

"You know your death was a setup. You know someone wanted you dead. You know the system failed you." She took a step closer. "And I can tell you who."

"I said shut up."

"I'll tell you one name. Free. Just to prove I'm serious."

Jiang Chen stopped walking. His grip on the staff tightened until his knuckles went white.

"One name," she repeated. "Then you decide whether to take me to Diyu or let me go in exchange for the rest."

"One name means nothing if I can't prove it."

"Then let me prove it." She spread her hands. "Take me somewhere. Let me show you. There's a man in the city. He keeps records. Old-fashioned paper records, not digital. He has proof of everything—judge bribes, fabricated evidence, false testimonies. If your case is in there, he'll have it."

"And you know this man?"

"I've been in his mind." She smiled. "Three years ago. He never knew. I took a secretary at his firm, read every file in his office over six months. He has connections in every courthouse in the province."

Jiang Chen's mind raced. It could be a trap. Everything about this woman was a trap. But if she was telling the truth—

"If I take you to this man, and he doesn't have anything—"

"Then you drag me back to Diyu and I never bother you again."

"You're already going to Diyu. The only question is whether you walk there willingly or get dragged there unconscious."

She laughed. "You're learning. I like that." She pointed at his staff. "But you won't use that on me again. You don't have enough spiritual energy. I can see it. You're running on fumes."

She was right. He'd felt it when he hit her—the staff had drained him more than he expected. His vision was slightly blurry at the edges. His hands shook.

"How long until I recover?" he asked.

"With proper rest? A few hours. Without it?" She shrugged. "You'll be useless for combat for the rest of the night."

"Then I guess I'd better not need combat."

He grabbed her arm. She didn't resist.

"Where's this man?"

---

The office was on the fifteenth floor of a building that had seen better decades. The elevator smelled like cigarettes and despair. The carpet was the color of dried blood.

Jiang Chen stood in front of the door while the woman—she'd given her name as Lin Mei, though he suspected that was as fake as everything else about her—lingered behind him.

"He's still here," she said. "Working late. He always works late."

"How do you know?"

"I can feel him. His soul is loud. Anxious." She tapped her temple. "Being inside people teaches you to read them from outside too."

Jiang Chen pushed the door open.

The man inside looked up, startled. He was in his sixties, bald, wearing glasses that had slid halfway down his nose. His desk was covered in papers—stacks of them, organized in a system only he understood.

"Who are you?" he demanded. "This is a private office. You can't just—"

Jiang Chen raised his staff. The man's eyes went wide.

"What is that?"

"You're going to answer some questions," Jiang Chen said. "About a man named Jiang Chen. Executed three weeks ago for murder."

The man's face went pale. "I don't know what you're talking about."

"You keep records. Paper records. Bribes, fabricated evidence, false testimonies." Lin Mei stepped forward. "I've seen them. Don't lie to me."

The man stared at her. Recognition flickered in his eyes. "You. You're the one who was in my secretary's head. I remember. She quit afterward. Said she had nightmares for months."

"That's me." She smiled. "And now I'm back."

"What do you want?"

"The truth about Jiang Chen's execution." Jiang Chen stepped closer. "Tell me who paid to have me killed."

"You're dead," the man whispered. "You can't be alive."

"I'm not alive. I'm a soul reaper. And I need answers before I drag this woman back to hell."

The man looked between them. Sweat beaded on his forehead. His hands trembled as he reached for a file cabinet.

"I don't keep files on everyone," he said. "Only the ones that make me money. If your case was profitable, I'll have it."

He pulled open a drawer. Flipped through folders. His fingers stopped on one.

"Jiang Chen," he read. "Executed November 12th. Case number—" He stopped. "This can't be right."

"What?"

"The payment. It's not from a person. It's from a company." He pulled out a sheet of paper. "Longxing Financial Group. They paid three hundred thousand yuan to the judge, another hundred thousand to the prosecutor, and fifty thousand to the lead investigator."

Jiang Chen felt the world tilt.

Longxing Financial Group. He knew that name. He'd worked for them. As an accountant. For three years before his arrest.

"That's impossible," he said. "I was a nobody. I processed invoices. I didn't know anything important."

"You must have known something," Lin Mei said. "Someone important didn't want you talking."

"I didn't have anything to talk about! I caught a few discrepancies in the books, but I reported them. I did everything by the book."

The man laughed. A dry, raspy sound. "You reported discrepancies at Longxing? That's like reporting a tiger for being hungry. They don't fix the problem—they eat the reporter."

Jiang Chen grabbed the paper. The names were there. Judge Wang. Prosecutor Li. Investigator Zhang. All paid. All bought.

"What do I do with this?"

"Nothing," the man said. "That paper proves nothing. It's just a record. The real evidence was destroyed when you were executed."

"There has to be something else."

"There isn't." The man's eyes flickered to Lin Mei. "But she might have something. She's been in hundreds of minds. She might have seen something useful."

Jiang Chen turned to her.

"I've been in government officials," she said slowly. "I've seen things. But I need time to remember. Time to sort through seventeen years of memories."

"How much time?"

"A few days."

"I don't have a few days. I have until dawn, and then I have to go back to Diyu and pretend everything is normal."

"Then take me with you."

"No."

"I'm useful. I know things. I can help you find the truth."

"You're a murderer."

"Your own hands aren't clean." She looked at the staff. "You've killed too. Two times tonight. I felt their souls when you sent them on. You're not innocent."

"I never said I was."

"Then stop pretending you're better than me." She stepped closer. "We're both dead. We both want something. Let's get it together."

Jiang Chen looked at the paper in his hands. Longxing Financial Group. Three hundred thousand yuan. Judge Wang.

He thought about his execution. The needle. The cold table. The witnesses who watched him die without a flicker of emotion.

He thought about the people who put him there. The judge who read the sentence. The prosecutor who argued for death. The investigator who found the evidence.

All paid.

All bought.

"I need to think," he said.

"Dawn is in four hours," Lin Mei said. "You don't have time to think."

"Then I'll make time."

He grabbed her arm and dragged her out of the office. The man watched them go, already reaching for his phone to call someone.

They had maybe ten minutes before the police showed up.

Jiang Chen pulled Lin Mei into the stairwell. They ran down fifteen flights. By the time they hit the street, his legs were burning and his spiritual energy was almost gone.

"Where now?" she asked.

"Somewhere safe."

"There's nowhere safe for you. You're dead. You're a ghost. You don't belong anywhere."

"Then I'll make somewhere safe."

He led her through alleys, over walls, into the basement of a building that had been condemned for years. The air smelled like mold and rust. Water dripped somewhere in the darkness.

"This is your safe place?" she asked.

"It's quiet."

"It's disgusting."

"It's temporary."

He sat down against the wall. The staff rested across his knees. His head was pounding.

"You look terrible," she said.

"I feel terrible."

"You used too much energy. You need to rest."

"I know."

"Then rest. I'll watch."

"Like I trust you."

"You don't have a choice." She sat down across from him. "If I wanted to kill you, I'd have done it already."

"You tried to possess me."

"You stopped me. And now I need you alive."

"Why?"

She was quiet for a long moment. "Because you're the first person in years who's treated me like a person instead of a problem to be solved."

Jiang Chen stared at her.

"That's not a good reason."

"It's the only one I have."

He closed his eyes. The darkness was thick and heavy and warm.

He thought about Longxing Financial Group. He thought about the discrepancies he'd found. The ones he'd reported. The ones no one had done anything about.

He thought about the question that had been eating at him since his arrest: why him? Why frame a nobody accountant for murder when there were so many other, easier targets?

The answer was simple.

He hadn't been the target.

He'd been the message.

Someone at Longxing wanted to send a signal to every other accountant in the company: keep your mouth shut, or die.

And he'd been the example.

The realization hit him like a physical blow.

"They killed me to scare everyone else," he whispered.

"What?"

"I wasn't the target. I was the warning." He opened his eyes. "They didn't need me dead. They needed everyone else to see me dead."

Lin Mei's expression shifted. Something almost like sympathy flickered across her face.

"That's worse than being the target," she said. "Being the target means you mattered. Being the warning means you were disposable."

"I know."

"What are you

title: Chapter 7
content:
CHAPTER 7: The Weight of Truth

The black light consumed her scream.

Jiang Chen felt her soul fracture against the staff's power—not destroy, but crack. The con woman's spirit splintered into a thousand pieces, each one a fragment of memory, of lie, of truth. He watched them scatter like startled birds, each piece carrying a different part of her.

*No.*

He swung the staff again, but the damage was done. She'd been holding herself together for seventeen years—possessing bodies, stealing lives, wearing identities like clothes. And now, with one desperate attack, he'd shattered her into pieces that might never reform.

The fragments swirled around him. He saw flashes:

*A courtroom. A judge's gavel. A different face—younger, terrified.*

*A ledger. Numbers. Names. His own signature on a document he'd never signed.*

*A woman's voice: "Do it clean. No witnesses."*

*A handshake in a dark room. Money changing ownership. His death warrant being stamped before trial.*

The last fragment coalesced in front of him. Not the con woman's face—the face she'd worn when she was alive. Seventeen years younger. Twenty-three. A student at a university, studying law, until someone had killed her for what she'd seen.

"I'm sorry," she whispered, and the voice was different now. Softer. Real. "I was trying to survive."

Jiang Chen's hands trembled. "You were a student."

"I was a witness." Her ghostly form flickered. "I saw your trial. I was in the gallery. I saw the judge take the envelope. I saw the prosecutor hand over the forged evidence. I took pictures."

"Then how—"

"They caught me leaving the courthouse." She laughed, but it was hollow. "Three men. A van. A quiet disposal. I woke up in the river three days later, and my body was already cold. I've been running ever since."

The staff felt heavy in his hands. He'd been ready to condemn her. To drag her back to Diyu and let the judges erase everything she was. But she wasn't a monster. She was a victim. Just like him.

"Why didn't you tell me?"

"Would you have believed me?" Her form wavered. "You were ready to kill me, Jiang Chen. You had your staff raised. You had your orders. The judges told you I was dangerous, and you believed them because it was easier than asking questions."

He wanted to argue. He wanted to say she was wrong. But she wasn't.

The fragments were drifting apart, losing cohesion. He could see her fading—not dying, but dissolving. Scattering into the ether like smoke in wind.

"Stop," he said. "Come back together."

"I can't." Her voice was barely a whisper. "The staff shattered my bindings. I'm dispersing."

"Then let me—"

"There's no time." She reached out, and her hand passed through his. "Listen. The names. I stored them in the ledger. The real one. Not the copy the judges have."

"Where is it?"

"In the safe of the man who killed me." Her form flickered. "Judge Zhao. He's one of the Diyu judges. He's been feeding information to the mortal world for decades. He's the one who sentenced you."

Jiang Chen's blood ran cold. Judge Zhao. He remembered the face—the ancient, weathered judge who'd processed his soul when he'd first arrived in Diyu. The one who'd assigned him to be a soul reaper. The one who'd told him he could earn redemption.

"The judges serve justice," Jiang Chen said, but the words tasted like ash.

"The judges serve order," she corrected. "Justice is optional. Zhao has been selling verdicts for centuries. Your trial? Mine? We're not the first. We won't be the last."

Another fragment drifted away. She was losing form faster now.

"Find the ledger," she said. "It's in Zhao's personal vault. The one hidden beneath his chambers in Diyu. The one that doesn't exist on any map."

"How do I get in?"

"You'll find a way." She smiled, and it was the first genuine smile he'd seen on her face. "You're a soul reaper. You can walk through walls. You can break chains. You can do what no dead soul can do—move between worlds."

"I'm not—"

"You're the only one who can." Her form began to glow, not with power, but with release. "I held on for seventeen years, Jiang Chen. I possessed bodies I hated. I stole lives I didn't want. I became a monster because I thought if I survived long enough, I could expose the truth."

"And now?"

"Now I'm tired." She closed her eyes. "I'm going to scatter. My memories will fade. My consciousness will dissolve. But the truth won't. It's in the ledger. It's in Zhao's vault. It's waiting for you."

"Wait—"

"One more thing." Her eyes opened, and they were clear. "The man who ordered your death. The one who paid Judge Zhao. He's still alive. He's still powerful. He's still watching."

"Who?"

She opened her mouth to speak, but her form shattered before the words could come.

The fragments scattered like ash in a hurricane, spinning outward until they were nothing but dust, then nothing at all.

Jiang Chen stood alone in the empty warehouse, staff in hand, the weight of seventeen years of secrets pressing down on him.

---

The journey back to Diyu was different.

Jiang Chen had walked the path between worlds before—a grey corridor that connected the mortal realm to the Chinese underworld. It was always cold, always silent, always empty. But tonight, every shadow seemed to move. Every whisper seemed to carry a name.

He reached the gates of Diyu to find the city of the dead unchanged. Spirits drifted through the streets, waiting for judgment. Lesser judges sat at their posts, processing souls with mechanical efficiency. The air smelled of incense and regret.

Judge Ma was waiting for him.

"You returned," she said, and her voice was flat. "Alone."

"The con woman is dead. Scattered. Her soul won't trouble the mortal realm again."

Ma studied him with ancient eyes. "And the ledger?"

"What ledger?"

"The ledger she kept. The one with the names of everyone she'd conned, every family she'd destroyed."

Jiang Chen's heart skipped. She knew about the ledger. But which one? The mortal one? Or the one hidden in Zhao's vault?

"I didn't find one," he said carefully. "She had nothing on her when I engaged."

Ma's eyes narrowed. "Strange. She was known for keeping records."

"Maybe she destroyed them before I caught her."

"Maybe." Ma didn't sound convinced. "The judges will want a full report. Report to the Hall of Judgment at dawn."

She turned and walked away, her robes trailing behind her like smoke.

Jiang Chen waited until she was out of sight, then moved in the opposite direction. He'd been in Diyu for three weeks. He'd learned the layout. He'd learned the patrols. He'd learned where the judges slept and where they held court.

And now, he needed to find Judge Zhao's chambers.

---

The vault was exactly where the con woman said it would be.

Beneath Judge Zhao's chambers, hidden behind a wall that didn't exist on any map, was a door made of shadow and bone. Jiang Chen pressed his hand against it, and the door recognized him—not as a soul reaper, but as a dead soul. It opened.

Inside, the vault was filled with ledgers.

Hundreds of them. Thousands. Stacked on shelves that stretched into darkness. Each one labeled with a year, a case number, a verdict.

Jiang Chen found his ledger quickly. It was recent. It was near the front. It was labeled with his name and execution date.

He opened it.

The first page was his arrest report. The second page was the evidence against him—photographs, testimonies, bank records. All of it was fake. He could see the forgeries now. The signatures that didn't match. The dates that were wrong. The witnesses who'd never seen his face.

The third page was the payment.

*Received: 500,000 RMB*
*From: [REDACTED]*
*For: Verdict of Guilty, Execution Order 4721-B*

The name was redacted. But the handwriting was familiar. He'd seen it before. On documents he'd signed. On reports he'd filed. On a promotion letter he'd received three years ago.

His boss. The man who'd mentored him. The man who'd taught him how to cook the books, how to look the other way, how to make problems disappear.

Zhang Wei.

The name hit him like a physical blow. His boss had been the one to frame him. His boss had paid the judge. His boss had watched him die.

But why?

He flipped through the ledger, searching for more entries. He found them. Dozens of them. Hundreds. Each one a life sold to the highest bidder. Each one a verdict bought and paid for. Each one signed by Judge Zhao, with the buyer's name carefully recorded.

And at the bottom of each entry, a note in Zhao's handwriting:

*Delivered to Tribunal Leader Chen for approval.*

Tribunal Leader Chen. The head of the Diyu justice system. The ultimate authority on who lived and who died. The ancient, immortal being who sat at the top of the underworld's hierarchy.

Jiang Chen's hands started shaking.

The con woman wasn't lying. The corruption went all the way to the top. The entire system was rotten. Every verdict. Every judgment. Every execution.

He was a cog in a machine that killed innocent people for profit.

The door behind him creaked.

He turned, staff raised, to find Judge Ma standing in the doorway. Her face was unreadable, but her eyes were cold.

"I thought you might come here," she said. "I was hoping you wouldn't."

"Did you know?"

"Know what?"

"That the system is corrupt. That Zhao has been selling verdicts. That the Tribunal Leader approves the payments."

Ma was silent for a long moment.

"I suspected," she said finally. "But I couldn't prove it. And I couldn't act without proof."

"I have proof now." Jiang Chen held up the ledger. "These are the records. Every transaction. Every buyer. Every victim."

Ma looked at the ledger, then at him.

"Do you know what happens to soul reapers who expose the truth?" she asked.

"They get justice."

"They get erased." She stepped forward. "The Tribunal Leader controls the records. Controls the memories. Controls the very fabric of Diyu. If you try to expose him, he'll simply rewrite history. Your existence will be removed. Your case will be destroyed. Your soul will be scattered across the void."

"Then what do I do?"

Ma reached into her robes and pulled out a small, jade pendant. It glowed with inner light.

"You take this," she said. "And you run."

"What is it?"

"A key. To the mortal realm. But not the mortal realm you know. A different one. A hidden one. A place where the Tribunal Leader's power cannot reach."

"Why are you helping me?"

"Because I was like you once." Ma's voice cracked. "Before I became a judge, I was a soul reaper. I found the truth. I tried to expose it. And they turned me into what I am now." She pressed the pendant into his hand. "I've been waiting three centuries for someone else to find the truth. Someone who could finish what I started."

Jiang Chen looked at the pendant. It

title: Chapter 8
content:
CHAPTER 8: The Warden's Invitation

The scream tore through the alley like a wounded animal.

Luo Su's soul ripped backward out of Jiang Chen's mind, her form flickering and distorting as the staff's black light pushed her away. She hit the wall hard, leaving a crack in the brick where her spectral body made contact.

"You—" She was gasping, though ghosts didn't need to breathe. Habit. "You could have killed me."

"You tried to possess me again."

"I was testing you." She straightened, her eyes burning with something between anger and respect. "You passed. Most Soul Reapers would have lost control the second I touched their death. You held it together long enough to fight back."

Jiang Chen didn't lower the staff. "Stop testing me."

"Then stop being predictable." She brushed off her dress, though there was nothing to brush. "You were going to take me back to Diyu and let them wipe me. Don't deny it. I saw it in your thoughts before you blocked me."

"I changed my mind."

"Liar."

"I changed my mind when you mentioned my death." He lowered the staff slightly, but kept it ready. "You said you could prove it wasn't legal. I want to know how."

Luo Su's smile returned, but it was different now. Less predatory. More calculating. "I've been possessing people for seventeen years. Do you know how many corrupt officials I've inhabited? How many judges, lawyers, police commissioners? I've seen their files. I've sat in their meetings. I've read their secret documents."

"And you remember all of it?"

"When you possess someone, you don't just borrow their body. You borrow their memories. It's like reading a book while wearing their skin." She tapped her temple. "I've read thousands of books. Yours happened to be interesting."

Jiang Chen felt cold. "You saw everything. My trial. My conviction. My execution."

"I saw the real file too. The one they deleted from the system. The one that shows who actually committed the murder you were executed for."

"Who?"

Luo Su held up a finger. "Ah-ah. I told you. Take me to Diyu first."

"You'll just escape the moment we cross the veil."

"I can't escape Diyu. I'm already dead." She gestured at her translucent form. "The only reason I've been running is because I don't want to face judgment. But if I give them information about a corrupt judge, maybe they'll be lenient. Maybe I'll get a lighter sentence."

"Or maybe you'll get possession privileges."

She laughed. "You learn fast, Soul Reaper. Yes. That's the deal. I give you the names, you vouch for me, I get a reduced sentence and the ability to possess bodies for official business. It's a win-win."

Jiang Chen studied her. She was good. Every word was calculated, every expression measured. He couldn't tell if she was telling the truth or weaving another elaborate lie.

But she had seen his death. She knew details no one else could know. The needle. The cold table. The witnesses.

"What guarantee do I have that you'll tell the truth once we're in Diyu?"

"I'll let you bind me."

He blinked. "What?"

"A soul binding. I've heard of them. You can attach a piece of your soul to mine. If I lie, you'll know. If I try to escape, you can pull me back." She stepped closer. "It's dangerous for you. If I damage the binding, it damages your soul. But it's the only way you'll trust me."

"How do you know about soul bindings?"

"I told you. Seventeen years of possessing people. I've possessed two Soul Reapers before you. They both knew the technique. I didn't get a chance to use it before you killed them, but I remember the theory."

Jiang Chen's grip tightened on the staff. "You want me to bind my soul to a convicted murderer and fraudster."

"I want you to trust me enough to get what we both want." She extended her hand again. "You want justice for your death. I want leniency for my crimes. We can help each other."

"You tried to possess me three times in the last hour."

"I'm persistent. It's one of my charms."

He almost laughed. Almost. The situation was absurd. He was a dead man who worked for the Chinese underworld, negotiating a deal with a ghost who'd killed his predecessors, all because she claimed to know who framed him for murder.

The night was quiet. The city hummed in the distance. Somewhere, a dog barked. Normal life continued for everyone else while he stood in an alley, holding a weapon made of pure darkness, talking to a woman who wasn't there.

"If I do this binding, I need you to promise me something."

"Anything."

"Stop trying to possess me."

She smiled. "Deal."

"Stop smiling like that."

"I can't promise that."

He sighed. "How do I do the binding?"

Luo Su's expression shifted to something almost serious. "You need to touch my soul directly. No staff. No barriers. Just your hand against mine. Then you focus on a thread of your soul—the part that feels most like you—and you push it into me. I do the same. The threads twist together, and we're bound."

"That sounds intimate."

"It is." Her voice softened. "That's why most Soul Reapers don't do it with strangers. It creates a connection. You'll feel what I feel. I'll feel what you feel. It's dangerous."

Jiang Chen considered his options. He could drag her back to Diyu by force. The judges would extract the information from her memories, but they might miss details. Or they might decide she was too dangerous to keep alive and erase her entirely.

Or he could bind himself to her and trust that she wanted leniency more than freedom.

"You tried to kill me," he said.

"Twice. And you killed me twice." She shrugged. "We're even."

"You're impossible."

"I've been told."

He stepped forward and extended his hand. "If you betray me, I'll find a way to make your afterlife worse than anything the judges could imagine."

"I don't doubt it."

Their hands touched. Her soul was cold, but not the cold of death—the cold of emptiness. She'd been a ghost for seventeen years. She'd lost pieces of herself along the way.

Jiang Chen closed his eyes and searched for the thread of his soul that felt most like him. It was easy to find. It was the part that still remembered being alive. The part that still felt anger at his death. The part that still wanted justice.

He pushed it forward.

Luo Su gasped.

Her soul wrapped around his, and he felt her for the first time—really felt her. Not the con woman, not the murderer, not the fraudster. The woman underneath. The one who'd been a journalist before she died. The one who'd discovered a corruption ring in her city and been killed for it. The one who'd been framed as a fraudster and executed, just like him.

*You were framed too.*

Her thought slipped into his mind.

*I was. And I've been looking for the people who killed me ever since.*

*Why didn't you tell me?*

*Because I don't trust anyone. And neither should you.*

The binding snapped into place. He felt her presence in the back of his mind, a constant humming awareness. She felt him too. He could sense her surprise, her fear, her hope—all carefully hidden beneath layers of deception.

"Done," she whispered.

Jiang Chen pulled his hand back. The connection remained. "What now?"

"Now we go to Diyu." She looked at him strangely. "But there's something you should know first."

"What?"

"Your death wasn't just framed. It was ordered." She paused. "By someone inside Diyu."

The words hit him like a physical blow. "What?"

"One of the judges. I don't know which one. But the order came from the underworld. Someone wanted you dead before you could testify about something."

"Testify about what?"

"I don't know. The file was redacted. But it mentioned something about a ledger. A debt collector's ledger."

Jiang Chen's blood ran cold. The ledger. The one he'd been investigating before his arrest. The one that contained the names of every official in the city who'd taken bribes from the underworld.

He'd never told anyone about it. He'd hidden it before his arrest.

*Where did you hide it?*

Luo Su's voice in his mind.

*In my apartment. Behind the loose tile in the bathroom.*

*Then we need to get it before the judges find out you have it.*

A new voice cut through the night. Deep. Resonant. Coming from everywhere at once.

"Too late for that."

The air split open. A figure stepped through the tear—tall, robed in black, face hidden beneath a hood. In one hand, he held a ledger. The same ledger Jiang Chen had hidden.

"Hello, apprentice." The figure's voice was familiar. "I see you've made a new friend."

Judge Liu.

The judge who'd recruited him. The judge who'd trained him. The judge who'd sent him on his first mission.

And the judge who'd probably ordered his death.

Jiang Chen raised his staff. "You."

"Me." Liu's voice was calm. "I was wondering when you'd find out. I had hoped it would be later. Much later."

Luo Su moved beside him. "You knew all along."

"Of course. I chose him specifically." Liu held up the ledger. "This was always going to lead back to me. I just needed time to find it before he did."

Jiang Chen's hands shook. "You killed me."

"Technically, the executioner killed you. I just arranged for the charges to stick." Liu's voice hardened. "You were getting too close. You found the ledger. You started asking questions. I couldn't have that."

"So everything. The recruitment. The training. The first mission. All of it was to get me away from the truth."

"Partly." Liu stepped closer. "But also because you were good. You are good. I genuinely wanted you as my apprentice. I just needed to make sure you didn't expose me before I was ready."

Luo Su laughed. "And now? What's your plan? Kill us both?"

"Nothing so dramatic." Liu opened the ledger. "I'm going to offer you a choice. Join me. Work for me. Help me expand my operation across all of Diyu. Or die. Really die. The kind of death that erases your soul from existence."

Jiang Chen gripped his staff. "I choose option three."

"Which is?"

He lunged forward, swinging the staff at Liu's head. The judge sidestepped easily and grabbed the staff with one hand.

"You forget," Liu said. "I trained you. I know every move you're going to make."

He twisted. The staff flew from Jiang Chen's hands.

Luo Su attacked from behind, her soul claws extended. Liu didn't even turn. He flicked his wrist, and she was thrown backward into the wall.

"Seventeen years of possessing people," Liu said. "And you never learned to fight."

Jiang Chen scrambled for his staff. Liu stepped on it.

"Accept my offer, apprentice. It's the only way you survive."

"No."

"Then you die." Liu raised his hand. Black energy gathered around it. "It's a shame, really. You had potential."

The energy shot forward.

Luo Su dove in front of Jiang Chen.

It hit her square in the chest.

Her soul began to dissolve

title: The Warden's Secret
content:
The black light from Jiang Chen's staff sent Su Lin sprawling across the alley floor. She scrambled backward, her stolen face contorting between her own features and the dead woman's, like two masks fighting for dominance over the same skull.

"You think you're clever," she spat. "You think you're different from the others."

Jiang Chen advanced, the staff crackling with residual energy. "I'm not letting you possess me again."

"I don't need to possess you." She laughed, that horrible wet sound. "I already saw what I needed. The execution. The witnesses. The judge's face when he read the verdict."

"I warned you."

"You warned me." She stood, brushing ash from her stolen clothes. "But you didn't stop me. You wanted to know. You wanted to see if I was telling the truth. And now I know exactly what you know."

Jiang Chen's grip tightened on the staff. "What are you talking about?"

"The judge, Jiang Chen. You recognized his face when I showed you the memory. You've seen him before."

He hadn't. He'd tried to remember, tried to place the gray-haired man in the black robe who'd sentenced him to death with such casual indifference. But the memory was foggy, like looking through dirty glass.

"I don't—"

"You do." She smiled. "You just don't want to admit it. Judge Zhao. The same judge who presided over your friend's corruption case three years ago."

The world tilted.

Three years ago. Wang Lei. His boss. His mentor. The man who'd taught him everything about forensic accounting, who'd vouched for him when he was just a junior clerk, who'd taken the fall for a scandal that should have destroyed them both.

Wang Lei had been convicted of embezzlement. Sentenced to fifteen years. Died in prison six months later. Heart attack, they said.

Jiang Chen had cried at the funeral.

"That's not possible," he whispered.

"Isn't it?" Su Lin circled him like a predator. "You were Wang Lei's protégé. You knew where the bodies were buried. And when Wang Lei died, someone thought you might talk."

"I never knew anything."

"You knew enough to be dangerous. You knew the accounts. You knew the discrepancies. You knew who was paying who." She stopped, her eyes boring into his. "You just didn't know what you knew."

The staff felt heavy. Heavy as the truth pressing down on his chest.

"I'm going to take you to Diyu," he said, but his voice had lost its edge.

"Good. I want to go to Diyu." She stepped closer. "I want to stand before the judges and tell them everything. Every name. Every crime. Every soul I've possessed who knew something they shouldn't have."

"Why would you do that?"

"Because I'm tired." For a moment, her mask slipped, and he saw something raw underneath. "I've been running for seventeen years. Possessing body after body, stealing life after life. I'm exhausted, Jiang Chen. I want it to end."

"You want to be judged."

"I want to be seen." She touched her chest, where the staff had struck her. "I want someone to look at me and see more than a monster."

Jiang Chen stared at her. The con woman. The murderer. The thief of lives.

And he saw himself.

"We need to find a gate," he said.

---

The gate to Diyu wasn't a physical door. It was a crack in reality, a thin place where the veil between worlds was weak enough to tear. Master Fang had shown him three in the city: one in the subway tunnels beneath the financial district, one in the basement of a abandoned temple, and one in the morgue of the city hospital.

He chose the morgue.

"Why here?" Su Lin asked as they walked through the hospital's back corridors, passing nurses who didn't seem to notice them.

"The dead don't ask questions when souls pass through."

The morgue was cold and sterile. The attendant was asleep at his desk, a half-eaten bowl of noodles beside him. Jiang Chen led Su Lin past the body drawers, past the autopsy table, to a small supply closet in the back.

"There's nothing here," she said.

He pressed his palm against the back wall. The tiles rippled like water. A door materialized, black and ancient, covered in characters he couldn't read.

"They told me only the dead can open it."

"Then I suppose I qualify."

The door swung open, revealing a corridor of swirling darkness. The air changed—thicker, heavier, carrying the smell of incense and rot.

Su Lin stepped forward, but Jiang Chen grabbed her arm.

"If you're lying to me—"

"I'm not." She met his eyes. "I told you. I want this to end."

They walked into the darkness together.

---

The corridor stretched for what felt like hours. The walls were made of something that wasn't stone, wasn't flesh, wasn't anything Jiang Chen could name. Voices whispered from the shadows, calling out names he didn't recognize, telling secrets he didn't want to know.

Su Lin walked beside him, silent for once. Her stolen face had settled back into the young woman's features, but her eyes were different now. Ancient. Tired.

"How many bodies have you taken?" he asked.

"Seventeen. No, eighteen. I lost count."

"How many souls have you consumed?"

She was quiet for a long moment. "Does it matter?"

"It matters to the judges."

"Then let them count."

The corridor ended at a vast cavern. The sky—if you could call it that—was a churning mass of gray clouds, lit by flashes of crimson lightning. Below, a river of black water wound through a city of bone-white towers.

Diyu. The underworld.

And standing at the entrance, waiting for them, was Master Fang.

"Apprentice." His voice was flat. "You've returned."

"I caught her." Jiang Chen stepped forward. "The soul reaper killer. I brought her to face judgment."

Master Fang's eyes moved to Su Lin. Something passed between them—a recognition that made Jiang Chen's blood run cold.

"Have you now?" Master Fang said.

"I know what she did. I know she killed two of ours. But she has information. About my death. About Judge Zhao. About—"

"About Wang Lei." Master Fang finished the sentence.

Jiang Chen stopped. "You knew?"

"Of course I knew." Master Fang's voice was gentle now, which somehow made it worse. "I've known since the day you arrived. I was there when the judges reviewed your case. I saw the evidence."

"Why didn't you tell me?"

"Because you weren't ready." Master Fang stepped closer. "Because knowing the truth would have destroyed you before you were strong enough to carry it."

"I'm carrying it now."

"Are you?" Master Fang looked at Su Lin. "Tell him."

Su Lin's face went pale. "Master Fang—"

"Tell him what you told me when we first met. Fifteen years ago. When I caught you the first time."

Jiang Chen's heart stopped.

"Fifteen years—" He looked between them. "You know each other?"

Su Lin wouldn't meet his eyes. "He was my first reaper."

"Your first—" The pieces clicked into place, ugly and sharp. "You let her go."

"I did what I had to do." Master Fang's voice was steel. "She had information about a conspiracy that reached higher than any of us imagined. I made a deal with her. Freedom in exchange for names."

"And the reapers she killed?"

"Collateral damage." Master Fang said it like he was ordering tea. "I didn't know she would keep killing. I didn't know she would find the gate on her own. I didn't know—"

"You didn't know." Jiang Chen's hands were shaking. "You didn't know that the woman who murdered two of your own would murder more? You didn't know that a con woman would keep conning?"

"I made a mistake."
