"You made me her accomplice!" The words echoed across the cavern. "You sent me to capture her, knowing she had information about my death, knowing she would use it against me, knowing—"

"I gave you the chance to learn the truth."

"You used me!"

Master Fang's face hardened. "I trained you. I prepared you. I gave you a purpose when you had nothing but a death sentence and a lie." He stepped forward, close enough that Jiang Chen could smell the incense on his robes. "Do you want the truth, Apprentice? Then listen. Judge Zhao is dead."

The words landed like a punch.

"What?"

"Found in his chambers three days ago. His soul was missing. Someone had taken it before the reapers could collect it."

"Taken? By who?"

Master Fang's eyes flickered to Su Lin.

"No." Jiang Chen shook his head. "She's been with me. She couldn't have—"

"I didn't take it." Su Lin's voice was small. "But I know who did."

"Who?"

She looked at him, and for the first time, he saw real fear in her eyes. "The same people who framed you. The same people who killed Wang Lei. The same people who have been trading souls on the black market for decades."

"Trading souls?"

"Did you think Diyu was the only power in the underworld?" Master Fang's voice was bitter. "Did you think the judges controlled everything? There are factions, Jiang Chen. Groups that have been fighting for control of the soul trade for centuries. And they've been patient. They've been careful. And now they've taken a judge."

"Why?"

"Because Judge Zhao was the one who sentenced you." Su Lin's voice was barely a whisper. "And he knew who paid him. He knew the names. And when I tell the judges what I know, those names will come out."

"You said you would tell them."

"I will." She looked at Master Fang. "But not here. Not now. Because the moment I speak those names, everyone in this room becomes a target."

The cavern was silent except for the distant wail of lost souls.

And then, from behind them, a voice.

"I see you've returned, Master Fang."

They turned. A figure stood at the entrance of the corridor—tall, cloaked in shadows, carrying a staff of black jade.

"Third Judge." Master Fang bowed. "I was just bringing in the prisoner."

"Were you?" The Third Judge's eyes moved to Su Lin, then to Jiang Chen. "Interesting. Because I've been watching this conversation. And it sounds like my brother's killer is standing right in front of me."

"Your brother?"

"Judge Zhao." The Third Judge stepped forward, and the shadows seemed to cling to him like living things. "He was my brother. And someone in this room knows who killed him."

The staff in his hand began to glow.

title: The Name in the Ledger
content:
Jiang Chen's staff blazed with black light as Consort Wei's soul form shattered backward, her grip on his mind tearing away like ripped flesh. She hit the wall of the alley hard, phasing through brick and mortar, scattering into wisps of angry smoke before reforming twenty meters away, gasping.

"You broke my concentration," she spat, rubbing her chest where the staff had struck. "I was so close to seeing everything."

"You were close to getting erased." Jiang Chen's voice came out rougher than he intended. His hands were still shaking. The names. She'd promised him names. But she'd also tried to possess him again, and that meant she couldn't be trusted with anything except a binding circle and a one-way trip back to Diyu.

But she knew about his execution.

"Let's try this again," he said, advancing slowly. "You give me the names now, or I drag you back and let the judges rip them out of your soul manually. It hurts. I've seen it."

"You've seen nothing." She straightened her stolen dress, smoothing the fabric with theatrical dignity. "I've been possessing people since before you were born, boy. Do you think a little light show scares me?"

"I think you're stalling."

"I think you're desperate." She smiled, but there was something new in her eyes now. Calculation. She was reassessing him, looking for different angles. "You want to know who killed you? Fine. I'll tell you one name. A down payment."

"I'm listening."

"Zhang Wei."

The name meant nothing to him. He ran through his mental catalogue of colleagues, superiors, acquaintances—nothing. "Never heard of him."

"You wouldn't have." She stepped closer, emboldened by his confusion. "He's not the one who signed your death warrant. He's not the judge, not the prosecutor, not the witness who lied on the stand. He's the one who paid them all."

"That's still not a name I recognize."

"Because he doesn't exist on paper. He's a ghost in the system. A fixer. When powerful people need something done—someone eliminated, evidence buried, inconvenient truths made to disappear—they call Zhang Wei. He handles the logistics. He finds the right people, makes the right payments, ensures no trace leads back to the client."

Jiang Chen's grip tightened on his staff. "So he's a middleman."

"He's the middleman. The best in the country. Maybe the best in Asia." She tilted her head, studying him. "You were killed by professionals, Jiang Chen. Not street thugs, not jealous coworkers. Professionals who spend more on a single bribe than you made in a year. Do you understand what that means?"

It meant he was in over his head. It meant whoever wanted him dead had resources he couldn't imagine. It meant that if he went back to Diyu and demanded a case review, he'd be throwing himself against a wall built by people who'd already proven they could kill him once.

It meant he should be afraid.

He wasn't.

"I want to meet Zhang Wei."

Consort Wei laughed, a sharp, honest sound. "You can't. No one meets Zhang Wei. He doesn't exist. He's a phone number that changes every month, a bank account in a different country, a voice that sounds different every time you hear it. He's a ghost among ghosts."

"Then how do you know about him?"

Her smile faded. "Because seventeen years ago, before I was condemned, I worked for him."

The admission hung in the air between them, heavy and unexpected. Jiang Chen studied her face, looking for the lie. He found none.

"You were his employee?"

"His best. I was his best for three years." She looked away, and for a moment, she seemed almost human. Almost vulnerable. "I was a different person then. Younger. Dumber. I thought I was untouchable because I could walk through walls and read minds. I thought I was special."

"You were a soul reaper."

"I was a thief. A ghost who learned to steal bodies. Zhang Wei found me after my second year on the run. He didn't try to capture me. He offered me a job. Said I had talents that were being wasted hiding in shadows. Said he could make me rich."

"And you believed him."

"I was nineteen. Alone. Hungry. I'd been possessing beggars just to eat." She met his eyes again. "He gave me a purpose. He gave me targets—corrupt businessmen, rival fixers, people who'd crossed his clients. I'd possess them, learn their secrets, expose their crimes. Sometimes I'd stay in their bodies long enough to destroy their lives from the inside."

"That's when you became the Black Widow."

"That's when I became the monster the judges condemned." She laughed bitterly. "I was good at it. Too good. I made Zhang Wei millions. And when I finally got caught, when the soul reapers cornered me in Shanghai, do you know what he did?"

"He abandoned you."

"He sent someone to kill me. A real soul reaper, not a thief playing dress-up. He wanted me dead because I knew too much. I barely escaped with my soul intact." She touched her chest, where the staff had struck. "I've been running ever since. Seventeen years of running. And now you want to find him."

"To find the person who paid him."

"Same thing. He'll never tell you. He'll die before he gives up a client name. That's the first rule of his business—you take the secret to the grave."

Jiang Chen considered this. Then he said, "Then I'll make him tell me before he dies."

Consort Wei stared at him for a long moment. Then she laughed again, but this time there was respect in it. "You really are desperate. I like it."

"I don't care if you like it. I care about the name."

"I gave you one. Zhang Wei. That's your down payment." She spread her hands. "Now take me to Diyu. I'll give you the rest when we're safe."

"Not a chance. You tried to possess me thirty seconds ago."

"I'm a con woman. It's reflex."

Jiang Chen raised his staff. "Then you'll forgive me for not trusting reflex."

He stepped forward, ready to bind her soul, when his phone buzzed. He ignored it. It buzzed again. Then again.

"I think someone wants you," Consort Wei said.

He pulled out the phone. Ten missed calls from the same number. The Diyu emergency line. His blood went cold.

"What?"

He answered. The voice on the other end was Xiao Bao's, and she was whispering, which meant she was somewhere she shouldn't be.

"Jiang Chen. Don't come back to Diyu."

"What? Why?"

"They know. The judges know you didn't bring her in. Judge Jiu sent a retrieval team. They're coming for both of you."

Jiang Chen's mind raced. Judge Jiu. The Seventh Judge. The one who'd been suspicious of him from the start. The one who'd argued against recruiting a dead man.

"How do they know?"

"The other soul reaper. The one you replaced. His soul fragment was recovered from the alley. He saw everything before he died. Saw you let her go. Saw you make a deal with her."

Jiang Chen closed his eyes. Of course. Of course there was a witness. He'd been so focused on Consort Wei that he'd forgotten about the dead reaper's lingering soul.

"Where are you?"

"Hidden. There's a place in Diyu, the Third Court archives. No one goes there. I can stay hidden for a few days, but—Jiang Chen, they're going to declare you rogue. They're going to execute her on sight. They're going to—"

The line went dead.

"Xiao Bao? Xiao Bao!"

Nothing.

Consort Wei was watching him with sharp, knowing eyes. "Trouble?"

"Shut up." He redialed. No answer. He tried again. Nothing. The call hadn't dropped—it had been cut. By someone on the other end.

"Your friends in Diyu aren't happy with you," she said. "I told you. Trust no one. Not even the dead."

"Especially not the dead." Jiang Chen shoved the phone back into his pocket. His hands were steady now. The shaking had stopped. In its place was something cold and focused, the same clarity he'd felt on the execution table when he'd realized there was no point in begging.

He was going to die anyway. Might as well go down fighting.

"There's a retrieval team coming for us. Soul reapers. Probably three or four of them."

"Can you handle them?"

"I don't know. I've never fought another soul reaper." He looked at her. "Can you?"

"I'm a thief, not a fighter. I possess. I run. I hide." She shrugged. "But I've killed two reapers already. Maybe I'm better than I think."

"You got lucky. They underestimated you."

"Underestimation is a kind of luck I'm happy to exploit."

Jiang Chen turned and started walking. "We need to move. They'll track my soul signature. We have maybe ten minutes before they find us."

"Where are we going?"

"To find Zhang Wei."

"You said you can't find him."

"I can't." He stopped at the mouth of the alley, looking back at her. "But you can."

She raised an eyebrow. "I haven't spoken to him in seventeen years."

"Then it's time for a reunion."

"You're insane."

"Maybe." He held out his hand. "But I'm your only way out of this. Either we find Zhang Wei and get the names, or the retrieval team drags us both back to Diyu and the judges erase everything we are. Those are the options."

She looked at his hand. Looked at him. Then, slowly, she took it.

"You're going to get us both killed."

"Probably."

"Good. I've been wanting to die properly for seventeen years." She smiled, and it was the most honest expression he'd seen on her face. "Let's go find my old boss."

They stepped out of the alley and into the neon-lit streets of Shanghai, two ghosts walking among the living, with a retrieval team closing in and a fixer who didn't exist somewhere in the shadows.

And in the Third Court archives of Diyu, Xiao Bao's phone lay shattered on the floor, next to a pool of fresh blood that was already starting to dissolve into nothing.

title: The Ghost's Price
content:
The black light from Jiang Chen's staff sent Ma Xiaoling flying backward. She crashed through a stack of cardboard boxes, scattering old newspapers and plastic bottles across the alley. Her body—no, the body she'd stolen—lay crumpled, but her soul had already detached, hovering in the air like a serpent coiling to strike.

"You think that hurt?" She laughed, her voice echoing off the brick walls. "I've been beaten by better men with better weapons. You're just a rookie with a stick."

Jiang Chen's hands were still shaking. The names. She'd promised him names. But she'd also tried to possess him again, and that meant she couldn't be trusted. Could she?

She was a con woman. Lying was what she did.

But she'd seen his death. She'd seen the execution, the needle, the cold table. She'd seen the witnesses who weren't supposed to be there, the judge who'd barely looked at the evidence, the paperwork that had been rushed through in three days.

Three days. From arrest to execution. Even in China's justice system, that was impossibly fast.

"What's the matter, Soul Reaper?" Ma Xiaoling circled him, her ghostly form flickering. "You've got questions. I've got answers. But you're too scared to hear them."

"I'm not scared."

"Then why are you gripping that staff like it's the only thing keeping you alive?" She stopped circling, faced him directly. "Oh wait. You're not alive, are you? You're dead. Just like me. The only difference is, I chose to stay. You chose to serve."

"I didn't choose anything." The words came out harsher than he intended. "They gave me a job. They said if I did it, I could have a second chance."

"A second chance at what? Life?" She laughed again. "Look at yourself, Jiang Chen. You walk among the living, but you're not one of them. You eat their food, breathe their air, pretend to be their friend. But when the sun goes down, you're hunting souls like a dog hunting rats. That's not a second chance. That's a leash."

He knew she was manipulating him. He knew. But that didn't make it hurt less.

"I can give you names," she said again, her voice softening. "Three names. The men who framed you. The man who paid the judge. The man who signed your death warrant."

"Give them to me now."

"Take me to Diyu first."

"You'll try to escape."

"Of course I will." She smiled. "I'm a con woman. It's what I do. But here's the thing, Jiang Chen—I've been running for seventeen years. I'm tired. I'm old. My soul is frayed from jumping bodies every few months. I don't want to escape. I want to rest."

"Then why did you try to possess me?"

"Because I wanted to see if you were worth trusting." She floated closer, her face inches from his. "And I saw your death. I saw the fear. The confusion. The moment when you realized no one was coming to save you." Her voice dropped to a whisper. "I've been inside hundreds of minds. I've seen thousands of deaths. But yours... yours was special. Because you didn't deserve it."

The words hit him like a physical blow. He'd known, deep down, that something was wrong with his case. But he'd pushed it aside, told himself it was just the system being the system, that he was guilty of enough other things that it balanced out.

But justice didn't work that way. And neither did the afterlife.

"If I take you to Diyu, the judges will judge you," he said. "They'll send you to punishment. Maybe worse."

"I know."

"Then why do you want to go?"

She was quiet for a long moment. The alley was silent except for the distant hum of traffic and the drip of water from an air conditioning unit.

"Because I've been running so long I forgot what I was running from," she finally said. "I want to remember. I want to know if it was worth it. I want to know if the person I was before I started stealing bodies deserved to die."

"You killed two soul reapers."

"I know."

"You stole bodies. You ruined lives."

"I know." Her voice cracked. "I know what I am, Jiang Chen. I know what I've done. But I also know that I didn't start this way. Someone made me this way. And I want to see their face when the judges sentence them."

He wanted to believe her. He wanted to trust her. But every instinct screamed that she was playing him, that this was just another con, another angle.

"What guarantee do I have that you'll tell me the names?"

"None." She shrugged. "But what guarantee do I have that you'll actually let me see the judges? You could drag me back, have them wipe my memory, and I'd never know. I'd just wake up in some hell and spend eternity wondering who I used to be."

"Then we're at a stalemate."

"Not necessarily." She pointed at his staff. "You can bind me. Not to the staff—that's temporary. A soul contract. Permanent. If I try to escape, the contract burns. I'll feel it. I'll stop."

"Soul contracts are dangerous. If you break it, you could be destroyed."

"I know."

"You'd be giving me complete control over you."

"I know." She smiled, but it was a sad smile. "I've been in control my whole afterlife. Maybe it's time I let someone else steer for a while."

Jiang Chen hesitated. Soul contracts were ancient magic, rarely used because they required both parties to be completely willing. The binding had to be mutual. If either side had reservations, the contract wouldn't hold.

And he had plenty of reservations.

But he also had a dead end. He'd been a Soul Reaper for three weeks. He had no contacts, no network, no way to investigate his own death. Ma Xiaoling was his only lead.

"Fine," he said. "But if you try anything—"

"I know. You'll burn me." She rolled her eyes. "You said that already."

He raised his staff, and black light flickered along its length. "Do you, Ma Xiaoling, formerly known as Lin Yue, willingly enter into a soul contract with me, Jiang Chen, Soul Reaper of Diyu?"

"I do."

"Do you swear to provide me with the names of those who conspired to end my life, and to submit to the judgment of the Ten Judges of Diyu without resistance?"

"I do."

"Do you swear to not possess or attempt to possess my body or any other living body for the duration of this contract?"

She hesitated. "That's a big one."

"Do you swear?"

"Fine. I do."

"Do you swear to not deceive or mislead me regarding the information you possess about my death?"

Another hesitation. "I do."

"Then by the power vested in me by the Lord of Diyu, I accept your soul into my custody." He touched the staff to her chest, and black light erupted. She gasped as the contract took hold, a thin black thread connecting her soul to his staff.

When the light faded, she looked smaller. Weaker. The bravado was gone.

"It hurts," she whispered.

"Soul contracts always do."

"Now what?"

"Now we go to Diyu." He turned toward the alley entrance. "But first, I need to make sure your stolen body doesn't raise any alarms." He walked over to the crumpled form of the woman Ma Xiaoling had been possessing. She was still alive, but barely. Her soul had been damaged by the sudden eviction.

"Can you fix her?" Ma Xiaoling asked.

"Why do you care?"

"Because she was innocent. She was just a woman who trusted the wrong person." Ma Xiaoling's voice was quiet. "I've stolen a lot of bodies. But I never killed anyone who didn't deserve it. She didn't deserve this."

Jiang Chen knelt beside the woman and placed his hand on her forehead. He'd learned this trick from the training: soul mending. It was slow work, delicate work. But it could heal the damage.

He closed his eyes and let his power flow into her. He felt her soul, weak and frayed, and began to knit it back together. It was like weaving a rope from broken threads. Every few seconds, a thread would snap, and he'd have to start over.

Minutes passed. Maybe hours. He lost track of time.

Finally, he opened his eyes. The woman was breathing steadily. Her color was returning. She'd be fine.

"Thank you," Ma Xiaoling said.

"Don't thank me yet." He stood up, his legs aching. "We still have a long way to go."

"We're going to Diyu now?"

"First, I need to check something." He pulled out his phone—the one the judges had given him, which doubled as a soul-tracking device. He opened the app and searched for recent disturbances.

There was a red alert in the next district.

"What is it?" Ma Xiaoling asked, floating closer to look at the screen.

"Soul disturbance. Big one." He frowned. "It's coming from the Central Hospital."

"Someone died?"

"Someone's dying." He looked at the data. "But the soul isn't moving on. It's stuck. Trapped."

"That's not normal."

"Nothing about tonight is normal." He pocketed the phone. "We'll go to Diyu after I check this out."

"You're going to investigate a soul disturbance with a ghost criminal attached to your staff?"

"You're not attached to my staff. You're contracted to me."

"Same thing." She sighed. "Fine. But if we run into any other soul reapers, try not to look suspicious."

Jiang Chen started walking toward the hospital. Ma Xiaoling floated beside him, her ghostly form barely visible in the dim streetlights.

"You know," she said, "you're not what I expected."

"What did you expect?"

"Someone harder. Colder. Someone who'd already given up on being human." She looked at him. "But you're still trying. You're still hoping. That's rare among the dead."

"I'm not dead."

"You're a Soul Reaper. You're not alive either." She smiled. "You're something in between. Something new. Something that doesn't have a name yet."

"Is that supposed to be comforting?"

"Take it however you want." She drifted ahead of him, her translucent form glowing faintly in the darkness. "But I've been dead for seventeen years, and in all that time, I've never met anyone like you. That has to mean something."

He didn't respond. He didn't know how.

They reached the hospital ten minutes later. It was a massive building, all glass and steel, with a red cross glowing above the entrance. Ambulances came and went. Nurses smoked cigarettes in the parking lot. Patients in wheelchairs stared at nothing.

The soul disturbance was coming from the fifth floor.

"This way," Jiang Chen said, entering through the emergency room.

"Shouldn't we be more discreet?"

"Discretion takes time. Time means people die." He pushed through a crowd of waiting patients and headed for the stairs.

"People die anyway," Ma Xiaoling muttered, following.

The fifth floor was the oncology ward. The air was thick with the smell of antiseptic and despair. Jiang Chen walked past rooms filled with patients hooked up to machines, their souls flickering like dying candles.

The disturbance was at the end of the hall. Room 517.

He opened the door.

Inside, a man in his fifties lay in a hospital bed, his body

title: The Thirteenth Floor
content:
Jiang Chen slammed the staff deeper. Black light poured from Liu Mei's chest like smoke from a wound. Her scream cut through the warehouse, high and sharp, then dropped into something lower. Something that wasn't human.

"Fool," she said, but it wasn't her voice. It came from everywhere at once. The shadows on the walls twisted. "You think a soul reaper's weapon can hurt me?"

The staff bucked in his hands. Liu Mei's body went rigid, eyes rolling back, mouth open in a silent howl. Then something else pushed through.

A face emerged from her chest. Not Liu Mei's face. A man's face, pale and gaunt, with eyes like frozen lakes. It pushed against the skin from inside, distorting her ribs, stretching her sternum until bone cracked.

Jiang Chen ripped the staff free and stumbled backward. "What the hell—"

"Seventeen years." The face spoke through Liu Mei's mouth. Her lips split at the corners. "Seventeen years inside this vessel, waiting for someone stupid enough to bring me to Diyu."

"You're not Liu Mei."

"I'm the debt she never paid." The face pushed harder. Liu Mei's body began to tear at the seams, black light bleeding from every crack. "She owed me. They all owed me. And now I collect."

The staff vibrated in Jiang Chen's grip. The soul chain rattled against his hip. Both were reacting to something old. Something wrong.

"Who are you?"

"Your ticket home." Liu Mei's body collapsed. The skin folded inward like an empty bag, and from the folds rose a figure made of smoke and ledger paper. Numbers crawled across its form—debts, payments, interest rates, all written in blood that hadn't dried yet. "But I'm not going to Diyu. I'm going to the surface. And you're going to take me."

Jiang Chen raised the staff. "I'm taking you to the judges."

"You're taking me to your killer."

The words hit like a physical blow. Jiang Chen's grip faltered.

"I know who framed you," the figure said. "I know why. I know the date, the time, the bank account that received the payment. I know the judge's name and his mistress's name and the name of the man who signed your death warrant." It floated closer. Numbers dripped from its form and evaporated before hitting the ground. "I've been inside Liu Mei for seventeen years. I've seen every soul she possessed. I've read every memory. Your executioners are in here." It tapped its own chest. "Let me out of Diyu, and I'll give you every name."

"Why would you help me?"

"I want to live." The figure's eyes flickered—for a moment, it looked almost human. Almost desperate. "The judges will destroy me. I'm an old soul. An illegal soul. I wasn't supposed to exist after death, but I found a way. Liu Mei was my host, my shelter. Without her, I'm exposed. I need another body. I need time."

Jiang Chen's mind raced. The chain. The staff. The rules. Soul reapers couldn't make deals with rogue spirits. The punishment was immediate dismissal—and worse.

"I can't let you possess anyone else."

"Then take me to your killer yourself." The figure extended a hand made of smoke and numbers. "I'll tell you everything on the way. You don't have to let me go. Just delay my judgment. Give me three days. One day. One hour." Its voice cracked. "I've been hiding for two centuries. I just want to see the sun one more time."

Two centuries. Jiang Chen stared at the creature. It wasn't just an old soul. It was ancient. A ghost that had survived dynasties, wars, purges. A ghost that had learned to hide inside other ghosts, burrowing deep enough that even the judges couldn't find it.

"Why haven't the judges detected you?"

"Because I'm not a soul anymore." The figure smiled, and its teeth were numbers. "I'm a debt. A concept. A piece of paper that never got filed. I exist in the spaces between accounts. The judges see ledgers, not the ink that writes them."

Jiang Chen thought of his own ledger. The one Liu Mei had shown him. The one with the missing pages. Someone had removed evidence from his file. Someone had made sure his case would never be re-examined.

"Three days," he said. "You get three days. Then you go to Diyu."

"Three days is a lifetime." The figure flowed toward him, wrapping around his shoulders like a coat. It was cold. Colder than death. "Take me to the surface. I'll show you where the money went."

The soul chain went taut. It was pulling toward Diyu, demanding he return. Jiang Chen grabbed it and held firm.

"Not yet," he whispered. "Soon."

He walked out of the warehouse with a two-hundred-year-old debt collector wrapped around his shoulders and a name burning in his mind.

The name of the man who'd paid to have him killed.

---

The night air hit him like a wall. Jiang Chen hadn't realized how stale the warehouse was until he breathed fresh air again. The figure—it called itself The Ledger—settled into the fabric of his coat, invisible to mortal eyes.

"There," it said. "Turn left. The first transfer went through a shell company in Hong Kong."

Jiang Chen walked. The streets were empty. It was past midnight, and the living were asleep. The dead, however, were watching. He felt them in the shadows, in the corners of his vision—rogue souls that hadn't been collected yet. They sensed the Ledger. They feared it.

"What did Liu Mei owe you?"

"Everything." The Ledger's voice was flat. "She borrowed life. Stole it from others. I financed her possessions. Without me, she would have faded after her first death. I gave her seventeen years. She gave me shelter."

"And now she's gone."

"Now I'm free." The Ledger paused. "But freedom is just another word for exposure. I need a new contract. A new host. Or I'll dissolve."

"Then why help me?"

"Because you're my best bet." The Ledger's voice hardened. "I've seen what's coming for you, Soul Reaper. The man who killed you isn't finished. He's been watching the underworld. He knows the judges sent you. He has people in Diyu."

Jiang Chen stopped walking. "People in Diyu?"

"Corrupt judges. Bureaucrats who take bribes. Soul reapers who look the other way." The Ledger laughed, a dry sound like pages turning. "You think death is pure? Death is the biggest business in existence. And business always has dirty money."

The words hit harder than they should have. Jiang Chen had believed—naively—that the underworld was just. That the judges were incorruptible. That once you crossed the veil, the rules changed.

But rules were made by people. And people could be bought.

"How do I stop him?"

"You don't." The Ledger's voice was cold. "You find out who he is. You find out why he wanted you dead. And then you decide if the truth is worth dying for again."

Jiang Chen started walking. His feet carried him through the city, past shuttered shops and sleeping apartments, past a graveyard where the headstones glowed faintly with trapped souls. The Ledger directed him left, right, straight—following the ghost of a money trail that had gone cold years ago.

"The account was closed six days after your execution," the Ledger said. "The money was moved to a private bank in Macau. From there, it was converted to gold and shipped to—" It stopped.

"What?"

"Interesting." The Ledger's voice sharpened. "The gold was shipped to a temple. A Buddhist temple in the mountains outside Chengdu."

"A temple?"

"Not just any temple. A temple built on a fault line. A temple that sits directly above a vein of yin energy." The Ledger's form tightened around Jiang Chen's shoulders. "Someone is mining the dead."

Jiang Chen's blood went cold. Mining the dead. Harvesting souls for power. It was the oldest crime in the underworld—and the most punished.

"Who?"

"I don't know yet. But I know where the gold went." The Ledger pointed with a smoky finger. "There. The last transfer was to a numbered account. The account belongs to a man named—" It froze.

"What?"

The Ledger didn't answer. Its form flickered, numbers scrambling across its surface like a corrupted file.

"Ledger?"

"I know this name." The Ledger's voice was barely a whisper. "I know this name because it's the same name written at the bottom of your death warrant."

Jiang Chen's heart stopped. Not metaphorically. It actually stopped. His body seized, and he collapsed to his knees, gasping for air that wouldn't come.

"A soul reaper's body still needs a heartbeat," the Ledger said calmly. "And right now, yours has stopped. Because the name on that account—the man who paid for your execution—is also a soul reaper."

The world went dark.

When Jiang Chen opened his eyes, he was lying on a cold stone floor. The Ledger was gone. The warehouse was gone. He was in a room with no windows, no doors, and a single candle burning in the center.

"Welcome," said a voice he recognized, "to the thirteenth floor."

Standing in the corner, staff in hand, was the woman who'd trained him. The soul reaper who'd taught him the chains, the staff, the rules.

His mentor.

"You're wondering how this is possible," she said. "You're wondering why I killed you. You're wondering if any of it was real."

She stepped into the light. Her face was calm. Her eyes were cold.

"It was all real, Jiang Chen. The training. The mission. The betrayal." She smiled. "I needed a soul reaper who could cross the veil without setting off alarms. A fresh death. A clean slate. And you were perfect."

Jiang Chen's throat was dry. "Why?"

"Because the judges are getting suspicious. They're auditing the ledgers. They're going to find my accounts." She tilted her head. "Unless they find yours first. Unless they discover that the soul reaper they sent to hunt rogue spirits was actually—" She paused. "Mining the dead himself."

"You framed me."

"I didn't frame you." She knelt beside him, her face inches from his. "I made you. Every choice you made led you here. Every mistake. Every weakness. I didn't kill you, Jiang Chen. I just—" She smiled. "Expedited the process."

The candle went out.

And in the darkness, Jiang Chen heard the Ledger scream.

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER 13: The Soul Cage

The scream echoed through the underground parking garage, bouncing off concrete pillars and parked cars. Jiang Chen didn't let go. He pushed harder, driving the staff deeper into her spectral chest.

Black light pulsed between them. The con woman's form flickered like a dying flame, her face contorting between rage and pain.

"You're making a mistake," she gasped.

"You're the one who tried to possess me again."

"I was testing you." Her voice cracked. "I needed to see if you'd actually follow through, or if you were just another weak-willed puppet."

"I'm not a puppet."

"Then prove it." She released his mind, pulling her claws back. "Let me go. Let me explain."

Jiang Chen held the staff steady. "Explain from there."

She laughed, but it was weaker now. The black light had drained something from her. Her edges were frayed, translucent. "You don't even know how to use that thing properly, do you? You're just stabbing and hoping."

"I'm learning."

"Learn faster. You're going to need it."

A sound cut through the garage. Footsteps. Heavy, deliberate, coming from the stairwell.

Jiang Chen's grip tightened. "Who's that?"

The con woman's eyes widened. "No. Not now. Not yet."

"Who?"

"Let me go. Let me hide. If they find me—"

The stairwell door burst open.

Three figures stepped out. They wore black robes, identical to the ones the judges wore in Diyu, but their faces were hidden behind white masks with no eyeholes. Blind. They moved like they could see everything.

Soul Reapers.

But not like him. These were different. Their presence pressed down on the air itself, making it thick and hard to breathe.

"What are they?" Jiang Chen whispered.

"Judge's Enforcers," the con woman said. "Pure execution units. No negotiation. No mercy. They're here for me."

"How did they find us?"

She looked at him. "They didn't find *us*."

The lead Enforcer stopped ten feet away. Its head tilted, the blank mask somehow conveying attention. When it spoke, the voice came from everywhere at once, vibrating through the concrete.

"Jiang Chen. Soul Reaper Designate. You are in possession of a rogue soul classified for immediate termination. Release her."

"I'm supposed to take her to Diyu," he said. "She has information."

"The soul has been judged. Sentence is execution. No appeal. No delay."

"She knows who framed me. She knows who paid the judge to kill me."

Silence. The Enforcer's head tilted further, almost unnaturally so.

"Your personal grievances are irrelevant to the order. Release her."

"Like hell."

The con woman stared at him. "You're defending me?"

"I'm not defending you. I'm defending my only lead."

"Same thing."

"Shut up."

The Enforcer took a step forward. "Last warning, Soul Reaper Designate. You are interfering with an official execution order. The penalty for obstruction is—"

"I know the penalty." Jiang Chen shifted his stance, keeping the staff between him and the Enforcer. "I've read the handbook."

"You've read the handbook for three weeks. I have enforced Diyu law for three centuries. Do not mistake our patience for permission."

Jiang Chen's mind raced. Three Enforcers. One staff he barely understood. A rogue soul he didn't trust. And somewhere out there, the people who'd framed him were still breathing, still living, still free.

"If I let you kill her," he said slowly, "how do I know you won't kill me too?"

The Enforcer didn't answer.

"That's what I thought."

"You have been designated as a Soul Reaper by the Ten Judges. Your position is protected. The soul is not."

"So you'll just pretend I don't exist? Let me walk away?"

"Your cooperation will be noted."

"And if I don't cooperate?"

The Enforcer raised its hand. Black energy coiled around its fingers, forming into a blade made of shadow and silence.

"Then your cooperation will be noted differently."

The con woman grabbed his arm. "We can't fight them. I've seen Enforcers work. They don't stop. They don't tire. They don't feel pain."

"Then what do we do?"

"Run."

She yanked him backward. Jiang Chen stumbled, nearly dropping the staff. The Enforcer lunged.

They ran.

Jiang Chen didn't know where they were going. He just followed the con woman's lead, weaving between parked cars, ducking under concrete beams, heading deeper into the garage. The Enforcers didn't run. They moved. Gliding. Silent. Always closing.

"They're faster than us," he gasped.

"I know."

"Then what's the plan?"

"There's a maintenance tunnel at the far end. Leads to the subway system. If we can get underground—"

"They'll follow us underground."

"Then we'll go deeper. There are places in the city that even the Enforcers don't go. Forgotten stations. Old bomb shelters. Places where the dead don't reach."

"You've been there?"

"I've possessed people who've been there." She glanced back. The Enforcers were fifty feet away. "It's not safe. But it's better than dying."

"Again."

"What?"

"Dying *again*." Jiang Chen's chest burned. "I've already died once. I'm not doing it twice."

They reached the maintenance tunnel. A rusted door, chained and padlocked. The con woman grabbed the chain and pulled. Nothing.

"Locked."

"No shit."

"Can your staff break it?"

Jiang Chen looked at the staff. Black wood. Carved with characters he still couldn't read. It had hurt the con woman. It had killed the other soul reapers. But could it break a physical lock?

Only one way to find out.

He swung.

The staff struck the chain, and black light exploded. The metal shattered like glass. The door swung open.

"Okay," the con woman said. "That works."

They plunged into darkness. The tunnel was narrow, barely wide enough for two people. Water dripped from overhead pipes. The air smelled of rust and mold and something else. Something old.

Behind them, the Enforcers entered the tunnel.

They didn't run. They didn't need to.

"How much further?" Jiang Chen asked.

"Five minutes. Maybe less."

"They're going to catch us in five minutes."

"Then run faster."

They ran. The tunnel sloped downward, then curved left. Jiang Chen's legs burned. His lungs ached. He'd been dead for three weeks, and his body still remembered how to be tired.

The Enforcers were close now. He could feel them. Their presence pressed against his back like a weight.

"Left!" the con woman shouted.

They burst out of the tunnel into an abandoned subway station. Tiled walls, cracked and stained. Benches overturned. A train car rusting on the tracks. Dim emergency lights flickered overhead.

"There's an exit on the other side," she said. "We can—"

She stopped.

Three more Enforcers stood on the platform ahead of them. White masks. Black robes. Waiting.

"You led us into a trap," Jiang Chen said.

"I didn't know—"

"You're a con woman. You know everything."

She turned to face him. Her eyes were wild, desperate, but there was something else there. Something calculating. "I didn't know they'd be here. But I know how to deal with them."

"How?"

She grabbed his staff. Not to take it, but to hold it with him. "Together. You draw from Diyu. I draw from the mortal world. If we combine our power—"

"You want me to trust you."

"I want you to survive."

The Enforcers closed in. Six of them now. Six blind, silent, deathless things.

Jiang Chen looked at the staff. He looked at the con woman. He thought about his execution. The needle. The cold table. The witnesses.

He thought about the names she promised.

"Fine," he said. "But if you betray me—"

"I won't."

"You will. But do it after we survive."

She smiled. It was the first genuine expression he'd seen on her face. "Deal."

They raised the staff together. Black light and white light intertwined, spiraling up the wood, crackling against the ceiling. The Enforcers paused. For the first time, they hesitated.

"Now," the con woman said. "Hit them with everything."

Jiang Chen closed his eyes. He thought about his death. His life. The injustice. The rage. The grief. He poured it all into the staff.

The light exploded.

The Enforcers were thrown back. Their bodies shattered against the walls, reforming instantly, but the blast bought them time.

"Run!" she screamed.

They ran across the platform, jumped onto the tracks, and plunged into the next tunnel. Darkness swallowed them.

They ran for what felt like hours. Through tunnels. Through forgotten stations. Through places that didn't exist on any map.

Finally, they stopped. Jiang Chen collapsed against a wall, gasping.

"Did we lose them?"

"For now." The con woman stood over him, breathing hard. "But they'll find us eventually. They always find us."

"Then we need to get to Diyu. Fast."

"No. We need to get *you* to Diyu. If I show up with you, the judges will listen. They'll have to."

"You're using me."

"Of course I'm using you." She crouched down, meeting his eyes. "But I'm also telling you the truth. Your execution was a setup. I can prove it. I can give you names. I can give you *evidence*."

"Then give it to me now."

"I can't. It's in Diyu. In my case file. The judges confiscated everything when I was captured."

Jiang Chen stared at her. Every instinct told him she was lying. But every instinct had also told him he deserved to die.

"One wrong move," he said. "One hint of betrayal. And I'll put this staff through your soul."

"Understood."

He pushed himself up. "Let's go. We need to find a way back to Diyu before those Enforcers find us."

The con woman nodded. But as she turned to lead the way, Jiang Chen caught something in her expression. A flicker of something. Not fear. Not guilt.

Satisfaction.

*She's still playing me*, he realized. *She's still running a con.*

But he followed her anyway.

Because she was right.

She was his only lead.

And somewhere, in the halls of Diyu, evidence of his innocence was waiting.

Assuming he survived long enough to find it.

---

They walked in silence through the dark tunnels, two dead souls moving through the spaces between worlds. Behind them, in the abandoned subway station, the Enforcers stood motionless.

Their masks turned toward the tunnel where Jiang Chen and the con woman had disappeared.

One of them raised a hand to its featureless face.

And spoke.

Not to the others.

To someone far away.

"The target has been located," it said. "But there is a complication."

A pause.

"The Soul Reaper Designate is with her."

Another pause.

"Yes. We will proceed as ordered."

The Enforcer lowered its hand.

And smiled.

Behind its blank white mask, something that should not exist in an execution unit stirred.

Something hungry.

title: Chapter 14
content:
CHAPTER 14: The Memory That Wasn't Mine

The black light from the staff didn't throw her off.

It passed through her like she was already dead, which she was, but the con woman's grip on my mind didn't loosen. Her claws dug deeper, past my conscious thoughts, past my defenses, straight into the parts of me I'd locked away.

I saw the execution again. The cold table. The needle. The witnesses behind glass.

But this time, I saw something else.

A face in the observation room. A man I didn't recognize. He was standing in the back, away from the other witnesses, holding a briefcase. He wasn't watching me die. He was checking his watch.

*Who is that?*

The con woman's voice echoed in my skull. I tried to push her out, but she was stronger than me. Older. More experienced.

*Let me show you more.*

The scene shifted. I was in a courtroom, but not as the defendant. I was sitting in the gallery, watching someone else's trial. A woman. Young. Pretty. Tears streaming down her face as the judge read her sentence.

*Do you remember this?*

I didn't. I'd never been in this courtroom. I'd never seen this woman before.

But the con woman was showing me her memories. Her real memories. Not the ones she'd fabricated for her marks.

*She was innocent,* the con woman said. *Just like you.*

The woman in the courtroom screamed. Her lawyer patted her shoulder mechanically. The judge didn't meet her eyes.

*I was in the gallery that day. I was supposed to possess her after the trial. Someone paid me to take her body. She was supposed to disappear.*

The scene shifted again. A hospital. The con woman—wearing a different body, a nurse's uniform—was pushing a wheelchair through a corridor. In the wheelchair sat an old man. His eyes were vacant. Drool ran down his chin.

*This man knew something he shouldn't have. His family paid me to take his memories before he could talk.*

*Why are you showing me this?*

*Because you need to understand what I am.*

She released my mind. I stumbled backward, gasping. The alley spun around me. The con woman stood a few feet away, her stolen arms crossed, her stolen face unreadable.

"You're not just a con woman," I said, my voice hoarse. "You're a cleaner."

She nodded. "I take people who know too much. I possess them, extract their secrets, then discard them. Sometimes I leave them alive, empty shells. Sometimes I don't."

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because the person who hired me for those jobs is the same person who framed you."

The words hit me like a physical blow. I leaned against the alley wall, the rough brick digging into my back.

"His name is Zhang Wei. He's a fixer for a network of corrupt officials, businessmen, and judges. He's the one who paid your prosecutor. He's the one who paid your judge. He's the one who put you on death row."

"I don't know any Zhang Wei."

"You don't have to. He knows you." She stepped closer. "You saw something, Jiang Chen. Something you didn't even realize you saw. That's why you had to die."

I racked my brain. My last weeks as a living man had been unremarkable. I'd gone to work. I'd taken bribes. I'd looked the other way. I'd done nothing heroic, nothing dangerous, nothing that would warrant my execution being a cover-up.

"I didn't see anything," I said.

"You did. You just don't remember it yet." She tapped her temple. "But I can help you remember. I can dig through your memories and find the one that got you killed. But I can't do it here. The longer I stay in the mortal realm, the more my soul degrades. I need to return to Diyu. I need to be judged."

"You're asking me to take you back to the judges who sentenced me to death."

"I'm asking you to take me back to the judges who can give us both justice."

I looked at my staff. It pulsed with black light, hungry for her soul. One swing and I could end this. Drag her back to Diyu, collect my credit, move on to the next soul.

But if she was telling the truth—if Zhang Wei existed, if my execution was a setup—then dragging her back without proof would accomplish nothing. The judges didn't care about mortal injustice. They cared about souls. My case was closed. My file was stamped.

Unless I could reopen it.

"How do I know you're not lying?"

She smiled. It was a sad smile, the first genuine expression I'd seen on her stolen face. "I've been possessing people for seventeen years. I've seen every secret, every crime, every hidden truth. I've seen things that would make your nightmares blush. But in all those years, I've never met someone who deserved to die as much as you didn't."

"Pretty words."

"Pretty words are my specialty." She extended her hand again. "But this time, they're true. I have nothing to gain by lying to you now. If you take me to Diyu, I'll tell the judges everything. I'll give them names, dates, locations. I'll give them Zhang Wei. And in exchange, they'll re-examine your case."

"And if they don't?"

"Then you'll spend eternity knowing the truth. That's more than most dead people get."

I stared at her hand. It was the same hand she'd offered before, the same hand that had tried to possess me twice. She was dangerous. She was manipulative. She was probably still lying.

But she was also my only lead.

I took her hand.

"Don't try anything," I said.

"I wouldn't dream of it."

I raised my staff and slammed it into the ground. The black light expanded, tearing a hole in reality. The veil between worlds ripped open, revealing the gray mist of Diyu beyond.

"After you," I said.

She stepped through without hesitation. I followed, and the veil closed behind us.

---

Diyu was quiet.

Too quiet.

The gray plains stretched endlessly in all directions, dotted with the occasional soul shuffling toward their judgment. But something was wrong. The air felt heavy. The mist was thicker than usual.

"Something's different," the con woman said.

"No shit."

We walked toward the Hall of Judgment. The path was familiar now—I'd traveled it enough times to know every hill, every rock, every twisted tree. But the landmarks were wrong. A hill that should have been on the left was on the right. A tree that should have had three branches had four.

"The geography is shifting," I said.

"That's not possible. Diyu is fixed."

"Tell that to the tree."

We pressed on. The Hall of Judgment appeared in the distance, its black spires cutting through the mist. But as we got closer, I saw something that made my blood run cold.

The doors were open.

The Hall of Judgment never had open doors. The judges conducted their business in secret, behind closed doors, shielded from prying eyes and wandering souls.

"This is bad," the con woman whispered.

We approached cautiously. The doors hung broken, splintered, as if something had forced its way through from the inside. Inside, the Hall was empty. The judge's thrones were overturned. Papers were scattered everywhere.

"What happened here?" I asked.

No one answered.

We moved deeper into the Hall. The silence was oppressive, broken only by the crunch of paper under our feet. The con woman picked up a document, scanned it, dropped it.

"This is a judgment," she said. "Someone's case file. It's been torn."

I picked up another. The same. Page after page of judgments, ripped apart, discarded like trash.

"This isn't a break-in," I said. "This is a cover-up."

The con woman nodded slowly. "Someone doesn't want the judges to remember something."

"Or someone doesn't want the judges to remember anything."

A sound echoed from the back of the Hall. Footsteps. Slow. Deliberate.

I raised my staff. The con woman moved behind me, pressed against my back.

A figure emerged from the shadows. Tall. Wearing a black robe. A familiar face.

Judge Chen.

"Jiang Chen," he said, his voice flat. "You're back early."

"Judge Chen. What happened here?"

He didn't answer. He just stood there, staring at me with empty eyes.

"Judge Chen?"

He blinked. Once. Twice.

"Zhang Wei sends his regards," he said.

His hand shot out, and black energy exploded toward me. I dove sideways, dragging the con woman with me. The energy hit the wall behind us, leaving a smoking crater.

"Your judge is possessed," the con woman hissed.

"No shit."

Judge Chen advanced, his eyes dead, his movements mechanical. Someone was controlling him. Someone had gotten to him.

Someone had gotten to all of them.

I raised my staff, ready to fight. But the con woman grabbed my arm.

"Don't," she said. "You can't kill a judge. And you can't save him."

"Then what do we do?"

She looked at me, her stolen eyes suddenly sharp. "We find Zhang Wei. We kill him. And then we unmake what he's done."

"And how do we find him?"

She smiled. It was not a comforting smile.

"We let him find us."

title: The Weight of Shadows
content:
The black light from Jiang Chen's staff sent Madam Wei hurtling backward. She crashed through a row of parked bicycles, metal twisting around her borrowed body. The soul within her flickered like a dying candle, but she held on.

"You'll have to do better than that," she coughed, pulling herself up. Blood dripped from her lip. "I've been thrown out of better bodies than this."

Jiang Chen's hands were still shaking. The names. She'd promised him names. But she'd also tried to possess him again. Twice now. Trust wasn't an option.

"You said you could prove it," he said, keeping the staff between them. "Prove it now. No conditions."

Madam Wei spat blood onto the pavement. "And let you drag me back to Diyu? I've seen what happens to souls who break the rules. They don't get reincarnation. They get the eighteenth level of hell. Boiling oil. Mountain of knives. For eternity."

"That's not my problem."

"It will be." She wiped her mouth with the back of her hand. "The judges don't care about justice. They care about order. They'll wipe my memory, sure. But they'll wipe yours too if you make trouble. You think they want a soul reaper questioning his own death sentence? That sets a bad precedent."

Jiang Chen's grip tightened on the staff. She was manipulating him again. He knew it. But she was also right.

"Who framed me?" he asked.

"A man named Xu Liang. He's the deputy director of the Municipal Tax Bureau. Three years ago, you audited his brother's company. Found discrepancies. Reported them to your supervisor. The case got buried, but Xu Liang never forgot your name."

The name hit Jiang Chen like a physical blow. Xu Liang. He remembered now. The audit had been routine, but the numbers didn't add up. Millions missing. He'd flagged it, written the report, handed it up the chain. Nothing happened. He'd assumed it was incompetence. Not conspiracy.

"Why didn't he just kill me then?"

"Because you were useful." Madam Wei stepped closer. "After you flagged the audit, your supervisor made sure you were promoted. Given access to more accounts. You became a patsy. Every time someone needed numbers cooked, you were the one who signed off. You thought you were just doing your job, but you were building a paper trail. For yourself."

"That's not—" He stopped. The bribes. The small favors. The way people smiled at him at office parties but never invited him to dinner. He'd thought he was climbing the ladder. He'd been climbing a scaffold.

"By the time they needed a fall guy, you were perfect," Madam Wei continued. "Your signature on a dozen suspicious reports. Your bank account showing deposits you didn't remember making. A dead woman's DNA on your clothes. They built the case over two years. Air-tight."

"How do you know all this?"

"I possessed a secretary at the prosecutor's office. For three months. I read every file, every email, every private conversation." She smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "I was looking for leverage on a different case. Found yours instead. Kept it in case I needed a bargaining chip."

Jiang Chen's mind raced. If what she said was true, then his execution wasn't just a mistake. It was a hit job. Someone in the government had signed his death warrant. And if he went back to Diyu with this information, what would the judges do? Investigate? Or bury it?

"You said you had names," he said. "More than Xu Liang."

"I have a list. Judges who took bribes. Police officers who planted evidence. A forensic technician who faked the DNA report. A dozen names. All connected."

"Give me one. Right now. Something I can verify."

Madam Wei studied him for a long moment. Then she reached into her pocket and pulled out a crumpled business card. She tossed it to him. It was wet with blood, but he could still read the name: *Detective Huang Wei, Special Investigations Unit.*

"He was the lead investigator on your case. He's still alive. Still working. Ask him about the evidence chain. Watch his face when you mention the word 'fabricated.'"

Jiang Chen stared at the card. This was real. Tangible. Something he could act on.

"Why are you helping me?"

"I'm not helping you. I'm helping myself." Madam Wei's eyes narrowed. "You're going to let me go. Not because you trust me, but because you need me. I know things about Diyu that you don't. About the judges. About corruption in the underworld. About souls that were sentenced unfairly and sent to hell anyway."

"You're lying."

"Ask your teacher. He's been a soul reaper for sixty years. He knows more than he tells you." She tilted her head. "Or don't. Keep being a good little apprentice. Follow the rules. Let them wipe your memory and give you a new identity. In a few decades, you won't even remember your own name."

The words cut deep. Because they were true. He'd seen it happen to other soul reapers. Old ones who'd served their time and been reincarnated. They came back as blank slates, eyes empty, souls scrubbed clean. He'd thought it was mercy. Now he wondered if it was just another form of execution.

"I need to think," he said.

"Take your time. But remember: every hour you waste is an hour Xu Liang uses to cover his tracks. He doesn't know you're alive. He doesn't know a soul reaper is hunting him. Keep it that way."

Jiang Chen pulled out his phone. It was almost 4 AM. The sun would rise in two hours. He needed to get back to his apartment before his roommate woke up. But first, he needed answers.

"Where can I find Detective Huang?"

"Central Police Station. Ninth floor. Special Investigations Unit. He works nights." Madam Wei smiled. "He's been having trouble sleeping. Guilt, probably."

"You're coming with me."

"Absolutely not."

"It's not a choice." Jiang Chen raised the staff. "I can't watch you every second. But I can keep you close. If you try to run, I'll bind your soul to the staff. You'll spend eternity as a lamp."

Madam Wei's face darkened. "You wouldn't."

"Try me."

For a long moment, they stared at each other. Then Madam Wei laughed. It was a cold, brittle sound. "Fine. But when we get there, you're going to see things you don't want to see. And when that happens, remember: I didn't make this world. I just live in it."

---

The Central Police Station was a grey concrete block that looked like it had been designed by someone who hated windows. Jiang Chen stood across the street, watching the entrance. His body was still solid, still warm, still breathing. He'd learned to control his form, to keep the death energy hidden. To any normal person, he looked like a tired office worker who'd pulled an all-nighter.

Madam Wei stood beside him, her soul tethered to his wrist by a thin thread of black energy. He'd learned that trick from Teacher Zhang. It allowed him to keep her close without letting her wander.

"Comfortable?" she asked.

"Shut up."

"I'm just saying, if we're going to do this, we should do it right. You can't just walk in and ask questions. You need a reason to be there."

"I have a reason."

"What? 'Hello, I'm a dead man investigating my own murder'? That'll go great."

Jiang Chen ignored her. He crossed the street, walked through the front doors, and approached the front desk. A tired-looking officer looked up from his computer.

"Can I help you?"

"I need to speak with Detective Huang Wei. Special Investigations Unit."

The officer's eyes narrowed. "Do you have an appointment?"

"It's urgent."

"It's 4 AM. Nothing's urgent at 4 AM except dead bodies and drunk drivers. Which one are you?"

Jiang Chen reached into his pocket and pulled out his old ID card. The one from before his death. It had his photo, his name, his employee number. He'd kept it as a memento. Now he used it.

"I'm a former colleague. From the Tax Bureau. I have information about a case he worked on. Three years ago. The Jiang Chen murder case."

The officer's face changed. He picked up the phone and dialed a number. "Detective Huang? Someone here to see you. Says it's about the Jiang Chen case." A pause. "Yeah. I'll send him up."

The officer hung up and pointed to the elevator. "Ninth floor. He's waiting."

Jiang Chen nodded and walked toward the elevator. Madam Wei followed, her footsteps silent on the tile floor.

"You're good," she whispered. "Using your old ID. That was smart."

"I'm not smart. I'm desperate."

"Same thing."

The elevator doors opened. Jiang Chen stepped inside. As the doors closed, he caught a glimpse of his reflection in the polished metal. He looked tired. Hollow. Like a man who'd already died once and was beginning to wonder if death was the easy part.

The ninth floor was quieter than the rest of the station. Fewer desks. Better carpet. Special Investigations Unit. The elite. The ones who handled high-profile cases.

Detective Huang Wei was waiting outside his office. He was a thick man, broad-shouldered, with a face that looked like it had been carved from stone. His eyes were hard, suspicious.

"You say you're from the Tax Bureau?"

"Former. I worked under Director Zhao. I reviewed the financial records for the Jiang Chen case."

Huang's expression didn't change. "That case is closed. The suspect was executed three weeks ago."

"I know. But I found something. An irregularity in the evidence chain. I thought you should know."

Huang studied him for a long moment. Then he gestured toward his office. "Come in."

Jiang Chen followed him inside. The office was small, cluttered with files and case notes. A single desk lamp cast harsh shadows across the walls. Huang sat down and gestured for Jiang Chen to take a seat.

"Show me what you found."

Jiang Chen reached into his pocket. He didn't have any documents. He didn't have anything. He was bluffing, and he knew it. But he'd learned something from Madam Wei: when you have nothing, you make them believe you have everything.

"I need to ask you a question first," he said. "Off the record."

Huang's eyes narrowed. "What kind of question?"

"The kind that gets people killed." Jiang Chen leaned forward. "The DNA evidence in the Jiang Chen case. Who collected it?"

"Standard procedure. Crime scene investigators."

"Which ones? I want names."

Huang's hand moved toward his desk drawer. Slowly. Casually. "Why do you need names?"

"Because I think the evidence was planted. And I think you know it."

The room went still. Huang's hand stopped moving. His eyes locked onto Jiang Chen's, and for a moment, something flickered in them. Recognition. Fear.

"You're dead," Huang whispered.

"I got better."

Jiang Chen stood up. The staff materialized in his hand, black energy swirling around it. Huang reached for his gun, but Jiang Chen was faster. He slammed the staff against the desk, and a wave of death energy exploded outward, knocking Huang out of his chair.

"Who paid you?" Jiang Chen demanded.

"I don't know what you're talking about—"

title: Chapter 16
content:
CHAPTER 16: The Warden's Secret

The black light consumed everything.

Jiang Chen felt himself falling, but not through space—through memory. Images flashed past him like cards being dealt too fast to read. A child's birthday party. A hospital room. A courtroom. His own face reflected in a window, younger, more hopeful, before everything went wrong.

No. Not his memories. Hers.

The con woman's life poured through him in fragments. He saw her first possession—an old man on his deathbed, terrified, helpless. She'd slipped into him like water through a crack, felt his heart stop, and then kept breathing. She'd been fourteen years old.

He saw her victims. Dozens of them. Families she'd torn apart, fortunes she'd stolen, lives she'd discarded like worn-out clothes. But he also saw something else—a face that appeared again and again in her memories. A man in a gray suit, always standing in shadows, always watching.

She called him the Warden.

"He's been following me since I was seventeen," her voice echoed through the connection. "Every time I thought I'd escaped, he found me. Every time I built something, he burned it down. He's the one who forced me into this life."

Jiang Chen's feet hit solid ground. The black light faded. They were standing in a corridor he didn't recognize—stone walls, flickering torchlight, the faint smell of incense. Not the mortal realm. Not Diyu either.

"Where are we?" He kept his staff pointed at her chest.

"The space between." She looked around, her confidence gone. "This shouldn't be possible. The staff isn't supposed to—"

"It did something new." He could feel it humming in his hands, warm and alive. "You triggered something."

"Your anger triggered something. When I grabbed you, you wanted to hurt me so badly that the staff responded." She laughed, but it was hollow. "Congratulations. You just unlocked a feature."

"Stop deflecting. What did you show me?"

"Everything." She sat down against the wall, suddenly looking exhausted. "The Warden. My handler. The man who turned me into this. He's the one who told me about the soul reapers. He's the one who gave me the technique to fight them. And he's the one who wants you."

"Why me?"

"Because you're dead but not dead. Because you can cross the veil. Because you're the first one who's ever done what you do." She looked up at him. "The judges created the soul reaper system centuries ago. But they've never been able to make one who could truly live in both worlds. You're the prototype. And the Warden wants to study you."

"Study me or use me?"

"Both. Neither. I don't know." She pressed her palms against her eyes. "He never tells me the whole plan. He just gives me orders and punishes me when I fail. I've been his puppet for fifteen years. And now I'm trapped in a void between worlds with the only person who might be able to stop him."

Jiang Chen studied her. The con woman act was gone. What remained was something raw and broken. He'd seen that look before—in the mirror, after his execution, when he realized he couldn't scream because he no longer had lungs.

"Why should I believe you?"

"Because I just let you see my memories." She lowered her hands. "You think I'd do that willingly? I've spent my entire life hiding from people like you. But when you grabbed me with that staff, I couldn't stop it. You saw everything. My first kill. My worst failures. The faces of everyone I've hurt."

"I saw fragments."

"More than enough to destroy me." She stood up, brushing dust off her stolen clothes. "You know what I am. You know what I've done. And you know I'm not the real enemy."

"The real enemy is the Warden."

"Yes." She walked toward him, stopping just out of staff range. "And I can help you find him. But only if you trust me."

"You just tried to possess me."

"I was desperate. I've been running for so long that I forgot there's another way." She took a breath. "The Warden collects things. Secrets. Power. People. He has a vault in the mortal realm where he keeps his most valuable acquisitions. If we can get inside, we'll find evidence of everything—your frame-up, his network, the judges he's corrupted."

"Corrupted judges?"

"You don't think he's been working alone, do you?" She smiled, but there was no humor in it. "Someone in Diyu has been feeding him information. That's how he knew about you. That's how he knew the soul reapers were coming. He has a mole inside the underworld."

Jiang Chen's grip tightened on his staff. The chain rattled against the stone floor. He thought of Judge Zhao, the one who'd recruited him. The one who'd seemed so certain that Jiang Chen was exactly what they needed. Was it possible—

"Don't trust anyone," the con woman said, as if reading his mind. "That's the first lesson the Warden taught me. Everyone has a price. Everyone has a weakness. And everyone can be turned."

"Except you?"

"I was turned. I'm just choosing to turn back." She extended her hand again. "Third time's the charm. Deal?"

He didn't take it. "What happens to you after we stop him?"

"I don't know. Prison, probably. Execution, maybe." She shrugged. "It's what I deserve. But at least I'll die knowing I did one good thing."

"And if I refuse?"

"Then we stand here forever, I suppose. This space between worlds doesn't have an exit unless one of us opens it." She gestured at the empty corridor. "I don't have a staff. You do. So the choice is yours."

Jiang Chen closed his eyes. He thought about his execution. The cold needle. The witnesses who'd watched him die. The judge who'd sentenced him without ever meeting his eyes. And somewhere out there, in the mortal world, the man who'd orchestrated it all was still walking free.

He opened his eyes. "What's your real name?"

The con woman blinked. "What?"

"Your real name. Not your alias. Not your victim's name. The name your parents gave you."

She was silent for a long moment. When she spoke, her voice was barely a whisper. "Lin Mei."

"Lin Mei." He tested the name. "That's the woman I'm making a deal with. Not the con woman. Not the killer. Her."

"You're going to trust me because you know my real name?"

"I'm going to trust you because I saw your memories. I know what he did to you. And I know you want to stop him more than you want to save yourself." He lowered his staff. "Show me the way out."

Lin Mei nodded. She pressed her hand against the wall, and the stone rippled like water. A door appeared—wooden, old, with a brass handle shaped like a serpent eating its own tail.

"The Warden's vault," she said. "We're going in through the back."

"Won't he notice?"

"He'll notice eventually. But I've spent years mapping his security. I know every blind spot, every timer, every guard rotation." She turned the handle. "We have exactly seventeen minutes before the system alerts him. Stay close, stay quiet, and don't touch anything."

The door swung open.

They stepped into a warehouse. It was massive—easily the size of a football field—with rows upon rows of shelves stretching into darkness. Each shelf held objects that glowed with their own light. A jade bracelet that pulsed like a heartbeat. A bronze mirror that showed scenes from ancient battles. A scroll that whispered in a language older than humanity.

"The vault," Lin Mei confirmed. "Everything here is stolen from Diyu. Judgment artifacts. Memory crystals. Soul anchors. If the judges knew what he'd taken, they'd burn the mortal realm to get it back."

Jiang Chen's staff hummed louder, responding to the power around them. "Where's the evidence about my case?"

"Shelf 7-C. The Warden likes to keep recent acquisitions close to his desk." She pointed toward a glass office in the corner of the warehouse. "That's his workspace. We need to be quiet. He doesn't sleep, and he doesn't leave."

"He's here?"

"He's always here."

They moved through the shelves, keeping to the shadows. Each step echoed in the vast space. Jiang Chen could feel the artifacts watching him, aware of his presence, hungry for his attention. The staff kept them at bay, but barely.

They reached Shelf 7-C. Lin Mei scanned the row, her fingers brushing against various objects until she found what she was looking for—a simple manila folder, worn and stained, with his name written on the tab.

Jiang Chen's hands shook as he opened it.

Inside were documents. Photographs. Bank records. Phone transcripts. His entire life, reduced to evidence of a crime he didn't commit. But there was more—letters between the Warden and someone else. Someone with an official seal.

The seal of Diyu.

"Who is it?" Lin Mei whispered.

Jiang Chen held up the letter. The signature was clear. The name was familiar.

It was Judge Zhao.

"Your recruiter," Lin Mei breathed. "The one who chose you. The one who trained you. He's been working with the Warden from the beginning."

The office door behind them clicked open.

"Ah, Mei. You brought me a present."

The voice was calm, pleasant, almost friendly. Jiang Chen turned to see a man in a gray suit standing in the doorway. He was ordinary in every way—average height, average build, forgettable face—except for his eyes. They were hollow. Empty. Like looking into a pit with no bottom.

The Warden smiled.

"I've been waiting for you, Soul Reaper. I have so many questions." He stepped forward, and the shadows seemed to move with him. "Don't worry. This won't hurt. Much."

Jiang Chen grabbed his staff, ready to fight.

But Lin Mei was faster.

She threw herself between them, her hands raised. "Run," she hissed. "Take the folder. Find someone you can trust. I'll hold him off."

"Mei—"

"I owe you this." She looked back at him, and for the first time, her eyes were clear. Honest. "Go."

The Warden laughed. "You think you can stop me? You, who I made from nothing? You, who I saved from the streets?" He raised his hand, and the air around him began to twist. "You were my greatest creation, Mei. Don't make me unmake you."

"Everyone has a price," she said. "You taught me that. And I've finally found someone willing to pay mine."

She turned and charged.

Jiang Chen ran.

The warehouse blurred around him as he sprinted toward the door, the folder clutched against his chest. Behind him, he heard screaming—Lin Mei's screaming—and then nothing.

He burst through the door and into the mortal realm, landing hard on a city street. Cars honked. Pedestrians stared. He was in Shanghai, covered in dust, holding evidence that could destroy the underworld.

His phone buzzed.

A text from an unknown number: *She's alive. For now. Bring me the folder, or I'll send her to you in pieces. -W*

And below it, an address.

Jiang Chen looked at the folder. Looked at the phone. Looked at the sky, where the sun was setting and the veil between worlds was thinning.

He

title: Chapter 17
content:
CHAPTER 17: The Fifth Judge's Summons

The black light from Jiang Chen's staff sent Lin Mei sprawling across the rooftop. She skidded across the gravel, tearing her stolen dress, and came to a stop against the low wall that bordered the edge. Below, the city hummed with night traffic, oblivious to the battle above.

"That was stupid," Jiang Chen said, advancing on her. "I was actually going to listen to you."

Lin Mei pushed herself up, wiping blood from her split lip. Her eyes glowed faintly red in the darkness. "You still need to listen. You just proved my point."

"I proved that you can't be trusted."

"No." She stood, brushing gravel from her torn sleeves. "I proved that you're too scared to hear the truth. You'd rather drag me back to Diyu, let them wipe my memory, and live in comfortable ignorance. Because if you knew who really killed you, you'd have to do something about it."

Jiang Chen stopped three feet from her. The staff hummed in his hands, hungry for more soul-light. "I know who killed me. The state did. A judge signed the order. A doctor pushed the needle."

"Those are tools. I'm talking about the hand that held them."

He wanted to strike her again. He wanted to knock her unconscious, drag her back to Diyu, and collect his promotion. But a small part of him—the accountant's part, the part that had spent years following paper trails—wanted to hear the rest.

"One name," he said. "Give me one name I can verify."

Lin Mei's smile returned. She knew she had him. "Zhang Wei."

The name meant nothing to him. "Who's that?"

"Senior Deputy Prosecutor for the Eastern District. He handled your case. He was the one who presented the evidence to the judge." She paused. "He's also the one who had an offshore account opened three days before your trial with a deposit of fifty thousand US dollars."

Jiang Chen's chest tightened. "How do you know about an offshore account?"

"I possessed a bank manager last year. I saw the records. Zhang Wei's account. The deposit came from a shell company registered in the Cayman Islands." She tilted her head. "Do you want to know who owns the shell company?"

"Tell me."

"I don't know the name. The paperwork was buried too deep. But I know the company's registered address. And I know it was the same company that paid twelve thousand dollars to the judge who presided over your case."

The air left Jiang Chen's lungs. He'd suspected corruption—he'd even expected it—but hearing the details made it real. Someone had spent over sixty thousand dollars to make sure he died.

"Why?" he asked. "I was nobody. I took bribes. I looked the other way. But I never killed anyone. I never threatened anyone important. Why would someone spend that kind of money to frame me?"

Lin Mei shrugged. "Maybe you saw something you shouldn't have. Maybe you had something someone wanted. Maybe you were just in the wrong place at the wrong time." She stepped closer. "But I can help you find out. I can show you the files I saw. I can take you to the people who know."

"You're a con woman. You'd say anything to avoid judgment."

"I would. I am." She smiled. "But I'm also telling the truth. The two aren't mutually exclusive."

Jiang Chen stared at her. She was infuriating. She was manipulative. She was also his only lead.

Before he could respond, a cold wind swept across the rooftop. The temperature dropped twenty degrees in seconds. Lin Mei's smile vanished, replaced by genuine fear.

"What is that?" she whispered.

Jiang Chen turned. A figure stood at the far end of the rooftop, silhouetted against the City God Temple's red walls. It was tall and thin, wearing a long black robe that seemed to drink the light. Two blue flames floated where its eyes should have been.

"You're late, Soul Reaper," the figure said. Its voice was like grinding stones. "The Fifth Judge has been waiting."

Jiang Chen gripped his staff. "Who are you?"

"Li Qian, Clerk of the Fifth Court." The figure stepped forward, and Jiang Chen saw that it was a man—or had been a man once. His skin was pale gray, stretched tight over sharp bones. His fingers were too long, too thin, ending in black nails. "I've been sent to retrieve the soul you were assigned to capture."

"She's my assignment. I'll deliver her when I'm ready."

"You're not ready. You've been compromised." Li Qian's blue eyes fixed on Lin Mei. "She's poisoned your mind with lies. The Fifth Judge anticipated this. That's why I'm here."

Lin Mei backed away, moving toward Jiang Chen. "Don't let him take me. The Fifth Judge—he's the one who handles corruption cases. If I'm taken to his court, I'll never see the surface again."

"She's right," Li Qian said. "The Fifth Court doesn't offer deals. It offers judgment. Immediate. Permanent." He pulled a scroll from his robe and unrolled it. "I have authorization to take custody of spirit 4732-Lin Mei. If you resist, I'm authorized to take you as well."

Jiang Chen looked at the scroll. It bore the seal of the Fifth Court—a gavel crossed with a sword. Official Diyu business. He couldn't refuse without disobeying a judge.

"This is convenient," he said. "She starts telling me about my case, and suddenly a clerk shows up to take her away."

"You're paranoid."

"You're suspicious."

Lin Mei grabbed his arm. Her grip was cold, desperate. "Jiang Chen. If you let him take me, you'll never know the truth. The Fifth Judge will wipe my memory and destroy my soul. Your case will be buried forever."

"She's lying," Li Qian said. "She'll say anything to avoid the Fifth Court. That's what she does. She's a manipulator. A parasite. She's already manipulated you."

Jiang Chen looked at Lin Mei. He looked at Li Qian. Two liars, each telling a version of the truth.

"Why now?" he asked. "Why send a clerk now? She's been on the loose for weeks."
