Li Qian's blue eyes flickered. "The Fifth Judge has been busy. He only recently became aware of her capture."

"Bullshit. Diyu has informants everywhere. You knew she was captured the moment I caught her."

"The Fifth Judge has his own timeline."

"Or," Jiang Chen said, "someone in Diyu doesn't want me talking to her."

Li Qian's face tightened. The blue flames in his eyes burned brighter. "You're a probationary Soul Reaper. You've held your position for less than a month. You have no authority to question the Fifth Judge's orders."

"I'm not questioning the judge. I'm questioning you." Jiang Chen raised his staff. "You show up in the middle of my interrogation, waving a scroll, demanding I hand over my prisoner. You expect me to just obey?"

"Yes. That's exactly what I expect."

"Then you don't know me very well."

Li Qian laughed. It was a dry sound, like dead leaves scraping concrete. "I know you, Jiang Chen. I know everything about you. I know you were executed for a murder you didn't commit. I know you're desperate for answers. I know you're afraid that if you hand over this woman, you'll lose your only lead." He stepped closer. "But I also know that if you don't hand her over, you'll lose your position. Your chance at redemption. Your only hope of avoiding the Fifth Court yourself."

The threat hung in the air. Jiang Chen could feel the weight of it, pressing down on him.

Lin Mei's grip tightened on his arm. "Don't listen to him. He's working for whoever framed you. The Fifth Court is compromised."

"Silence!" Li Qian's hand shot out, and a chain of black energy flew from his palm, wrapping around Lin Mei's throat. She choked, clawing at the chain.

Jiang Chen acted on instinct. He swung his staff, cutting through the chain. Black energy dissipated. Lin Mei gasped, stumbling backward.

"You attacked a court official," Li Qian said, his voice cold. "That's a crime."

"You attacked my prisoner."

"Your prisoner is bound for the Fifth Court. She's no longer your concern."

"She's still my concern until I deliver her to Diyu personally." Jiang Chen planted his staff on the ground. "If the Fifth Judge wants her, he can send a formal summons to my supervisor, Officer Wu. Until then, she stays with me."

Li Qian stared at him for a long moment. The blue flames in his eyes flickered, dimmed, then flared again. "You're making a mistake."

"Maybe. But it's my mistake to make."

"Very well." Li Qian stepped back, rolling up his scroll. "I'll inform the Fifth Judge of your defiance. He'll handle you personally." He turned to leave, then paused. "Oh, and Jiang Chen? The woman you're protecting? She's right about one thing. Someone in Diyu is corrupt. But it's not me."

He vanished into shadow.

Lin Mei collapsed to her knees, massaging her throat. "That was close."

"Shut up." Jiang Chen grabbed her arm, pulling her up. "We're leaving. Now."

"Where are we going?"

"Somewhere he can't find us." He dragged her toward the edge of the rooftop. "And then you're going to tell me everything. Every name. Every file. Every detail you remember."

"And then?"

Jiang Chen looked at her. In the dim light, she looked almost human. Almost vulnerable. But he knew better.

"And then I'm going to decide whether to give you to the Fifth Judge, or use you to find the truth."

She smiled. "I knew you were smart."

"Save it." He jumped off the rooftop, pulling her with him. They landed in the alley below, and he started running, dragging her through the winding streets of the old city.

Behind them, the blue flames of Li Qian's eyes flickered in the darkness, watching.

Waiting.

title: The Eighth Judge's Summons
content:
The black light consumed her scream. Jiang Chen felt the staff vibrating in his hands, the soul-capturing runes blazing hot against his palms. But something was wrong. Wei Yinglan's soul didn't retreat into the staff's prison. It shattered.

He stumbled backward as fragments of her consciousness scattered across the interrogation chamber like broken glass catching moonlight. Each fragment held a piece of her face, a fragment of her laughter, a shard of her memories.

"You fool," her voice echoed from everywhere and nowhere. "Did you think I'd let myself be captured twice?"

The fragments swirled around him, forming a cyclone of stolen lives. He saw faces flash past—the accountant she'd possessed in Shanghai, the police officer in Beijing, the university professor in Nanjing. Seventeen years of identities, seventeen years of collected secrets.

"You're not a soul," Jiang Chen realized, gripping his staff tighter. "You're a collection."

"Sharp boy." The fragments coalesced into her shape, but she was translucent now, flickering between forms. "I'm what happens when a soul learns to consume other souls. The judges don't teach you that in reaper school, do they?"

She lunged. He swung the staff, but she passed through it like smoke, her fingers finding his throat. Cold. So cold. He felt her parasites—tiny pieces of other souls—burrowing into his skin.

"I'm going to ride you all the way back to Diyu," she whispered in his ear. "And when we get there, I'm going to show the judges exactly what kind of weapon they've been training."

The door exploded inward.

Not wood splintering. Not hinges breaking. The entire door *dissolved* into golden dust, and through the cloud walked a woman who radiated authority like heat from a furnace. She wore modern clothes—a black business suit, heels that clicked against the floor—but her eyes held the weight of centuries.

Wei Yinglan screamed.

Not a battle cry. A scream of recognition. Of terror.

The woman raised her hand, and golden chains erupted from the floor, wrapping around Wei Yinglan's fragmented form, pulling her together, compressing her into a tight ball of struggling light.

"Jiang Chen," the woman said, her voice carrying echoes of gongs and incense smoke. "I am Judge Zhao. Eighth Court of Diyu. You've been summoned."

The golden chains contracted. Wei Yinglan's struggles weakened, then stopped.

"Summoned?" Jiang Chen's voice cracked. "I'm in the middle of a mission."

"Your mission has been reassigned." Judge Zhao walked toward him, her heels making no sound on the floor. "There's been a development. The judges need your testimony regarding your death."

"My death? But Wei Yinglan said—"

"We know what she said." Judge Zhao stopped inches from him. Up close, he could see the cracks in her perfect composure. Something was wrong. Something had shaken her. "That's why you're being summoned."

"What about her?" He gestured at the compressed soul in the golden chains.

"She'll be processed. Her crimes will be judged." Judge Zhao's eyes flickered. "But your case takes priority."

She turned and walked toward the dissolved doorway. The golden dust had reformed into a shimmering portal, and through it, Jiang Chen could see the endless gray expanse of Diyu.

"Come," she said without looking back. "The other judges are waiting."

---

The Eighth Court looked nothing like the First.

Where Judge Qin's court had been ancient and formal, Judge Zhao's domain was a modern high-rise office. Glass walls. Fluorescent lights. Cubicles staffed by clerks who moved with mechanical precision. The only reminder of where he was came from the windows—they didn't show a city skyline. They showed the Yellow Springs, endless and churning, souls drifting through it like fish in murky water.

Judge Zhao led him to a conference room at the center of the floor. Through the glass walls, he could see nine figures seated around a circular table. Judge Qin was there, looking uncomfortable in the modern setting. The others he recognized from his training—Judge Yan of the Fourth Court, Judge Song of the Second. But there were faces he didn't know, judges he'd never met.

"Sit," Judge Zhao said, gesturing to an empty chair.

He sat. The chair was warm, as if someone had just vacated it.

"Jiang Chen," Judge Qin began, his voice carrying the weight of formality. "Three weeks ago, you were executed for the murder of Liu Wen, a crime you did not commit."

"I know." The words came out bitter. "I just found out."

"We know you just found out." Judge Song leaned forward. She was younger than the others, her face sharp and calculating. "We've known since the day you arrived."

The room went silent.

"You *knew*?" Jiang Chen stood up, his chair scraping against the floor. "You knew I was innocent, and you still made me a reaper?"

"We suspected," Judge Qin corrected. "There were irregularities in your file. Missing documents. A judge who couldn't be reached for verification. But we had no proof, and the underworld is understaffed. We needed reapers. You were available."

"So you used me."

"We gave you a second chance at existence," Judge Zhao said coldly. "Don't confuse mercy with altruism."

"Then why now?" Jiang Chen sat back down, his hands shaking. "Why summon me now?"

Judge Zhao pressed a button on the table. The glass walls went opaque, blocking out the view of the Yellow Springs. Holographic documents appeared above the table—his file, his execution order, photographs of the crime scene.

"Because three days ago," Judge Zhao said, "Judge Liu of the Seventh Court went missing."

"Missing?"

"Disappeared. His court found his robes folded neatly on his chair. His soul-recording crystal was blank. No one has seen him since."

"What does that have to do with me?"

"Judge Liu was the one who signed your execution order."

The words hung in the air like smoke. Jiang Chen stared at the documents, at the signature at the bottom of his death warrant. The characters were bold and deliberate, the stroke of someone who didn't hesitate.

"Someone killed a judge," he said slowly, "to cover up my case?"

"Or someone killed a judge to prevent him from talking." Judge Yan's voice was gravelly, ancient. "Either way, the corruption runs deeper than we thought."

"What do you want me to do?"

"We want you to investigate." Judge Zhao's holographic display shifted, showing a map of the mortal realm with markers scattered across it. "You have connections to the living world that we don't. You can walk among them without raising suspicion. Find Judge Liu. Find out who killed him. And find out why."

"I'm not a detective. I'm an accountant."

"Was an accountant. Now you're a soul reaper." Judge Song smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "And you have personal stakes in this investigation. The person who framed you is connected to whoever killed Judge Liu. We're certain of it."

"How?"

"Because Judge Liu was the one who falsified your evidence. He was paid to do it. And whoever paid him didn't want him alive to testify about it."

Jiang Chen's mind raced. Someone in the living world had enough money and influence to corrupt a judge of the dead. A judge. One of the Ten. The thought was terrifying.

"If I do this," he said, "what do I get?"

"Justice," Judge Zhao said. "The truth about your death. And if you succeed, we'll expunge your record. You'll be given a new identity, a new life. You can start over."

"And if I refuse?"

"You won't." Judge Qin's voice was gentle but firm. "You've already tasted the truth. You can't un-taste it."

He was right. Jiang Chen knew he was right. The moment Wei Yinglan had shown him the truth, he'd lost the ability to pretend everything was fine.

"Fine," he said. "I'll do it."

"Good." Judge Zhao stood up. "You'll start tonight. We've arranged a meeting with Judge Liu's last known contact in the mortal realm. A woman named Chen Mei. She was his informant for the past decade."

"Where do I find her?"

"Shanghai. She runs a funeral home in the old district." Judge Zhao handed him a business card. It was plain white, with only an address printed on it. "She's expecting you."

Jiang Chen took the card. It felt heavier than it should have.

"One more thing," Judge Zhao said. "Wei Yinglan wasn't lying about everything. She really could have given you names. But she was also right about something else—you're not ready for this."

"I passed the trials."

"The trials test your ability to follow orders. This will test your ability to make decisions." She looked at him, and for a moment, he saw something like pity in her eyes. "Don't make the wrong ones."

---

The portal deposited him in a Shanghai alley, two hours before dawn.

The funeral home was exactly where the address said it would be—a narrow building wedged between a convenience store and a massage parlor. The sign above the door read "Chen's Last Journey" in faded gold characters.

He knocked.

The door opened immediately, as if she'd been waiting. Chen Mei was older than he expected, maybe fifty, with gray-streaked hair and eyes that had seen too many bodies. She wore a traditional black qipao and smelled of incense and formaldehyde.

"You're the reaper," she said. It wasn't a question.

"I'm here about Judge Liu."

"He's dead." She stepped aside, letting him in. "I felt it when he died. Three days ago. At midnight."

"How?"

She led him through a hallway lined with urns and photographs. The air was thick with smoke from burning paper money. "He was investigating something. Something big. He didn't tell me what, but he was scared. I'd never seen him scared before."

"What was he investigating?"

"My death."

Jiang Chen stopped. "What?"

Chen Mei turned to face him. Her eyes were wet, but she wasn't crying. "I died seven years ago. Judge Liu helped me fake my death so I could keep working as his informant. But someone found out. Someone killed him to keep the secret."

"Who?"

"I don't know. But I have something that might help." She walked to a desk in the corner and opened a drawer. Inside was a small jade box, ancient and carved with characters he couldn't read.

"What's that?"

"A soul-recording crystal. Judge Liu's backup. He gave it to me for safekeeping." She held it out to him. "It contains everything he knew. Every case. Every secret. Every name."

Jiang Chen reached for it.

The door exploded.

Not like before. This time, it was violence. Wood and metal screamed as two figures crashed through, moving too fast to be human. They wore masks—white, featureless, like the faces of corpses.

Chen Mei screamed.

Jiang Chen grabbed his staff and swung.

The first figure caught it with bare hands. The impact sent shockwaves up Jiang Chen's arms, and he felt something crack in his wrist. The figure twisted, wrenching the staff from his grip, and threw it against the wall.

The second figure grabbed Chen Mei.

"No!" Jiang Chen lunged, but the first figure intercepted him, driving a fist into his chest. He felt ribs break. He hit the ground hard, gasping.

The figure with Chen Mei raised a hand. Black energy gathered around its fingers.

"Stop!" Jiang Chen tried to stand, but his legs wouldn't

title: Chapter 19
content:
CHAPTER 19: The Man Who Didn't Die

The explosion of black light threw Jiang Chen backward. He crashed through a display of ceramic cats, shattering them into porcelain confetti. Pain lanced through his ribs. The taste of copper filled his mouth.

The woman was gone.

Where she'd stood, a dark smear of soul residue clung to the floor like oil. The staff hummed in his hands, vibrating with residual energy. He'd hit her hard. Too hard. Maybe killed her.

*Shit.*

He scrambled to his feet, ignoring the shards of ceramic digging into his palms. The apartment was empty. The windows were still open. The curtains still danced in the wind. But the presence that had filled the room—that oppressive, suffocating weight of her soul—had vanished.

*No no no.*

He ran to the window. The street below was quiet. A stray dog trotted past a pile of trash bags. A single streetlamp flickered, casting jumping shadows across the asphalt. No sign of her.

"You've got to be kidding me."

He'd just lost his only lead. The only person who could tell him the truth about his death. The only person who'd given him names—*names*—and he'd let her slip through his fingers because he'd panicked.

He punched the wall. The plaster cracked.

Then he heard it.

A sound. Faint. Coming from the bathroom.

He turned, staff ready. The bathroom door was slightly ajar, and a sliver of light spilled across the tile floor. He approached slowly, each step deliberate, his senses stretched to their limit. The sound came again—a wet, choking gasp. Like someone drowning.

He kicked the door open.

The man from the bathtub—the soul whose body the woman had stolen—was sitting up.

Water and blood sloshed over the sides of the tub. His skin was gray, mottled with patches of blue-black bruising. His eyes were wide, unfocused, pupils dilated. He was breathing in short, ragged gasps, each one a struggle. His hands gripped the edges of the tub, knuckles white, fingernails cracked and bleeding.

"Help," he croaked. "Help me."

Jiang Chen stared. This wasn't possible. The man had been dead for hours. He'd seen the body. Cold. Stiff. No pulse. No breath. The soul had been completely severed, driven out by the woman's possession. There was no coming back from that.

And yet.

"Stay still," Jiang Chen said, his voice barely steady. "I'm going to call an ambulance."

"No." The man's hand shot out, grabbing Jiang Chen's wrist. His grip was surprisingly strong for someone who should be dead. "No hospital. They'll ask questions. They'll take me away."

"You need medical attention."

"I need answers." The man's eyes locked onto Jiang Chen's. There was something feverish in them, something desperate and hungry. "My body. She took my body. I felt everything. I was trapped inside my own head, watching her move my hands, speak with my voice, and I couldn't—" His voice cracked. "I couldn't do anything."

Jiang Chen pried the man's fingers off his wrist. "I know. I dealt with her."

"Did you kill her?"

"No."

"Then she's still out there. She's still wearing my face." The man tried to stand, but his legs gave out. He collapsed back into the tub, sending another wave of pink-tinged water over the edge. "I need to find her. I need to get my life back."

"Your life is over." The words came out harsher than Jiang Chen intended. "I'm sorry, but you died. Your body should be dead. I don't know how you're still—"

"I'm not dead." The man's voice dropped to a whisper. "I'm undead. I'm a revenant."

Jiang Chen felt the temperature in the room drop. He'd read about revenants in the judge's manuals. They were rare. Extremely rare. They happened when a soul was violently displaced from its body but somehow managed to claw its way back before the connection was completely severed. The result was a creature caught between life and death—alive enough to function, dead enough to smell the rot.

And they always, *always* went mad.

"You need to come with me," Jiang Chen said. "There are people who can help you. Judges in Diyu—"

"I'm not going to hell." The man's eyes widened. "I'm not dead. I don't belong there."

"You don't belong here either. You're a revenant. Your soul is damaged. If you don't get treatment, you'll—"

"Treatment?" The man laughed, a hollow, broken sound. "There's no treatment. I know what I am. I've heard the stories. Revenants decay. Their minds rot. They become monsters." He looked at his hands. "I can already feel it. The hunger. The anger. I want to hurt someone. I want to tear something apart."

Jiang Chen tightened his grip on the staff. "Then let me take you to Diyu. The judges can help you cross over peacefully."

"Cross over?" The man's head snapped up. "You mean die. You want to kill me."

"You're already dead. I'm just trying to help you—"

"You're trying to silence me." The man stood, and this time his legs held. Water dripped from his body, pooling around his bare feet. "She wore my face. She used my hands to steal from people. She ruined my reputation, my relationships, my *life*. And now you want to erase what's left of me so no one ever finds out the truth."

"I don't care about the truth. I care about stopping her before she kills someone else."

"Then help me find her."

"I can't. You're unstable."

"I'm your best lead." The man stepped closer. The smell of decay was stronger now, mixed with the metallic tang of blood. "I know her habits. I know where she likes to hide. I know the names of the people she's conned, the families she's ruined, the lives she's destroyed. I've been inside her head for the past three months, and I know everything she knows."

Jiang Chen hesitated.

"Think about it," the man continued. "You need her. She has information about your death. She told you she could prove you were framed. She's not going to give you that information willingly. But I can help you find her. I can help you force her to talk."

"Or you could lead me into a trap."

"Why would I do that?"

"Because revenants hunger for living souls. And I'm the closest thing to a living soul you're going to find in this apartment."

The man's face flickered. For a split second, Jiang Chen saw it—the hunger. The raw, primal need that burned in the depths of his eyes. The same hunger that drove revenants to murder. The same hunger that turned them into the very monsters the judges hunted.

But then it was gone, replaced by a weary smile.

"You're right," the man said. "I do hunger. Every second, I feel it. The urge to tear your throat out, to drink your blood, to absorb your life force. It's constant. It's exhausting. But I'm not going to act on it. I'm not a monster. Not yet."

"How do I know you're telling the truth?"

"You don't." The man shrugged. "But you're a soul reaper. If I try anything, you'll put me down. And honestly?" He laughed again, a bitter, broken sound. "Part of me wants you to. At least then this would be over."

Jiang Chen studied him. The man was a wreck—physically, mentally, spiritually. His soul was fractured, leaking energy like a cracked vessel. He was a danger to himself and everyone around him. The smart thing to do would be to take him to Diyu, let the judges process him, let them extract whatever information they could before his mind completely deteriorated.

But the smart thing wasn't always the right thing.

"What's your name?" Jiang Chen asked.

"Zhang Wei." The man straightened, trying to look dignified despite being naked, soaking wet, and covered in blood. "I was a debt collector. Three months ago, I came home from work, took a bath, and woke up trapped in my own body while a stranger used me like a puppet."

"I'm Jiang Chen."

"I know. I heard your conversation with her. You were framed for murder." Zhang Wei's eyes gleamed. "She showed me your death. The execution. The witnesses. The judge who took the bribe."

Jiang Chen's blood ran cold. "You saw it?"

"I saw everything she saw. For three months, I was a passenger in her mind. I saw every face, every crime, every secret. I know who killed you." Zhang Wei smiled. "I know the names."

"Tell me."

"Not yet. First, we find her. Then I'll tell you everything."

"You're bargaining with me."

"I'm offering you a deal. You help me get my life back. I help you get your revenge." Zhang Wei extended his hand. "Deal?"

Jiang Chen looked at the hand. It was the hand of a dead man, pale and trembling. It was the hand of a man who could snap at any moment, who could turn on him and try to devour his soul. It was a dangerous gamble, trusting a revenant.

But Zhang Wei was right about one thing. He was Jiang Chen's best lead.

He took the hand.

"Deal."

They shook. Zhang Wei's grip was cold and clammy, like gripping a corpse. Jiang Chen tried not to think about that.

"First," Zhang Wei said, "we need clothes. I can't exactly walk around like this."

"I'll find something."

"And then we need to get to the train station. She'll be heading for the south, trying to leave the city. I know her escape routes. I know her safe houses. We can cut her off."

"How do you know all this?"

"Because I was her. For three months, I was her." Zhang Wei's voice dropped to a whisper. "She showed me everything. She wanted me to understand her suffering, to sympathize with her, to accept what she was doing. She thought if I understood, I would stop fighting. She thought she could break me."

"Did she?"

"No." Zhang Wei's eyes hardened. "She made me stronger. She showed me her weaknesses. She showed me how to find her, how to trap her, how to—"

He stopped.

His body convulsed.

"Zhang Wei?"

The man's face contorted. His eyes rolled back. His mouth opened, and a sound came out—not his voice, but *hers*. The woman's voice, distorted and echoing, as if speaking from a great distance.

*"You think you can find me?"*

Jiang Chen grabbed his staff. "She's possessing him again."

*"I'm always watching, little reaper. I have eyes everywhere. I have bodies everywhere."*

"Where are you?"

*"Close. Very close. I'm coming for you."*

Zhang Wei's body went limp. He collapsed to the floor, twitching.

And then the lights went out.

Jiang Chen stood in the darkness, staff raised, heart pounding. The apartment was silent except for Zhang Wei's ragged breathing.

Then he heard it.

Footsteps.

Coming from the hallway.

Coming closer.

And a voice—her voice—whispering from the shadows:

*"Let's play, little reaper."*

title: Chapter 20
content:
CHAPTER 20: The Third Body

The black light from Jiang Chen's staff didn't just push her back.

It *tore* her.

Lin Shuiying's soul fragmented like glass hitting concrete, shattering into a dozen shimmering pieces that scattered across the abandoned warehouse floor. Each piece writhed independently, trying to reform, trying to remember what it was supposed to be.

Jiang Chen stumbled backward, gasping. The staff's feedback burned up his arms, through his chest, into his skull. He'd never used that much power before. He'd never *needed* to.

"Look what you did," the pieces whispered. Dozen voices overlapping, out of sync, speaking in fragments. "Look look look what you *did*."

The largest piece—her torso, her head, one arm—crawled across the concrete toward him. Her face was half there, half scattered. One eye blinked. The other was empty space.

"You broke me."

"I'll do worse." His voice shook. "Stay back."

"Why?" The fragments laughed, a chorus of discordant notes. "You need me. You need my *names*."

She was right.

He hated that she was right.

Jiang Chen forced himself to breathe. The warehouse smelled like rust and rat droppings. Moonlight filtered through broken windows, illuminating the swirling dust motes that danced around Lin Shuiying's scattered soul.

"I can put myself back together," she said, the largest piece dragging itself closer. "But it'll take time. And every second we waste, your truth gets buried deeper."

"Stop moving."

"Make me."

He raised the staff again. The black light flickered weakly. He'd spent too much of whatever fuel powered it. The Judge's Seal on his wrist felt cold, distant. He was running on empty.

"Ah." Lin Shuiying's half-face smiled. "You're tapped out. How unfortunate."

The scattered fragments began to move faster, sliding across the floor like mercury, converging toward the central mass. She was right—she *was* putting herself back together. And he was too exhausted to stop her.

Jiang Chen did the only thing he could think of.

He sat down.

"What are you doing?"

"Thinking."

"While I regenerate?"

"You're a con woman." He rubbed his face. "You've been lying for seventeen years. You lied to your victims. You lied to the soul reapers who caught you. You're lying to me right now."

"I'm not—"

"You *are*. Because that's what you do. It's not even about survival anymore. It's just who you are." He looked at her. "So I need to figure out which parts of what you said were true and which were bait."

Her pieces stopped moving.

"Let's start with the obvious. You said you could prove I was framed. You said you had names. But you're a ghost who's been possessing people for seventeen years. You haven't been in the mortal world. You haven't talked to anyone connected to my case."

Her face flickered.

"So how would you know anything about my death?"

The silence stretched.

"You want me to answer that." Her voice was different now. Softer. Almost careful. "You want me to prove I'm telling the truth."

"I want you to stop playing games."

"Then listen." The fragments began to merge, slower now, deliberate. "When I possess someone, I don't just see their memories. I see their *connections*. Every person they've met. Every secret they've kept. Every crime they've witnessed."

"And?"

"And your execution was witnessed by three hundred people. Reporters. Officials. A judge. A doctor. Guards." Her face reformed, piece by piece. "But one of those witnesses wasn't who he said he was."

Jiang Chen's chest tightened.

"His name was Zhang Wei. He was listed as a medical examiner's assistant. But when I possessed the real Zhang Wei three years ago, I saw his memories. He never worked at that prison. He never even visited."

"Someone replaced him."

"Someone replaced him for exactly one day. The day of your execution." She tilted her head. "Do you want to know who paid him?"

"You're going to say you don't know."

"No." She smiled. "I *do* know. Because the real Zhang Wei was curious. He tried to investigate the imposter. And the imposter caught him. Threatened him. Told him that if he ever spoke about it, his wife would die."

Jiang Chen's hands were shaking again. "Who paid him?"

"A man named Chen Feng. A mid-level bureaucrat in the Ministry of Justice." She paused. "But Chen Feng didn't act alone. He was just a messenger. The real money came from someone higher."

"Who?"

"I don't have that name. Chen Feng never knew it. He only knew that the payments came from an offshore account registered to a shell company called..." She frowned, searching her fragmented memories. "Jade Mountain Holdings."

The name meant nothing to him.

But it was something. A thread.

"Take me back to Diyu," she said. "Let me speak to the judges. I'll give them everything I have. Not just about your case. About every crime I've witnessed, every secret I've stolen, every body I've worn."

"You're still trying to make a deal."

"I'm trying to *survive*." Her voice cracked. "You don't understand what happens to ghosts like me in Diyu. They don't just judge us. They *erase* us. Our memories. Our identities. Everything we were."

"That's the point of judgment."

"Easy for you to say. You're alive. You get to walk back to the mortal world. You get to have a future." Her eyes burned. "I've been dead for twenty-three years. I've spent seventeen of them clinging to existence. I don't want to be erased."

Jiang Chen stared at her.

He understood her.

That was still his mistake.

"Fine," he said. "I'll take you to Diyu. But not to the judges. Not yet."

"Then where?"

"To the records hall. I need to check something first."

She studied him. "You don't trust me."

"I trust that you're using me. The question is whether I can use you back." He stood, gripping his staff. The exhaustion was bone-deep, but he could manage a few more hours. "If the information you gave me is real, I'll advocate for you. I'll tell the judges you cooperated."

"And if it's not?"

"Then I'll personally make sure they erase you twice."

She laughed. It was almost genuine.

"You're learning, Soul Reaper. Give you a few more decades, and you might actually be good at this."

"Shut up and move."

They left the warehouse together.

---

The path to Diyu was different every time he crossed it.

Sometimes it was a dark tunnel. Sometimes a staircase descending into fog. Sometimes a river he had to wade through. Tonight, it was a mirror.

He found it in an alley behind a noodle shop. A full-length mirror propped against a wall, its surface shimmering like water. The reflection showed a street that didn't match the one behind him.

"Through there," he said.

Lin Shuiying hesitated. "I've never returned voluntarily."

"There's a first time for everything."

She stepped through first. Her soul rippled as it crossed the threshold, stretching like taffy before snapping back into shape. Jiang Chen followed, and the world turned upside down.

Diyu was cold.

Not the cold of winter or high altitude. The cold of *absence*. The cold of places where warmth had never existed and never would. The sky was a bruised purple, perpetually twilight. The ground was cracked obsidian that reflected nothing.

In the distance, the Ten Courts rose like black teeth against the horizon.

"Records hall is in the Fourth Court," Jiang Chen said. "Stay close. Don't talk to anyone."

"Anyone? There's no one here."

He grabbed her arm and pulled her behind a pillar just as a procession of Soul Scribes walked past. They were humanoid but wrong—their bodies made of paper, their faces blank except for mouths that moved constantly, whispering the deeds of the dead.

Lin Shuiying pressed herself against him, her soul shivering.

"I forgot how terrible this place is."

"You get used to it."

"No. You really don't."

They moved through the shadows of Diyu, avoiding patrols, slipping through gates that shouldn't have opened for them. Jiang Chen's Judge's Seal got them through most checkpoints. The rest, he bribed with memories—small ones, worthless ones, the taste of his mother's cooking, the smell of rain on concrete.

After an hour, they reached the Fourth Court's records hall.

It was a building the size of a city block, filled with shelves that stretched into infinity. Each shelf held scrolls. Each scroll held a life. Every human who had ever lived was recorded here, their deeds and misdeeds catalogued in a script that only the judges could read.

"Stay by the door," Jiang Chen said. "If anyone comes, warn me."

"What are you looking for?"

"My file."

He walked into the maze of shelves, running his fingers along the scrolls. They were organized by date of death. Recent decades were near the front. He found the shelf for his execution date and pulled a scroll at random.

*Name: Li Wei. Born: 1987. Died: 2024. Cause: Cardiac arrest. Judgment: Reincarnation (Animal Realm, third tier).*

He put it back. Pulled another.

*Name: Zhang Xiaoling. Born: 1992. Died: 2024. Cause: Suicide by jumping. Judgment: Penance Hall, 47 years.*

Another.

*Name: Wang Jun. Born: 1985. Died: 2024. Cause: Execution by lethal injection. Judgment: Pending.*

His hands stopped.

The scroll was labeled with his name. His execution date. His cause of death.

He unrolled it.

And the world stopped.

*Name: Jiang Chen. Born: 1991. Died: 2024. Cause: Execution by lethal injection.*

*Judgment: Pending.*

Below that, in a different hand, a note had been added.

*Case flagged for review. Original sentencing judge found to have accepted bribes. Evidence of tampering confirmed. Recommend full exoneration and compensation.*

*Signed: Judge Zhao, Third Court.*

Jiang Chen stared at the words.

Exoneration.

Compensation.

He'd been dead for three weeks. And Diyu already knew he was innocent.

"Found something interesting?"

He spun around.

Lin Shuiying was standing behind him, reading over his shoulder.

"You said the judges didn't know."

"I said they might not care." She shrugged. "Looks like they do."

"Then why didn't anyone tell me?"

"Because you're a Soul Reaper now. Your old life doesn't matter. Your death doesn't matter. What matters is that you collect souls and stay out of trouble." She tapped the scroll. "They probably planned to tell you after your probation period. Or never."

Jiang Chen's hands curled into fists.

"Who signed the review?"

"Judge Zhao. Third Court." She pointed. "But look at the date."

He looked.

*Date of review: Day 4 of your death.*

Four days after his execution. They'd found out almost immediately.

And they hadn't told him.

"Why?" His voice was barely a whisper.

"Because keeping you angry keeps you useful." Lin Shuiying's eyes glittered. "A Soul Reaper with nothing to lose is dangerous. A Soul Reaper with something to *gain* is predictable."

"I'm not—"

"You are." She stepped closer.

title: Chapter 6
content:
CHAPTER 6: The Price of Answers

The black light threw her across the room.

She crashed through a display case, shattering glass and scattering porcelain figurines across the floor. The old woman's body lay twisted among the debris, but the soul had already separated—a shimmering form rising from the broken shell.

"You're quicker than the others," she said, floating above her stolen vessel. "But just as stupid."

Jiang Chen gripped his staff, the wood warm against his palms. The ghost of a rich couple watched from the corner, huddled together, their forms flickering with fear. He'd deal with them after.

"I'm not letting you go," he said.

"You're not letting me go?" She laughed, the sound echoing off the walls. "Child, I've been possessing bodies since before you were born. I've seen dynasties rise and fall. I've worn emperors and beggars, generals and whores. You think a stick of wood and a fancy title scare me?"

She lunged.

Not at him—at the window.

Jiang Chen swung the staff, extending it mid-arc. The chain shot out, wrapping around her ankle, yanking her back. She twisted, claws raking across his chest. The pain was sharp, immediate, blood soaking through his shirt.

But he didn't let go.

"Impressive," she snarled. "You can take a hit. Most of your kind can't."

"Most of your kind don't kill soul reapers."

"I told you. I'm not most of anything."

She dissolved, reforming behind him. He spun, too slow. Her hand passed through his back, ice spreading through his lungs. He gasped, dropping to one knee. The staff clattered against the floor.

"Here's what's going to happen," she said, standing over him. "You're going to let me leave. You're going to pretend you never found me. And in return, I'll give you something better than names."

"Nothing's better than—"

"The truth about your death."

He looked up. Her form wavered, translucent, but her eyes were solid—black pits that swallowed light.

"I told you. I can show you who framed you. But I can do more than that. I can show you *why*."

"You're lying."

"I'm a liar. That's my nature." She crouched down, bringing her face close to his. "But I'm also a survivor. And right now, surviving means convincing you not to drag me back to Diyu. So I'll give you a sample. Free of charge."

She touched his forehead.

The world dissolved.

---

He was back in the courtroom.

Not the execution chamber. The trial. Six months ago. He sat in the defendant's box, wearing a cheap suit that didn't fit, his hands cuffed to the rail. The judge stared down at him with cold, bored eyes.

"The prosecution calls He Wei."

A man rose from the gallery. Mid-fifties. Expensive suit. Gold-rimmed glasses. The prosecutor. He walked to the witness stand with the confidence of someone who'd never lost a case.

"Mr. He, can you describe the defendant's relationship with the victim?"

"Jiang Chen was his accountant. He handled all his financial affairs."

"And did you find any irregularities in those accounts?"

"Significant ones. Over three million yuan was transferred to offshore accounts over a period of eighteen months. The trail leads directly to the defendant's personal holdings."

The courtroom murmured. The judge banged his gavel.

"Mr. Jiang, how do you plead?"

"Not guilty."

"On what basis?"

"The accounts were altered. Someone changed the numbers."

"And do you have evidence of this tampering?"

He'd frozen. Of course he didn't have evidence. The evidence had been destroyed. The files had been deleted. The witnesses had disappeared. He was a low-level accountant who'd made the mistake of trusting the wrong people.

The judge nodded, as if he'd expected nothing less. "The court finds the defendant guilty. Sentencing will be—"

The scene shattered.

---

Jiang Chen gasped, tearing away from the ghost's touch. He was back in the antique shop, on his knees, sweat pouring down his face.

"That was real," he whispered.

"Every detail. I saw it in your memories, but I also saw it in others. I've possessed people connected to your case." She stood, brushing off her spectral robes. "Your prosecutor is clean. Too clean. His finances are immaculate. His reputation is spotless. No one gets that clean without help."

"Who helped him?"

"Now that would be telling." She smiled. "And telling requires payment."

"I already told you. I can't let you go."

"You can't stop me either." She gestured to the broken window. "I could leave right now. Find another body. Start over. You'd spend years hunting me, if you survived the night. But I'm offering you a shortcut. A direct line to the people who killed you."

"Why would you help me?"

"Because I'm tired." For a moment, something flickered in her eyes. Genuine exhaustion. "I've been running for seventeen years. I've worn so many faces I've forgotten my own. I want to rest. But I don't want to be erased. I don't want to become a blank slate for the judges to reprogram."

"That's not what they—"

"Yes it is. Don't lie to me. I've seen what happens to souls who return to Diyu. They get processed. Cleaned. Reborn as blank pages. Everything they were, everything they did, everything they loved—gone." She shook her head. "I'd rather be destroyed."

Jiang Chen understood.

He'd felt the same way, three weeks ago, strapped to that table. He'd accepted oblivion because he thought he deserved it. But now he knew he didn't. Someone had stolen his life, his death, his legacy.

"You want a deal," he said slowly.

"I want a compromise. You don't drag me back. I don't run. We work together."

"For how long?"

"Until you find your answers. Then I'll go willingly. I'll face the judges. I'll tell them everything I know. If they decide to erase me, so be it. At least I'll have done something meaningful."

It was a trap. It had to be a trap. Everything about this woman screamed manipulation.

But she was also right. He couldn't stop her. If she ran, he'd never catch her. He was one soul reaper against a ghost who'd killed two of his predecessors. He was outmatched, outclassed, out-experienced.

He needed help.

"Fine," he said. "But we do this my way."

"Name your terms."

"You're coming with me to meet someone. Another soul reaper. She's older, more experienced. If she agrees to the arrangement, I'll go along with it."

"And if she doesn't?"

"Then you run, and I hunt you."

She considered this, tilting her head like a bird. "Acceptable. But if she tries to bind me, I'll kill her. And you."

"You won't have the chance."

He pulled out his phone. The screen was cracked from the fight, but it still worked. He dialed the number he'd memorized from his training.

It rang three times.

"Zhao Min," a voice said. Female. Sharp. "This better be important. I'm in the middle of—"

"I need help."

Silence.

"Who is this?"

"Jiang Chen. The new soul reaper. The one who—"

"I know who you are. Why are you calling me?"

"Because I caught a ghost. A powerful one. She's killed two of us. I can't handle her alone."

Another silence. Longer this time.

"Where are you?"

He gave her the address.

"Don't move. Don't do anything stupid. I'll be there in twenty minutes."

The line went dead.

Jiang Chen pocketed the phone. The ghost watched him, amused.

"She sounds nice."

"She's terrifying."

"Good. I like terrifying people. They're honest about what they are."

She drifted to the corner, where the couple still huddled. They flinched as she approached.

"Don't worry," she said. "I won't hurt you. I'm done hurting people for now." She looked back at Jiang Chen. "Though I make no promises about the future."

Jiang Chen sat down, his back against a display case. His chest still throbbed where she'd clawed him. Blood had soaked through his shirt, staining the floor.

"You need to heal that," she said.

"I'll live."

"Will you?" She smiled. "That's the question, isn't it? What exactly are you, Jiang Chen? Alive enough to walk among the living. Dead enough to see the dead. But where's the line? When does one become the other?"

"I don't know."

"Neither do I. But I'm curious to find out."

---

Twenty minutes passed like hours.

Zhao Min arrived on a motorcycle, killing the engine outside the shop. She walked in wearing leather pants and a jacket, her hair pulled back in a tight ponytail. She looked at the destruction—the shattered glass, the broken furniture, the old woman's body in the corner—and sighed.

"You've been busy."

"It wasn't my fault."

"It never is." She looked at the ghost. "You're the Debt Collector."

"I've been called worse."

"I've heard stories about you. Thirty-two confirmed possessions. Seventeen stolen identities. Two dead soul reapers." Zhao Min's hand drifted to her belt, where a staff similar to Jiang Chen's hung. "You're quite the legend."

"I try."

"And now you want to work with us."

"Temporarily. Until your apprentice gets his answers."

Zhao Min looked at Jiang Chen. "What answers?"

"She knows who framed me. She can prove it."

"And you believe her?"

"She showed me my trial. She knew details I'd never told anyone."

"That's impressive." Zhao Min didn't sound impressed. "But possession isn't mind reading. She could have extracted those details from your own memories and reconstructed them. It's a common trick."

The ghost laughed. "Sharp. I like her."

"I'm not trying to be liked." Zhao Min stepped closer. "I'm trying to decide whether to kill you or not."

"You can't kill me. I'm already dead."

"Don't be pedantic. You know what I mean."

The ghost's amusement faded. "Yes. I know."

Zhao Min studied her for a long moment. Then she turned to Jiang Chen.

"Her soul is unstable. I can see the cracks. She's been jumping bodies for too long without proper anchoring. Another year, maybe two, and she'll dissolve on her own."

"That's not—"

"It's the truth," the ghost interrupted. "I told you. I'm tired. I don't have much time left. That's why I'm willing to negotiate."

Zhao Min nodded slowly. "So this is a deathbed confession."

"Something like that."

"Fine." Zhao Min pulled out a small jade seal, engraved with characters Jiang Chen couldn't read. "I'll bind you. Not to Diyu, but to us. You stay within a hundred meters of Jiang Chen at all times. You don't possess anyone without my permission. You answer all questions truthfully."

"And in return?"

"We'll hear you out. If your information leads to justice, I'll personally advocate for a lighter sentence. Maybe a memory wipe instead of full erasure."

"That's not—"

"It's the best offer you'll get. Take it or leave it."

The ghost looked at the seal. Her form flickered, uncertainty bleeding through.

"Fine," she said. "

title: Chapter 7
content:
CHAPTER 7: The Price of Names

The black light from Jiang Chen's staff ripped through Lin Mei like fire through dry grass. She screamed—a sound that wasn't entirely human, layered with echoes of every voice she'd ever stolen.

He should have pulled back. Should have given her space to recover, to negotiate, to keep the deal alive.

Instead, he pushed harder.

The staff sank deeper into her spectral chest, and he felt something break. A seal. A lock. Something she'd been hiding behind for years.

Her form flickered. For a split second, he saw her true face—not the middle-aged woman she'd stolen, not the young girl she'd once been, but the moment of her death. Thirty years old. Strangled. Her body dumped in a river.

Then the mask snapped back into place.

"Stop!" She clawed at the staff, her fingers passing through the black energy. "You'll destroy me!"

"That's the idea."

"You'll lose the names forever!"

He hesitated. The staff wavered.

She saw it. Her eyes—those dead, calculating eyes—locked onto his hesitation like a wolf spotting weakness. "They're in my head. All of them. The judge who signed your death warrant. The prosecutor who fabricated evidence. The witness who lied." She gasped, her form flickering again. "Kill me, and you'll never know."

Jiang Chen's grip tightened on the staff. "You'll tell me anyway. Before I take you back."

"You think I'm afraid of Diyu?" She laughed, bloodless and bitter. "I've been running from them for seventeen years. I know every loophole, every delay, every appeal. Your precious judges can't touch me without evidence, and the only evidence is in my head." She tapped her temple. "Which I will erase the moment I feel them prying."

He'd heard of soul erasure. A technique some ghosts used to destroy their own memories before capture. It left them hollow, empty shells—but it also left their captors with nothing.

"You wouldn't."

"Watch me." Her eyes went cold. "I've lived this long by always having an exit. This is mine. You take me to Diyu, I wipe my mind. You take the names from me here, I wipe them too. The only way you get what you want is if you help me escape."

Jiang Chen's hands shook. The staff felt like lead.

"You want revenge," she said softly. "I can feel it in you. That cold, quiet rage. The kind that builds for years because you never let yourself feel it. They took your life, Jiang Chen. They took your future. They made you a murderer in the eyes of everyone who knew you."

"Shut up."

"Your mother. Does she visit your grave? Does she know the truth?"

"I said shut up."

"She probably blames herself. Thinks she raised a monster. Cries herself to sleep every night wondering where she went wrong."

He swung the staff. She dodged, barely, the black energy grazing her shoulder. She hissed in pain.

"Hit a nerve?"

"You know nothing about my mother."

"I know she's alive. I know she visits the cemetery every Sunday. I know she leaves oranges at your grave because those were your favorite as a child." Lin Mei smiled. "I saw it in your memories when I tried to possess you. You love her. You'd do anything to clear your name so she doesn't have to carry that shame."

Jiang Chen's vision blurred. He blinked rapidly, refusing to let the tears fall.

"Help me escape," she said, "and I'll give you everything. The names. The evidence. The location of every document, every witness, every piece of proof you need to overturn your case. You'll die again when your time comes, but you'll die innocent. Your mother will know the truth."

"And you?" His voice cracked. "You'll keep stealing bodies. Keep conning families. Keep killing anyone who gets in your way."

"Probably."

"Then I'd be responsible for every person you hurt from now on."

She shrugged. "That's the price of your justice."

He stared at her. This woman—this monster—had killed two soul reapers. She'd stolen a woman's body and worn it like a costume for three years. She'd taken everything from the family she'd conned, leaving them broken and bankrupt.

And now she was offering him his life back.

Not literally. He was still dead. But his name. His honor. The truth.

Was it worth the cost?

"One condition," he said slowly.

Her eyebrow raised.

"You stop possessing people. From now on, you find other ways to hide. You don't kill anyone else."

"And when the judges send another reaper after me?"

"I'll handle it."

She laughed. "You'll protect me? A reaper protecting a rogue soul?"

"I'm not protecting you. I'm making sure you don't add to your debt before I collect it."

"Collect it?"

"When I get what I need, I'm coming for you. And next time, I won't listen to a word you say."

She studied him for a long moment. Her eyes searched his face, looking for the lie, the trap, the angle. She found nothing because there was nothing. He meant every word.

"Deal," she said.

She extended her hand again. Same gesture as before. Same trap.

Jiang Chen grabbed her wrist—not her hand, but her wrist, where he could feel her pulse (ghosts didn't have pulses, but stolen bodies did) and pulled her close.

"The names. Start talking."

"Here? In the middle of a cemetery?"

"Now. Before I change my mind."

She sighed, but there was a flicker of respect in her eyes. "Fine. But not all of them. Just one. Enough to prove I'm telling the truth."

"One name."

"Judge Zhang Wei. He presides over the Third Criminal Court in Jiangzhou. He's the one who signed your death warrant."

Jiang Chen's blood ran cold. He knew that name. Everyone in Jiangzhou knew that name. Judge Zhang was famous—revered, even. He'd presided over dozens of high-profile cases, always delivering harsh sentences for corruption. He'd built his career on being incorruptible.

"Impossible."

"Impossible?" Lin Mei laughed. "He's the most corrupt judge in the province. He just hides it better than the rest."

"Prove it."

"Look at your case file. The evidence was submitted by his nephew. The witness was a paid informant who worked for his brother-in-law. The prosecutor was his golfing partner." She counted on her fingers. "Every single person involved in your conviction was connected to him. You just didn't see it because you were too busy being terrified."

Jiang Chen's mind raced. He tried to remember the trial. The faces. The names. It had all been a blur of fear and confusion, of lawyers speaking in legal jargon he couldn't understand, of a gavel falling and a sentence being read.

But she was right about one thing. He hadn't looked. He'd been too scared, too broken, too convinced of his own guilt to question anything.

"Why?" he whispered. "Why would he want me dead?"

"That's the question, isn't it?" Lin Mei's eyes gleamed. "What did a nobody accountant do to earn the attention of one of the most powerful judges in Jiangzhou?"

He didn't know. He'd worked for a small firm, handling books for local businesses. He'd taken a bribe once—fifty thousand yuan to overlook some discrepancies in a client's tax returns. It was stupid. It was wrong. But it wasn't murder.

"Think." Lin Mei's voice dropped. "Think about your clients. Think about who might have connections to Judge Zhang. Think about what you might have seen without realizing it."

His mind went blank. Then, slowly, a memory surfaced.

A client. A man named Chen Wei. He'd come to the firm six months before Jiang Chen's arrest, asking for help with his accounts. Nothing unusual. But there was something about his books that didn't add up. Discrepancies that went beyond simple tax evasion. Large sums of money moving through shell companies, then disappearing.

Jiang Chen had noticed. He'd even asked about it.

And Chen Wei had smiled, patted him on the shoulder, and said, "Don't worry about it. Just do your job."

A week later, Jiang Chen was arrested for murder.

"Chen Wei," he breathed.

Lin Mei's smile widened. "Now we're getting somewhere."

"Who is he?"

"Chen Wei is Judge Zhang's silent partner. He handles the money. Moves it around. Keeps it clean. Or tries to." She leaned closer. "When you asked about those discrepancies, you became a liability. You didn't know enough to be a threat, but you knew enough to be a risk. So they eliminated you."

"They framed me for murder."

"They framed you for a murder they committed. The victim was a woman who'd found evidence of their scheme. She was going to go to the authorities. So they killed her, then pinned it on you."

Jiang Chen felt sick. He'd been executed for a crime he didn't commit, committed by the people who'd set him up. And the man who'd sentenced him was the mastermind.

"I need proof."

"Look in Chen Wei's office. There's a safe behind his desk. Inside, you'll find ledgers. Records. Everything you need." She paused. "But you can't get there as a ghost. You need a body."

"Then I'll find one."

"No." She grabbed his arm. "You'll be clumsy. You'll make mistakes. You'll get caught." She smiled. "Let me do it."

"Absolutely not."

"I can possess Chen Wei. I can walk into his office, open the safe, and walk out with everything. No one will suspect a thing."

"And then what? You'll have all the evidence. You'll hold it over my head forever."

"I'll give it to you. I swear."

"You swore before. Then you tried to possess me."

"That was before we had a deal."

"You tried to possess me five minutes ago!"

She had the decency to look sheepish. "Old habits."

Jiang Chen ran his hands through his hair. He was dead. He was standing in a cemetery, talking to a ghost who'd killed people, considering a plan that involved letting her possess another innocent person.

This was insane.

"I'll do it myself," he said. "I'll find a way."

"How? You're dead. You can't touch anything in the living world without a body."

"I'll possess someone."

"You don't know how."

"I'll learn."

"It takes years to master possession. You don't have years. Chen Wei is already suspicious. If he moves those ledgers, you'll lose everything."

She was right. He hated that she was right.

"Fine," he said through gritted teeth. "But I'm coming with you. Every step of the way."

"Wouldn't have it any other way." She grinned. "Now, let's find you a body."

She turned and walked toward the cemetery gates, her stolen body moving with an ease that made Jiang Chen's skin crawl. He followed, his staff heavy in his hands, his mind churning with doubt.

This was a mistake. He knew it was a mistake. But it was also his only chance.

They reached the gates. Lin Mei stopped, looking back at him.

"One more thing," she said.

"What?"

"The body we're going to take. It belongs to someone who deserves it."

Jiang Chen's stomach dropped. "What does that mean?"

"It means I've been watching Chen Wei for months. I know his schedule. I know his habits

title: Chapter 8
content:
CHAPTER 8: The Thirteenth Judge

The black light consumed her.

For a moment, Jiang Chen thought he'd destroyed her soul entirely. The staff had never released that much power before. It felt like he'd grabbed a live wire and channeled a thunderstorm through a woman's chest.

But when the light faded, she was still there.

Hunched. Gasping. But alive.

"You idiot," she wheezed. "You almost killed the only person who can help you."

"You tried to possess me."

"I tried to *negotiate*." She straightened, rubbing her chest where the staff had struck. "There's a difference."

Jiang Chen kept the staff pointed at her throat. "The difference is you attacking me versus me defending myself."

"Semantics." She smiled, but it was weaker now. The hit had drained her. "You're thinking about it, though. What I said. You're wondering if it's true."

He was. He hated that she was right, but he was.

The execution had been fast. Efficient. The evidence had been clear—too clear, maybe. A signed confession he didn't remember writing. Fingerprints on a weapon he'd never touched. A judge who never met his eyes.

He'd assumed it was just how the system worked. Quick convictions. Clean paperwork. No loose ends.

But what if he was the loose end?

"What's your name?" he asked.

"Does it matter?"

"I'm not calling you 'the con woman' for the rest of this conversation."

"Madam Zhao." She bowed slightly, mocking him. "Though I prefer Zhao Lihua. It has a nicer ring to it, don't you think?"

"Madam Zhao." He tested the name. It felt wrong in his mouth. "You killed two soul reapers."

"Self-defense."

"They were trying to bring you to justice."

"Your version of justice." Her eyes hardened. "You people drag souls back to Diyu, process them through a bureaucratic nightmare, and then toss them into reincarnation or punishment based on paperwork that's probably forged. You don't investigate. You don't question. You just *process*."

"That's not—"

"Isn't it? You were dead for three weeks before they recruited you. Did anyone ask *why* you were executed? Did anyone look at your case file?"

Jiang Chen opened his mouth. Closed it.

No. No one had.

Judge Liu had read him the rules. Given him the staff. Pointed him at the nearest rogue soul and told him to get to work. No questions. No background check. Just a uniform and a job.

"You see?" Madam Zhao spread her hands. "You're all cogs in a machine. And machines don't care about justice. They only care about function."

"I'm not a cog."

"You're the newest cog. The shiniest cog. But eventually, you'll get worn down like the rest of them." She stepped closer, ignoring the staff. "I've been possessing people for seventeen years. I've lived in their bodies, read their memories, felt their fears. I know how the system works. And I know that the people who killed you are still walking around, still breathing, still *alive*."

Jiang Chen's grip tightened on the staff.

"Take me to Diyu," she said. "Let me speak to the judges. I'll tell them everything. Your case gets reopened. Justice gets served."

"And you?"

"I get a lighter sentence. Maybe a chance to redeem myself." She shrugged. "It's not a perfect deal, but it's better than being wiped and recycled."

"Recycled?"

"Souls that can't be rehabilitated get broken down. Their energy gets used to power Diyu's infrastructure. You didn't know that either, did you?"

He didn't. Judge Liu had conveniently left that part out.

"Stop trying to confuse me."

"I'm not trying to confuse you. I'm trying to *educate* you." She tapped her temple. "I've seen things, Jiang Chen. Things that would make your hair stand on end. And I'm offering to share them with you. All you have to do is let me live long enough to testify."

He should say no.

He should drag her back to Diyu, throw her at Judge Liu's feet, and let the system handle her. That was his job. That was what he'd been trained to do.

But the system had killed him.

The system had framed him, executed him, and then hired him like nothing had happened.

And now the system wanted him to trust it again.

"I have conditions," he said.

"Name them."

"First: you don't try to possess me again. If I feel so much as a twitch from your soul, I'll destroy you."

"Fair."

"Second: you tell me the truth. If I catch you lying, the deal's off."

"I'm a con woman. Lying is instinct. But I'll try."

"Third: if this is a trap—"

"It's not."

"—if this is a trap, I'll make sure your soul suffers before it gets recycled. I'll drag it out for days. Weeks. I'll make you wish you'd never been born."

Madam Zhao's smile faded. For a moment, she looked almost human. Almost afraid.

"I believe you," she said quietly.

"Good."

They walked in silence through the spirit world. The sky was gray, the ground was gray, everything was gray. Madam Zhao floated beside him, her soul tethered loosely to his staff. She wasn't a prisoner—not technically—but she wasn't free either.

"How do we get to Diyu from here?" she asked.

"I open a gate."

"With the staff?"

"With my mind."

He focused. The staff hummed. A crack appeared in the gray air, widening into a doorway of swirling black and gold. Beyond it, he could see the first gate of Diyu: the Gate of Ghosts, where souls entered to be judged.

"After you," he said.

"You're not going to push me through and slam the door behind me?"

"Tempting. But no."

She glided through the gate. He followed.

The Gate of Ghosts was busier than usual. Thousands of souls lined up in neat rows, waiting to be processed. Spirit guards patrolled the perimeter, their armor gleaming with dark light. Banners hung from the ceiling, each one bearing a character from the Ten Judges' names.

Madam Zhao looked around with something like nostalgia.

"I haven't been here in seventeen years," she said. "It hasn't changed."

"You escaped from here?"

"I was sentenced to recycling. I escaped before they could process me." She pointed at a distant building. "The memory wipes are in that tower. They don't destroy your soul—they just erase everything that makes you *you*. Your memories, your personality, your sense of self. You come out a blank slate, ready to be reborn as something new."

"That sounds worse than death."

"It is. Death is just the end. Recycling is being unmade."

They walked through the crowd. Souls parted around them like water around stones. Some of them recognized Madam Zhao—their eyes widened, and they whispered to each other.

"She's back."

"The soul thief."

"She's with a reaper."

"What's happening?"

Jiang Chen ignored them. He led Madam Zhao through the main courtyard, past the Hall of Mirrors, past the Tower of Sighs, toward the central palace where the judges held court.

A spirit guard blocked their path.

"Halt. No rogue souls beyond this point."

"I'm a soul reaper," Jiang Chen said. "I'm bringing this soul in for questioning."

"By whose authority?"

"Judge Liu's."

The guard frowned. "Judge Liu is in a closed session. He can't be disturbed."

"Then disturb him. This is urgent."

"Nothing is more urgent than the judges' council."

"Tell him it's about the Thirteenth Judge."

The voice came from behind them. Madam Zhao. She was staring at the guard with an expression Jiang Chen couldn't read.

"Thirteenth Judge?" the guard repeated. "There are only Ten Judges."

"There used to be thirteen," she said. "Before the Purge. Before the system was changed. Before they erased the records."

The guard's face went pale.

"How do you know about that?"

"I was there." Madam Zhao stepped forward. "I was a judge once. Before they stripped me of my title and sentenced me to death."

Jiang Chen stared at her.

She turned to him, and her eyes were old. Ancient. Full of centuries of pain.

"Surprise," she said. "I'm not just a con woman. I'm the Ghost of Judgment Past. And I know exactly who killed you, Jiang Chen. Because I was there when they ordered it."

title: The Ripple in the Ledger
content:
The black light from Jiang Chen's staff didn't just push her away—it *unmade* her.

For a split second, he saw through her. Every layer of stolen identity peeled back like rotting skin. The middle-aged woman she'd possessed. The teenager before that. The young man. The child. Seven bodies. Seven lives. Seven deaths.

And beneath them all, a girl. Maybe nineteen. Dark hair plastered to her skull. Drowning in a river that no longer existed.

Then the light faded, and she was whole again, gasping on the ground.

"What," she wheezed, "the hell was that?"

Jiang Chen's hands were still shaking. The staff had done something he hadn't commanded it to do. It had *shown* him her truth, the way it had shown him the debtor's ledger. "That was me not killing you."

"You should have."

"Maybe." He lowered the staff. "But you're right. I need those names."

She laughed, but it was hollow now. Broken. "You just stripped me naked in front of my own soul and you think I'm going to help you?"

"I think you don't have a choice." He stepped closer. "You said you've been possessing people for seventeen years. That means you started when you were—"

"Don't." Her voice cracked. "Don't you dare pity me."

"I don't pity you. I understand you." He crouched down to her level. "Someone killed you too, didn't they? Someone young and stupid and drowning in a river that shouldn't have been deep enough to kill anyone."

Her eyes went wide. "Get out of my head."

"I can't. The staff showed me. Just like it showed you my execution." He paused. "We're the same, you and I. We both died before our time. We both got cheated."

"We're nothing alike." She spat the words. "I killed people. Real people. Innocent people. I stole their lives because I couldn't have my own."

"Then why did you stop?"

"What?"

"You've been possessing people for seventeen years. But the last one—the woman whose body you're wearing—you've been in her for three years. That's longer than all the others combined." He'd seen it in the flash. The dates. The durations. "Why did you settle down?"

She was silent for a long time. The rain had stopped. The parking lot was quiet. Somewhere in the distance, a dog barked.

"Because she had a daughter," she finally said. "A little girl named Mei. I couldn't—" She stopped. Swallowed. "I couldn't take a mother away from her child. Not again."

Jiang Chen felt something twist in his chest. "The others had children too."

"Not that I knew about. Not that I saw." She looked up at him, and for the first time, she looked like that nineteen-year-old girl drowning in a river. "I told myself it didn't matter. They were just bodies. Just vessels. But then I saw Mei's face when her mother came home from work. The way she lit up. The way she ran to her." She laughed bitterly. "I couldn't kill that. I couldn't kill *her*."

"So you stayed."

"I stayed. I got a job. I paid rent. I went to parent-teacher conferences." She shook her head. "I became a person again. A real person. For three years, I almost forgot I was dead."

"But the soul reapers found you."

"The first one found me two years ago. I killed him with a kitchen knife and a lot of luck." She held up her hands. "The second one found me last month. He was smarter. Stronger. I had to use Mei to distract him."

"You used a child as bait?"

"I used a child to survive. There's a difference." She met his eyes. "I'm not a good person, Jiang Chen. I never was. But I'm not a monster either. I'm just someone who refuses to die twice."

Jiang Chen stood up. The staff was warm in his hands, pulsing with a faint rhythm that matched his heartbeat. He could feel Diyu pulling at him, demanding he return. Demanding he bring the soul.

But if he brought her back, she'd be judged. Her memory wiped. The names lost forever.

"I need to think," he said.

"Think fast. Your bosses are probably wondering why you're taking so long."

He ignored her. He walked to the edge of the parking lot, where the city lights blurred into the dark sky. Three weeks ago, he'd been strapped to a table, watching a needle approach his arm. Three weeks ago, he'd accepted his death.

Now he was standing in a parking lot with a soul that had seen the truth of his murder, and he had no idea what to do.

His phone buzzed.

He pulled it out. A text from an unknown number.

*The ledger has a ripple.*

He stared at the screen. The message was short, cold, and precise. No context. No explanation. Just six words.

*The ledger has a ripple.*

He typed back: *Who is this?*

The response came immediately: *Someone who knows you were framed. Someone who knows the names. Meet me at the Old Temple of the City God. One hour. Come alone.*

Jiang Chen's blood ran cold. The City God temple had been closed for years. Boarded up. Abandoned. It was the kind of place where people went to disappear.

"What is it?" the woman called from behind him.

He turned. "Someone else who knows about my case."

"That could be a trap."

"Everything is a trap." He pocketed the phone. "But I don't have a choice."

"You have a choice. You can drag me back to Diyu, get your promotion, and forget about all of this."

"And let the person who framed me walk free?"

"You're dead, Jiang Chen. What does justice matter to a dead man?"

He walked back to her, stopping inches away. "It matters because I'm still here. Because I can still do something about it. And because if I don't, I'll spend eternity wondering what would have happened if I'd had the courage to find the truth."

She studied him for a long moment. Then she nodded slowly. "You really are different from the others."

"I don't know if that's a compliment or an insult."

"It's neither. It's an observation." She stood up, brushing dirt off her stolen clothes. "What's your plan?"

"First, I'm going to this meeting. Second, I'm finding out who killed me. Third—" He paused. "I don't know what third is yet."

"Third is killing them," she said simply. "That's what I would do."

"Then it's a good thing you're not me."

She smiled, and there was something almost human in it. "Take me with you."

"No."

"I have seventeen years of experience possessing people. I can help you spot a lie from a mile away. I know how criminals think because I am one." She spread her arms. "You need me."

"I need the names."

"You need more than names. You need someone who knows how to survive." She held his gaze. "I'm offering. No tricks. No possession. Just help."

Jiang Chen wanted to say no. Wanted to drag her back to Diyu and wash his hands of this mess. But she was right. He was out of his depth. He was a dead accountant with a magic staff and a lot of anger.

"I'm going to regret this," he said.

"Probably."

"Fine. But if you try anything—"

"You'll shove that staff through my soul. I know." She stepped beside him. "Lead the way, Soul Reaper."

They walked through the empty streets of the city, two dead people pretending to be alive. The woman—she still hadn't given him her real name—moved with a fluid grace that spoke of years of practice. She knew how to be human.

Jiang Chen still felt like he was faking it.

The Old Temple of the City God sat at the edge of the city, surrounded by a wall of overgrown bamboo and crumbling brick. The gates were chained shut, but the woman picked the lock with a bobby pin she pulled from her hair.

"How do you know how to do that?"

"I was a criminal for seventeen years. I learned things."

They slipped through the gates. The courtyard was overgrown, the stone paths cracked and broken. The main hall loomed ahead, its roof sagging, its doors hanging crooked on rusted hinges.

"Charming," the woman muttered.

Jiang Chen stepped into the hall. Inside, the air was thick with dust and the smell of rotting wood. Moonlight streamed through holes in the roof, casting silver beams across the floor.

Someone was sitting in the center of the hall.

A man. Old. Dressed in a simple gray robe. His face was lined and weathered, his eyes sharp and dark. He was holding a cup of tea, steam rising from the surface.

"You're early," he said without looking up.

"You texted me to come alone." Jiang Chen held his staff ready. "Who are you?"

The old man took a sip of tea. "I'm someone who's been watching you since the day you died." He set the cup down and looked up. "I'm someone who knows that your death was just the beginning of something much bigger."

"Names," Jiang Chen said. "Give me names."

"Names without context are just words." The old man stood, his joints popping. "You need to understand the full picture. The ledger. The ripple. The people who pull strings from shadows."

"Then explain it to me."

The old man smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "Your death wasn't just a frame job. It was a test. A trial run. They killed you to see if they could get away with it."

"Who is 'they'?"

"A group. A network. People who have learned that death is not the end—it's just a change of address." He stepped closer. "They're building something, Jiang Chen. Something that bridges the world of the living and the world of the dead. And they need people like you to make it work."

"People like me?"

"Soul Reapers. The judges of Diyu. The entire apparatus of the afterlife." The old man's eyes glowed faintly in the darkness. "They're going to overthrow the Ten Judges. Take control of the cycle of reincarnation. And you—" He pointed a gnarled finger. "You're the key."

Jiang Chen felt the staff pulse in his hands. "I don't believe you."

"You don't have to believe me. You just have to live long enough to see it for yourself." The old man reached into his robe and pulled out a leather-bound book. "This is the real ledger. The one that the judges don't know exists. It contains every soul that's been stolen from Diyu over the past fifty years."

He tossed it to Jiang Chen, who caught it with his free hand. The book was heavy. Old. The pages were yellowed and brittle.

"Fifty years of missing souls," the old man said. "Fifty years of corruption. And it all leads back to one person."

"Who?"

The old man smiled. "Open the book. Look at the last entry."

Jiang Chen opened the book. The pages were filled with names, dates, and locations. Thousands of them. He flipped to the last page.

The last entry was fresh. The ink still wet.

*Name: Jiang Chen*
*Date of Death: Three weeks ago*
*Location: Execution Chamber, City Prison*

title: The Thirteenth Soul
content:
The black light from Jiang Chen's staff sent Madam Zhao flying backward. She crashed through a display of ceramic dogs, shattering them into a rain of porcelain shards. Her body—no, the body she'd stolen—lay twisted among the debris, but her soul had already detached, hovering like smoke above the destruction.

"Clever boy," she said, her voice echoing from everywhere and nowhere. "But you still don't understand what you're dealing with."

Jiang Chen gripped his staff with both hands. The tremors in his fingers had stopped. Good. Anger was better than fear. "I understand you're a dead woman who doesn't know when to quit."

"Dead woman?" She laughed. "I've been dead for seventeen years. I've possessed thirty-seven bodies. I've seen the inside of four prisons, two asylums, and one embassy. You think a glowing stick scares me?"

The door behind him burst open.

Officer Wang stood in the doorway, his service pistol drawn, his eyes wide. "Jiang Chen? What the hell—"

"Get back!" Jiang Chen shouted. "She's a—"

But Madam Zhao was faster. Her soul streaked past him, a blur of gray smoke, and slammed into Officer Wang's chest. The officer's body went rigid. His eyes rolled back, then snapped forward again—but they weren't his eyes anymore. They were hers.

"Hello again," she said with Officer Wang's mouth. She raised his pistol and pointed it at Jiang Chen's head. "Now. Let's try this again. You're going to take me to Diyu, and I'm going to talk to your judges, and you're going to get your precious revenge. Or..."

She pulled back the hammer.

"Or I put a bullet in your head and find another soul reaper. There are always more of you. The turnover rate in Diyu is terrible."

Jiang Chen didn't move. He could feel his own death in the shape of that barrel, remembered the cold table, the sting of the needle, the slow fade into nothing. But he wasn't that man anymore. He was something else now.

"You know what I learned in prison?" he said.

"I don't care."

"Everyone's afraid of the thing that kills them. But once you've already died, there's nothing left to fear."

He raised his staff and swung it not at her, but at the ground between them. Black light erupted like a geyser, splitting the floorboards, sending her stumbling backward. Before she could recover, he was already moving, closing the distance in three steps, bringing the staff around in a horizontal arc aimed at her—Wang's—head.

She ducked. The staff whistled past, taking a chunk of the doorframe with it.

"You'll kill him!" she screamed.

"I'll save him."

He reversed his grip and drove the butt of the staff into her—Wang's—solar plexus. The officer's body folded. His soul flickered, and for a split second, Jiang Chen saw two faces superimposed over each other: Wang's confused expression and Madam Zhao's snarling grin.

He grabbed her soul by its throat.

"You want to possess someone?" he said, pulling her out of Wang's body like a fish from water. "Try possessing a soul reaper."

She twisted in his grip, her form writhing like smoke in a hurricane. But she couldn't escape. The staff's power held her, draining her strength, pulling her deeper into his grasp.

"You're making a mistake," she hissed. "I know things. I know who killed you. I know who paid the judge. I know the whole chain—from the hit to the cover-up to the clean-up. And if you take me to Diyu, the judges will wipe me clean, and you'll never know."

"I'll find out on my own."

"No. You won't." Her voice dropped, became something almost gentle. "Because the person who killed you isn't some random criminal. It's not a gangster or a politician. It's someone inside Diyu."

Jiang Chen's grip faltered.

"What?"

"Think about it. Who had access to your file? Who could forge evidence? Who could corrupt a mortal judge without leaving a trace?" She smiled with Wang's borrowed lips, which were now blue and trembling. "A soul reaper. Maybe even one of your trainers."

"That's impossible."
