"Is it? You were executed three weeks ago. You were recruited within days. You were trained, armed, and sent into the field with barely any supervision. Doesn't that seem a little convenient?" She tilted Wang's head. "They needed you dead and working for them. Fast. Before anyone could ask questions."

Jiang Chen's mind raced. The old woman at the gate. The judges in their chambers. The other trainees—there were only five of them. The turnover rate, she'd said. The terrible turnover rate.

"Who?" he demanded. "Give me a name."

"I told you. I'll talk to the judges."

"I don't believe you."

"You don't have to." She pointed at Officer Wang, who was groaning on the floor, clutching his chest. "Look at him. He has a family. A wife. Two kids. Picture of them on his desk. You want to be responsible for what happens to them when I leave his body with a bullet in his brain?"

Jiang Chen looked at Wang. The officer was trying to stand, his face pale, his hands shaking. He looked like a man who'd just seen a ghost—which, technically, he had.

"You're a monster," Jiang Chen said.

"I'm a survivor." Madam Zhao's soul pulsed, growing stronger again. "And I've survived this long because I know how to make deals. Here's my final offer: Take me to Diyu. Let me speak to the judges. I'll tell them everything I know about your death, and I'll submit to judgment afterward. No tricks. No escapes. You have my word."

"Your word means nothing."

"Then bind me." She extended her hands. "Use your staff. Tie my soul to yours. If I try anything, you can destroy me instantly."

Jiang Chen hesitated. It was possible. The staff could bind souls, create chains of black light that could only be broken by the reaper who forged them. He'd seen it done in training. He'd never done it himself.

"I don't know how."

"I'll teach you." She smiled. "I've been possessing people for seventeen years. You think I don't know soul magic? I've learned from every reaper who tried to catch me. Their techniques. Their weaknesses. Their secrets."

She was playing him. He knew she was playing him. But she was also the only person who could give him the truth.

"Fine." He raised his staff. "Tell me what to do."

"Focus on the staff. Feel the energy flowing through it. That's your connection to Diyu, to the judges, to the laws of death." Her voice became soft, hypnotic. "Now imagine that energy as a rope. A chain. Something physical. See it in your mind."

He did. He saw black threads emerging from the staff, winding around her soul like serpents.

"Now bind me. Wrap the chain around my soul. Tie it tight. Make it so I can't move without your permission, can't speak without your consent, can't think without your knowledge."

The threads tightened. They wrapped around her like a cocoon, pulsing with dark light. He felt her inside his mind now, a presence at the edge of his consciousness. He could feel her thoughts, her memories, her intentions.

And she could feel his.

"Interesting," she murmured. "You really don't know who killed you. You don't even have a guess."

"No."

"Then let's go to Diyu." She smiled, and for the first time, it wasn't a cruel smile. It was almost sad. "Let's find out together."

---

The journey back to the underworld was faster this time. The veil parted like water, and the land of the dead opened before them: gray plains stretching to infinity, rivers of souls flowing toward judgment, and in the distance, the black towers of Diyu rising like thorns from the earth.

Madam Zhao floated beside him, bound by the chains of his staff. She didn't speak. She didn't try to escape. She just watched the landscape with an expression he couldn't read.

"How many times have you been here?" he asked.

"Seventeen years ago, when I first died. They judged me, sentenced me to the nineteenth level of hell for my crimes. I escaped before they could take me." She paused. "I've been running ever since."

"And now?"

"Now I'm tired." She looked at him. "You think I want to spend eternity in hell? I don't. But I've been alive for seventeen years after my death, stealing bodies, stealing lives, stealing moments. That's more than most souls get. Maybe it's time to pay my debts."

They approached the gates. The old woman was there, sitting on her stool, knitting something that looked like a shroud. She looked up as they approached, her eyes narrowing.

"You caught her."

"Yes."

"And bound her to your soul." The old woman's needles stopped. "That's dangerous, boy. She can see everything you see. Know everything you know. If she finds a weakness—"

"I'll handle it."

The old woman shrugged. "Your funeral. Or hers. At this point, who can tell?" She gestured toward the gates. "The judges are waiting. They've been expecting you."

"Expecting me?"

"They always know when a reaper returns with a soul." She smiled, showing teeth like yellowed pearls. "Especially a soul as old as this one."

The gates swung open.

Jiang Chen led Madam Zhao through the courtyard, past the halls of judgment, toward the chamber where the Ten Judges sat. The air grew heavy with the weight of centuries. The walls pulsed with the screams of the damned.

"Remember," Madam Zhao whispered. "They're not your friends. They're not on your side. They're judges. They serve the law, not justice."

"I know."

"Do you? Because when they hear what I have to say, they're going to have a choice: believe a dead con woman or believe one of their own." She looked at him. "And they'll always choose their own."

They reached the chamber doors. They were black iron, carved with the faces of the dead, their mouths open in eternal screams.

Jiang Chen pushed them open.

The Ten Judges sat on their thrones, their faces hidden in shadow. The air crackled with power. The temperature dropped below freezing.

"Jiang Chen," the First Judge said, his voice like grinding stones. "You have returned with the soul of Madam Zhao."

"Yes."

"And you have bound her to your staff."

"Yes."

"A dangerous choice." The First Judge leaned forward. "But we trust your judgment. Bring her forward."

Jiang Chen stepped aside. Madam Zhao floated past him, her chains dragging on the floor. She stopped before the judges and bowed her head.

"Madam Zhao," the First Judge said. "You have been a fugitive for seventeen years. You have killed two soul reapers. You have possessed thirty-seven bodies. Your crimes are numerous and grave."

"I know."

"Have you anything to say before we pass judgment?"

She raised her head. "Yes." She turned to look at Jiang Chen. "I want to tell him who killed him."

The chamber went silent.

The First Judge's voice, when it came, was ice. "We do not discuss mortal affairs here."

"Then why did you recruit him?" Madam Zhao smiled. "Why did you give him power? Why did you send

title: Chapter 11
content:
CHAPTER 11: The Dead Man's Testimony

The black light from Jiang Chen's staff didn't just push her back. It tore something.

Mrs. Zhao's soul ripped away from him like a bandage pulled from wet skin, and in that instant of separation, he saw them. Not her memories. His. Bleeding out of her and back into him. The execution room. The needle. The cold table. The witnesses. But now there were more details he'd never noticed before. A man in the observation window. Suit. No tie. Holding a phone. Recording.

"Interesting choice," Mrs. Zhao said, rubbing her chest where the staff had struck her. Her voice was steady again. The panic was gone. "You could've dragged me straight to Diyu. But you didn't. You want to know who killed you more than you want to complete your mission."

"Shut up."

"Or what? You'll hit me again with your little flashlight?" She gestured at his staff. "We both know that was a love tap. If you wanted to send me back, I'd already be there."

She was right. He'd held back. Not consciously. But his hand had pulled the strike. Somewhere in that split second between seeing the suit man and swinging the staff, his body had made a choice his mind hadn't approved.

"You saw him too," Jiang Chen said.

"I saw everything. The room. The needle. The witnesses." She smiled. "And the man in the window. You don't remember him, do you? He wasn't part of your memory. You didn't notice him during the execution. But your subconscious did. Your brain recorded him even when you weren't looking."

"That's not how memories work."

"I've been possessing people for seventeen years. That's exactly how memories work." She stepped closer. "Every face you've ever seen, every voice you've ever heard, every detail you've ever ignored — it's all in there. You just need someone to pull it out."

"Stay back."

"I'm not going to possess you again. That was a mistake. You have too much death in you. It's like trying to swim through tar." She sat down on the curb, crossing her legs like she was having a casual conversation. "But I can help you see. Properly. Safely."

"And why would you do that?"

"Because I need you to trust me." She said it plainly. No games. "I need you to believe that I can give you what you want. Because if you believe that, you'll take me to Diyu. And if you take me to Diyu, I get to make my case to the judges."

"Your case being that I was framed."

"My case being that the mortal justice system is corrupt and the underworld should care. Which they won't, by the way. They never do. But if I can prove that a soul was sent to them illegally, they might be lenient with my sentencing."

"You'd sell me out for a lighter sentence."

"I'm a con woman. I sell everyone out for a lighter sentence." She shrugged. "But in this case, what's good for me is also good for you. I get leniency. You get the truth. Everyone wins."

Jiang Chen gripped his staff. The street was empty. The sun was starting to rise, painting the buildings in shades of orange and pink. He needed to be back in his body soon. A dead spirit caught in the mortal realm during daylight was a dead spirit that got cooked.

"One look," he said. "One. And if I see you trying to slip into my head again, I'm sending you straight to Judge Zhang with a recommendation for maximum suffering."

"Fair enough."

She stood up and walked toward him. Slow. Hands visible. No sudden movements. When she was close enough to touch, she stopped.

"Close your eyes," she said.

"No."

"Then don't. But it's easier with them closed."

He kept his eyes open. She reached out and placed her palm on his forehead. Her skin was warm. Alive. He'd forgotten what that felt like.

"Relax your mind. Don't fight me. Just let me in."

"I don't—"

"*Shh.*"

Her voice softened. Lost its edge. For a moment, she sounded almost kind.

"Let me in, Jiang Chen. I'm not going to hurt you."

He felt her presence at the edge of his consciousness. A gentle pressure. Like someone testing a door to see if it was locked. He'd barricaded himself so thoroughly against her earlier attack that letting her in felt wrong. Every instinct screamed at him to push her out.

But he wanted to know.

He unlocked the door.

Her presence flowed in like water. Not invasive. Not searching. Just... there. Waiting.

"Good," she whispered. "Now think about the execution. Don't force it. Just let it come."

He didn't want to. But he did.

The room. The table. The straps. The doctor with the needle. The witnesses behind the glass. Judge Lin in the corner, watching with empty eyes. The suit man in the window—

*Wait.*

He was looking at the suit man now. Really looking. The man's face was half-turned, talking to someone next to him. A woman. Long black hair. Familiar.

*Focus on the man,* Mrs. Zhao's voice echoed in his mind. *Don't get distracted.*

But Jiang Chen couldn't look away from the woman. She was young. Maybe thirty. Sharp features. Expensive clothes. She was nodding at whatever the suit man was saying, but her eyes were fixed on him. On Jiang Chen. On the table.

She was smiling.

Not a cruel smile. Not a triumphant smile. A sad smile. Like she was watching something tragic and couldn't look away.

*Who is she?*

He didn't know. But his heart was pounding. His hands were shaking. The straps bit into his wrists.

*She knows me.*

Mrs. Zhao pulled back. The memory dissolved. Jiang Chen opened his eyes and found himself on his knees, gasping for air. He hadn't realized he'd fallen.

"Who was that?" he managed.

"I was hoping you'd know."

"I've never seen her before."

"Your body knows her." Mrs. Zhao crouched in front of him. "Your heart rate spiked. Your blood pressure went through the roof. Your pupils dilated. That's not fear, Jiang Chen. That's recognition."

"I don't—"

"Think. Before the execution. Before the trial. Before everything. Was there a woman in your life? Someone important?"

His mind went blank. He'd had a girlfriend once. Years ago. She'd left him. Said he was too boring. Too predictable. Too safe.

That wasn't her.

"Maybe she was at the trial," he said. "I don't remember anyone in the courtroom. I was too focused on the verdict."

"Look deeper."

"I can't. I don't have time. The sun's coming up."

Mrs. Zhao glanced at the horizon. The first rays of sunlight were creeping over the rooftops. In fifteen minutes, he'd be ash.

"Take me back to Diyu," she said. "I'll help you find her."

"You'll help me?"

"Like I said. What's good for me is good for you." She held out her hand. "Deal?"

He took it. This time, when she touched him, there was no attack. No invasion. Just a simple agreement between two people who needed each other.

"Close your eyes," he said.

She did.

He focused on the darkness. The cold. The pull of Diyu. The world twisted around them, reality folding like paper, and then they were falling.

They landed hard on stone floor. The Hall of Judgment was empty. No judges. No clerks. No guards. Just rows of empty benches and the massive bronze doors at the far end.

"Where is everyone?" Mrs. Zhao asked.

"Don't know. Don't care." Jiang Chen pulled out his ledger. "I need to process you before I—"

The bronze doors exploded inward.

A figure stood in the doorway. Tall. Broad-shouldered. Wearing a black uniform with gold trim. His face was scarred, one eye milky white, the other burning with cold fire.

"Reaper Jiang Chen," the figure said. "You've been summoned."

"Summoned by who?"

The figure didn't answer. He stepped aside, revealing the person behind him.

A woman. Sharp features. Expensive clothes. Long black hair.

The woman from the window.

She was standing in the Hall of Judgment, looking at him with that same sad smile.

"Hello, Jiang Chen," she said. "I'm sorry it took me so long to find you."

Jiang Chen's staff clattered to the ground.

"Who are you?"

She walked toward him, her footsteps echoing in the empty hall. When she reached him, she stopped. Close enough to touch. Close enough to kill.

"My name is Lin Yuxi," she said. "And I'm the one who had you executed."

The world went silent.

"You—"

"I didn't have a choice." Her voice cracked. "I've been trying to find you for weeks. I bribed the guards at the morgue. I bribed the crematorium workers. I had your body preserved, waiting for a chance to bring you back. But I couldn't get to the underworld. I couldn't—"

"Why?"

She reached into her jacket and pulled out a folder. Thick. Stuffed with papers.

"Because I need your help."

"My help." He laughed. It was an ugly sound. "You killed me. You framed me. You watched me die. And now you need my help?"

"Your father is alive."

The laughter died.

"What?"

"Your father. He's been in hiding for fifteen years. He faked his death to protect you. But someone found him. Someone's been hunting him. And they're going to kill him." She held out the folder. "Unless you stop them."

Jiang Chen took the folder. His hands were shaking so badly he could barely open it.

Inside was a photo.

His father. Older. Grayer. But alive. Standing in front of a building with Chinese characters he didn't recognize.

Below the photo was a name.

*Project Soul Harvest.*

And a single line of text:

*Estimated completion: 30 days.*

"You have one month," Lin Yuxi said. "One month to find your father and stop them. Or everyone you've ever loved will die."

Jiang Chen looked up from the folder.

"Why should I believe you?"

"Because I'm the only one who can prove you're innocent." She pulled out a second folder. "And because if you don't help me, I'll have no choice but to destroy the evidence."

She opened the second folder.

Inside was a signed confession. Dated three days ago.

Signed by Judge Lin of the Supreme People's Court.

Admitting that Jiang Chen's execution was a cover-up.

"Forced testimony," she said. "I'm sorry."

Judge Lin's body was found in his office this morning.

"Someone killed him before I could get him to Diyu."

She closed the folder.

"Which means someone in the mortal world knows about the underworld. And they're cleaning up loose ends."

Mrs. Zhao started laughing.

"Oh, this is beautiful," she said. "This is absolutely beautiful. You dragged me back to Diyu for a deal, and now you're getting dragged into something way bigger than a few dead soul reapers."

"Shut up," Jiang Chen said.

"No, no, let me finish." She stood up, brushing off her dress. "I was going to help you find your killer, but now it turns out your killer is standing right in front of you, asking for your help.

title: The Price of Knowing
content:
The black light from the staff didn't just push her back. It tore through something inside her—a knot of stolen memories, years of accumulated identities. They came loose and scattered like startled birds. I saw flashes of faces, names, lives. The accountant in Shanghai. The businessman in Beijing. The teenage girl in Guangzhou. All of them screaming as their souls were pushed aside, replaced by the parasite wearing their skin.

She fell to her knees, clutching her head. "What did you do?"

"I could ask you the same thing."

"I didn't—" She stopped. Her eyes went wide. "They're gone. The memories. The ones I was holding onto. They're gone."

"That's not possible. I just hit you with a light show."

But even as I said it, I knew she was telling the truth. The frantic way she touched her temples, the panicked scanning of her own mind—it was too raw to be faked. She'd been holding hundreds of stolen identities in her head, and I'd just scattered them like dominoes.

"You broke me," she whispered.

"I was trying to stop you from possessing me."

"Well, congratulations. You succeeded." She laughed bitterly. "I can't even remember my own name now. Not the one I was born with. Not the one I chose. Nothing."

I should have felt bad. She was a murderer, a con woman, a parasite who'd stolen more lives than I could count. But watching her collapse in on herself, watching the mask crumble to reveal nothing underneath—it wasn't satisfying. It was just sad.

"I'm taking you back to Diyu," I said. "The judges will decide what to do with you."

"The judges." She looked up at me, and for a moment, I saw something other than the con artist. Something raw and broken. "They'll kill me. Or worse, they'll wipe me. They'll take everything I am and throw it into the River of Forgetfulness."

"You should have thought of that before you killed two soul reapers."

"I didn't kill them because I wanted to. I killed them because they were going to take me back to Diyu, and I couldn't—" She stopped. Her jaw clenched. "There's something in Diyu. Something that's been hunting me. Not the judges. Not the soul reapers. Something older. Something that's been following me for years."

"Is this another con?"

"No. This is the truth." She met my eyes. "I've been running from it my whole life. I stole bodies not because I wanted to, but because I had to. Every time I stayed in one body too long, it found me. It came for me. I had to keep moving, keep changing, keep hiding."

"What is it?"

"I don't know. I've never seen it clearly. But I've felt it. It's cold. Ancient. It doesn't belong to Diyu or the mortal realm. It's something else entirely." She shivered. "And it's been getting closer. The last few years, it's been closing in faster. That's why I took the accountant's body. That's why I tried to disappear into a new life. But it found me anyway. It always finds me."

I wanted to believe she was lying. It would be easier. But the fear in her voice was real. The kind of fear that comes from years of running, from never feeling safe, from knowing that something is always just behind you.

"What does it want?"

"I don't know that either. But I have theories." She paused. "I think it wants to consume me. Take everything I am and add it to itself. I've felt it reaching for me, trying to pull me into something darker than death."

"Why would it want you?"

"Because I'm not supposed to exist." She laughed, but there was no humor in it. "I was born without a soul. Or rather, with a soul that was never meant to survive. I should have died at birth. But I didn't. I clung to life, and in doing so, I became something wrong. Something that doesn't belong in the natural order."

"That's why you can steal bodies."

"Exactly. I have no fixed identity. No anchor. I can slip into anyone's soul because I have no real soul of my own." She looked at her hands. "I've spent my whole life trying to be someone, anyone. But I'm nothing. I'm a hole in the shape of a person."

The staff hummed in my grip. I could feel Diyu pulling at me, urging me to complete my mission. Take her back. Let the judges handle it. But something held me back.

"Say I believe you," I said slowly. "What happens if I take you to Diyu?"

"They'll try to fix me. Reforge my soul, mold me into something that fits their rules. But whatever's hunting me will follow. It'll come to Diyu, and it will tear through everything to get to me."

"That sounds like a Diyu problem, not mine."

"Is it?" She tilted her head. "You think the judges haven't noticed this thing? You think they don't know it's out there? They've been trying to catch it for centuries. And failing. Every time they get close, it slips away. But if you bring me to them, they'll have bait. And they'll use me to draw it out."

"Then they'll catch it. Problem solved."

"No." Her voice dropped. "You don't understand. Whatever's hunting me isn't something that can be caught. It's not a rogue spirit. It's not a demon. It's not even a god. It's something that exists in the spaces between things. In the gaps of reality. And if the judges try to trap it, they'll only make it angry."

I wanted to dismiss her. She was manipulative. She'd already tried to possess me twice. But there was something in her words that rang true. Maybe it was the fear. Maybe it was the way she talked about the thing hunting her—not as an enemy, but as an inevitable force.

"What's your real name?" I asked.

"I told you, I don't remember."

"Try."

She closed her eyes. Her face twisted with concentration. "It's... there's something there. A name. But it's not mine. It's the name of the first body I stole. A girl in a village. She was dying. I pushed her out and took her place for three years before her family realized something was wrong."

"Before that. What was your name before you stole any bodies?"

"Nothing." She opened her eyes. "I had no name. I was just a thing that existed. My mother called me nothing. My father pretended I didn't exist. I was an empty vessel waiting to be filled."

"And when did you learn to fill yourself?"

"The first time I died." She smiled grimly. "I was seven. I fell into a well. Drowned. My body was at the bottom for three days before they found me. But I didn't stay dead. I woke up in the middle of my own funeral and crawled out of my coffin. The first person I saw was a neighbor who'd come to pay respects. I was so hungry, so empty, that I reached for her soul and took it without thinking. She died. I woke up in her body."

I stared at her. "You've been dead since you were seven."

"Longer than that. I've been dead since the moment I was born. I just didn't realize it until I stopped breathing."

The wind picked up. The willow tree rustled. Somewhere in the distance, I heard a bell tolling. Not a funeral bell. Something older. Something that made the hair on my arms stand up.

She heard it too. Her face went pale. "It's here."

"What?"

"The thing that's been hunting me. It found me. It found us."

I turned, scanning the darkness. There was nothing. Just the graveyard, the trees, the moon. But the air had changed. It was thicker now. Heavier. Like something was pressing down on the world.

"Is it coming for you?"

"It's coming for both of us." She grabbed my arm. "It doesn't just want me. It wants you too. It wants anyone who knows about it. Anyone who's touched the truth."

"I don't know anything."

"You know I exist. You know I'm an anomaly. You know there's something in the gaps of reality. That's enough." She pulled at my sleeve. "We need to go. Now."

"Go where?"

"Anywhere." She was shaking now. "Run. Hide. Disappear. It's the only way."

The bell tolled again. Closer this time. And I felt it—a cold that wasn't physical. A cold that came from inside, from the part of me that was already dead.

"What's your decision?" she asked. "Take me to Diyu and let the judges bait this thing? Or help me run and learn the truth about what's really out there?"

I looked at my staff. Looked at the path to Diyu. Looked at the woman who'd killed two soul reapers and stolen more lives than she could remember.

And then I looked at the darkness that was slowly oozing between the graves like black smoke, taking shape, taking form, reaching for us with fingers that weren't quite real.

"I don't trust you," I said.

"I don't trust you either."

"Good. Then we understand each other."

I grabbed her hand and ran.

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER 13: The Thirteenth Judge

Jiang Chen's staff burned black in his hands. The con woman's scream cut through him like glass, shredding the air of the alley. But she didn't let go.

Her soul wrapped around his like smoke, invasive and cold. He could feel her thoughts bleeding into his—not words, but impressions. A courtroom. A gavel. A face he didn't recognize. The smell of bleach and stale coffee.

*You think I'm afraid of pain?* Her voice echoed inside his skull. *I've been tortured before. Better men than you have tried.*

"Stop reading my memories," he growled.

*Stop showing them to me.*

He twisted the staff. More black light. She screamed again but held on tighter. Her grip on his mind didn't loosen—it deepened, spread, found cracks he didn't know he had.

And then she found it.

Not his death. Not the execution. Something older. Something he'd buried so deep he'd almost forgotten it existed.

A room. Red curtains. A woman's voice humming a lullaby. The smell of jasmine tea.

The con woman's consciousness recoiled. *What was that?*

Jiang Chen's blood went cold. "Get out of my head. Now."

*That memory. It doesn't belong to you.*

"GET OUT."

He stopped holding back. He channeled everything—every spark of black light his staff could produce—and blasted her out of his mind. She flew backwards, her stolen body slamming into the wall of the alley. Bricks cracked. Dust fell.

She slid to the ground, coughing, laughing.

"My god," she whispered, wiping blood from her lip. "You have no idea who you are, do you?"

"I know exactly who I am. I'm a dead man with a job."

"No." She pushed herself up, unsteady, her eyes fixed on him with something that looked almost like fear. "That memory. The red room. The woman. That wasn't yours. It was implanted."

Jiang Chen's grip on his staff tightened. "Implanted by who?"

"I don't know. But whoever did it was powerful. Really powerful. The kind of power that can rewrite a soul." She took a step toward him. "You weren't just framed for murder, Jiang Chen. Your whole identity was constructed. Your memories. Your past. Your life. It's all fake."

The words hit him like a physical blow. He staggered, caught himself on the wall.

"That's impossible."

"Is it? Think about it. Can you remember your mother's face? Clear and detailed? Or is it blurry, like a photograph you've seen once?"

He tried. His mother. She was... She had... Dark hair. Warm hands. He remembered her hands, but not her face. Not her voice. Not her laugh.

Nothing.

"I remember her," he said, but his voice cracked.

"You remember the *idea* of her. There's a difference." The con woman's expression softened, almost sympathetic. "I've been possessing people for seventeen years. I know what real memories feel like. Yours are hollow. Like someone painted them on top of a blank canvas."

Jiang Chen's hands were shaking. His whole body was shaking.

*She's lying. She's a con woman. This is what she does.*

But the doubt had already taken root.

"Why would anyone do that?" he asked, his voice barely a whisper.

"I don't know. But I can find out." She extended her hand again. "Let me help you."

"Help me? You just tried to possess me."

"Old habits." She smiled, that infuriating, charming smile. "I'm offering you a trade. You take me to Diyu. I'll tell the judges everything I know about your case. But more than that—I'll help you find the people who did this to you. I have connections. Living connections. People who owe me favors."

"What kind of favors?"

"The kind that get answers."

Jiang Chen stared at her hand. He thought about the judges. About Judge Shen, who had given him this chance. About the Book of Life and Death, which recorded every soul's destiny. If his memories were fake, would the Book show it?

"Yes," he said slowly. "Let's go to Diyu."

She smiled wider. "Excellent."

"But if you try anything—"

"You'll blast me again. I know." She rolled her eyes. "You made your point. I'm a prisoner. You're the warden. Let's get this over with."

He raised his staff and sliced the air. The veil between worlds tore open, revealing the grey, endless expanse of Diyu. The cold wind hit them both, smelling of ash and rain.

"After you," he said.

She walked through without hesitation. Brave, or stupid, or both.

---

They emerged on the plains of Diyu, where the sky was a permanent twilight and the ground was grey ash that crunched beneath their feet. In the distance, the great gates of the First Court rose like black teeth against the horizon.

The con woman looked around, her expression unreadable. "So this is the underworld. I always imagined it more... fiery."

"It gets worse the deeper you go."

"I can't wait."

They walked in silence. Jiang Chen kept his staff ready, watching her every move. But she seemed genuinely subdued, her eyes taking in everything—the wandering souls, the distant towers, the rivers of black water that wound through the plains like veins.

"You know," she said, "I've sent a lot of people here. Never thought I'd end up here myself."

"Most people don't."

"Are you always this cheerful?"

"I'm dead. I've earned the right to be grim."

She laughed, a genuine laugh this time. "I like you, Jiang Chen. I hope we can stay friends after you inevitably betray me."

"I'm not going to betray you."

"Everyone betrays everyone eventually. It's human nature."

"Then why trust me?"

"I don't trust you. I trust that you want answers more than you want to punish me." She glanced at him sideways. "And I'm right."

He didn't answer. Because she was right.

---

The First Court rose before them, its doors standing open as always. But something was different. There were more soul reapers than usual. Dozens of them, standing in formation, their black staves glowing.

"Trouble," the con woman murmured.

Jiang Chen grabbed her arm. "Stay close."

They approached the doors. A soul reaper blocked their path—a tall woman with silver hair and eyes like white fire. "Halt. The court is in session. No unauthorized entry."

"I'm a soul reaper. I have a prisoner for judgment."

The silver-haired reaper looked at the con woman, then back at Jiang Chen. "You're the new one. The dead man."

"That's me."

"Your prisoner will have to wait. The judges have convened an emergency meeting."

"About what?"

The reaper's expression flickered—uncertainty, or maybe fear. "Another judge has gone missing. The Thirteenth."

Jiang Chen frowned. "I thought there were only ten judges."

"There are ten *official* judges. But there's a thirteenth. A secret one. She governs the souls that don't fit anywhere else. The ones the system can't classify." The reaper lowered her voice. "She's been missing for three days. Her court is empty. Her souls have started escaping."

The con woman stepped forward. "Three days? That's when I was captured."

Everyone turned to look at her.

"What?" she said. "I'm just pointing out a coincidence."

"There are no coincidences in Diyu," the silver-haired reaper said. "Perhaps your prisoner knows more than she's telling."

"I'm sure she does," Jiang Chen said. "But she's my prisoner. And I'm taking her to Judge Shen."

"You can't. Like I said, the court is in session."

"I don't care. This is important."

The silver-haired reaper's hand tightened on her staff. "Don't make this difficult, dead man."

"Then let me through."

The two of them stared at each other. The other reapers watched, hands on their own staves. The tension in the air was thick enough to cut.

And then the doors of the First Court swung open.

Judge Shen stood in the doorway, his robes flowing, his face carved from stone. "Let him through."

The silver-haired reaper bowed and stepped aside.

Jiang Chen walked past her, dragging the con woman with him. As he passed Judge Shen, the old judge's eyes met his.

"You found her."

"I found her. And she has information. About me. About my death."

Judge Shen's expression didn't change. "We'll discuss it in private. After the meeting."

"No. Now."

Everyone froze. No one spoke to a judge like that. No one.

But Jiang Chen was past caring. "She knows things. About my memories. About who I really am. And she told me about the Thirteenth Judge. Three days missing. Same time she was captured. That's not a coincidence."

Judge Shen was silent for a long moment. Then he nodded slowly. "Very well. Bring her."

He turned and led them through the hallways of the First Court, past the endless shelves of records, past the weeping souls, past the scribes who never looked up from their work.

They entered a small chamber at the back of the court. A table. Two chairs. A single lamp that cast long shadows.

"Sit," Judge Shen said.

The con woman sat. Jiang Chen remained standing.

"The Thirteenth Judge," he said. "Who is she?"

Judge Shen closed the door. "She is the keeper of the forgotten. The souls that don't belong to any crime, any virtue, any destiny. The ones that fall through the cracks. She governs them."

"And she's missing."

"Yes."

"Because of her." Jiang Chen pointed at the con woman.

"Maybe." Judge Shen sat down across from her. "But there's something you should know, Jiang Chen. The Thirteenth Judge isn't just missing. She's been erased."

"Erased?"

"From the Book of Life and Death. Her name. Her history. Her records. All gone. As if she never existed."

The con woman leaned forward. "That's not possible. Not even I could do that."

"No," Judge Shen agreed. "Not even you. Which means someone else did it. Someone with access to the Book. Someone within Diyu."

Jiang Chen's mind raced. "You're saying there's a traitor among the judges."

"I'm saying there's a traitor among the *gods*."

The words hung in the air like a death sentence.

The con woman smiled. "Well, this just got interesting."

Jiang Chen ignored her. "If someone can erase a judge from existence, what's stopping them from erasing me?"

"Nothing," Judge Shen said. "Which is why I'm sending you to find her."

"Find who?"

"The Thirteenth Judge. If she's still alive, she's the only one who can tell us what's really happening. And she's the only one who can protect you."

"Protect me from what?"

Judge Shen's eyes met his. "From the people who killed you. The people who fabricated your memories. The people who are now coming for you again."

The lamp flickered. The shadows stretched.

And somewhere in the depths of Diyu, a bell began to toll.

The con woman's smile faded. "What's that?"

"The death bell," Judge Shen said quietly. "It only tolls when a soul reaper has been killed."

One. Two. Three.

The bell kept tolling.

"Someone's hunting us," Jiang Chen said.

"Not hunting *us*,"

title: Chapter 14
content:
CHAPTER 14: The Thirteenth Judge

The black light from Jiang Chen's staff didn't just push her back. It tore through her like a blade through paper. He watched in horror as her soul form split, fragments scattering like startled birds, then snapping back together. She reformed in a crouch, gasping, her eyes wide with genuine fear for the first time.

"What was that?" she whispered.

Jiang Chen looked at his staff. The black light was gone, but he could still feel it humming in his bones, a vibration that wasn't physical. It felt like hunger. Like the staff had tasted something it wanted more of.

"I don't know," he admitted.

She laughed, but it was shaky. "You're a rookie. You've been dead three weeks. You shouldn't be able to do that."

"You're a con woman who's been dead seventeen years. You shouldn't be able to kill soul reapers."

She was about to retort when the air between them split open. Not a tear, not a portal. A door. A wooden door with brass hinges and a brass handle, floating in the middle of the abandoned warehouse like someone had misplaced an entrance. The door creaked open.

A hand emerged. Old, wrinkled, with long yellow fingernails. Then an arm. Then a face.

The man who stepped through was ancient. His beard reached his waist. His robes were the color of dried blood. His eyes were completely white, no iris, no pupil, just milk-white orbs that seemed to look through everything.

"You're making noise," he said. His voice was dry, like leaves crumbling. "The living shouldn't hear the dead scream."

Jiang Chen gripped his staff. "Who are you?"

"I'm surprised they didn't tell you about me." The old man smiled. His teeth were black. "I'm the Thirteenth Judge."

"There are only ten judges in Diyu."

"For now." The old man stepped forward. His feet didn't touch the ground. He floated an inch above the concrete, leaving no prints. "But the system is broken. Has been for centuries. Too many souls. Too few judges. Too much corruption." He looked at Madam Zhao. "And too many clever ghosts slipping through the cracks."

"You're not a judge," Jiang Chen said. "You're a deserter."

The old man's smile widened. "Clever boy. Wang Chuan told you about me?"

"He told me some judges abandoned their posts. Sold their seals to the highest bidder."

"I didn't abandon my post. I saw the truth." The old man spread his arms. "Diyu is a prison for the dead, but who guards the guards? Who judges the judges? I saw my colleagues taking bribes. I saw them letting wealthy souls skip punishment. I saw them torturing the innocent while the guilty bought their way to reincarnation."

"So you left?"

"I evolved. I created my own court. A court that judges the living."

Madam Zhao straightened. "You're the one who's been helping me."

"I've been helping many." The old man turned to her. "You've failed me, Zhao Liling. Two soul reapers dead, yes. But the boy is still standing. And he knows too much."

"He knows nothing. He's a confused rookie."

"He knows you killed his predecessors. He knows you're connected to someone powerful. And now he knows about me." The old man shook his head. "You've become a liability."

"Wait." Jiang Chen stepped between them. "She made a deal with me. She's coming back to Diyu to testify—"

"She's coming nowhere." The old man raised his hand. His palm was covered in symbols, characters carved into his skin, bleeding ink instead of blood. "She's going to disappear. And you're going to forget you ever met her."

The symbols glowed. Jiang Chen felt something pressing against his mind, trying to erase memories, trying to smooth over the rough edges of the past hour. He fought it. The staff vibrated in his hands. The black light flickered.

"Interesting," the old man murmured. "The staff recognizes me."

"It recognizes you're a traitor."

"Careful, boy. I've been doing this since before your great-grandparents were born. I've killed more soul reapers than Zhao Liling has conned victims."

"You've killed soul reapers?"

"Only the ones who got too close. Only the ones who refused to see the truth." The old man lowered his hand. The symbols stopped glowing. "I'm not your enemy. I'm offering you a choice."

"Some choice."

"Let me finish. I know who framed you. I know the names. I know where the evidence is buried. I can give you everything you need to clear your name, to go back to your life, to watch your daughter grow up."

Jiang Chen's heart stopped. Not metaphorically. Literally. His dead heart seized in his chest. "My daughter?"

"Didn't they tell you? You have a daughter. Six years old. Lives with her grandmother in Guangzhou. Thinks her father died a hero." The old man's smile was gentle now, almost kind. "Imagine her face when she finds out her father was executed for a murder he didn't commit. Imagine her growing up knowing the world killed her daddy because someone wanted him silent."

"Stop."

"I can give that back to you. I can give you a new body. A new identity. A new life. You can hold your daughter again. You can tuck her into bed. You can be there when she graduates, when she gets married, when she has children of her own."

"How?"

"There are bodies. People who die alone, with no family to claim them, no records to track them. I have connections. I can slide you into one of those bodies, erase your death certificate, make you alive again."

Jiang Chen stared at him. The offer was everything he wanted. Everything he'd lost. Every dream he'd buried in his coffin.

"And in exchange?"

"Nothing. Just walk away. Forget about Madam Zhao. Forget about Diyu. Go back to your life."

"You want me to abandon my duty."

"I want you to choose your daughter over a system that killed you."

The words hit harder than any attack. Because they were true. Diyu had recruited him, trained him, given him purpose. But Diyu had also let him die. The judges knew his execution was a setup. They had access to all records, all truth, all secrets. They knew he was innocent.

And they didn't save him.

"They couldn't," the old man said, as if reading his thoughts. "The judges of Diyu don't interfere with mortal justice. It's their first law. They can punish the dead, but they cannot save the living."

"Then why recruit me?"

"Because you're useful. Because you're strong. Because they can mold you into a weapon." The old man stepped closer. His white eyes bore into Jiang Chen's. "But I'm offering you a chance to be a father instead."

Madam Zhao was watching him. Her expression was unreadable. She'd known. She'd known about his daughter the whole time. That's why she'd offered to help. Not because she cared about justice, but because she knew it would break him.

"You're using my daughter against me," Jiang Chen said.

"I'm using your love for her to save you both."

"Don't twist it."

"I'm not twisting anything. I'm stating facts. You can return to Diyu, report to your superiors, hand over Madam Zhao, and spend eternity hunting souls. Or you can take my offer and hold your daughter again."

Jiang Chen's hand tightened on the staff. The black light flickered. He wanted to attack. He wanted to destroy this old man for even mentioning his daughter, for using her as leverage, for turning his greatest pain into a bargaining chip.

But he also wanted to see her. Just once. He'd never even known she existed. The woman he'd dated, Mei Lin, she'd never told him she was pregnant. She'd disappeared six months before his arrest. He'd assumed she'd left him, found someone better.

"She didn't leave," the old man said. "She found out she was pregnant and went to her mother's house to tell her parents. They convinced her to keep the baby. They convinced her not to tell you because you were involved in... questionable business."

"The bribery."

"Yes. They didn't want their granddaughter raised by a criminal."

Jiang Chen felt something crack inside him. Not his heart. His resolve. The wall he'd built between his old life and his new one. The wall that let him pretend he'd already accepted his death.

"Give me the names," he said. "The people who framed me."

"And then?"

"Then I'll consider your offer."

The old man laughed. "You're a terrible negotiator. You just told me what I wanted to hear."

"Take it or leave it."

"I'll take it." The old man reached into his robe and pulled out a scroll. Yellowed paper, red seal, black ink. "This contains everything. Names, dates, bank accounts, phone records. Enough to exonerate you and implicate everyone involved."

Jiang Chen took the scroll. It felt heavy. Heavier than it should have been. He unrolled it. The names hit him like physical blows.

Chen Wei. His boss at the accounting firm. The man who'd introduced him to the bribery scheme.

Inspector Zhang. The detective who'd arrested him.

Judge Liu. The man who'd sentenced him.

And at the bottom, a name he didn't recognize. A name written in red ink, as if to distinguish it from the others.

Wang Jian. Businessman. Crime boss. Someone who'd paid half a million yuan to have him killed.

"Why?" Jiang Chen whispered. "I don't know this man. I've never met him."

"You were auditing his accounts. You didn't even know it. Your boss gave you a file labeled 'Client X.' You spent three weeks going through it. You found discrepancies. You made notes. You didn't know you were mapping out his entire money laundering operation."

Jiang Chen remembered. A thick file. Endless spreadsheets. Numbers that didn't add up. He'd flagged them for his boss, asked if they should report the irregularities.

His boss had smiled and said he'd handle it.

Two weeks later, Jiang Chen was arrested for murder.

"He had you killed to protect his business," the old man said. "Simple as that."

"Simple."

"Nothing is simple, boy. But the solution is. Take the scroll. Take the new identity. Go to your daughter. Live your life."

Jiang Chen rolled the scroll up. He tucked it into his robe. He looked at Madam Zhao. She was watching him with something like pity.

"You're going to take his offer," she said. "Aren't you?"

"I don't know."

"Yes you do. I can see it in your eyes. You've already decided."

"She's my daughter."

"And what about the other dead? What about the souls who need you? What about the oath you took?"

"I didn't take any oath. They just handed me a staff and told me to hunt."

"And you did. You hunted me. You almost killed me." She shook her head. "You're a soul reaper, Jiang Chen. It doesn't matter if you swore an oath or not. The staff chose you. That means something."

"It means nothing if I can't see my daughter."

"Then go." She stepped back. "Go be with your daughter. Let the world burn. Let Diyu fall apart. Let the dead roam free. None of it matters as long as Jiang Chen is happy."

"Shut up."

"I'm just saying what you're thinking."

"You don't know what I'm thinking."

"

title: The Warden's Invitation
content:
The black light faded. The con woman lay crumpled on the floor, her borrowed body twitching. Jiang Chen stood over her, staff still extended, breath ragged.

"You almost had me," he said.

She laughed through broken teeth. "Almost. That's my whole life story."

He should drag her back now. Chain her soul, deliver her to the judges, collect his reward. Three more souls and he'd be promoted from probationary to full reaper. Three more souls and he'd get access to the deeper archives, the ones that might hold records of his own trial.

But her words burned in his chest.

*You were framed.*

He crouched beside her. "The names. Tell me now, or I'll drag you back and let Old Yan scrape them from your memory himself. He's not gentle."

"He'll destroy me."

"You'll be dead either way. One death is cleaner than the other."

She studied him with those calculating eyes. Even broken, even defeated, she was still working angles. He could see her mind spinning, searching for leverage.

"There's a man named Zhao," she said finally. "Minister Zhao. He handles... acquisitions. For powerful people. Land, properties, companies. And when someone won't sell, he handles that too."

"Never heard of him."

"You wouldn't have. He operates in the shadows. But I possessed his secretary two years ago. Saw his files. He keeps records of everything—every payment, every threat, every death."

"And I'm in these records?"

"You're a footnote. A minor inconvenience that was handled." She smiled. "But a footnote is still a record. If you can get to his files, you'll see who ordered your death. And why."

Jiang Chen stood. "Where do I find this Zhao?"

"You can't. He's protected. Bodyguards, security systems, the whole fortress." She paused. "But his daughter isn't."

"Explain."

"Zhao Yuxuan. Twenty-three years old. Studies at Beijing University. Lives in a dormitory, drives a modest car, tries to pretend she's not rich. But every month, her father's men come to check on her. They report everything—her friends, her grades, her boyfriends."

"You want me to kidnap a college student."

"I want you to use her as leverage. Threaten her, and Zhao will give you anything. Including access to his files."

Jiang Chen's grip tightened on his staff. "I'm not a criminal."

"You're a dead man who was killed for someone else's crime. What's a little kidnapping between ghosts?"

He turned away. The offer was poison, and he knew it. But the truth—the real truth about his death—was the only thing he wanted more than revenge.

"I'll find another way."

"There is no other way." Her voice dropped. "Listen to me. I've been possessing people for seventeen years. I've seen the inside of every government office, every corporate boardroom, every criminal enterprise in this city. Do you know what they all have in common? Paper trails. Everyone keeps records. But the really powerful people keep their records where no one can find them. Zhao's files are the only complete archive of the underground. If you want answers, he's the only door."

"And what do you get out of this?"

"Time. Every minute you spend chasing Zhao is a minute I'm not being dragged back to Diyu." She grinned. "I told you. I'm always working an angle."

Jiang Chen pulled out his communicator. The glass surface glowed with Old Yan's waiting messages. He'd been gone too long. The judges would be wondering.

"I'm going to bind you," he said. "Temporarily. You'll stay in your current body, but you won't be able to leave it. If you behave, I'll consider your deal."

"If I don't?"

"Then I'll deliver you to Old Yan in pieces."

He raised his staff. Black chains emerged from the wood, wrapping around her soul. She gasped as they tightened, fusing her spirit to the borrowed flesh.

"There," he said. "You're anchored. Don't test it."

She tested it anyway—he felt her soul push against the chains, searching for weakness. Finding none, she relaxed.

"Fine. But if we're going to be partners, you should know my name."

"I know your name. Chen Mei, age forty-seven, convicted of fraud seventeen times."

"My legal name. My real name is Lin Shu. And I never killed those soul reapers. They killed themselves chasing me."

"Doesn't matter. You're still a fugitive."

"Semantics."

He grabbed her arm and pulled her to her feet. She stumbled, still adjusting to the body's limitations. "Where are we going?"

"Beijing University. I want to see this Zhao Yuxuan for myself."

---

The campus was alive with night activity. Students hurried between buildings, laptops under arms, phones glowing in the dark. Jiang Chen moved through them like a ghost—which, technically, he was. Lin Shu followed close behind, her chains invisible to mortal eyes.

"She's in the library," Lin Shu said. "I can feel her soul signature. Weak, but distinct."

"How do you know her soul?"

"I did my research before offering you the deal. I don't make offers I can't fulfill."

They found her on the third floor, tucked into a corner with a stack of textbooks. Zhao Yuxuan was pretty in an unremarkable way—the kind of face that would blend into any crowd. She was reading something about international trade law, her brow furrowed in concentration.

"She looks normal," Jiang Chen said.

"That's the point. Wealthy criminals always hide their families in plain sight. No bodyguards, no armored cars, no obvious protection. Just a normal girl living a normal life."

"Until someone like you comes along."

"Exactly."

Jiang Chen watched her for a long moment. She was innocent. She had to be. Her father's crimes weren't hers. Threatening her would make him no better than the people who'd framed him.

But the truth. The truth was worth something.

He walked over and sat across from her.

She looked up, startled. "Can I help you?"

"I need to talk to your father."

Her face went through three expressions in rapid succession: confusion, fear, then careful neutrality. "I don't know what you're talking about."

"I think you do. Minister Zhao. Your father. I need to reach him."

"He's a businessman. A legitimate one."

"I'm sure he is." Jiang Chen leaned forward. "But I also know he keeps records. Detailed records. And I need to see them."

Zhao Yuxuan's hands were shaking, but her voice stayed steady. "If you're trying to threaten me, you're wasting your time. My father doesn't negotiate with—"

"I'm not threatening you. I'm asking for an introduction. That's all."

"Why would I help you?"

"Because I could have done worse." He stood. "I have your contact information. Think about it. If you change your mind, call this number." He slid a card across the table—one of his reaper business cards, which would connect directly to his communicator.

He turned and walked away, Lin Shu trailing behind.

"That was pathetic," she hissed. "You had leverage and you wasted it."

"I'm not going to threaten an innocent girl."

"She's not innocent. She knows what her father does. She's been benefiting from it her whole life."

"Still not her fault."

Lin Shu grabbed his arm, stopping him. "You're too soft for this world, Jiang Chen. You were too soft when you were alive, and you're too soft now. That's why you're dead."

He pulled free. "Maybe. But I'd rather be dead than become the thing I'm hunting."

Their eyes met. Something flickered in her gaze—respect, maybe. Or pity. He couldn't tell.

"Your phone," she said suddenly. "It's vibrating."

He pulled out the communicator. It wasn't Old Yan. The number was unknown, but the message was clear:

*You made a mistake coming here. I know what you are. Meet me at the East Gate in ten minutes, or I'll report you to the authorities.*

"Who sent this?" Lin Shu asked.

Jiang Chen looked at the message again. There was only one person who could have seen him for what he was.

He turned. Zhao Yuxuan was packing her books, pretending not to watch him.

"The daughter," he said. "She knows."

---

The East Gate was empty when they arrived. Jiang Chen scanned the area, searching for traps or ambushes. Lin Shu stood beside him, her chains rattling softly.

"This is a bad idea," she said.

"Most of my ideas are."

A car pulled up—a black sedan with tinted windows. The rear door opened, and Zhao Yuxuan stepped out. She was alone.

"You're the soul reaper," she said. It wasn't a question.

"How do you know about soul reapers?"

"My father told me. He's been preparing me for years." She walked closer, her eyes fixed on him. "He said someone would come eventually. Someone from the other side, looking for answers."

"Your father knows about Diyu?"

"He knows about everything. That's why he's still alive."

Jiang Chen's mind raced. This changed everything. If Zhao knew about the underworld, knew about soul reapers, then he wasn't just a criminal—he was connected to something much larger.

"What does he want?"

"He wants to meet you. Tonight." She handed him a folded piece of paper. "This address. Come alone. If you bring anyone else, the meeting is off, and you'll never get your answers."

He took the paper. "Why would he agree to meet me?"

Zhao Yuxuan smiled. It was a cold smile, nothing like the innocent student she'd pretended to be. "Because he's been waiting for you, Jiang Chen. He's the one who ordered your death. And he wants to see the look on your face when he tells you why."

She turned and walked back to the car. The door closed. The sedan drove away.

Lin Shu let out a low whistle. "Well. That escalated quickly."

Jiang Chen stared at the address in his hand. The name of his killer. The answer to every question he'd had since the moment the needle went in.

And he had no idea if he was walking into a negotiation or a trap.

"Are you going?" Lin Shu asked.

He folded the paper and put it in his pocket.

"I'm going."

---

The address led to a warehouse on the outskirts of the city. Jiang Chen approached alone, leaving Lin Shu bound and waiting in the shadows. The building was dark, silent, abandoned.

He pushed open the door.

Inside, a single lamp illuminated a desk. Behind it sat a man in a expensive suit, his face half-hidden in shadow.

"Close the door," the man said. "We have a lot to discuss."

Jiang Chen stepped inside. The door slammed shut behind him.

"I've been watching you, Jiang Chen. Ever since you came back from the dead." The man leaned forward, and the light caught his face. He was older than expected, with sharp features and cold eyes. "You've been very busy. Three souls collected. One fugitive captured. Impressive for a probationary reaper."

"Who are you?"

"My name is Zhao Wei. But you already knew that."

"Your daughter said you ordered my death."

"I did." He said it casually, like ordering takeout. "And I'll tell you why. But first, I want to make you an offer."

"I don't make deals with my killers."

"You will. Once you hear what I have to say."

title: Chapter 16
content:
CHAPTER 16: The Ledger's True Owner

The black light from Jiang Chen's staff sent Chen Cuifen flying backward. She crashed through a stack of cardboard boxes, sending old clothes and children's toys scattering across the floor. Her soul flickered, translucent and unstable.

"You're stronger than I thought," she said, pulling herself up. "But not strong enough."

She lunged again. Jiang Chen raised his staff to block, but she wasn't attacking him. She was attacking the air around him. Her spectral hands twisted, and the room's temperature dropped. Frost formed on the walls. His breath became visible.

"Cold," she whispered. "You remember cold, don't you, accountant? The cold table. The cold needle. The cold room where they pronounced you dead."

Her words found their mark. Jiang Chen felt his jaw tighten. The memory surged unbidden—the sterile white ceiling, the fluorescent lights, the doctor's detached voice reading the time of death.

"I'll show you the names," she said, circling him. "But you have to let me in. Just for a moment. Just long enough to pull the memory."

"Why should I trust you?"

"Because I've got nothing left to lose." She stopped moving. Her soul settled into a shape that almost looked human. "I've been running for seventeen years. I've conned, cheated, stolen, and killed. I've inhabited bodies that weren't mine and lives that weren't my own. I'm tired, Jiang Chen. I'm so tired."

For a moment, he saw something genuine in her eyes. Then she smiled, and it was gone.

"But more than that, I want to see the faces of the people who put you on that table. Because if they're willing to frame an accountant for murder, they're willing to do a lot worse. And I've been possessing people long enough to know that the worst people are the ones with power and nothing to fear."

Jiang Chen's grip on his staff loosened slightly. "One moment. If you try to take control—"

"You'll stab me with your flashlight stick. I know." She stepped closer. "I've been stabbed by it already. It hurts like hell. I won't try again."

He lowered his staff. "Do it."

Her soul pressed against his consciousness. It was gentler this time, less like an invasion and more like a key turning in a lock. He felt her sifting through his memories, looking past the execution, past the trial, past the arrest.

Back. Further back. To the office where he'd worked. The files he'd handled. The numbers he'd fudged. The man who'd handed him the envelope full of cash.

"Stop," she said suddenly. "Go back to that envelope. Who gave it to you?"

"Manager Zhao. He said it was a bonus for good work."

"Look at his face. Really look."

Jiang Chen focused. Manager Zhao was a middle-aged man with thin hair and thick glasses. He smiled easily and never raised his voice. He'd been the one to hire Jiang Chen fresh out of university. He'd been the one to promote him. He'd been the one to hand him the file that would eventually send him to death row.

"He's just a middle manager," Jiang Chen said. "He doesn't have that kind of power."

"Look deeper. Behind him. At the wall."

Jiang Chen's memory shifted. In the background of that moment, hanging on the wall of Manager Zhao's office, was a framed photograph. A group of men in expensive suits at a banquet. Manager Zhao was in the back row, smiling. In the center, holding a glass of wine, was a man Jiang Chen recognized.

Commissioner Lin. Deputy Director of Public Security. The man who'd personally overseen the investigation into the murder Jiang Chen was convicted of.

"That's not proof," Chen Cuifen said, but her voice had changed. There was something like excitement in it. "Keep going. The week before your arrest. What did you do?"

Jiang Chen scrolled through his memory. It was the same as any other week. He went to work. He filed reports. He took lunch at the same noodle shop. He went home. He slept. He woke up. He did it again.

"Nothing," he said. "I did nothing."

"Bullshit. Nobody gets framed for murder by a deputy director of public security because they did nothing. Think harder. What did you see? What did you hear? What did you accidentally stumble into?"

The memory was buried deep, behind the trauma of his execution, behind the horror of his trial. But Chen Cuifen's soul was persistent. She pushed past his mental walls, past his defenses, past the parts of himself he'd locked away.

And then she found it.

Six months before his arrest. A late night at the office. He'd stayed behind to finish a report, and he'd heard voices in Commissioner Lin's office. The commissioner rarely visited their building, and when he did, it was always with an entourage. But that night, he was alone. Or rather, he was alone with one other person.

Jiang Chen had peeked through the crack in the door. He'd seen Commissioner Lin handing a leather-bound ledger to a man whose face he couldn't quite see. The man's hand had a tattoo on the wrist. A snake coiled around a dagger.

"Burn this," Commissioner Lin had said. "By tomorrow morning, I want every trace gone."

The man had nodded and left. Jiang Chen had slipped back to his desk, heart pounding. He'd told himself it was nothing. A routine transfer of documents. Office politics. Nothing to do with him.

He'd been wrong.

"That's it," Chen Cuifen breathed. "That's the key. You saw something you weren't supposed to see."

"But I didn't see anything. I don't even know who the other man was."

"You don't need to know. They don't know that. All they know is that you were in the building that night, and that you had access to the commissioner's office. They couldn't take the chance."

"Then why didn't they just kill me? Why go through the trouble of framing me for murder?"

"Because killing a government employee is messy. Making it look like justice is clean." She pulled out of his mind, leaving him shaken. "You were a loose end. They tied you off. Neatly. Legally."

Jiang Chen's hands were shaking again. "The ledger. If I can find that ledger—"

"It's gone. Commissioner Lin told the man to burn it."

"But the man with the tattoo. He could still be alive. He could still know what was in it."

Chen Cuifen smiled. It was a terrible smile. "Now you're thinking like a con woman."

"I'm not thinking like anything. I'm thinking like someone who wants to know why he died."

"Same thing." She floated toward the door. "That man with the tattoo. I've seen him before. In the memories of a businessman whose body I borrowed three years ago. He runs a gambling den in the old city. Goes by the name Snake Face."

"You know where he is."

"I know where he was three years ago. Gambling dens move. But the old city doesn't change much." She paused at the threshold. "You're going to go find him. And when you do, you're going to drag him back to Diyu and let the judges rip the truth out of his soul."

Jiang Chen picked up his staff. "You're coming with me."

"Of course I am. I want to see the look on his face when he realizes the man he helped kill is still walking around."

They left the warehouse together. Chen Cuifen's soul drifted beside him, tethered by a thin thread of spiritual energy he'd wrapped around her wrist. She was still dangerous, still conniving, but for now, they wanted the same thing.

The old city was a maze of narrow alleys and crumbling buildings. Neon signs flickered in the rain. Street vendors hawked counterfeit goods and stolen phones. It was the kind of place where people went to disappear, and where people went to find things that wanted to stay hidden.

Jiang Chen stopped at a mahjong parlor on the third floor of a building that had long since lost its fire safety certificate. The air was thick with cigarette smoke and the click of tiles. Men with hard eyes looked up as he entered.

"I'm looking for Snake Face," he said.

No one answered. The mahjong tiles kept clicking.

"I'm not a cop. I'm not a rival. I just want to talk."

Still nothing. A man in a leather jacket stood up, cracked his knuckles, and walked toward Jiang Chen with the kind of casual menace that came from years of violence.

"Snake Face doesn't talk to strangers," the man said. "And you look like a stranger."

"I'm looking for a ledger. A leather-bound ledger with names and numbers. Commissioner Lin's ledger."

The man's eyes flickered. Just for a moment. But it was enough.

"I don't know what you're talking about."

"Then let me talk to Snake Face. He'll know."

"You're not listening." The man grabbed Jiang Chen by the collar and lifted him off the ground. "Snake Face doesn't talk to anyone. Snake Face is dead."

Jiang Chen's blood went cold. "When?"

"Three months ago. Someone put a bullet in his head and dumped his body in the river. The ledger's gone. Whatever you were looking for, it's gone too."

Chen Cuifen's soul pressed against his mind. *He's lying. Snake Face isn't dead. I can sense him. He's in the back room.*

Jiang Chen grabbed the man's wrist and squeezed. Hard. The man's eyes went wide as Jiang Chen's death-grip crushed bone.

"I'm going to the back room now," Jiang Chen said. "You're going to let me."

The man screamed and let go. Jiang Chen pushed past him, past the mahjong tables, past the gamblers who were too shocked to move. He threw open the door to the back room.

Snake Face was sitting at a desk, counting money. He looked up, and his tattooed wrist froze mid-motion.

"Who the hell are you?"

Jiang Chen raised his staff. "I'm the man you helped kill."

Snake Face's hand went for a drawer. Jiang Chen was faster. The staff cracked against his skull, and Snake Face crumpled.

Chen Cuifen floated forward, her soul casting a pale light across the room. "Check the desk. There's a false bottom."

Jiang Chen pulled open the drawer. Under a stack of counterfeit bills, he found it. The ledger. Not burned. Not destroyed. Hidden.

He opened it. The pages were filled with names, dates, and amounts. Bribes. Kickbacks. Payments from local businesses to government officials. And at the bottom of the last page, written in Commissioner Lin's own hand:

*Jiang Chen - case closed - payment received in full.*

The world tilted. His hands trembled as he traced the words.

"This is it," he whispered. "This is proof."

"Proof of what?" Chen Cuifen asked.

"Proof that my death was a transaction." He looked up. "I was a line item in a budget."

Before he could say more, the door burst open. A man in a dark suit stood in the doorway, flanked by two others. The man in the suit held up a badge.

"Jiang Chen. You're under arrest for the unauthorized use of spiritual energy in the mortal realm. By order of the Fifth Judge of Diyu."

Jiang Chen's grip tightened on the ledger. "I'm a soul reaper. This is my jurisdiction."

"Your jurisdiction ended the moment you made a deal with an escaped soul." The man stepped forward.

title: Chapter 17
content:
CHAPTER 17: THE MEMORY THIEF

The black light from Jiang Chen's staff sent Madam Lan flying across the warehouse. She crashed into a stack of wooden crates, splintering them like matchsticks. Dust and debris filled the air.

Jiang Chen stood over her, breathing hard. His hands wouldn't stop shaking. "You think you can manipulate me?"

She coughed, pulling herself upright. Her borrowed body was broken—arm bent at the wrong angle, blood trickling from her mouth. But her soul remained intact, a dark silhouette flickering beneath the skin. "I'm not manipulating you, boy. I'm giving you a gift."

"A gift." He laughed, but there was no humor in it. "You tried to possess me."

"To show you the truth." She spat blood. "You think I wanted your body? Please. You're a dead man walking. Literally. I was looking for a better vessel." She gestured at her broken form. "This one was getting old anyway."

Jiang Chen tightened his grip on the staff. The runes along its length pulsed with cold light. One strike—that's all it would take. One clean strike through her chest, and her soul would be bound. He could drag her back to Diyu and be done with it.

But the names. The truth.

"Tell me," he said. "Who framed me?"

"Take me to Diyu."

"Tell me first."

She laughed again, blood bubbling at her lips. "You really think I'd tell you without insurance? You're a Soul Reaper now. You work for the Judges. They find out I know something about a corrupt execution, what do you think happens to me?"

"They'll wipe your memory anyway."

"Exactly." She pulled herself to her feet, wincing as her broken arm dangled uselessly. "So I need a deal. Official. Written. Signed by Judge Zhao himself."

"You're insane."

"I'm realistic." She took a step toward him. Her soul flickered, and for a moment he saw her true face—a woman in her forties, sharp features, eyes like blades. "I've been possessing people for seventeen years. I know how the system works. The Judges aren't gods. They're bureaucrats. And bureaucrats can be bought."

Jiang Chen's blood ran cold. "You're saying the Judges are corrupt?"

"I'm saying everyone is corrupt. The only question is price." She stopped a few feet away, her eyes locked on his. "Your execution was illegal. Someone paid to have you killed. And whoever paid, paid a lot. That kind of money doesn't come from nowhere."

"Who?"

"I don't know yet."

"You said—"

"I said I could find out." She smiled through bloody teeth. "I've been possessing people for seventeen years. I've seen every secret, every crime, every hidden truth. But I haven't seen yours. I don't know who paid for your death. But I know how to find out."

"How?"

"Give me a body. Let me possess someone. Let me walk the mortal realm for seven days. I'll find your answers."

Jiang Chen stared at her. She was asking him to release a convicted soul back into the world. She was asking him to betray his oath as a Soul Reaper. She was asking him to trust a woman who'd killed two of his predecessors.

"No."

"Then you'll never know."

"I'll find out another way."

"There is no other way." Her voice dropped, soft and dangerous. "Your case is closed. Filed. Forgotten. The Judges have moved on. They don't care about one wrongfully executed accountant. They have millions of souls to process. You're nothing to them."

"I'm their Reaper."

"You're their apprentice. You've been on the job three weeks. You don't know the first thing about how Diyu really works." She tilted her head. "How do you think they found you? There are thousands of executed criminals every year. Why did they pick you?"

Jiang Chen's throat tightened.

"Think about it, boy. They chose a man who was framed for murder. A man who was killed by a corrupt system. A man who would have every reason to want justice." She smiled. "They chose you because you're angry. Because they can use your anger."

"You're lying."

"I'm a con woman. Of course I'm lying." She spread her hands. "But I'm also right. You know I'm right."

He did know. He'd felt it from the beginning—the way Judge Zhao had looked at him during the recruitment. The way the old man had smiled when Jiang Chen accepted the staff. The way he'd said "good" when Jiang Chen agreed to hunt souls.

They'd chosen him because he was desperate. Because he had nothing left to lose.

And now this woman was offering him everything.

"Seven days," he said slowly. "One possession. You find the names."

"And you don't drag me back to Diyu until I do."

"Deal."

She smiled. "Good. Now help me find a new body. This one's broken."

---

They found a body in the alley behind a nightclub. A man in his thirties, slumped against a dumpster, dead of an overdose. Needle still in his arm. Empty wallet in his pocket.

Madam Lan studied him like a tailor studying fabric. "Thin. Drug habit. Weak will." She shrugged. "It'll do."

"You can just... take anyone?"

"I can take anyone whose soul has left their body." She crouched beside the corpse. "The freshly dead are easiest. No resistance. No fight." She glanced up at him. "You're dead too, you know. I could take you if I wanted."

"You tried."

"I wasn't trying." She smiled. "I was testing."

She pressed her hand to the dead man's chest. Her soul rippled, dark smoke pouring from her borrowed vessel into the new one. The old body crumpled, empty. The new one twitched, gasped, opened eyes that weren't quite human.

Madam Lan sat up, examined her new hands. "Younger. Stronger." She flexed her fingers. "Not bad for a junkie."

"Where do we start?"

"We start with your execution record. Someone forged documents, paid witnesses, corrupted a judge. Those things leave trails." She stood, stretching her new limbs. "Money trails. Document trails. Memory trails."

"You can read memories?"

"I can read minds when I possess them." She tapped her temple. "I told you. Seventeen years. I've been inside prosecutors, defense attorneys, judges, cops, gangsters, politicians. I've seen how the system works from the inside."

"Then you already know—"

"I know pieces." She cut him off. "I know a lot of pieces. But I don't know how they fit together." She walked past him, out of the alley. "Come on. First stop: the courthouse where you were sentenced."

---

The courthouse was a concrete block in the center of the city, all gray stone and dark windows. The night watchman was asleep at his desk, snoring softly.

Madam Lan walked right past him.

"Wait," Jiang Chen hissed. "He'll see us."

"He'll see me." She gestured at her borrowed body. "I'm alive. You're not. He won't see you unless you want him to."

Jiang Chen looked down at himself. He'd forgotten—he was a soul wrapped in spirit-stuff, visible only to the dead and those with supernatural sight. To living humans, he was invisible.

He followed her through the building, past empty hallways and locked doors, until they reached the records room. File cabinets stretched to the ceiling, row after row of metal drawers.

"It'll take hours to find—"

Madam Lan wasn't listening. She walked to the third row, fifth cabinet, opened the third drawer, and pulled out a file.

"How did you—"

"I've been here before." She opened the file. "Three years ago. I possessed the head clerk. I know exactly where everything is."

She flipped through the pages, her eyes scanning fast. Faster than human. Her soul-enhanced senses processing information at supernatural speed.

"Here." She pulled out a document. "Your indictment. Signed by Prosecutor Chen Wei."

Jiang Chen took it. His hands were shaking again. The name at the bottom—Chen Wei—meant nothing to him. He'd never met the man.

"The evidence list," Madam Lan continued, pulling out another sheet. "Fingerprints. DNA. Witness testimony." She frowned. "The fingerprints are yours. They matched your apartment."

"But I never—"

"Someone planted them. The question is who had access to your apartment." She pulled out another file. "Your case was assigned to Detective Liu Feng. He collected the evidence."

"I've never heard of him."

"Of course not. He was hired for the job." She smiled. "But here's the interesting part." She pulled out a financial record. "Detective Liu Feng deposited fifty thousand yuan into his personal account three days after your arrest."

"Someone paid him."

"Someone paid everyone." She pulled out more records. "Prosecutor Chen Wei received a promotion two weeks after your conviction. Judge Zhang received a new car." She looked up at him. "Someone spent a lot of money to make sure you were convicted."

"Who?"

"That's the question." She closed the file. "The money came from a shell company. The shell company was owned by another shell company. I'll need to follow the trail."

"How long?"

"A few days. Maybe a week." She tucked the file under her arm. "But there's someone I need to talk to first."

"Who?"

"Detective Liu Feng." She smiled. "He's still alive. And I know exactly where he lives."

---

Detective Liu Feng lived in a nice apartment in the eastern district. Nice furniture. Nice view. Nice life, paid for by Jiang Chen's execution.

Madam Lan rang the doorbell. When Liu Feng opened the door, she smiled and said, "Hello, Detective. I'm here about the Chen case."

Liu Feng's face went pale. "Who are you?"

"Someone who knows what you did." She pushed past him into the apartment. "Close the door. We need to talk."

Jiang Chen followed, invisible, watching. Liu Feng looked around nervously, as if sensing something wrong, but he couldn't see the dead man standing in his living room.

"I don't know what you're talking about," Liu Feng said.

"You planted evidence in the Jiang Chen murder case. You took fifty thousand yuan for your trouble." Madam Lan sat down on his couch, crossing her legs. "I have the records."

Liu Feng's hand drifted toward his waistband. Toward a gun.

"I wouldn't," Madam Lan said. "I'm not here to hurt you. I'm here to ask questions."

"Who are you?"

"I'm a friend of Jiang Chen's." She smiled. "He wants to know who paid you."

Liu Feng's eyes went wide. "He's dead."

"He's very persistent." She leaned forward. "Tell me who paid you, and I'll leave. Refuse, and I'll make sure those records reach the right people."

Liu Feng's hand was shaking. "I don't know. I never met him. It was all done through intermediaries."

"Names."

"A man named Wang. I don't know his full name. He was just a messenger."

"Where can I find him?"

"He's dead." Liu Feng swallowed. "Killed six months ago. Car accident."

Madam Lan's eyes narrowed. "Convenient."

"That's all I know. I swear." Liu Feng was sweating now. "I took the money, planted the evidence, and moved on. I didn't ask questions."

title: The Names She Carried
content:
The black light consumed her scream.
