Jiang Chen felt the backlash through his staff—a shockwave of stolen memories, fragmented lives, seventeen years of lies compressed into a single detonation. The con woman's soul form flickered like a candle in a hurricane, her edges dissolving into smoke.

"You'll kill me!" she shrieked.

"No." He twisted the staff, channeling less power. "But I'll make you wish I had."

The light receded. She collapsed to the ground, solidifying from smoke back into human shape. Her face was pale, her eyes wide with genuine fear for the first time since he'd met her.

"What... what was that?" she whispered.

"Something I learned from Judge Li's library." Jiang Chen kept the staff pointed at her throat. "Soul Reapers have a technique called the Truth Extraction. It's supposed to be used on high-value prisoners who refuse to cooperate." He leaned closer. "It hurts. A lot. And if I do it wrong, it scrambles your memories permanently."

"You're bluffing."

"Am I?" He touched the staff to her forehead. "I've only read the theory. Never practiced it. Want to be my first test subject?"

She stared at him. Her eyes darted left, right, calculating odds, searching for exits. He saw the exact moment she realized she had none.

"What do you want?" she asked.

"The names. Now."

"And if I give them to you? You'll still take me back to Diyu. The judges will still wipe my memory. I lose either way."

"You lose either way. That's justice."

"Justice." She laughed bitterly. "You're a dead man chasing dead men for a system that executed you illegally. You don't even know what justice is anymore."

The words hit harder than they should have. He'd been a Soul Reaper for three weeks. He'd captured two rogue souls, nearly died twice, and discovered his own execution was a lie. What was he doing? Hunting ghosts for a bureaucracy that might be just as corrupt as the world he'd left behind?

No. Focus.

"You tried to possess me twice," he said. "You killed two Soul Reapers. You stole a body and a life. Whatever happens to you, you've earned it."

"I earned it." She smiled—a sad, knowing smile. "We've all earned something, haven't we? You earned a needle in your arm. I earned a lifetime of running. The judges earned their thrones of bone and jade." She tilted her head. "And the people who killed you? They've earned something too. But they're still walking around, breathing your air, spending your money."

Jiang Chen's grip tightened on the staff.

"You want to hurt me," she continued. "I can feel it. You want to make me scream. You want to drag me back to Diyu and watch the judges erase everything I am. But that won't bring you closer to the truth. It won't give you the names."

"Then give me the names. I'll find them myself."

"You won't." She shook her head. "The people who framed you aren't just criminals. They're connected. They have money, power, influence. They probably know you're dead. They probably celebrated your execution. And if you start asking questions, they'll know you're still active in the spirit world." She paused. "They'll come for you again."

"I'm already dead. What more can they do?"

"They can destroy your soul. Erase you from existence. Make it so you never existed at all." Her voice dropped to a whisper. "There are fates worse than death, Jiang Chen. I've seen them. I've collected them. I've sold them to desperate people who paid with everything they had."

She was lying. She was manipulating. He knew that. But there was something in her eyes—a flicker of genuine fear—that made him hesitate.

"Give me one name," he said. "One name, and I'll consider letting you speak to the judges before they process you."

"One name gets me nothing."

"One name gets you a chance. The judges might show leniency if you cooperate fully."

"They won't. I know how Diyu works. I've been escaping them for seventeen years. They don't show leniency. They show chains and suffering and endless punishment."

"Then what do you want?"

She looked at him. Really looked at him. Her eyes searched his face like she was reading a document, looking for loopholes, looking for leverage.

"I want a deal," she said. "Real terms. Both sides."

"I'm not authorized to make deals."

"You're authorized to use that staff. You're authorized to drag souls back to Diyu. You're authorized to decide whether I arrive conscious or broken." She gestured at herself. "I'm offering you information that could change everything. Not just for you, but for the entire system. The judges have corruption in their ranks. I can prove it."

"Prove it now."

"I can't. The proof is in Diyu. Hidden. I hid it there before I escaped the first time."

Jiang Chen stared at her. She was dangerous. She was a liar. But she was also desperate, and desperate people told the truth more often than they intended.

"What kind of proof?"

"Records. Transaction logs. Names of judges who took bribes, souls who were wrongly condemned, executions that were ordered without proper investigation." She met his eyes. "Your case isn't the only one, Jiang Chen. It's not even the most egregious. There are people in Diyu who have been suffering for centuries because someone with money wanted them gone."

His mind raced. If she was telling the truth, this was bigger than his personal vendetta. This was systemic. This was the kind of corruption that could bring down the entire underworld.

And if she was lying, she was leading him into a trap.

"How do I know you're telling the truth?"

"You don't." She shrugged. "That's the problem with trust. You have to take a leap."

"I'm not leaping anywhere with you."

"Then don't leap. Test me. Take me to Diyu. Let me speak to the judges. If I'm lying, they'll punish me worse than you ever could. If I'm telling the truth..." She smiled. "You get to watch the whole system burn."

It was tempting. Too tempting. He could already imagine it—the judges' faces when they realized their corruption was exposed, the souls who would be freed, the justice that would finally be served.

But he wasn't a fool. He'd been a con man's mark before. He knew how they operated.

"You're offering me justice," he said. "But you're a thief. You steal lives. You steal identities. You steal hope from families who just want closure."

"I steal because I have to."

"Everyone has to. We all make choices."

She was silent for a long moment. Then she said, quietly, "What if I could give you something more valuable than justice?"

"Like what?"

"Revenge."

The word hung in the air between them. Jiang Chen felt something stir in his chest—a cold, dark thing he'd been trying to ignore since the moment he'd learned his execution was a setup.

He wanted revenge. He wanted to find the people who'd framed him, who'd paid the judge, who'd watched him die. He wanted to make them suffer. He wanted to drag their souls down to Diyu and personally escort them to the worst of the eighteen hells.

But he was a Soul Reaper now. He was supposed to be better than that.

"I don't want revenge," he said.

"Liar."

"I want justice."

"Same thing, different word." She stepped closer, her chains clinking. "Justice is revenge with a stamp of approval. It's the same pain, the same punishment, the same satisfaction. The only difference is who's holding the knife."

"That's not true."

"Isn't it?" She gestured at his staff. "You carry a weapon designed to capture souls. But you could use it to destroy them. You could use it to hurt them. You have that power. And I can see in your eyes that you want to use it."

He did. He hated that she could see it.

"I'm not like you."

"You're exactly like me." She smiled. "You're just too scared to admit it."

The accusation burned. He wanted to deny it, to prove her wrong, to show her that he was different. But the words wouldn't come.

Because she was right.

He wanted revenge. He wanted blood. He wanted to watch his killers suffer the way he'd suffered on that execution table.

And that frightened him more than anything else.

"Give me the names," he said again, his voice hoarse.

"Not yet."

"Then I'm taking you to Diyu."

"Fine." She held out her chains. "Take me. But when the judges process me, they'll find everything I've hidden. They'll cover it up. They'll destroy the evidence. And you'll never know who killed you."

She was right again. If the corruption went as deep as she claimed, the judges would protect themselves. They'd erase the records. They'd silence her permanently.

And he'd be left with nothing.

"One name," he said. "Give me one name, and I'll consider a longer conversation before we reach Diyu."

She studied him. Weighing. Calculating.

"Chen Wei," she said finally.

The name hit him like a physical blow.

Chen Wei. His former boss. The man who'd introduced him to the world of bribes and kickbacks. The man who'd promised him a promotion, a better life, a future.

The man who'd testified against him at trial.

"Chen Wei framed you," she continued. "He paid the judge. He fabricated the evidence. He arranged everything."

"Why?"

"Because you knew too much. You'd seen something you weren't supposed to see. A ledger. A transaction. Something that could have brought down a network of corruption that stretched from your company all the way to the provincial government."

Jiang Chen's hands were shaking. He remembered the ledger. He remembered finding it in the back of a filing cabinet, numbers that didn't add up, names he didn't recognize.

He'd been planning to report it. He'd been planning to do the right thing.

And then he'd been arrested for murder.

"Chen Wei," he repeated.

"Yes."

"I'm going to kill him."

"No." She grabbed his arm. "If you kill him, you become a murderer. You prove the system right. You give them exactly what they want."

"Then what do I do?"

"Expose him. Bring him to justice. Use your position as a Soul Reaper to gather evidence, to find witnesses, to build a case that even the judges can't ignore." She squeezed his arm. "Don't become the monster they made you. Become the judge they fear."

He looked at her. For a moment, he saw something in her eyes that wasn't calculation or manipulation.

It was sincerity.

"You're helping me," he said. "Why?"

"Because I've been running for seventeen years. I'm tired. And maybe..." She paused. "Maybe I want to do one good thing before I'm erased."

He didn't believe her. He couldn't. She was a liar. A thief. A manipulator.

But he needed her.

"Fine," he said. "We'll talk more on the way to Diyu. You give me everything you know, and I'll make sure the judges hear your full testimony before they make any decisions."

"That's all I ask."

She held out her hand again.

This time, he took it.

And as they walked together through the gray landscape between life and death, Jiang Chen couldn't shake

title: Chapter 19
content:
CHAPTER 19: The Price of a Name

The black light from Jiang Chen's staff sent Ling Wei hurtling backward. She crashed through a stack of cardboard boxes in the alley, scattering rotten vegetables and discarded newspapers across the wet asphalt. Her stolen body convulsed, smoke rising from her chest where the staff had struck.

But she was still conscious. Still aware. Still *inside his head*.

"You'll kill me," she gasped, clutching her chest. "Then you'll never know."

Jiang Chen advanced, the staff crackling with residual energy. The burn on his hand from earlier had stopped hurting, replaced by a cold numbness that spread up his arm. He didn't care. "I don't need you alive to read your ledger."

"You can't read what doesn't exist." She laughed, blood trickling from her lip. "I burned it. After I killed the last reaper. Every name, every crime, every secret—all in my head now. You kill me, it dies with me."

He stopped. The rain had started again, falling in sheets that blurred the neon signs overhead. The alley smelled like garbage and blood and something else—ozone, maybe. The smell of souls leaving bodies.

"You're bluffing."

"I'm a con woman." She pushed herself up, leaning against the wall. "Bluffing is my nature. But I'm also a survivor. And survivors keep leverage." She tapped her temple. "I've been inside two hundred and forty-seven people. I've seen their bank accounts, their affairs, their crimes. I know where the bodies are buried—literally, in some cases. You think I don't have insurance?"

Jiang Chen's grip tightened on the staff. "The judges can extract your memories."

"They can try. But soul extraction is messy. They might miss something. They might destroy something. Or they might get it right and still not care." She smiled, her teeth stained red. "Your case isn't their priority, boy. You're one of thousands. They'll process me, wipe me, and move on. You'll never get your answers."

He wanted to kill her. The urge was physical—a pressure behind his eyes, a tightness in his chest. She'd killed two of his kind. She'd stolen lives. She'd—

*She knew who framed him.*

"What do you want?" The words tasted bitter.

"I want to live."

"Not possible. You're dead. You're a fugitive soul. The judges will—"

"Then I want time." She pushed off the wall, standing unsteadily. "Give me three days. I'll give you one name per day. The last name will be the person who ordered your death."

"And after three days?"

"I'll go quietly. No fight. No possession attempts. I'll let you drag me back to Diyu and face whatever judgment they give me."

Jiang Chen studied her. Her face was earnest, but that meant nothing. She'd worn a thousand faces. She'd played a thousand roles. This was just another performance.

"Why should I trust you?"

"You shouldn't." She wiped blood from her chin. "But you're going to anyway, because you have nothing else. The judges haven't investigated your case. They won't. You're just another dead man to them, and dead men don't get justice."

The words hit harder than they should have. She was right. Judge Song had been clear—his training was the priority. His past was irrelevant. The underworld didn't care about the living world's injustices.

"What's the first name?"

"Not yet." She reached into her pocket and pulled out a crumpled business card. "Tomorrow night. Midnight. The Golden Lotus Hotel, room 307. I'll be there. I'll give you the name then."

"Where are you going now?"

"To find a new body." She tossed the card at him. "This one's dying. You hit me harder than you think."

He caught the card. It was cheap, the kind they handed out at street corners. The address was in the old district, near the river.

"One more thing," she said, turning to leave. "Don't follow me. Don't try to track me. If I feel someone watching, the deal's off. I'll find a way to destroy my memories, and you'll get nothing."

She limped down the alley, disappearing into the rain.

Jiang Chen stood there for a long moment, the staff humming in his hand. He should go after her. He should drag her back to Diyu and let the judges deal with her. He should—

*He should find out who killed him.*

He pocketed the card and walked back to the main street. The night was young. The city was alive. And somewhere out there, a man was sleeping peacefully, thinking he'd gotten away with murder.

---

The next day passed in a blur.

Jiang Chen went through the motions—ate breakfast, avoided his landlord, pretended to look for jobs. But his mind was elsewhere. Ling Wei's words echoed in his head. *Your death wasn't legal. The evidence was fabricated. The judge was paid.*

He'd always known something was wrong. The case against him had been too clean, too perfect. The evidence had appeared exactly when needed. The witnesses had been too certain. The judge had refused to hear his appeals.

But knowing and proving were different things.

At dusk, he felt the pull—the call of Diyu. His body grew heavy, his vision blurred, and then he was standing in the Hall of Judgment, facing Judge Song.

"You're late," Song said, not looking up from his scrolls.

"I had a situation."

"Explain."

Jiang Chen hesitated. Should he tell the truth? Should he mention Ling Wei, the deal, the names? The judges had rules. They wouldn't approve of him making deals with fugitives.

But they also wouldn't approve of him investigating his own death.

"I tracked an escaped soul last night," he said carefully. "It was a difficult capture."

Song looked up, his ancient eyes narrowing. "Difficult how?"

"Possession. The soul had taken a body. It took longer to extract."

"Hmm." Song returned to his scrolls. "You're healing well. Your soul density has increased. The staff is responding to you."

"Yes, Judge."

"Your training continues tonight. We'll be working on soul projection—the ability to separate your consciousness from your body while keeping your physical form alive."

Jiang Chen nodded, but his mind was elsewhere. Midnight. The Golden Lotus Hotel. Room 307.

*One name per day.*

---

The training lasted five hours.

By the time Jiang Chen returned to the mortal world, it was nearly eleven. His body felt strange—disconnected, like he was wearing someone else's skin. The soul projection technique left him nauseous and lightheaded.

He grabbed a taxi to the old district.

The Golden Lotus Hotel was a crumbling building wedged between a noodle shop and a pawn shop. The neon sign flickered, the "L" burned out so it read "GOLDEN OTUS." The lobby smelled like cigarettes and desperation.

The night clerk didn't look up as Jiang Chen walked past. He climbed the stairs to the third floor, his footsteps echoing in the empty hallway.

Room 307 was at the end. The door was slightly ajar.

He pushed it open.

The room was dark. The curtains were drawn. The only light came from a flickering television, the volume muted, showing a late-night infomercial.

Ling Wei sat on the bed, cross-legged, wearing a different body. This one was younger—a woman in her twenties, with short hair and a face that looked like it had been crying recently.

"New host?" Jiang Chen asked.

"Old one died an hour ago." She sounded tired. "This one was dying anyway. Cancer. I just sped things up."

"That's not how possession works."

"It is when you're good at it." She gestured to a chair. "Sit. You're early. I like that."

He didn't sit. "The name."

"Direct. I like that too." She reached into her pocket and pulled out a folded piece of paper. "First name. He's not the mastermind, but he's a piece of the puzzle. The chain of evidence that convicted you—he forged the forensic report."

Jiang Chen took the paper. The name was written in precise characters:

*Lin Wei. Forensic Analyst. City Police Department.*

"Lin Wei," he repeated. "I don't know him."

"You wouldn't. He's a nobody. A lab technician who was paid to say the blood on your clothes matched the victim's. He's the foundation of the case against you."

"How do I find him?"

"He works the night shift. City morgue. He's there right now." She smiled. "I told you I'd deliver."

Jiang Chen stared at the paper. This was it. The first thread. Pull it, and the whole tapestry might unravel.

"Why are you helping me?"

"Because I'm selfish." She leaned back on the bed. "I've been running for seventeen years. I'm tired. But I'm also proud. I don't want to die having accomplished nothing. If I can help you find justice, maybe—" She shrugged. "Maybe it means something."

"It doesn't change what you've done."

"No. It doesn't." She closed her eyes. "But it's all I have left."

Jiang Chen turned to leave.

"One more thing," she said. "Be careful with Lin Wei. He's not the only one who was paid. There are people watching him. If you're too obvious, they'll know someone's investigating."

"I can handle myself."

"I know you can. But can you handle them?" She opened her eyes. "The people who framed you—they're not petty criminals. They're professionals. They've done this before. They'll kill you again if they have to."

"I'm already dead."

"Dead things can be unmade." She pulled the blanket over herself. "Good luck, Soul Reaper. You're going to need it."

He left without saying goodbye.

---

The city morgue was a concrete building on the north side of town, surrounded by a chain-link fence and dying trees. Jiang Chen approached from the shadows, the staff hidden under his coat.

The night was quiet. Too quiet.

He found the side entrance unlocked. The hallway inside was sterile, white tiles reflecting fluorescent light. The air smelled of formaldehyde and decay.

Lin Wei's office was at the end of the hall. The door was closed, but light shone through the frosted glass.

Jiang Chen approached carefully, listening. No voices. No movement.

He pushed the door open.

Lin Wei was at his desk, slumped forward, a pen still in his hand. For a moment, Jiang Chen thought he was asleep.

Then he saw the blood.

Someone had slit his throat. Cleanly. Professionally.

And on the desk, written in blood, was a message:

*STOP LOOKING.*

Jiang Chen's phone buzzed. An unknown number.

He answered.

"You're too late." Ling Wei's voice was strained, panicked. "They found me. They're here."

The line went dead.

He ran.

title: The Witness in the Walls
content:
The black light consumed her scream.

Jiang Chen felt his staff pulse with cold fire, felt the con woman's soul unraveling from his mind like silk threads pulled from skin. She was strong—stronger than any soul he'd faced—but the staff was older than her, older than Diyu itself, and it did not tolerate invasion.

She collapsed to her knees, gasping. Her stolen body shimmered, the edges of her form flickering between solid and spectral. The little girl's face twisted, contorted, became a mask of raw fury.

"You broke our deal," she spat.

"You tried to possess me."

"I *am* a possession artist. It's what I do."

Jiang Chen kept the staff pointed at her throat. His head throbbed where she'd dug into his memories. She'd seen his execution. She'd seen the needle. The witnesses. The cold table. And she'd seen something else—something he'd buried so deep he'd almost forgotten it himself.

The face of a man in the observation room.

A man he hadn't recognized at the time.

A man who'd smiled.

"You saw who was there," Jiang Chen said. It wasn't a question.

"Of course I did. I saw everything." She rubbed her temples. The little girl's hands looked too small for the gesture. "I saw the judge take the envelope. I saw the prosecutor read the fabricated evidence. I saw the witnesses lie under oath. And I saw the man who paid for all of it."

"Who?"

"You're not ready."

"I'll decide that."

"No." She looked up at him, and for the first time, there was no mockery in her eyes. Only something that looked almost like pity. "You're not ready because you'll do something stupid. You'll go after him directly. You'll get yourself killed—permanently. And then where will I be?"

"Still in Diyu."

"Charming." She stood slowly, brushing imaginary dust from her stolen clothes. "I'm not asking you to trust me. I'm asking you to be smart. Take me to the judges. Let me tell them what I know. They'll investigate your case. They'll find the truth."

"And if they don't?"

"Then you'll have a very angry, very resourceful con woman on your side. Trust me—I'm more useful as an ally than an enemy."

Jiang Chen stared at her. The staff trembled in his grip. Every instinct told him to end this now, to drag her soul back to Diyu and let the judges sort it out. But her words gnawed at him like rats in the walls.

Someone had killed him.

Someone had paid to have him executed.

And that someone was still walking free.

"One wrong move," he said, "and I'll send you straight to the 18th level. They have a special chamber for soul stealers."

"I'm sure they do." She smiled—the smile of a woman who'd spent seventeen years smiling her way out of worse situations. "Now then. How do we get to Diyu from here?"

---

The journey took them through the veils between worlds.

Jiang Chen had only made the crossing twice before—once when he died, once when he was sent back as a reaper. Both times, he'd been guided by judges or senior reapers. This time, he was alone with a soul stealer and a staff that hummed with ancient power.

The veils were not kind to the living.

They shimmered like heat haze over a summer road, distorting space and time. The air grew thick, heavy, saturated with the weight of a billion souls pressing against the membrane between worlds. Shadows moved in the corners of his vision—lost spirits, trapped souls, things that had no names and wanted none.

"Stay close," Jiang Chen said.

"Where would I go?" the con woman asked. She'd taken the form of a middle-aged man now—someone she'd possessed years ago, perhaps, whose shape she'd memorized. "I don't know the way."

"Then shut up and follow."

The path twisted. It coiled and turned back on itself like a snake eating its own tail. Jiang Chen followed the pull of the staff, the ancient connection that bound reapers to Diyu. It was like following a thread through a labyrinth of mirrors.

And then they were there.

Diyu spread before them like a city carved from bone and shadow. Towers rose into a sky that had no sun. Rivers of souls flowed through channels of black stone, their whispers rising like wind through dead leaves. The gates loomed—massive, ancient, covered in inscriptions that predated human civilization.

The guardian at the gate was new.

He stood nine feet tall, his body wrapped in chains that clinked with every breath. His face was a mask of bronze, featureless except for two pinpricks of red light where eyes should be. He held a spear made of crystallized screams.

"Halt," he said. His voice was the sound of metal grinding against metal. "State your purpose."

"I'm a soul reaper," Jiang Chen said. "Returning from a mission."

The guardian's red eyes shifted to the con woman. "And this?"

"A prisoner."

The guardian tilted his head. The chains around his body tightened, then loosened. "I have no record of this retrieval."

"It's recent."

"The records are never late." The guardian stepped closer. His spear hummed. "There is an imbalance in your story."

Jiang Chen's hand tightened on his staff. "I don't know what you're talking about."

"You know exactly what I'm talking about." The guardian's mask split open, revealing a mouth filled with rows of needle-thin teeth. "You have been corrupted."

"What?"

"The soul stealer has touched you. She has seen your death. She has planted seeds in your mind." The guardian raised his spear. "You are no longer a reaper of Diyu. You are a liability."

"I'm carrying out my mission—"

"Your mission was to retrieve the soul. You have retrieved her." The guardian's red eyes flared. "Your mission is complete. Hand her over."

Jiang Chen looked at the con woman. She was watching him with an expression he couldn't read—wary, calculating, waiting to see which way he would jump.

The guardian took another step. "Hand her over, and I will let you return to the mortal realm. Your training will continue. Your status will remain intact."

"And if I don't?"

"Then you will be judged as a traitor."

The word hung in the air like smoke. Traitor. It was the same word they'd used at his execution. The same word the judge had spoken before reading the sentence. The same word that had followed him into death.

"Let me talk to Judge Zhao," Jiang Chen said. "She'll confirm my mission."

"Judge Zhao is... unavailable."

"What does that mean?"

"It means she has been recalled for review." The guardian's voice dropped. "Your case has attracted attention, reaper. There are those in the higher courts who believe you were appointed prematurely. There are those who believe you were appointed... irregularly."

A cold hand gripped Jiang Chen's heart. "Irregularly how?"

"The judge who sentenced you to death has been investigated. He was found to have accepted bribes. His cases are being reviewed." The guardian's red eyes fixed on Jiang Chen's face. "Yours was one of them."

The world tilted.

"You knew," the con woman whispered.

"No," Jiang Chen said. "I didn't—"

"You knew your death was a setup. You knew someone paid to have you killed. And you still took the job." She laughed, but it was hollow. "You really are a fool."

"Shut up."

"You're working for the same system that killed you!"

"I said shut up!"

The staff exploded with black light. The con woman staggered back, shielding her eyes. The guardian raised his spear, ready to strike.

But Jiang Chen wasn't aiming at either of them.

He aimed at the gates.

The black light slammed into the ancient inscriptions, cracking the stone. The gates shuddered. Alarms began to wail—deep, resonant bells that echoed through every level of Diyu.

"What are you doing?!" the guardian roared.

"I'm not going back," Jiang Chen said. His voice was steady. Calm. Dead. "I'm not going to let them bury me again. I'm going to find the truth. And I'm going to find the man who killed me."

"You'll never leave Diyu alive—"

"Then I'll leave dead."

He grabbed the con woman's arm and pulled her through the broken gates as the guardian's spear whistled past his ear, missing by inches.

They ran.

Behind them, the bells of Diyu rang out across the underworld, summoning every reaper, every guardian, every judge to hunt them down.

Ahead of them, the darkness stretched into eternity.

And somewhere in the mortal world, a man was still smiling.
